This tendency to gravitate toward complexity is perpetually at war with the need to prioritize. This difficult quest—the need to wrestle priorities out of complexity—was exactly the situation that James Carville faced in the Clinton campaign of 1992.

“如果你说三件事，

那就等于什么都没说。”

“If You Say Three Things,

You Don’t Say Anything.”

政治竞选是决策焦虑的温床。如果你觉得你的组织问题重重，不妨想象一下这个挑战：你必须从零开始建立一个全国性的组织，而且主要依靠无偿且技能匮乏的志愿者。你只有大约一年的时间来组建团队，并确保甜甜圈供应充足。组织里的每个人都必须步调一致，但你却没有多少时间来排练。媒体每天都在催促你唱新歌。更糟糕的是，你还得时刻提防对手，他们会抓住你说的每一个错误之处大做文章。

A political campaign is a breeding ground of decision angst. If you think your organization has problems, imagine this challenge: You must build a nationwide organization from scratch, using primarily unpaid and largely unskilled workers. You’ve got about a year to pull the team together and line up an endless supply of doughnuts. Everyone in the organization needs to sing from the same hymnal, but you don’t have much time to rehearse the choir. And the media prod you to sing a new song every day. To make matters worse, you must constantly contend with opponents who will seize on every errant word.

比尔·克林顿1992年的竞选活动是顽固思想在复杂环境下发挥作用的经典案例。这场竞选不仅本身就充满各种复杂因素，克林顿本人还增添了一些新的变数。首先是“花瓶效应”事件，这里就不赘述了。其次，克林顿天生是个政策专家，这意味着他倾向于对几乎每一个被问到的问题都发表长篇大论，而不是专注于几个关键原则。

Bill Clinton’s 1992 campaign was a classic example of sticky ideas at work in a difficult environment. Not only did the campaign have the normal set of complexities, Clinton himself added a few new wrinkles. First, there were the “bimbo eruptions,” which need not be reexamined here. Second, Clinton was a policy wonk by nature, which meant that he was inclined to pontificate on virtually every issue that he was asked about, instead of staying focused on a few key principles.

作为克林顿的关键政治顾问，詹姆斯·卡维尔不得不应对这种复杂局面。一天，他难以集中注意力，便在白板上写下三句话，让所有竞选工作人员都能看到。其中一句是“问题在于经济，笨蛋！”。这句话后来成为克林顿竞选成功的核心。

As his key political adviser, James Carville had to cope with this complexity. One day, struggling to maintain his focus, he wrote three phrases on a whiteboard for all the campaign workers to see. One of the phrases on the impromptu list was “It’s the economy, stupid.” This message would become the core of Clinton’s successful campaign.

“愚蠢”一词被添加进来，作为对竞选工作人员的嘲讽。他告诫自己不要偏离重点。“这很简单，也很谦逊，”卡维尔解释说。“我当时想说的是，‘别太自作聪明，别觉得自己很了不起。咱们还是记住基本原则吧。’”

The word “stupid” was added as a taunt to the campaign workers themselves, reminding them not to lose focus on what was important. “It was simple and it was self-effacing,” Carville explained. “I was trying to say, ‘Let’s don’t be too clever here, don’t come down here thinking we’re too smart. Let’s just remember the basics.’”

这种聚焦的需求也延伸到了比尔·克林顿本人，或许尤其如此。有一次，克林顿感到沮丧，因为有人建议他停止谈论平衡预算，尽管1992年总统大选的第三方候选人罗斯·佩罗因其在平衡预算问题上的立场而备受关注。克林顿说：“我已经谈论这些问题两年了，为什么现在佩罗参选了，我就应该停止谈论它们呢？”克林顿的顾问不得不告诉他：“信息传递必须分清轻重缓急。如果你只说三件事，那就等于什么都没说。”

The need for focus extended to Bill Clinton himself, perhaps especially to Clinton himself. At one point, Clinton was frustrated that he’d been advised to stop talking about balanced budgets despite the fact that Ross Perot, the third-party candidate for president in 1992, was getting positive attention for his stand on the balanced budget. Clinton said, “I’ve been talking about these things for two years, why should I stop talking about them now because Perot is in?” Clinton’s advisers had to tell him, “There has to be message triage. If you say three things, you don’t say anything.”

“问题出在经济上，笨蛋！”这是克林顿竞选故事的导语——而且这确实是个好导语，因为1992年美国经济正深陷衰退泥潭。但如果“问题出在经济上，笨蛋！”是导语，那么平衡预算的必要性就不能再成为导语了。卡维尔必须阻止克林顿掩盖真正的导语。

“It’s the economy, stupid” was the lead of the Clinton story—and it was a good one, because in 1992 the U.S. economy was mired in a recession. But if “It’s the economy, stupid” is the lead, then the need for a balanced budget can’t also be the lead. Carville had to stop Clinton from burying the lead.

决策瘫痪

Decision Paralysis

为什么确定优先级这么难？从理论上讲，这似乎并不难。你把重要的目标放在次要目标之前，把“至关重要”的目标放在“有益”的目标之前。

Why is prioritizing so difficult? In the abstract, it doesn’t sound so tough. You prioritize important goals over less important goals. You prioritize goals that are “critical” ahead of goals that are “beneficial.”

但如果我们无法分辨什么是“关键的”，什么是“有益的”呢？有时，这一点并不明显。我们常常需要在不同的“未知”选项之间做出选择。这种复杂性可能会让人不知所措。事实上，心理学家发现，过多的复杂性和不确定性会导致人们做出非理性的决定。

But what if we can’t tell what’s “critical” and what’s “beneficial”? Sometimes it’s not obvious. We often have to make decisions between one “unknown” and another. This kind of complexity can be paralyzing. In fact, psychologists have found that people can be driven to irrational decisions by too much complexity and uncertainty.

1954年，经济学家L.J.萨维奇描述了他认为的人类决策的基本原则，他称之为“确定性原则”。他用以下例子来说明：一位商人他正考虑买房。临近选举，他最初认为选举结果可能会影响他是否愿意购买。为了明确自己的决定，他仔细考虑了两种可能的情况。他决定，如果共和党获胜，他就买；如果民主党获胜，他也一样。既然无论结果如何他都打算买，那么即使不知道结果，他也决定购买。这个决定似乎合情合理——很少有人会质疑萨维奇的逻辑。

In 1954, the economist L. J. Savage described what he perceived as a basic rule of human decision-making. He called it the “sure-thing principle.” He illustrated it with this example: A businessman is thinking about buying a piece of property. There’s an election coming up soon, and he initially thinks that its outcome could be relevant to the attractiveness of the purchase. So, to clarify his decision, he thinks through both scenarios. If the Republican wins, he decides, he’ll buy. If the Democrat wins, he’ll do the same. Seeing that he’d buy in either scenario, he goes forward with the purchase, despite not knowing the outcome. This decision seems sensible—not many people would quibble with Savage’s logic.

两位心理学家对此争论不休。阿莫斯·特沃斯基和埃尔达·沙菲尔后来发表了一篇论文，证明“确定性原则”并非总是确定的。他们发现，即使不确定性与结果无关，例如商人的购买行为，仅仅存在不确定性似乎也会改变人们的决策方式。举例来说，假设你是一名大学生，在圣诞假期前几周刚刚结束了一场重要的期末考试。你为了这场考试已经复习了好几周，因为这门课对你未来的职业生涯至关重要。

Two psychologists quibbled. Amos Tversky and Eldar Shafir later published a paper proving that the “sure-thing principle” wasn’t always a sure thing. They uncovered situations where the mere existence of uncertainty seemed to alter how people made decisions—even when the uncertainty was irrelevant to the outcome, as with the businessman’s purchase. For instance, imagine that you’re in college and you’ve just completed an important final exam a couple of weeks before the Christmas holidays. You’d been studying for this exam for weeks, because it’s in a subject that’s important to your future career.

考试成绩要两天后才能出来。与此同时，你发现假期期间去夏威夷度假的价格非常划算。你有三个选择：今天就买，今天放弃，或者支付五美元的手续费锁定两天价格，这样你就可以在拿到成绩后再做决定。你会怎么做？

You’ve got to wait two days to get the exam results back. Meanwhile, you see an opportunity to purchase a vacation during the holidays to Hawaii at a bargain-basement price. Here are your three options: You can buy the vacation today, pass on it today, or pay a five-dollar fee to lock in the price for two days, which would allow you to make your decision after you got your grade. What would you do?

你可能和最初实验中面临同样选择的学生一样，希望在做决定前知道考试结果。因此，特沃斯基和沙菲尔干脆为两组参与者消除了这种不确定性。他们事先告知了这两组学生考试成绩。一些学生被告知他们通过了考试，其中57%的学生选择参加旅行（毕竟，这的确是一次不错的庆祝）。另一些学生被告知他们考试不及格，其中54%的学生选择参加旅行（毕竟，这的确是一次不错的庆祝）。（恢复良好）。无论通过考试还是未通过考试，所有人都想尽快去夏威夷。

You may feel some desire to know the outcome of your exam before you decide, as did the students who faced this choice in the original experiment. So Tversky and Shafir simply removed this uncertainty for two groups of participants. These groups were told up front how they did on the exam. Some students were told that they passed the exam, and 57 percent of them chose to go on the trip (after all, it makes for a good celebration). Other students were told that they failed the exam, and 54 percent of them chose to go on the trip (after all, it makes for good recuperation). Both those who passed and those who failed wanted to go to Hawaii, pronto.

事情出现了转折：和你一样不知道期末考试成绩的那群学生，他们的行为却截然不同。他们中的大多数（61%）竟然花了五美元等了两天。想想看！如果你及格了，你想去夏威夷。如果你不及格了，你也想去夏威夷。如果你不知道自己考得好不好，你……就等着看结果？这可不是“稳赚不赔原则”该有的样子。这就好比那位商人，明明无论选举结果如何都愿意买房，却偏偏要等到选举之后才买。

Here’s the twist: The group of students who, like you, didn’t know their final exam results behaved completely differently. The majority of them (61 percent) paid five dollars to wait for two days. Think about that! If you pass, you want to go to Hawaii. If you fail, you want to go to Hawaii. If you don’t know whether you passed or failed, you … wait and see? This is not the way the “sure-thing principle” is supposed to behave. It’s as if our businessman had decided to wait until after the election to buy his property, despite being willing to make the purchase regardless of the outcome.

特沃斯基和沙菲尔的研究表明，不确定性——即使是无关紧要的不确定性——也会使我们不知所措。沙菲尔和他的同事唐纳德·雷德尔迈尔进行的另一项研究表明，选择也会导致这种不知所措。例如，想象一下，你是一名大学生，一天晚上你面临以下选择。你会怎么做？

Tversky and Shafir’s study shows us that uncertainty—even irrelevant uncertainty—can paralyze us. Another study, conducted by Shafir and a colleague, Donald Redelmeier, demonstrates that paralysis can also be caused by choice. Imagine, for example, that you are in college and you face the following choice one evening. What would you do?

  1. 去听一位你很欣赏的作家的讲座，他/她只来这里待一个晚上；

  2. Attend a lecture by an author you admire who is visiting just for the evening, or

  3. 去图书馆学习。

  4. Go to the library and study.

与一生一次的讲座相比，学习似乎并没有那么吸引人。当让大学生在两者之间做出选择时，只有21%的人选择了学习。

Studying doesn’t look so attractive compared with a once in a lifetime lecture. When this choice was given to actual college students, only 21 percent decided to study.

假设你当时有三个选择：

Suppose, instead, you had been given three choices:

  1. 去听讲座。

  2. Attend the lecture.

  3. 去图书馆学习。

  4. Go to the library and study.

  5. 看一部你一直想看的外国电影。

  6. Watch a foreign film that you’ve been wanting to see.

你的答案是否有所不同？值得注意的是，当另一组学生面临这三个选择时，40% 的学生决定学习——人数是之前的两倍。给学生提供两种有效的学习选择，而不是一种，反而会降低他们选择任何一种的可能性。这种行为并非“理性”，但却符合人性。

Does your answer differ? Remarkably, when a different group of students were given the three choices, 40 percent decided to study—double the number who did before. Giving students two good alternatives to studying, rather than one, paradoxically makes them less likely to choose either. This behavior isn’t “rational,” but it is human.

优先排序能帮助人们摆脱决策焦虑的泥潭，因此找到核心问题至关重要。我们的听众将在一个充满不确定性的环境中不断做出决策。即使只是在两个不错的选择之间做决定，比如听讲座和看外国电影，他们也会因为需要做出选择而感到焦虑。

Prioritization rescues people from the quicksand of decision angst, and that’s why finding the core is so valuable. The people who listen to us will be constantly making decisions in an environment of uncertainty. They will suffer anxiety from the need to choose—even when the choice is between two good options, like the lecture and the foreign film.

核心信息通过提醒人们什么才是最重要的，帮助他们避免做出错误的选择。例如，在赫伯·凯莱赫的寓言故事中，有人必须在鸡肉沙拉和不吃鸡肉沙拉之间做出选择——而“廉价航空公司”这条信息促使她放弃了鸡肉沙拉。

Core messages help people avoid bad choices by reminding them of what’s important. In Herb Kelleher’s parable, for instance, someone had to choose between chicken salad and no chicken salad—and the message “THE low-fare airline” led her to abandon the chicken salad.

创意诊所

Idea Clinics

本书旨在帮助您将想法付诸实践。因此，本书将不时穿插“想法诊所”环节，以实际案例阐释如何让想法更具说服力。这些诊所的灵感源自减肥中心常用的经典“前后对比”照片——这些照片直观地证明了减肥方法的有效性。正如尝试新减肥方法的患者一样，诊所中最初提出的想法也各有不同，需要的改变程度也各不相同；有些需要进行诸如胃部缝合和抽脂等剧烈手术，而有些则只需要减掉腰围几斤即可。

The goal of this book is to help you make your ideas stick. So, periodically throughout the book, we will present “Idea Clinics,” which illustrate, in practical terms, how an idea can be made stickier. The Clinics were inspired by the classic “before and after” photos used by weight-loss centers—visible evidence that the diet works. Like patients trying a new diet, the initial ideas in the Clinics vary in their need for change; some need dramatic help, like a stomach-stapling and liposuction, and some only need to lose a few pounds around the waistline.

这些“创意诊所”的目的并非炫耀我们的创意才华，这对读者和作者来说都是件幸事，因为我们并非创意天才。我们的目的仅仅是示范如何让创意更深入人心。与传统的免责声明不同，这套方法值得您在家尝试。仔细思考每条信息，并思考如何运用书中的原则来改进它。

The point of the Clinics is not to wow you with our creative genius, and it’s fortunate for readers and authors alike that this is not the goal, because we are not creative geniuses. The point is simply to model the process of making ideas stickier. In contrast to traditional disclaimers, this is something you should try at home. Think about each message and consider how you would improve it using the principles in the book.

您可以放心地跳过“诊所”部分——它们旨在作为文本的侧边栏，而不是作为组成部分——但我们希望您会发现它们有用。

You can safely skip the Clinics—they are intended as sidebars to the text, rather than as building blocks—but we hope you’ll find them useful.

诊所

CLINIC

警告：过度日晒有害健康

Warning: Sun Exposure Is Dangerous

情况： 俄亥俄州立大学的健康教育工作者希望让学术界了解日晒的风险。

THE SITUATION: Health educators at Ohio State University want to inform the academic community about the risks of sun exposure.

• • •

• • •

信息 1：以下是俄亥俄州立大学提供的关于日晒的网页信息。我们在每个段落中添加了数字，以便稍后分析信息：

MESSAGE 1: Here’s a Web page with facts about sun exposure from Ohio State University. We’ve added numbers to each paragraph so that we can analyze the message later:

日光照射：预防措施和防护

Sun Exposure: Precautions and Protection

（1）古铜色的肌肤常被视为身份的象征。这或许印证了这样一种观念：那些有足够时间晒日光浴、晒出古铜色肌肤的人，或者那些能在冬季前往温暖地区旅行的人，比“普通人”拥有更多的金钱或闲暇时间。然而，许多人的目标是在早春时节拥有健康的小麦色肌肤，或者在度假归来时容光焕发、健康亮丽。无论肤色是否代表身份，不加节制地暴露在阳光下都可能造成伤害。阳光中的紫外线不仅会损伤皮肤，还会导致视力问题、过敏反应和免疫系统抑制。

(1) A golden, bronze tan is often considered a status symbol. Perhaps this supports the idea that people who have time to lie in the sun long enough to develop a deep tan, or who can travel to warm climates during winter, have more money or leisure time than “common folk.” Nevertheless, the goal of many is a deep tan early in spring or to return from vacation with that hearty, healthy glow. Whether a tan suggests status or not, careless exposure to the sun can be harmful. Ultraviolet rays from the sun will damage skin but can also create vision problems, allergic reactions, and depressed immune systems.

（2）晒黑和晒伤是由阳光中的紫外线引起的。这些射线肉眼看不见也感觉不到，但它们可以穿透皮肤，刺激含有黑色素（一种棕色色素）的细胞。黑色素通过吸收和散射紫外线来保护皮肤。肤色较深的人黑色素含量较高，对紫外线的天然防护能力更强，因此更容易晒黑。金发、红发和肤色较浅的人黑色素含量较低，因此更容易晒伤。

(2) Tanning and burning are caused by ultraviolet rays from the sun. These rays cannot be seen or felt, but penetrate the skin and stimulate cells containing a brownish pigment called melanin. Melanin protects the skin by absorbing and scattering ultraviolet rays. People with dark skins have high amounts of melanin, have greater natural protection from ultraviolet rays, and tan more easily. Blondes, redheads, and people with fair skins have less melanin and, therefore, burn more quickly.

（3）紫外线刺激黑色素生成后，黑色素会上升到皮肤表面，形成晒黑的肤色，从而起到保护皮肤免受未来阳光照射的作用。然而，肤色较深的人，例如橄榄色、棕色或黑色皮肤的人，也并非不会因不注意防晒而导致晒伤和皮肤损伤。

(3) As melanin is stimulated by ultraviolet rays, it rises to the skin’s surface as a tan and provides protection against future sun exposure. Individuals with dark skins such as olive, brown, or black are not immune to burning and skin damage caused by careless exposure to the sun.

（4）太阳光中的紫外线（UV）有两种类型：UVA 和 UVB。UVB 会导致皮肤灼伤或晒伤引起的皮肤发红，还会导致皮肤癌和皮肤过早老化。UVA 射线可以促进皮肤晒黑，但也与其他一些问题有关，例如视力障碍、皮疹以及对药物的过敏或其他反应。

(4) Two types of ultraviolet rays (UV) from the sun exist: UVA and UVB. UVB cause burning of the skin or the red associated with sunburn, skin cancer, and premature aging of skin. UVA rays stimulate tanning but are also linked to other problems such as impaired vision, skin rashes, and allergic or other reactions to drugs.

（5）过度日晒造成的皮肤损伤会随着时间推移而累积，且不可逆转。一旦损伤发生，便无法修复。大多数严重且持久的损伤发生在18岁之前。因此，应尽早开始防晒，尤其对于喜欢在晴天进行户外活动的儿童而言。

(5) Skin damage from overexposure to the sun is cumulative over the years and cannot be reversed. Once damage occurs, it cannot be undone. Most serious and lasting damage occurs before age 18. Protection should start early, particularly with children who enjoy outdoor play on sunny days.

在阅读下面的评论之前，请先返回并重新阅读第 1 条信息。您可以做些什么来改进它？

Before you read our comments below, go back and reread Message 1. What can you do to improve it?

对第一条信息的评论：这篇文章的重点是什么？核心是什么？第一段深入探讨了晒黑的皮肤作为一种身份象征，但这其实只是一个有趣的障眼法。（事实上，文中也承认……）文中经常提到“晒黑是否代表身份地位……”。对我们来说，第五段犹如霓虹灯般闪耀，直击要害：皮肤损伤……会随着时间累积，而且不可逆转。哇！这难道不是我们最想告诉日光浴爱好者的吗？相比之下，第二至第四段则提供了一些多余的技术细节。举个例子，吸烟者真的需要了解肺部的运作原理才能意识到吸烟的危害吗？

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 1: What’s the lead here? What’s the core? The first paragraph dives into tanned skin as a status symbol, which is simply an interesting red herring. (In fact, the text acknowledges as much when it says, “Whether a tan suggests status or not …”) To us, Paragraph 5 flashes in neon lights as the core: Skin damage … is cumulative over the years and cannot be reversed. Wow. Isn’t that the single most important thing we’d want to tell sun-worshippers? By contrast, Paragraphs 2-4 provide superfluous mechanics. As an analogy, do smokers really need to understand the workings of the lungs in order to appreciate the dangers of smoking?

• • •

• • •

信息 2：在下面的文本中，我们重新排列了要点，并稍微修改了散文，希望能找出关键信息。

MESSAGE 2: In the text below, we have reordered the points and tinkered with the prose a bit in the hope of unburying the lead.

日晒：如何加速衰老

Sun Exposure: How to Get Old Prematurely

（5）过度日晒造成的皮肤损伤就像衰老一样：它会随着时间推移不断累积，而且不可逆转。一旦损伤发生，就无法修复。大多数严重且持久的损伤发生在18岁之前。幸运的是，与衰老不同，皮肤损伤是可以预防的。防晒应该从小开始，尤其是对于喜欢在阳光明媚的日子里进行户外活动的儿童。

(5) Skin damage from overexposure to the sun is like getting older: It is cumulative over the years and cannot be reversed. Once damage occurs, it cannot be undone. Most serious and lasting damage occurs before age 18. Fortunately, unlike aging, skin damage can be prevented. Sun protection should start early, particularly with children who enjoy playing outdoors on sunny days.

（2、3、4）晒黑和晒伤是由阳光中的紫外线引起的。紫外线会导致晒伤，这是皮肤深层损伤的暂时迹象。晒伤最终会消退，但深层损伤会持续存在，并最终可能导致皮肤过早老化或皮肤癌。

(2, 3, 4) Tanning and burning are caused by ultraviolet rays from the sun. Ultraviolet rays cause sunburn, which is a temporary sign of deeper underlying skin damage. Sunburns eventually disappear, but the underlying damage persists and may eventually cause premature aging or skin cancer.

（1）具有讽刺意味的是，古铜色的肤色通常被认为是健康的标志。但紫外线不仅会损伤皮肤，还会导致视力问题、过敏反应和免疫系统抑制。因此，与其称之为“健康的晒黑”，或许我们应该称之为“病态的晒黑”。

(1) Ironically, a golden, bronze tan is often considered a sign of good health. But ultraviolet rays not only damage skin, they can also create vision problems, allergic reactions, and depressed immune systems. So instead of a “healthy tan,” perhaps we should call it a “sickly tan.”

关于第二条信息的说明：这条信息的核心是皮肤损伤是累积性的且不可逆的。因此，我们重写了信息，以强调这一点并删除非必要信息。我们这样做是为了说明强制优先级排序的过程；为了让核心信息更加突出，我们不得不删除一些有趣的内容（例如关于黑色素的描述）。

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 2: The core of this message is that skin damage is cumulative and irreversible. So we’ve rewritten the message to stress that point and eliminate nonessential information. We’ve done this to illustrate the process of forced prioritization; we’ve had to eliminate some interesting stuff (such as the references to melanin) in order to let the core shine through.

我们尝试从几个方面强调核心观点。首先，我们把重点放在最显眼的位置。其次，我们用衰老作比喻，强调损伤是不可逆的。第三，我们用一个具体且或许出人意料的例子：晒伤是损伤的信号；晒伤可能会消失，但潜在的损伤却不会。

We’ve tried to emphasize the core in a couple of ways. First, we’ve unburied the lead—putting the core right up front. Second, we’ve added the analogy to aging to hammer home the idea that damage is irreversible. Third, we’ve added a concrete and perhaps unexpected image: Sunburns are a signal of damage; they may disappear, but the underlying damage does not.

记分卡
检查清单 消息 1    消息 2
简单的 -   
意外    -   
具体的 -   
可信的 -    -
情绪 -    -
故事 -    -

妙语：避免把重点埋没。不要为了取悦观众而先讲一些有趣但无关紧要的内容。相反，要努力让核心信息本身更有趣。

PUNCH LINE: Avoid burying the lead. Don’t start with something interesting but irrelevant in hopes of entertaining the audience. Instead, work to make the core message itself more interesting.

名字，名字，还是名字

Names, Names, and Names

北卡罗来纳州的邓恩是一个小镇，位于罗利以南约40英里处。它有14000名居民，劳动力主要为蓝领。当地的餐馆早上总是挤满了人，他们享用着丰盛的早餐，喝着咖啡。服务员会亲切地称呼你“亲爱的”。小镇最近新开了一家沃尔玛超市。

Dunn, North Carolina, is a small town about forty miles south of Raleigh. It has 14,000 residents and its workforce is primarily blue collar. The local diner is packed in the morning with people eating big breakfasts and drinking coffee. Waitresses call you “hon.” The town recently got a Wal-Mart.

总的来说，邓恩是个相当普通的地方，除了一个事实：那里几乎每个人都读当地报纸《每日记录报》。事实上，邓恩镇上读这份报纸的人比所有人都多。

All in all, Dunn is a pretty normal place, except for one fact: Almost everyone there reads the local paper, the Daily Record. As a matter of fact, more than everyone in Dunn reads the paper.

《每日记录报》在邓恩社区的发行率高达112%，是全美所有报纸中最高的。一个社区的发行率要超过100%，必然有两种可能：(1) 邓恩以外的人——比如通勤到邓恩上班的人——购买了这份报纸；(2) 一些家庭购买了不止一份报纸。也许邓恩的一些夫妇很难分摊报纸的费用。

The Daily Record’s penetration in the Dunn community is 112 percent, which is the highest penetration of any newspaper in the country. For a community penetration to exceed 100 percent, one of two things must be true: (1) People from outside Dunn—perhaps people commuting to jobs in Dunn—are buying the paper; or (2) some households are buying more than one paper. Maybe it’s hard for some couples in Dunn to share.

这种非凡的成功背后的原因是什么？邓恩镇的居民获取新闻的选择当然很多：《今日美国》、《罗利新闻与观察家报》、CNN、互联网以及数百家其他媒体。那么， 《每日记录报》为何如此受欢迎呢？

What’s the explanation for this remarkable success? The people of Dunn certainly have plenty of options for their news: USA Today, the Raleigh News & Observer, CNN, the Internet, and hundreds of other outlets. So why is the Daily Record so popular?

T《邓恩每日纪事报》由胡佛·亚当斯于1950年创办。亚当斯天生就与新闻打交道。他第一次署名发表文章，是在童子军营地寄送稿件的时候。高中时期，他就开始为罗利的一家报纸担任特约记者。二战后，亚当斯成为《邓恩快讯》的编辑。最终，他对《快讯》感到厌倦，决定创办自己的报纸——《每日纪事报》。1978年，经过28年的正面竞争，《快讯》最终认输，将报纸出售给了亚当斯。

The Dunn Daily Record was founded in 1950 by Hoover Adams. Adams was born with ink in his blood. He got his first byline by sending dispatches from his Boy Scout camp. By the time he was in high school he was serving as a stringer—a freelance reporter—for the Raleigh paper. After World War II, Adams became the editor of the Dunn Dispatch. Eventually, he grew restless at the Dispatch and decided to start his own paper, the Daily Record. In 1978, after twenty-eight years of head-to-head competition, the Dispatch finally gave up and sold out to him.

在担任出版人的五十五年间，亚当斯始终秉持着一贯的编辑理念。他坚信报纸的报道应该始终聚焦本地。事实上，他对社区报道近乎狂热。

Across the fifty-five years of his tenure as publisher, Adams has had a remarkably consistent editorial philosophy. He believes that newspapers should be relentlessly local in their coverage. In fact, he’s a zealot about community coverage.

1978年，他感到报纸对地方问题的关注不够，于是给员工写了一份备忘录，解释了他的观点：

In 1978, frustrated by what he felt was insufficient focus on local issues in the paper, he wrote a memo to his staff, explaining his views:

“我们都知道，人们阅读地方报纸的主要原因是为了了解当地的名字和照片。这是我们比任何人都做得更好的地方，也是我们的读者在其他任何地方都无法获得的。请记住，安吉尔市市长和利灵顿市市长对这两个城镇的重要性，就如同纽约市市长对纽约市民的重要性一样。”

“All of us know that the main reason anybody reads a local newspaper is for local names and pictures. That’s the one thing we can do better than anybody else. And that’s the thing our readers can’t get anywhere else. Always remember, the mayor of Angier and the mayor of Lillington are just as important to those towns as the mayor of New York is to his people.”

必须明确一点：亚当斯对本地新闻报道的重视并非什么革命性的观点。事实上，在小型报纸的出版商中，这完全不会引起争议。然而，显而易见的是，大多数报纸并未将这一理念付诸实践。一般的地方报纸充斥着电讯稿、职业体育队的分析以及几乎看不到人物的零星照片。

Let’s be clear: Adams’s focus on local coverage is not a revolutionary sentiment. In fact, among publishers of small newspapers it would be utterly uncontroversial. Yet it’s easy enough to see that the idea has not become a reality at most papers. The average local newspaper is loaded with wire stories, analyses of pro sports teams, and spot photos with nary a person in sight.

换句话说，找到核心并不等同于传达核心。高层管理人员可能知道优先事项是什么，但却完全无法有效地传达和实现这些优先事项。亚当斯成功地找到了核心并传达了核心。他是怎么做到的？

In other words, finding the core isn’t synonymous with communicating the core. Top management can know what the priorities are but be completely ineffective in sharing and achieving those priorities. Adams has managed to find and share the core. How did he do it?

分享核心

Sharing the Core

亚当斯找到了报纸运营的核心：聚焦本地。然后，他开始着手传播这一核心信息，并让员工牢记于心。在本章的剩余部分——实际上也是本书的剩余部分——我们将探讨如何让核心信息深入人心。我们将首先研究亚当斯是如何让他的“聚焦本地”信息深入人心的。

Adams found the core of his newspaper operations: local focus. Then he turned his attention to sharing his core message—making it stick with his staff. For the rest of the chapter—in fact, the rest of the book—we will discuss ways to get core messages to stick. And we will start by studying the way Adams has made his “local focus” message stick.

虽然许多出版商口头上都认可本地化的重要性，亚当斯在这方面走极端。他为了专注于本地市场，甚至不惜损害公司盈利：

While many publishers pay lip service to the value of local focus, Adams is an extremist about it. He’s willing to hurt the bottom line for local focus:

事实上，地方报纸永远不会嫌本地人名多。如果人名足够多，我很乐意再雇两个排版员，每期报纸都增加两页。

The fact is, a local newspaper can never get enough local names. I’d happily hire two more typesetters and add two more pages in every edition of each paper if we had the names to fill them up.

他愿意为了专注于本地市场而显得平庸：

He’s willing to be boring for local focus:

我敢打赌，如果《每日记录报》今晚重印整个邓恩电话簿，一半的人都会坐下来仔细核对自己的名字……如果有人跟你说，“哎呀，你们肯定不想要那么多名字”，请向他们保证，这正是我们最想要的！

I’ll bet that if the Daily Record reprinted the entire Dunn telephone directory tonight, half the people would sit down and check it to be sure their name was included…. When somebody tells you, “Aw, you don’t want all those names,” please assure them that’s exactly what we want, most of all!

他兴高采烈地夸大其词，以强调关注本地的重要性，并引用了他的朋友拉尔夫·德拉诺（Ralph Delano）的一句话，拉尔夫·德拉诺是本森当地报纸的老板：

He gleefully exaggerates in order to emphasize the value of local focus, quoting a saying of a friend, Ralph Delano, who runs the local paper in Benson:

如果一颗原子弹落在罗利，除非一些碎片和灰烬落到本森，否则这不会成为本森的新闻。

If an atomic bomb fell on Raleigh, it wouldn’t be news in Benson unless some of the debris and ashes fell on Benson.

事实上，当被问及《每日记录报》为何如此成功时，亚当斯回答说：“原因有三：名字，名字，还是名字。”

In fact, asked why the Daily Record has been so successful, Adams replies, “It’s because of three things: Names, names, and names.”

这是怎么回事？亚当斯找到了他想要传达的核心理念——聚焦本地是报纸成功的关键。这是第一步。第二步是将这个核心理念传达给其他人。而他在这方面做得非常出色。

What’s going on here? Adams has found the core idea that he wants to communicate—that local focus is the key to his newspaper’s success. That’s Step 1. Step 2 is to communicate the core to others. And he does that brilliantly.

看看亚当斯是如何运用技巧来传达他对本地事务的重视的。他用了一个类比：将安吉尔市市长比作纽约市市长。（我们将在本章后面详细讨论类比。）他说，如果……他会雇佣更多的排版员。搬运工或许能提供足够多的名字。这是强制性的优先排序：关注本地市场比降低成本更重要！（这种观点在小城镇报纸中并不常见。参见“意想不到”章节。）

Look at the techniques Adams uses to communicate his seriousness about local focus. He uses an analogy: comparing the mayor of Angier to the mayor of New York. (We’ll have more to say about analogy later in this chapter.) He says he’d hire more typesetters if the re porters could generate enough names. This is forced prioritization: Local focus is more important than minimizing costs! (Not a common sentiment among small-town papers. See the “Unexpected” chapter.)

他说话也清晰明了，具体易懂。他想要什么？名字。他希望每天的报纸上都能出现大量个人姓名。（参见“具体”章节。）这个想法足够具体，组织中的每个人都能理解并运用。这样还有可能产生误解吗？会有员工不理解亚当斯所说的“名字”是什么意思吗？

He also speaks in clear, tangible language. What does he want? Names. He wants lots of individual names in the newspaper every day. (See the “Concrete” chapter.) This idea is concrete enough that everyone in the organization can comprehend and use it. Is there any room for misunderstanding? Is there a staffer who won’t understand what Adams means by “names”?

“名字，名字，还是名字”这句简单的陈述象征着一个核心真理。名字的作用不仅仅在于此。在亚当斯看来，名字胜过成本。名字胜过优美的文笔。名字胜过邻近社区的核爆炸。

“Names, names, and names” is a simple statement that is symbolic of a core truth. It’s not just that names are helpful. In Adams’s mind, names trump costs. Names trump well-written prose. Names trump nuclear explosions in neighboring communities.

自亚当斯创办这份报纸以来，五十五年来，他秉持的以社区为中心的核心价值观，帮助报社数百名员工在无数情况下做出正确的决策。作为出版人，亚当斯主持出版了近两万期报纸。每一期都涉及无数的决策：我们报道哪些故事？故事的重点是什么？我们刊登哪些照片？为了节省版面，我们删减哪些照片？

For fifty-five years, since Adams founded the paper, his core value of community focus has helped hundreds of people at the paper, in thousands of circumstances, make good decisions. As a publisher, Adams has presided over close to 20,000 issues. And each of those issues involved countless decisions: Which stories do we cover? What’s important in the stories? Which photos do we run? Which do we cut out to save space?

亚当斯不可能亲自参与数百项细小决策中的绝大多数。但他的员工不会因此陷入决策瘫痪，因为亚当斯的指挥意图非常明确：“人名，人名，还是人名。”亚当斯不可能事事亲力亲为。但他通过找到核心并清晰地传达出来，让自己无处不在。这就是一个深入人心的理念的力量。

Adams can’t possibly be personally involved in the vast majority of these hundreds of small decisions. But his employees don’t suffer from decision paralysis, because Adams’s Commander’s Intent is clear: “Names, names, and names.” Adams can’t be everywhere. But by finding the core and communicating it clearly, he has made himself everywhere. That’s the power of a sticky idea.

简单 = 核心 + 紧凑

Simple = Core + Compact

亚当斯是个语言高手，但他最妙的文字游戏或许反而是最不高明的：“名字，名字，还是名字。” 这句话是它既实用又易于记忆，因为它非常具体，也非常简洁。这个例子说明了简洁性的第二个方面：简单的信息是核心且精炼的。

Adams is a clever wordsmith, but his most useful bit of wordplay is probably his least clever: “Names, names, and names.” This phrase is useful and memorable because it is highly concrete, but also because it is highly succinct. This example illustrates a second aspect of simplicity: Simple messages are core and compact.

从某种程度上来说，简洁明了的理念是毋庸置疑的。除非你是为信用卡公司撰写利率披露文件，否则很少有人会建议你把沟通内容写得冗长繁琐。我们都知道，句子比段落好，两个要点比五个要点更清晰，简单的词语比复杂的词语更有效。这其实是一个信息量的问题：我们越精简信息量，它就越容易被记住。

At one level, the idea of compactness is uncontroversial. Rarely will you get advice to make your communications lengthy and convoluted, unless you write interest-rate disclosures for a credit card company. We know that sentences are better than paragraphs. Two bullet points are better than five. Easy words are better than hard words. It’s a bandwidth issue: The more we reduce the amount of information in an idea, the stickier it will be.

但我们必须明确一点：简洁本身并不够。我们可能会抓住一个并非核心信息的简洁口号；换句话说，一个精辟的标语，却无法反映指挥官的意图。简洁的信息或许容易被记住，但这并不代表它们没有价值。我们可以想象一些简洁的信息是谎言（“地球是平的”），一些简洁的信息是无关紧要的（“山羊喜欢吃豆芽”），还有一些简洁的信息是不明智的（“每天都要买鞋”）。

But let’s be clear: Compactness alone isn’t enough. We could latch on to a compact message that isn’t core; in other words, a pithy slogan that doesn’t reflect our Commander’s Intent. Compact messages may be sticky, but that says nothing about their worth. We can imagine compact messages that are lies (“The earth is flat”), compact messages that are irrelevant (“Goats like sprouts”), and compact messages that are ill-advised (“Never let a day pass without a shoe purchase”).

在某些情况下，简洁本身似乎成了一个不值得追求的目标。我们很多人都拥有特定领域的专业知识。成为某个领域的专家意味着我们会越来越着迷于其中的细微差别和复杂性。这时，知识的诅咒就开始显现，我们开始忘记自己曾经对所知道的事物一无所知是什么感觉。在那种情况下，把事情简化似乎就等同于“降低标准”。作为专家，我们不想被指责传播空洞的口号或迎合大众的低级趣味。我们担心，简化可能会演变成过度简化。

In other cases, compactness itself can come to seem an unworthy goal. Lots of us have expertise in particular areas. Becoming an expert in something means that we become more and more fascinated by nuance and complexity. That’s when the Curse of Knowledge kicks in, and we start to forget what it’s like not to know what we know. At that point, making something simple can seem like “dumbing down.” As an expert, we don’t want to be accused of propagating sound bites or pandering to the lowest common denominator. Simplifying, we fear, can devolve into oversimplifying.

所以，如果我们把“简单”定义为核心且精炼，我们就需要确保追求精炼是值得的。我们已经有了核心，为什么还需要精炼呢？“精简版”的想法难道不比完整阐述的想法更有用吗？假设我们把精炼发挥到极致，是否有可能在短短几句话里表达出有意义的内容呢？

So if we’re going to define “simple” as core and compact, we need to assure ourselves that compactness is worth striving for. We’ve already got core, why do we need compact? Aren’t “stripped-down” ideas inherently less useful than fully elaborated ideas? Suppose we took compactness to its most extreme form. Is it possible to say something meaningful in the span of a sound bite?

“一鸟在手”

“A Bird in the Hand”

数千年来，人们一直在交流被称为谚语的精辟格言。谚语简洁却蕴含深刻的道理。塞万提斯将谚语定义为“从长期经验中提炼出的简短语句”。以英语谚语为例：“一鸟在手胜过二鸟在林。”它的核心是什么？核心在于告诫人们不要为了投机取巧而放弃稳妥之物。这句谚语虽然简短易懂，却蕴含着丰富的智慧，在许多情况下都适用。

For thousands of years, people have exchanged sound bites called proverbs. Proverbs are simple yet profound. Cervantes defined proverbs as “short sentences drawn from long experience.” Take the English-language proverb: “A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.” What’s the core? The core is a warning against giving up a sure thing for something speculative. The proverb is short and simple, yet it packs a big nugget of wisdom that is useful in many situations.

原来，这句谚语并非仅限于英语。瑞典有句谚语：“宁可一鸟在手，也不要十鸟在林。”西班牙有句谚语：“一鸟在手胜过百鸟在飞。”波兰有句谚语：“一雀在手胜过鸽子在屋顶。”俄罗斯有句谚语：“一山雀在手胜过鹤在天上。”

As it turns out, this is not just an English-language proverb. In Sweden, the saying is “Rather one bird in the hand than ten in the woods.” In Spain: “A bird in the hand is better than a hundred flying birds.” In Poland: “A sparrow in your hand is better than a pigeon on the roof.” In Russia: “Better a titmouse in the hand than a crane in the sky.”

罗马尼亚语、意大利语、葡萄牙语、德语、冰岛语，甚至中世纪拉丁语中都有类似的变体。英语中最早有记载的例子出自约翰·班扬1678年出版的《天路历程》。但这句谚语可能更为古老。在伊索寓言中，一只鹰抓起了一只夜莺，夜莺苦苦哀求，说自己太小，不足以满足鹰的胃口。鹰回答说：“我若放开手中的鸟去追逐一只根本不存在的鸟，岂不是愚蠢至极？”这个故事可以追溯到公元前570年。

Other variants can be found in Romanian, Italian, Portuguese, German, Icelandic, and even medieval Latin. The first documented case in English is from John Bunyan’s Pilgrim’s Progress in 1678. But the proverb may be much older still. In one of Aesop’s fables, a hawk seizes a nightingale, who pleads for its life, arguing that it is too tiny a morsel to satisfy the hawk. The hawk replies, “I would be foolish to release the bird I have in my hand to pursue another bird that is not even in sight.” This story dates from 570 B.C.

因此，“鸟在手”这句谚语的生命力惊人，它流传至今已有2500多年，跨越各大洲、各种文化和语言。要知道，没有人为“鸟在手”做广告宣传，它完全是自然而然地传播开来的。许多其他谚语也拥有如此长寿的品质。事实上，几乎在所有有文字记载的文化中，都能找到谚语。为什么？它们的目的是什么？

The “bird in hand” proverb, then, is an astoundingly sticky idea. It has survived for more than 2,500 years. It has spread across continents, cultures, and languages. Keep in mind that nobody funded a “bird in hand” advertising campaign. It spreads on its own. Many other proverbs share this longevity. In fact, a repertoire of proverbs has been found in almost every documented culture. Why? What is their purpose?

在拥有共同标准的环境中，谚语有助于指导个人决策。这些共同标准通常是伦理或道德规范。谚语为人们的行为提供了经验法则。个人。黄金法则——“己所不欲，勿施于人”——意义深远，足以影响人的一生。黄金法则完美地象征了我们本章所要探讨的内容：既要简洁精炼到便于记忆，又要意义深远到足以产生实际影响的理念。

Proverbs are helpful in guiding individual decisions in environments with shared standards. Those shared standards are often ethical or moral norms. Proverbs offer rules of thumb for the behavior of individuals. The Golden Rule, “Do unto others as you would have them do unto you,” is so profound that it can influence a lifetime of behavior. The Golden Rule is a great symbol of what we’re chasing in this chapter: ideas that are compact enough to be sticky and meaningful enough to make a difference.

好的简洁思想兼具优雅与实用性，其功能与谚语颇为相似。塞万提斯对“谚语”的定义与我们对简洁思想的定义不谋而合：言简意赅（精炼），却蕴含着丰富的经验（核心）。我们对那些哗众取宠的“金句”抱有怀疑态度是理所当然的，因为很多“金句”空洞无物或具有误导性——它们虽然精炼，却缺乏核心。但我们所追求的“简洁”并非“金句”，而是谚语：精炼而核心。

Great simple ideas have an elegance and a utility that make them function a lot like proverbs. Cervantes’s definition of “proverbs” echoes our definition of Simple ideas: short sentences (compact) drawn from long experience (core). We are right to be skeptical of sound bites, because lots of sound bites are empty or misleading—they’re compact without being core. But the Simple we’re chasing isn’t a sound bite, it’s a proverb: compact and core.

亚当斯成功地将他的核心理念——即持续关注本地问题——转化为一条新闻界的格言。“人名、人名、人名”这一理念有助于指导人们在拥有共同标准的社群中进行决策。如果你是一名摄影师，除非你打算拍摄姓名标签，否则这条格言的字面意义毫无价值。但是，当你意识到你的组织依赖于人名——也就是当地社群特定成员的具体行动——这种认知会影响你寻找的拍摄机会。你是选择拍摄枯燥乏味的委员会讨论，还是拍摄公园里壮丽的日落？答案是：枯燥乏味的委员会讨论。

Adams managed to turn his core idea—the need to focus relentlessly on local issues—into a journalistic proverb. “Names, names, and names” is an idea that helps guide individual decision-making in a community of shared standards. If you’re a photographer, the proverb has no value as a literal statement, unless you plan to shoot name tags. But when you know that your organization thrives on names—i.e., the specific actions taken by specific members of the local community—that knowledge informs the kinds of photo ops you look for. Do you shoot the boring committee deliberations or the gorgeous sunset over the park? Answer: the boring committee deliberations.

Palm Pilot 与视觉谚语

Palm Pilot and the Visual Proverb

简洁明了的理念有助于人们学习和记忆核心信息。但在需要帮助人们正确行事时，尤其是在需要做出大量选择的环境中，简洁明了的理念可能更为重要。

Compact ideas help people learn and remember a core message. But they may be even more important when it comes time to help people act properly, particularly in an environment where they have to make lots of choices.

为什么遥控器上的按钮比我们实际使用的要多得多？答案要从工程师们的初衷说起。大多数技术和产品设计项目都必须应对“功能蔓延”的问题。事物往往会变得越来越复杂，直到它们无法很好地履行其原有功能。录像机就是一个典型的例子。

Why do remote controls have more buttons than we ever use? The answer starts with the noble intentions of engineers. Most technology and product-design projects must combat “feature creep,” the tendency for things to become incrementally more complex until they no longer perform their original functions very well. A VCR is a case in point.

功能蔓延是一个无害的过程。一位工程师在查看遥控器原型时可能会想：“嘿，遥控器面板上还有一些额外的空间。芯片上也有一些额外的处理能力。与其浪费掉，不如让用户能够切换儒略历和格里历？”

Feature creep is an innocent process. An engineer looking at a prototype of a remote control might think to herself, “Hey, there’s some extra real estate here on the face of the control. And there’s some extra processing capacity on the chip. Rather than let it go to waste, what if we give people the ability to toggle between the Julian and Gregorian calendars?”

这位工程师只是想帮忙——想添加一个能让遥控器更酷炫的功能。与此同时，团队里的其他工程师对日历切换功能并不怎么在意。即使他们觉得这功能很鸡肋，他们大概也不会强烈抗议：“要么取消日历切换按钮，要么我就辞职！”就这样，遥控器——以及其他类型的技术——就这样悄无声息地被淘汰了。

The engineer is just trying to help—to add another gee-whiz feature that will improve the remote control. The other engineers on the team, meanwhile, don’t particularly care about the calendar-toggle. Even if they think it’s lame, they probably don’t care enough to stage a protest: “Either the calendar-toggle button goes or I quit!” In this way, slowly and quietly, remote controls—and, by extension, other types of technologies—are featured to death.

Palm Pilot团队意识到这种风险，因此对功能蔓延采取了强硬态度。该团队在20世纪90年代初开始研发时，个人数字助理（PDA）的失败记录可谓惨不忍睹。苹果公司Newton PDA的惨败更是让其他竞争对手望而却步。

The Palm Pilot team, aware of this danger, took a hard line against feature creep. When the team began its work, in the early 1990s, personal digital assistants (PDAs) had an unblemished record of failure. Apple’s famous debacle with its Newton PDA had made other competitors gun-shy.

1994年，PDA市场上的一款竞争对手看起来像一台营养不良的电脑。它体积庞大，配有键盘和多个外设接口。Palm Pilot团队负责人杰夫·霍金斯决心让他的产品避免落得如此下场。他希望Palm Pilot简洁易用，功能仅限于四项：日历、联系人、备忘录和任务列表。Palm Pilot虽然只有四项功能，但却能把每一项都做到极致。

One of the competitors on the PDA market in 1994 looked like a malnourished computer. It was a bulky device with a keyboard and multiple ports for peripherals. Jeff Hawkins, the Palm Pilot team leader, was determined that his product would avoid this fate. He wanted the Palm Pilot to be simple. It would handle four things: calendars, contacts, memos, and task lists. The Palm Pilot would do only four things, but it would do them well.

霍金斯为了防止功能蔓延，随身携带了一个和Palm掌上电脑大小一样的木块。Palm V设计团队成员特雷·瓦萨洛说：“这个木块很简单，这与产品简洁的技术目标相呼应，而且它也很小巧，这使得……”这款产品优雅而独特。”霍金斯会在开会时拿出木块“做笔记”，或者在走廊里“查看日程”。每当有人提出新的功能建议时，霍金斯都会拿出木块，询问他们这个功能应该放在哪里。

Hawkins fought feature creep by carrying around a wooden block the size of the Palm. Trae Vassallo, a member of the Palm V design team, says, “The block was dumb, which resonated with the simple technological goals of the product, but it was also small, which made the product elegant and different.” Hawkins would pull out the wooden block to “take notes” during a meeting or “check his calendar” in the hallway. Whenever someone suggested another feature, Hawkins would pull out the wooden block and ask them where it would fit.

瓦萨洛表示，Palm Pilot之所以能成为一款成功​​的产品，“几乎是因为它更多地被定义为‘不是什么’，而不是‘是什么’。”硅谷知名设计公司IDEO的汤姆·凯利也表达了类似的观点：“早期PDA面临的真正障碍……在于人们认为机器必须几乎无所不能。”

Vassallo said that the Palm Pilot became a successful product “almost because it was defined more in terms of what it was not than in terms of what it was.” Tom Kelley, from IDEO, a prominent Silicon Valley design firm, made a similar point: “The real barrier to the initial PDAs … was the idea that the machine had to do nearly everything.”

霍金斯深知，他项目的核心理念必须是优雅简洁（并且要坚决避免功能蔓延）。为了阐述这一核心理念，霍金斯和他的团队运用了一种本质上是视觉化的谚语。那块木头成为了一个视觉提醒：专注于少数几件事，并把它们做到极致。

Hawkins knew that the core idea of his project needed to be elegance and simplicity (and a tenacious avoidance of feature creep). In sharing this core idea, Hawkins and his team used what was, in essence, a visual proverb. The block of wood became a visual reminder to do a few things and do them well.

Palm Pilot 的开发与詹姆斯·卡维尔领导的希拉里竞选团队有着惊人的相似之处。在这两个案例中，团队成员都知识渊博，对工作充满热情。两个团队都拥有大量有能力且渴望做很多事情的人——他们能够就每个问题展开辩论，并设计出每一项功能。然而，在这两个案例中，团队都需要一个简单的提醒来抵制过度开发的诱惑。当你说了三件事时，你其实什么也没说。当你的遥控器上有五十个按钮时，你就无法换台了。

There is a striking parallel between the development of the Palm Pilot and the Clinton campaign led by James Carville. In both cases, the teams were composed of people who were knowledgeable and passionate about their work. Both teams boasted plenty of people who had the capability and the desire to do a lot of different things—argue every issue and engineer every feature. Yet in both cases the team needed a simple reminder to fight the temptation to do too much. When you say three things, you say nothing. When your remote control has fifty buttons, you can’t change the channel anymore.

利用现有资源

Using What’s There

我们的信息必须简洁明了，因为我们一次只能学习和记住有限的信息。但假设我们已经评估了信息的核心，却发现信息量过大，无法像谚语那样精炼。那么，当我们需要传达大量信息时，该如何操作呢？以下练习旨在强调简洁的重要性，并提供一些关于如何在简洁的信息中融入更多信息的提示。

Our messages have to be compact, because we can learn and remember only so much information at once. But suppose we’ve assessed the core of our message and we have too much information to aspire to the compactness of a proverb. How do we convey lots of information when we need to? The following exercise is designed to reinforce the need for compactness and to provide a hint about how to cram more information into a compact message.

练习规则如下：请花十到十五秒（不要超过这个时间）仔细阅读下面的字母。然后合上书，拿出一张纸，尽可能多地写下你能记住的字母。剧透警告：完成练习之前不要翻页。

Here are the rules of this exercise: Spend ten to fifteen seconds, no more, studying the letters below. Then close the book, pull out a sheet of paper, and write down as many letters as you can remember. Spoiler alert: Don’t turn the page until you’ve finished the exercise.

J FKFB INAT OUP SNA SAI RS

J  FKFB  INAT  OUP  SNA  SAI  RS

如果你和大多数人一样，可能只记住了七到十个字母。信息量太少了。简洁至关重要，因为我们一次能处理的信息量是有限的。

If you’re like most people, you probably remembered about seven to ten letters. That’s not much information. Compactness is essential, because there’s a limit to the amount of information we can juggle at once.

现在翻到下一页，再试一次。

Now turn the page and try the exercise again.

这次有点不一样。我们没有改变字母或顺序，只是改变了字母的组合方式。请再次仔细观察这些字母10到15秒，然后合上书，测试一下你的记忆力。

There’s a twist this time. We haven’t changed the letters or the sequence. All we’ve done is change the way the letters are grouped. Once again, study the letters for ten to fifteen seconds, then close the book and test your recall.

肯尼迪遇刺案、联邦调查局、北约、联合包裹服务公司、美国宇航局、美国国税局

JFK  FBI  NATO  UPS  NASA  IRS

你第二次的表现很可能好得多。突然间，这些字母有了意义，更容易记住。第一轮，你试图记住的是原始数据；第二轮，你记住的是概念：约翰·F·肯尼迪、联邦调查局、北大西洋公约组织、联合包裹服务公司、美国国家航空航天局、美国国税局。

Chances are you did much better the second time. Suddenly the letters meant something, which made them easier to remember. In Round 1, you were trying to remember raw data. In Round 2, you were remembering concepts: John F. Kennedy, the FBI, the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, UPS, NASA, the IRS.

等等。为什么记住“约翰·F·肯尼迪”比记住随机的字母F、J、K更容易呢？约翰·F·肯尼迪这个人所包含的信息肯定比这三个字母要多得多！想想肯尼迪身上所有与他相关的特质——政治、人际关系、遇刺身亡以及他显赫的家族。如果记忆就像举重一样，那么认为我们能比记住三个字母更容易“举起”肯尼迪这个人，岂不是太荒谬了？

But wait a second. Why is it easier to remember “John F. Kennedy” than the random letters F, J, K? Surely John F. Kennedy is a bigger bundle of information than the three random letters! Think of all the associations with JFK—politics, relationships, his assassination, and his famous family. If remembering was like weight lifting, it would be ridiculous to think we could “lift” JFK easier than three little letters!

当然，秘诀在于我们并非在“挖掘”肯尼迪。所有与肯尼迪相关的记忆工作早已完成。我们已经锻炼了这些记忆肌肉——肯尼迪的概念及其所有关联早已深深植根于我们的记忆之中。我们所记住的仅仅是指向这些信息的指针——我们只是在记忆的版图上插上一面小旗。如果是原始信件，我们实际上是插上了三面旗。最终，我们记住的只是一条信息（或一面旗子）与三条信息的区别，因此记住一条信息更容易也就不足为奇了。

The secret, of course, is that we’re not “lifting” JFK. All the remembering work related to JFK has already been done. We’ve already built those muscles—the concept of JFK, and all its associations, is already embedded in our memories. What we’re remembering is simply a pointer to this information—we’re posting a little flag on the terrain of our memory. With the raw letters, we’re posting three separate flags. In the end, it’s one bit of information (or one flag) versus three, and it’s no surprise that one is easier to remember.

所以呢？这只是些有趣的脑力知识吗？我们接下来要说的是：我们已经看到，简洁的理念更容易被记住，但仅仅简洁的理念本身并不值钱——只有那些精炼而深刻的理念才有价值。因此，要把深刻的理念精炼成简洁的，就必须用最少的文字传递大量的信息。那么该怎么做呢？使用标记。利用受众已有的记忆。利用他们已有的信息。

So what? Is this just neat brain trivia? Here’s where we’re going: We’ve seen that compact ideas are stickier, but that compact ideas alone aren’t valuable—only ideas with profound compactness are valuable. So, to make a profound idea compact you’ve got to pack a lot of meaning into a little bit of messaging. And how do you do that? You use flags. You tap the existing memory terrain of your audience. You use what’s already there.

柚子模式

The Pomelo Schema

到目前为止，我们列举了一些例子，在这些例子中，一个简单的理念或几个简单的理念就能有效地指导行为。但说实话，世界上大多数人都在做复杂的事情。我们并非想论证复杂的事物——比如法律、医学、建筑、编程、教学——都可以简化成两三个简洁的信息。我们显然无法用一个简单的理念（“防止建筑物倒塌”）来取代一所建筑学院。

So far we have presented situations in which one simple idea, or a handful of simple ideas, were useful in guiding behavior. But, let’s face it, most people in the world do complicated things. It’s not our intention to argue that complicated things—law, medicine, construction, programming, teaching—can be pared down to two or three compact messages. We obviously can’t replace a school of architecture with a single compact idea (“Keep the building from falling down”).

这引出了一个我们尚未讨论的重要问题：如何将一名大一新生培养成一名建筑师？复杂性如何从简单中涌现？我们将论证，巧妙运用简单可以创造复杂性。如果将简单的想法正确地布局和层层叠加，它们就能迅速变得复杂。

This leads us to an important issue that we haven’t discussed yet: How do you turn a freshman into an architect? How does complexity emerge from simplicity? We will argue that it is possible to create complexity through the artful use of simplicity. If simple ideas are staged and layered correctly, they can very quickly become complex.

让我们来教你什么是“柚子”。（如果你已经知道柚子是什么，请装作不知道。）以下是我们解释柚子的一种方式：

Let us teach you what a “pomelo” is. (If you already know what a pomelo is, be a good sport and feign ignorance.) Here is one way that we can explain to you what a pomelo is:

解释1：柚子是最大的柑橘类水果。它的果皮很厚，但质地柔软，很容易剥掉。剥开后，果肉呈浅黄色至珊瑚粉色，口感从多汁到略干不等，味道也从诱人的辛辣甜美到酸爽浓郁。

EXPLANATION 1: A pomelo is the largest citrus fruit. The rind is very thick but soft and easy to peel away. The resulting fruit has a light yellow to coral pink flesh and can vary from juicy to slightly dry and from seductively spicy-sweet to tangy and tart.

问个简单的问题：根据这个解释，如果把柚子汁和橙汁以1:1的比例混合，味道会好吗？你或许能猜到，但答案可能有点模棱两可。我们来看另一种解释：

Quick question: Based on this explanation, if you mixed pomelo juice half and half with orange juice, would it taste good? You might make a guess, but the answer is probably a bit ambiguous. Let’s move on to an alternative explanation:

解释2：柚子基本上就是个超大的葡萄柚，果皮很厚很软。

EXPLANATION 2: A pomelo is basically a supersized grapefruit with a very thick and soft rind.

解释 2 强调了一个你已经知道的概念：葡萄柚。当我们说柚子像葡萄柚时，你会想到它。先在脑海中想象一个葡萄柚。然后我们告诉你如何改变它：它“超大”了。你脑海中的葡萄柚也会相应变大。

Explanation 2 sticks a flag on a concept that you already know: a grapefruit. When we tell you that a pomelo is like a grapefruit, you call up a mental image of a grapefruit. Then we tell you what to change about it: It’s “supersized.” Your visualized grapefruit grows accordingly.

我们通过将新概念与您已知的概念联系起来，使您更容易学习新概念。在本例中，这个概念是“葡萄柚”。“葡萄柚”是您已有的认知图式。（“认知图式”是心理学中的一个专业术语，但它非常有用，因此我们认为值得在本书中继续使用。）

We’ve made it easier for you to learn a new concept by tying it to a concept that you already know. In this case, the concept is “grapefruit.” “Grapefruit” is a schema that you already have. (“Schema” is a bit of technical jargon from psychology, but it’s so useful that we think it’s worth carrying through the book.)

心理学家将图式定义为概念或类别的通用属性集合。图式包含大量预先记录在我们记忆中的信息。如果有人告诉你她看到了一辆很棒的新款跑车，你脑海中会立刻浮现出一幅画面，其中包含着各种通用属性。你知道“跑车”是什么样子的。你会想象它小巧、双门，或许还带敞篷。如果画面中的车在动，那它肯定跑得很快。它的颜色几乎肯定是红色的。同样，你对“葡萄柚”的图式也包含一系列通用属性：黄粉色、酸涩的味道、垒球大小等等。

Psychologists define schema as a collection of generic properties of a concept or category. Schemas consist of lots of prerecorded information stored in our memories. If someone tells you that she saw a great new sports car, a picture immediately springs to mind, filled with generic properties. You know what “sports cars” are like. You picture something small and two-door, with a convertible top perhaps. If the car in your picture moves, it moves fast. Its color is almost certainly red. Similarly, your schema of “grapefruit” also contains a cluster of generic properties: yellow-pink color, tart flavor, softball-sized, and so on.

通过调用你的葡萄柚模式，我们能够比机械地列出柚子的所有属性更快地教会你柚子的概念。另外，请注意，这样更容易回答关于柚子汁和橙汁混合的问题。你知道葡萄柚汁和橙汁混合效果很好，所以柚子模式从葡萄柚模式继承了这个属性。（顺便一提，为了完整起见，解释 1 本身也包含许多模式。“柑橘类水果”是一个模式，“果皮”是一个模式，“酸爽”也是一个模式。解释 2 之所以更容易理解，仅仅是因为“葡萄柚”是一个更高级别的模式——一个由其他模式组成的模式。）

By calling up your grapefruit schema, we were able to teach you the concept of pomelo much faster than if we had mechanically listed all the attributes of a pomelo. Note, too, that it’s easier to answer the question about the blend of pomelo and orange juice. You know that grapefruit juice blends well with OJ, so the pomelo schema inherits this property from the grapefruit schema. (By the way, to be complete, Explanation 1 is itself full of schemas. “Citrus fruit” is a schema, “rind” is a schema, and “tangy” is a schema. Explanation 2 is easier to parse only because “grapefruit” is a higher-level schema—a schema composed of other schemas.)

通过运用图式，解释 2 可以同时提升我们的理解力和记忆力。让我们用倒金字塔结构来思考“柚子”的两个定义。那么，核心信息是什么呢？在解释 1 中，核心信息是：柑橘类水果。核心信息之后，并没有明显的层级关系；人们可能会记住一些信息，例如“柚子皮很厚但很软，容易剥皮”，这取决于什么信息最能吸引他们的注意力。剥开包装”）或颜色信息（“浅黄色至珊瑚粉色”）或多汁性信息或口味信息。

By using schemas, Explanation 2 improves both our comprehension and our memory. Let’s think about the two definitions of “pomelo” in terms of the inverted pyramid structure. What’s the lead? Well, with Explanation 1 the lead is: citrus fruit. After the lead, there is no clear hierarchy; depending on what catches people’s attention, they might remember the rind info (“very thick but soft and easy to peel away”) or the color info (“light yellow to coral pink”) or the juiciness info or the taste info.

解释2的开头是：像葡萄柚一样。第二段是：超大。第三段是：非常厚而软的果皮。

With Explanation 2, the lead is: grapefruit-like. The second paragraph is: supersized. The third paragraph is: very thick and soft rind.

六个月后，人们充其量只会记得我们故事的开头。也就是说，如果一个故事里提到“水果”或“柑橘类水果”，他们可能只会记得“葡萄柚”。第二个故事显然更好——这毋庸置疑。

Six months from now, people will remember—at best!—the lead of our story. That means that with one story they’d remember “fruit” or “citrus fruit.” With the other story they’d remember “grapefruit.” The second story is clearly better—it isn’t a judgment call.

这大概是你最后一次接触到关于柑橘类水果的心理学探讨了。虽然“柚子”这个概念本身可能并不值得你浪费脑力，但它背后的基本概念——图式能够带来深刻的简洁性——却至关重要。

This concludes what will probably be the last psychological discussion of citrus fruit you’ll ever encounter. But though the concept of “pomelo” may not be worth the neurons you just burned on it, the underlying concept—that schemas enable profound simplicity—is critical.

优秀的教师会凭直觉运用大量的概念框架。例如，经济学教师会从简洁明了的例子入手，让那些没有任何经济学概念框架的学生也能理解。“假设你种苹果，我种橙子。周围只有我们两个人。假设我们都想吃一些苹果和一些橙子，而不是只吃其中一种。我们应该交换吗？如果应该，我们该如何交换呢？”

Good teachers intuitively use lots of schemas. Economics teachers, for instance, start with compact, stripped-down examples that can be understood by students who have no preexisting economics schemas. “Let’s say that you grow apples and I grow oranges. We’re the only two people around. Let’s also say that we’d prefer to eat some of both fruits rather than all of either. Should we trade? If so, how do we go about doing it?”

学生们最初会学习在这种简化的情境下如何进行贸易。这些知识反过来会成为他们基本的贸易模式。一旦掌握了这种模式，就可以将其调用并沿某个维度进行扩展。例如，如果你突然更擅长种植苹果，会发生什么？我们还会像以前那样进行贸易吗？为了解决这个问题，我们需要调用一个现有的模式并对其进行调整，就像我们用葡萄柚的模式构建柚子一样。

Students are initially taught how trade works in this simplified context. This knowledge, in turn, becomes a basic trade schema for them. Once learned, this schema can be called up and stretched along some dimension. For example, what happens if you suddenly get better at growing apples? Do we still trade the same way we did before? To solve this problem, we’re calling up a schema and adapting it, just as we did in making a pomelo out of our grapefruit schema.

化繁为简

Complexity from Simplicity

图式帮助我们用简单的材料构建复杂的信息。在学校里，许多科学课程都是通过巧妙运用图式来进行教学的。入门级物理学研究的是简单、理想化的情境：滑轮、斜面、沿无摩擦路径匀速运动的物体。当学生熟悉了“滑轮”模型后，就可以对其进行扩展，或与其他模型结合，从而解决更复杂的问题。

Schemas help us create complex messages from simple materials. In school, lots of science courses are taught by clever uses of schemas. Introductory physics deals with simple, idealized situations: pulleys, inclines, objects moving at constant rates along frictionless paths. As students become familiar with the “pulley” schema, it can be stretched in some way or merged with other schemas to solve more complicated problems.

另一种巧妙运用示意图的例子是原子结构的太阳系模型，我们很多人小时候都学过这个模型。该模型假设电子围绕原子核运行，就像行星围绕太阳运行一样。这种类比能让学生快速、简洁地理解原子的工作原理。

Another nice use of a schema is the solar system model of the atom, which many of us were taught as kids. This model posits that electrons orbit the nucleus, much as planets orbit the sun. This analogy gives students a quick, compact insight into how the atom works.

行星的比喻也揭示了为什么许多人宁愿使用详尽的描述（“一种果皮柔软厚实的柑橘类水果，等等等等……”），也不愿使用简洁的图式（“一个超大的葡萄柚”）。使用图式有时意味着通往“真相”的道路会稍显缓慢。例如，物理学家现在知道，电子绕原子核运行的方式与行星不同。实际上，电子是在“概率云”中运动的。那么，你该如何向六年级学生解释呢？是谈论行星的运动，这很容易理解，也能让你更接近真相？还是谈论“概率云”，这虽然难以理解，但却很准确？

The planetary analogy also provides an insight into the reason that many people avoid compact schemas (“a supersized grapefruit”) in favor of exhaustive description (“a citrus fruit with a soft, thick rind, blah blah blah …”). The use of schemas can sometimes involve a somewhat slower route to the “real truth.” For instance, physicists now know that electrons don’t orbit the nucleus the way that planets do. In reality, electrons move in “probability clouds.” So what do you tell a sixth grader? Do you talk about the motion of planets, which is easy to understand and nudges you closer to the truth? Or do you talk about “probability clouds,” which are impossible to understand but accurate?

这个选择看似艰难：（1）优先考虑准确性，但牺牲易用性；或者（2）优先考虑易用性，但牺牲准确性。但在许多情况下，这其实是一个伪命题，原因很简单：如果一条信息无法用于预测或决策，那么无论它多么准确或全面，都毫无价值。

The choice may seem to be a difficult one: (1) accuracy first, at the expense of accessibility; or (2) accessibility first, at the expense of accuracy. But in many circumstances this is a false choice for one compelling reason: If a message can’t be used to make predictions or decisions, it is without value, no matter how accurate or comprehensive it is.

赫伯·凯莱赫可能会告诉空乘人员，她的目标是“最大化股东价值”。从某种意义上说，这种说法比“成为一家低价航空公司”更准确、更完整。毕竟，“一家低价航空公司”这个说法显然并不全面——西南航空可以通过取消飞机维护或要求乘客共用餐巾纸来降低票价。显然，还有其他价值（例如乘客舒适度、安全评级）可以进一步完善航空公司的运营。西南航空的核心价值在于经济实惠。尽管“股东价值最大化”这个概念本身没错，但它的问题在于，它并不能帮助空乘人员决定是否提供鸡肉沙拉。一个正确但无用的想法仍然是无用的。

Herb Kelleher could tell a flight attendant that her goal is to “maximize shareholder value.” In some sense, this statement is more accurate and complete than that the goal is to be “THE low-fare airline.” After all, the proverb “THE low-fare airline” is clearly incomplete—Southwest could offer lower fares by eliminating aircraft maintenance, or by asking passengers to share napkins. Clearly, there are additional values (customer comfort, safety ratings) that refine Southwest’s core value of economy. The problem with “maximize shareholder value,” despite its accuracy, is that it doesn’t help the flight attendant decide whether to serve chicken salad. An accurate but useless idea is still useless.

我们在引言中讨论了“知识诅咒”——即难以回忆起无知时的感受。精确到近乎无用的地步正是“知识诅咒”的症状之一。对首席执行官而言，“股东价值最大化”或许是一条极其有用的行为准则；但对空乘人员来说，却并非如此。对物理学家而言，概率云是令人着迷的现象；但对孩子来说，它们却难以理解。

We discussed the Curse of Knowledge in the introduction—the difficulty of remembering what it was like not to know something. Accuracy to the point of uselessness is a symptom of the Curse of Knowledge. To a CEO, “maximizing shareholder value” may be an immensely useful rule of behavior. To a flight attendant, it’s not. To a physicist, probability clouds are fascinating phenomena. To a child, they are incomprehensible.

人们总是忍不住想把所有事情都一股脑儿地、准确地告诉你，但他们应该先给你足够的信息，让你觉得有用，然后再多给你一点，最后再多给你一点。

People are tempted to tell you everything, with perfect accuracy, right up front, when they should be giving you just enough info to be useful, then a little more, then a little more.

好莱坞的模式：

高概念提案

Schemas in Hollywood:

High-concept Pitches

避免无用的精确性，摆脱知识诅咒的一个好方法就是运用类比。类比的力量源于认知图式：柚子就像葡萄柚；好消息的结构就像倒金字塔；皮肤损伤就像衰老。类比之所以能帮助我们理解简洁的信息，是因为它能唤起我们已知的概念。

A great way to avoid useless accuracy, and to dodge the Curse of Knowledge, is to use analogies. Analogies derive their power from schemas: A pomelo is like a grapefruit. A good news story is structured like an inverted pyramid. Skin damage is like aging. Analogies make it possible to understand a compact message because they invoke concepts that you already know.

好的比喻可以发挥巨大的作用。事实上，在好莱坞，一部耗资1亿美元的电影能否获得批准，很大程度上取决于一句比喻的说服力。

A good analogy can wield a lot of power. In fact, in Hollywood $100 million movies can be green-lighted based largely on the strength of a one-sentence analogy.

好莱坞制片厂平均每部电影都要审阅数百份提案或剧本。我们或许很难对制片厂高管的生活感同身受，但不妨试着想想。想象一下他们必须做出的那些令人胆战心惊的决定。当他们投资一部电影时，实际上是将数百万美元——以及他们自身的声誉——押注在一个无形的创意上。

The average Hollywood studio considers hundreds of pitches or screenplays for every movie it makes. It may be hard to muster sympathy for the life of studio execs, but let’s try for a moment. Imagine the terrifying decisions they must make. When they invest in a movie, they are essentially betting millions of dollars—and their own reputations—on an intangible idea.

对比一下电影创意和房屋设计图。如果一位建筑师设计了一份精美的房屋设计图，并且有人出资建造，那么你完全可以放心，九个月后，你就会拥有一栋能够实现建筑师最初设想的房子。

Contrast a movie pitch with the blueprint for a home. If an architect creates a nifty blueprint for a home, and someone puts up the money for construction, you can feel pretty confident that, nine months later, you’ll have a home that realizes the architect’s original vision.

另一方面，电影剧本注定会不断变化。当编剧被聘请后，故事会改变；当导演被聘请后，电影的艺术风格会改变；当明星被选中出演角色后，他们的个性会影响我们对故事中人物的感知；当制片人被聘请后，故事的讲述方式会受到资金和后勤方面的限制。而当电影在数月或数年后完成时，营销团队需要找到一种方法，在短短三十秒内向公众概括剧情——同时又不能透露太多信息。

A movie pitch, on the other hand, is destined to change. When a screenwriter is hired, the story will change. When a director is hired, the artistic feel of the movie will change. When stars are hired to play the parts, their personalities will change how we perceive the characters in the story. When producers are hired, the storytelling will become subject to financial and logistical constraints. And when the movie is completed, months or years later, the marketing team will need to find a way to explain the plot to the public in about thirty seconds—without giving away too much.

想象一下，你投入数百万美元到一个创意上，而这个创意会随着一连串自负之人的意识不断渗透而改变：导演、明星、制片人、营销人员。这个创意最好真的不错。

Imagine investing millions in an idea that will change as it is filtered through the consciousness of a succession of individuals with giant egos: directors, stars, producers, marketers. That idea had better be good.

在好莱坞，人们会使用一些核心创意，称为“高概念提案”。你可能听说过其中一些。《生死时速》的提案是“巴士版的《虎胆龙威》”。 《女孩梦三十》的提案是“女孩版的《大人物》”。《异形》的提案是“太空飞船版的《大白鲨》”。

In Hollywood, people use core ideas called “high-concept pitches.” You’ve probably heard some of them. Speed was “Die Hard on a bus.” 13 Going on 30 was “Big for girls.” Alien was “Jaws on a spaceship.”

那些高概念的提案并非总是引用其他电影。例如， 《E.T.外星人》的提案是“迷失的外星人与孤独的男孩成为朋友，以此返回家园”。但很多提案确实会提及过去的电影。这是为什么呢？是因为好莱坞充斥着那些厚颜无耻地重复利用旧创意的愤世嫉俗的高管吗？

The high-concept pitches don’t always reference other movies. E.T., for instance, was pitched as “Lost alien befriends lonely boy to get home.” But a lot of pitches do invoke past movies. Why is that? Is it because Hollywood is full of cynical execs who shamelessly recycle old ideas?

没错，但这只是部分原因。电影《生死时速》的概念在被提出之前，显然并不存在于高管们的脑海中。这就像“柚子”这个词，在你了解它的含义之前，它并不存在。“公交车上的《虎胆龙威》”这句简洁的五个字短语，赋予了原本不存在的“生死时速”惊人的意义。速度的概念。为了理解这一点，想想你仅凭这五个字就能做出的所有重要决定。你会聘请动作片导演还是独立导演？动作片。你会给电影预算1000万美元还是1亿美元？1亿美元。你会选择大牌明星还是群戏？大牌明星。你会选择暑期档还是圣诞档？暑期档。

Well, yes, but that’s only part of the reason. The concept of the movie Speed, before it was pitched, obviously did not exist in the minds of the execs. It was like the word “pomelo,” before you knew what it meant. The compact, five-word phrase “Die Hard on a bus” pours a breathtaking amount of meaning into the previously nonexistent concept of Speed. To see this, think of all the important decisions you could make, just on the strength of those five words. Do you hire an action director or an indie director? Action. Do you budget $10 million for the movie or $100 million? $100 million. Big star or ensemble cast? Big star. Target a summer release or a Christmas release? Summer.

再举个例子，假设你刚刚受聘担任新电影《异形》的制作设计师。你的任务是设计电影中大部分场景发生的宇宙飞船。它会是什么样子呢？如果你对这部电影一无所知，那么明智的做法是从研究以往的宇宙飞船设计入手。例如，想想《星际迷航》中企业号那酷炫而精致的内部设计。

As another example, imagine that you were just hired to be the production designer on the new film Alien. It will be your job to design the spaceship where most of the movie takes place. What does it look like? If you knew nothing at all about the movie, you might sensibly start by looking at old spaceship designs. For instance, think of the cool, immaculate interior of the Enterprise on Star Trek.

然后你的老板告诉你，这部电影的构想是“太空飞船上的《大白鲨》”。这彻底改变了一切。《大白鲨》并不酷炫完美。理查德·德莱弗斯在一艘摇摇晃晃的旧船上摸索前行。决策仓促、草率，令人窒息，焦虑不安。环境闷热潮湿。当你思考《大白鲨》的成功之处时，你的想法开始成形：飞船将简陋、肮脏、压抑。船员不会穿着鲜艳的莱卡制服。房间不会光线充足、一尘不染。

Then your boss tells you that the vision for the movie is “Jaws on a spaceship.” That changes everything. Jaws was not cool or immaculate. Richard Dreyfus navigated around on a rickety old boat. Decisions were rushed, slapdash, claustrophobic, anxiety-ridden. The environment was sweaty. As you think about what made Jaws tick, your ideas start to take shape: The ship will be underdeveloped, dingy, and oppressive. The crew members will not wear bright Lycra uniforms. The rooms will not be well lit and lintless.

高概念提案是好莱坞版的经典格言。和大多数格言一样，它们运用了类比的力量。通过援引已有的模式（例如，电影《大白鲨》的模式），这些格言能够极大地加快新电影制作人员的学习过程。

High-concept pitches are Hollywood’s version of core proverbs. Like most proverbs, they tap the power of analogy. By invoking schemas that already exist (e.g., what the movie Jaws is like), the proverbs radically accelerate the learning process for people working on a brand-new movie.

显然，好的创意并不等同于好的电影。“太空飞船上的大白鲨”如果没有数百位才华横溢的人多年来的共同努力，很可能就会变成一部烂片。另一方面，糟糕的创意——或者说糟糕的谚语——足以毁掉一部电影。没有哪个导演能拯救太空飞船上的《母女情深》。

Obviously, a good pitch is not synonymous with a good movie. “Jaws on a spaceship” could have turned into a terrible movie if it weren’t for the contributions of hundreds of talented people over a period of years. On the other hand, a bad pitch—a bad proverb—is plenty to ruin a movie. No director could save “Terms of Endearment on a spaceship.”

如果高概念的提案在电影界都能拥有这种力量——在一个自我密度是正常水平四十倍的环境中——我们应该有信心，我们能够在自己的环境中运用同样的力量。

If high-concept pitches can have this power in the movie world—an environment filled with forty times the normal density of egos—we should feel confident that we can harness the same power in our own environments.

生成类比

Generative Analogies

有些类比非常有用，它们不仅能阐明某个概念，还能成为激发新思维的平台。例如，将大脑比作计算机的比喻，在过去五十年中一直是认知心理学家们获得诸多洞见的核心。定义计算机的工作原理比定义大脑的工作原理要容易得多。因此，心理学家可以利用计算机中各种已被充分理解的方面——例如内存、缓冲区或处理器——作为灵感，在大脑中寻找类似的功能，这往往是卓有成效的。

Some analogies are so useful that they don’t merely shed light on a concept, they actually become platforms for novel thinking. For example, the metaphor of the brain as a computer has been central to the insights generated by cognitive psychologists during the past fifty years. It’s easier to define how a computer works than to define how the brain works. For this reason it can be fruitful for psychologists to use various, well-understood aspects of a computer—such as memory, buffers, or processors—as inspiration to locate similar functions in the brain.

好的比喻是“生成性的”。心理学家唐纳德·肖恩提出了这个术语，用来描述那些能够产生“新的感知、解释和发明”的比喻。许多简单易懂、深入人心的想法实际上都是伪装的生成性比喻。例如，迪士尼称其员工为“演员”。这种将员工比作戏剧制作中的演员的比喻，在整个组织中始终如一地被传达：

Good metaphors are “generative.” The psychologist Donald Schon introduced this term to describe metaphors that generate “new perceptions, explanations, and inventions.” Many simple sticky ideas are actually generative metaphors in disguise. For example, Disney calls its employees “cast members.” This metaphor of employees as cast members in a theatrical production is communicated consistently throughout the organization:

  • 演员们不是去面试工作，而是去试镜角色。

  • Cast members don’t interview for a job, they audition for a role.

  • 当他们漫步在公园里时，他们就站在舞台上。

  • When they are walking around the park, they are onstage.

  • 去迪士尼乐园的人是客人，不是顾客。

  • People visiting Disney are guests, not customers.

  • 工作是表演；制服是戏服。

  • Jobs are performances; uniforms are costumes.

剧院比喻对迪士尼员工来说非常有用。它如此实用，以至于仅仅阅读最后几段，你大概就能预测出演人员在尚未讨论的情况下应该如何表现。例如，你大概可以猜到，员工们演员在公共区域穿着戏服时不允许休息。（演员绝不会在拍摄过程中边聊天边抽烟。）你或许会认为，街道清洁工的考核标准并非仅仅是人行道的清洁程度。事实上，街道清洁工是训练有素的演职人员之一，因为他们经常出现在公众视野中——再加上他们显然是迪士尼员工——这使得他们很容易成为游客询问游乐设施、游行和洗手间位置等问题的对象。让他们将自己的角色视为表演，而非维护，是乐园成功的关键因素之一。“员工如同演职人员”这一富有启发性的比喻，已在迪士尼成功运用了五十多年。

The theater metaphor is immensely useful for Disney employees. It is so useful that just by reading the last few paragraphs you can probably predict how cast members should behave in situations we haven’t discussed. For instance, you can probably guess that employees are not allowed to be on break while in costume and in a public area. (An actor would never have a chat and a cigarette in mid-scene.) You might guess that street sweepers are evaluated on criteria other than the cleanliness of their sidewalks. Indeed, street sweepers are some of the most highly trained cast members, since their very visible public presence—coupled with the fact that they are clearly Disney employees—makes them an obvious target for customers’ questions about rides, parades, and restroom locations. Having them think of their role as performance, rather than maintenance, is a key part of the park’s success. “Employees as cast members” is a generative metaphor that has worked for Disney for more than fifty years.

将迪士尼与赛百味进行对比。和迪士尼一样，赛百味也为其一线员工创造了一个比喻，称他们为“三明治艺术家”。这个比喻与迪士尼的“演职人员”截然相反，它完全无法指导员工的行为。迪士尼期望其演职人员像演员一样行事，但赛百味并不期望其柜台服务员像艺术家一样。“艺术家”的本质特征是个性表达。我们不禁要问，如果一位员工在赛百味展现出过多的个性——无论是着装、互动方式还是三明治的摆盘——她还能坚持多久？毫无疑问，赛百味的“三明治艺术家”被赋予了在12英寸潜艇堡上放一把洋葱的权力，这确实是一种自由裁量权。但人们不禁怀疑，柜台服务员的“艺术才能”是否足以让他们随意添加一片火鸡肉。

Contrast Disney with Subway. Like Disney, Subway has created a metaphor for its frontline employees. They are “sandwich artists.” This metaphor is the evil twin of Disney’s “cast members.” It is utterly useless as a guide to how the employee should act. Disney expects its cast members to behave like actors, but Subway does not expect its counter help to behave like artists. The defining trait of an “artist” is individual expression. We wonder how long an employee would last at Subway if she exhibited a lot of individual expression—in dress, in interaction, in the presentation of sandwiches. No doubt Subway’s sandwich artists are trusted to place a handful of onions on a twelve-inch sub, and it’s true that this is a certain kind of liberty. But one suspects that the counter person’s “artistry” can’t extend to adding an extra slice of turkey.

简约的力量

The Power of Simplicity

生成性隐喻和谚语的力量都源于巧妙的替代：它们用易于理解的事物代替了难以理解的事物。谚语“一鸟在手胜过二鸟在林”为我们提供了一个具体、易于理解的陈述，我们可以用它来指导我们应对复杂且充满情感纠葛的处境。生成性隐喻也发挥着类似的作用。迪士尼的“演员们”他们可能会发现，从受雇演员的角度处理新情况比从自己独特的个人角度处理新情况更容易。

Generative metaphors and proverbs both derive their power from a clever substitution: They substitute something easy to think about for something difficult. The proverb “A bird in hand is worth two in the bush” gives us a tangible, easily processed statement that we can use for guidance in complex, emotionally fraught situations. Generative metaphors perform a similar role. The “cast members” at Disney might find it easier to tackle a new situation from the perspective of a hired actor than from their own unique individual perspective.

谚语是简洁的典范。想出一句简短精炼的短语很容易，人人都能做到。但要想写出一句深刻精炼的短语却难上加难。本章我们试图阐明的是，这种努力是值得的——“找到核心”，并将其表达成简洁的理念，可以产生持久而强大的力量。

Proverbs are the Holy Grail of simplicity. Coming up with a short, compact phrase is easy. Anybody can do it. On the other hand, coming up with a profound compact phrase is incredibly difficult. What we’ve tried to show in this chapter is that the effort is worth it—that “finding the core,” and expressing it in the form of a compact idea, can be enduringly powerful.



意外



UNEXPECTED

B根据美国联邦航空管理局的规定，乘务员必须在客机起飞前进行安全广播。我们都听过这些广播：出口在哪里，如果“机舱压力突然变化”该怎么办，如何利用座椅作为漂浮装置，以及为什么不应该在洗手间吸烟（或擅自改动烟雾报警器）。

By FAA edict, a flight attendant must make a safety announcement before a passenger plane takes off. We’ve all heard it: where the exits are, what to do in case of a “sudden change in cabin pressure,” how to use your seat as a flotation device, and why you shouldn’t smoke in the lavatories (or tinker with the smoke alarm).

飞行安全广播或许可以被视为一种难以接受的信息环境。没人关心广播内容。乘务员不在乎，乘客也不在乎。相比之下，冗长辩论就显得有趣多了。

Flight-safety announcements might be labeled a tough message environment. No one cares about what’s being communicated. The flight attendant doesn’t care. The passengers don’t care. Filibusters are fascinating by comparison.

如果你被要求进行安全广播呢？更糟糕的是，如果你真的需要人们听你广播呢？你会如何处理？

What if you were asked to make the safety announcement? Worse, what if you actually needed people to listen to you? How would you handle it?

一位名叫凯伦·伍德的空乘人员就遇到了这种情况，她巧妙地解决了这个问题。在从达拉斯飞往圣地亚哥的航班上，她进行了如下广播：

A flight attendant named Karen Wood faced exactly this situation and solved it with creativity. On a flight from Dallas to San Diego, she made the following announcement:

如果您能抽出几分钟时间，我们非常乐意向您介绍这些安全功能。如果您自 1965 年以来就没坐过车，那么正确的系安全带方法是：将扁平的一端滑入扣环中。要解开，向上提起扣环即可。

If I could have your attention for a few moments, we sure would love to point out these safety features. If you haven’t been in an automobile since 1965, the proper way to fasten your seat belt is to slide the flat end into the buckle. To unfasten, lift up on the buckle and it will release.

正如歌里唱的，离开爱人或许有五十种方法，但离开这架飞机只有六种途径：两个前部紧急出口、两个可拆卸的机翼上方舷窗出口和两个后部紧急出口。每个出口的位置都有清晰的指示牌，过道地板上还装有红白相间的迪斯科灯。

And as the song goes, there might be fifty ways to leave your lover, but there are only six ways to leave this aircraft: two forward exit doors, two over-wing removable window exits, and two aft exit doors. The location of each exit is clearly marked with signs overhead, as well as red and white disco lights along the floor of the aisle.

让你看了！

Made ya look!

乘客们很快就被伍德的幽默讲话吸引住了。当她结束广播时，零星的掌声响起。（如果一条精心设计的广播信息能让人们为安全提示鼓掌，那么我们所有人都有希望了。）

It didn’t take long for passengers to tune into Wood’s comic spiel. When she wrapped up her announcement, scattered applause broke out. (And if a well-designed message can make people applaud for a safety announcement there’s hope for all of us.)

沟通的首要难题在于如何吸引他人的注意力。有些沟通者天生具有吸引他人注意力的能力。父母在这方面就做得很好：“鲍比，看我！” 然而，大多数时候，我们无法强求他人的注意力；我们必须吸引它。这才是真正的挑战。人们常说：“你不可能强迫别人关注你”，这话听起来似乎很有道理。但等等：凯伦·伍德恰恰做到了这一点。她成功地吸引了人们的注意力，而且她甚至不需要提高音量。

The first problem of communication is getting people’s attention. Some communicators have the authority to demand attention. Parents are good at this: “Bobby, look at me!” Most of the time, though, we can’t demand attention; we must attract it. This is a tougher challenge. People say, “You can’t make people pay attention,” and there is a commonsense ring to that. But wait a minute: That’s exactly what Karen Wood did. She made people pay attention, and she didn’t even need to raise her voice.

吸引他人注意力的最基本方法就是：打破常规。人类对一成不变的模式适应速度惊人。持续的感官刺激会让我们习以为常：想想空调的嗡嗡声、交通噪音、蜡烛的香味，或是书架的景象。只有当情况发生变化时，我们才会意识到这些事物的存在：空调关掉了，或者你的伴侣重新整理了书架。

The most basic way to get someone’s attention is this: Break a pattern. Humans adapt incredibly quickly to consistent patterns. Consistent sensory stimulation makes us tune out: Think of the hum of an air conditioner, or traffic noise, or the smell of a candle, or the sight of a bookshelf. We may become consciously aware of these things only when something changes: The air conditioner shuts off. Your spouse rearranges the books.

在信息传播氛围紧张的情况下，伍德通过避免重复乘客们已经听过无数遍的千篇一律的安全须知，成功吸引了人们的注意力。她讲笑话，这不仅吸引了人们的注意力，而且她保留了这种方式。但如果伍德的唯一目的只是为了吸引注意力，她就没必要如此卖弄风趣了。她完全可以先播报，然后突然停顿一下，或者切换到俄语几秒钟，这样就能轻松吸引乘客的注意力。

Wood got people’s attention in a message-hostile environment by avoiding the same generic safety spiel that her passengers had heard many times. She told jokes, which not only got people’s attention but kept it. But if getting attention had been Wood’s only concern, she wouldn’t have needed to be so entertaining. She could have gotten passengers’ attention just as easily by starting the announcement and then suddenly pausing in midsentence. Or switching to Russian for a few seconds.

我们的大脑天生对变化极其敏感。聪明的产品设计师深谙此道。他们会确保，当产品需要用户集中注意力时，某些东西会发生变化。警示灯会闪烁，因为我们会对持续亮着的灯视而不见。老式的紧急警报器发出两声短促的鸣叫，而现代警报器则发出更复杂的鸣叫，更加引人注目。汽车警报器巧妙地利用了我们对变化的敏感性。

Our brain is designed to be keenly aware of changes. Smart product designers are well aware of this tendency. They make sure that, when products require users to pay attention, something changes. Warning lights blink on and off because we would tune out a light that was constantly on. Old emergency sirens wailed in a two-note pattern, but modern sirens wail in a more complex pattern that’s even more attention-grabbing. Car alarms make diabolical use of our change sensitivity.

本章重点探讨两个关键问题：如何吸引人们的注意力？以及同样至关重要的，如何保持他们的注意力？如果我们的信息无法突破信息过载的困境，吸引人们的注意力，我们就无法成功。此外，我们的信息通常相当复杂，如果我们无法保持人们的注意力，我们也无法取得成功。

This chapter focuses on two essential questions: How do I get people’s attention? And, just as crucially, How do I keep it? We can’t succeed if our messages don’t break through the clutter to get people’s attention. Furthermore, our messages are usually complex enough that we won’t succeed if we can’t keep people’s attention.

要理解这两个问题的答案，我们必须了解两种基本情绪——惊讶和兴趣——这两种情绪通常会被一些具有吸引力的想法所激发。

To understand the answers to these two questions, we have to understand two essential emotions—surprise and interest—that are commonly provoked by naturally sticky ideas.

  • 出人意料总能吸引我们的注意力。一些天生具有吸引力的观点会提出一些令人惊讶的“事实”：长城是唯一能从太空看到的人造建筑！你只用了大脑的10%！你应该每天喝八杯水！都市传说中常常包含令人意想不到的情节转折。

  • Surprise gets our attention. Some naturally sticky ideas propose surprising “facts”: The Great Wall of China is the only man-made structure visible from space! You use only 10 percent of your brain! You should drink eight glasses of water a day! Urban legends frequently contain surprising plot twists.

  • 兴趣能吸引我们的注意力。有些想法经久不衰，能持续激发我们的兴趣。阴谋论让人们如饥似渴地搜集新信息。八卦则让我们不断地向朋友打听最新进展。

  • Interest keeps our attention. There are classes of sticky ideas that maintain our interest over time. Conspiracy theories keep people ravenously collecting new information. Gossip keeps us coming back to our friends for developments.

真正具有影响力的想法往往出人意料。如果我们能让自己的想法更加出人意料，它们就会更具影响力。但是，我们真的能制造出“出人意料”吗？“计划中的出人意料”难道不是自相矛盾吗？

Naturally sticky ideas are frequently unexpected. If we can make our ideas more unexpected, they will be stickier. But can you generate “unexpectedness”? Isn’t “planned unexpectedness” an oxymoron?

吸引人们的注意力

GETTING PEOPLE’S ATTENTION

从来没有人这样做过

No One Ever Does

全新Enclave小型货车的电视广告以Enclave停在公园前开场。一个男孩戴着橄榄球头盔爬进车里，他的两个妹妹紧随其后。“全新Enclave，隆重登场！”女声旁白响起。爸爸坐在驾驶座上，妈妈坐在副驾驶座上。车内到处都是杯架。爸爸发动汽车，驶离路边。“这是一款极致的小型货车。”

The television commercial for the new Enclave minivan opens with the Enclave sitting in front of a park. A boy holding a football helmet climbs into the minivan, followed by his two younger sisters. “Introducing the all-new Enclave,” begins a woman’s voice-over. Dad is behind the wheel and Mom is in the passenger seat. Cup holders are everywhere. Dad starts the car and pulls away from the curb. “It’s a minivan to the max.”

这辆小型货车缓缓驶过郊区街道。“它配备了遥控滑动后门、150个有线电视频道、全景天窗、温控杯架和六点导航系统……是忙碌家庭的理想之选。”

The minivan cruises slowly through suburban streets. “With features like remote-controlled sliding rear doors, 150 cable channels, a full sky-view roof, temperature-controlled cup holders, and the six-point navigation system … It’s the minivan for families on the go.”

恩克莱夫轿车在十字路口停了下来。镜头拉近，男孩正凝视着侧窗外，窗外映照着枝繁叶茂的大树。爸爸把车开进了路口。

The Enclave pulls to a stop at an intersection. The camera zooms in on the boy, gazing out a side window that reflects giant, leafy trees. Dad pulls into the intersection.

就在那时，事情发生了。

That’s when it happens.

一辆超速行驶的汽车冲进十字路口，与小型货车侧面相撞。碰撞声震耳欲聋，金属扭曲变形，玻璃碎片四溅。

A speeding car barrels into the intersection and broadsides the minivan. There is a terrifying collision, with metal buckling and an explosion of broken glass.

屏幕渐黑，出现一条信息：“没想到吧？”

The screen fades to black, and a message appears: “Didn’t see that coming?”

这个问题逐渐消失，取而代之的是一句陈述：“从来没有人这样做过。”

The question fades and is replaced by a statement: “No one ever does.”

伴随着卡住的喇叭声在背景中响起，屏幕上闪过最后几个字：“系好安全带……永远。”

With the sound of a stuck horn blaring in the background, a few final words flash across the screen: “Buckle up … Always.”

没有Enclave这款小型货车。此广告由广告公司制作。广告委员会。（Enclave 的广告由美国交通部赞助。）广告委员会成立于 1942 年，曾发起过许多成功的广告活动，从二战时期的“祸从口出”到最近的“朋友不让朋友酒后驾车”。Enclave 的广告，如同广告委员会的许多其他广告一样，充分利用了成功创意的第二个特点：出人意料。

There is no Enclave minivan. This ad was created by the Ad Council. (The Enclave spot was sponsored by the U.S. Department of Transportation.) The Ad Council, founded in 1942, has launched many successful campaigns, from the World War II—era “Loose Lips Sink Ships” to the more recent “Friends Don’t Let Friends Drive Drunk.” The Enclave ad, like many other Council ads, capitalizes on the second characteristic of sticky ideas: Unexpectedness.

Enclave 的广告之所以出人意料，是因为它打破了我们对汽车广告的固有认知。我们都知道汽车广告应该是什么样子：皮卡攀爬巨石堆；跑车在空旷蜿蜒的道路上飞驰；SUV 载着雅皮士穿过森林前往瀑布；而小型货车则接送孩子去踢足球。从来没有人因此丧命。

The Enclave ad is unexpected because it violates our schema for car commercials. We know how car commercials are supposed to behave. Pickups climb mountains of boulders. Sports cars zip along vacant curvy roads. SUVs carry yuppies through forests to waterfalls. And minivans deliver kids to soccer practice. No one dies, ever.

这则广告的第二个出人意料之处在于：它打破了我们对现实生活中社区出行的固有认知。我们每天在社区里出行成千上万次，绝大多数都能平安无事地结束。这则广告提醒我们，意外总是难以预料——我们应该系好安全带，以防万一。

The ad is unexpected in a second way: It violates our schema of real-life neighborhood trips. We take thousands of trips in our neighborhoods, and the vast majority of them end safely. The commercial reminds us that accidents are inherently unexpected—we ought to buckle up, just in case.

我们的认知图式就像猜谜机。认知图式帮助我们预测将会发生什么，从而指导我们如何决策。“飞地”会问：“没想到吧？”没错，我们确实没想到。我们的猜谜机失灵了，这让我们措手不及。

Our schemas are like guessing machines. Schemas help us predict what will happen and, consequently, how we should make decisions. The Enclave asks, “Didn’t see that coming?” No, we didn’t. Our guessing machines failed, which caused us to be surprised.

情绪经过巧妙的调节，能够帮助我们应对危机情况。它们让我们做好应对不同行为和思考方式的准备。我们都听说过，愤怒会让我们做好战斗的准备，恐惧会让我们做好逃跑的准备。然而，情绪和行为之间的联系可能更加微妙。例如，研究人员最近发现，愤怒的一个次要影响是，我们会更加确信自己的判断。当我们生气时，我们觉得自己是对的，任何有过恋爱经历的人都能证明这一点。

Emotions are elegantly tuned to help us deal with critical situations. They prepare us for different ways of acting and thinking. We’ve all heard that anger prepares us to fight and fear prepares us to flee. The linkages between emotion and behavior can be more subtle, though. For instance, a secondary effect of being angry, which was recently discovered by researchers, is that we become more certain of our judgments. When we’re angry, we know we’re right, as anyone who has been in a relationship can attest.

如果情绪具有生物学意义，那么惊讶的生物学意义是什么呢？惊讶能让我们集中注意力。当我们的认知图式失效时，惊讶就会被触发，它让我们做好准备去理解失效的原因。当我们的猜测机制出现故障时，惊讶会抓住我们的注意力，以便我们能够修复它，避免将来再次发生类似情况。

So if emotions have biological purposes, then what is the biological purpose of surprise? Surprise jolts us to attention. Surprise is triggered when our schemas fail, and it prepares us to understand why the failure occurred. When our guessing machines fail, surprise grabs our attention so that we can repair them for the future.

惊喜眉毛

The Surprise Brow

惊讶与一种在不同文化中都普遍存在的面部表情联系在一起。在《揭开面孔的面纱》一书中，保罗·埃克曼和华莱士·弗里森创造了“惊讶眉”一词来描述惊讶时独特的面部表情：“眉毛看起来弯曲且高挑……眉毛下方的皮肤因眉毛的抬起而被拉伸，比平时更加​​明显。”

Surprise is associated with a facial expression that is consistent across cultures.
