In a book called Unmasking the Face, Paul Ekman and Wallace Friesen coined a term, “the surprise brow,” to describe the distinctive facial expression of surprise: “The eyebrows appear curved and high…. The skin below the brow has been stretched by the lifting of the brow, and is more visible than usual.”

当我们扬起眉毛时，眼睛会睁大，视野也会更开阔——这种惊讶的扬眉动作是我们身体强迫我们看得更清楚的一种方式。我们可能还会仔细确认一下，确保自己没看错。与之相反，当我们生气时，眼睛会眯起来，以便集中精力关注已知的问题。除了扬眉之外，惊讶还会导致我们下巴下垂，嘴巴张大。我们会一时语塞，说不出话来。身体会暂时停止运动，肌肉也会放松。仿佛我们的身体想要确保我们在应该接收新信息的时候，不会说话或动来动去。

When our brows go up, it widens our eyes and gives us a broader field of vision—the surprise brow is our body’s way of forcing us to see more. We may also do a double take to make sure that we saw what we thought we saw. By way of contrast, when we’re angry our eyes narrow so that we can focus on a known problem. In addition to making our eyebrows rise, surprise causes our jaws to drop and our mouths to gape. We’re struck momentarily speechless. Our bodies temporarily stop moving and our muscles go slack. It’s as though our bodies want to ensure that we’re not talking or moving when we ought to be taking in new information.

所以，当我们遇到意料之外的事情，而我们的猜测机制失效时，惊讶就起到了一种紧急应对的作用。事情会停滞不前，正在进行的活动会被打断，我们的注意力会不由自主地集中在让我们感到惊讶的事件上。当一个小型货车的广告以一场惊心动魄的车祸结尾时，我们会停下来思考：到底发生了什么？

So surprise acts as a kind of emergency override when we confront something unexpected and our guessing machines fail. Things come to a halt, ongoing activities are interrupted, our attention focuses involuntarily on the event that surprised us. When a minivan commercial ends in a bloodcurdling crash, we stop and wonder, What is going on?

出乎意料的想法更容易被记住，因为惊喜会促使我们关注和思考。这种额外的关注和思考会将意外事件深深地烙印在我们的记忆中。惊喜能够吸引我们的注意力。有时这种注意力转瞬即逝，但在其他情况下，惊喜却能带来持久的关注。惊喜可以促使我们探寻其背后的原因，设想其他的可能性，并思考如何避免未来再次发生类似的意外。

Unexpected ideas are more likely to stick because surprise makes us pay attention and think. That extra attention and thinking sears unexpected events into our memories. Surprise gets our attention. Sometimes the attention is fleeting, but in other cases surprise can lead to enduring attention. Surprise can prompt us to hunt for underlying causes, to imagine other possibilities, to figure out how to avoid surprises in the future.

例如，研究阴谋论的学者注意到，许多阴谋论产生于人们应对意想不到的事件之时。一些事件，例如年轻貌美的人突然去世，往往会引发人们的猜测。关于肯尼迪、玛丽莲·梦露、猫王和科特·柯本的猝死，都有阴谋论的说法。相比之下，人们对九十岁老人的猝死似乎不太感兴趣。

Researchers who study conspiracy theories, for instance, have noted that many of them arise when people are grappling with unexpected events, such as when the young and attractive die suddenly. There are conspiracy theories about the sudden deaths of JFK, Marilyn Monroe, Elvis, and Kurt Cobain. There tends to be less conspiratorial interest in the sudden deaths of ninety-year-olds.

惊讶会让我们想要找到答案——想要弄明白为什么我们会感到惊讶——而巨大的惊讶则需要强有力的解释。如果我们想要吸引人们的注意力，就应该充分利用巨大惊讶的力量。

Surprise makes us want to find an answer—to resolve the question of why we were surprised—and big surprises call for big answers. If we want to motivate people to pay attention, we should seize the power of big surprises.

避免花哨噱头

Avoiding Gimmickry

然而，试图制造惊喜可能会引发大问题。很容易就会越界，沦为哗众取宠的噱头。

Going for a big surprise, though, can cause a big problem. It’s easy to step over the line into gimmickry.

上世纪90年代末是互联网泡沫的鼎盛时期。风险投资支持的初创公司投入数百万美元用于广告宣传，以打造品牌。随着越来越多的资金争夺有限的消费者注意力，广告商不得不绞尽脑汁，才能激发消费者的好奇心和兴趣。

The late 1990s was the heyday of the dot-com bubble. Venture-backed start-ups poured millions of dollars into advertising to establish their brands. With increasing amounts of money chasing a finite amount of consumer attention, ads had to work harder and harder to provoke surprise and interest.

2000 年超级碗比赛期间，一则广告以一支大学行进乐队在橄榄球场上练习的场景开场。镜头特写乐队成员们精准的演奏动作。随后画面切换到通往球场的球员通道——突然，十几头饥饿的狼冲进球场。乐队成员惊恐地四散奔逃，狼群开始追捕并攻击他们。

During the Super Bowl of 2000, an ad ran that opened with a college marching band practicing on a football field. We’re shown close-ups of the band members as they execute their precision movements. Then we cut to the stadium tunnel, which leads out onto the field—and suddenly a dozen ravenous wolves rush onto the field. Band members scatter in terror as the wolves hunt them down and attack.

这则广告到底想表达什么？我们完全摸不着头脑。毫无疑问，这则广告确实出人意料，令人印象深刻。时至今日，我们依然记得那幅略显低俗的滑稽画面：一群狼追赶着惊恐万分的乐队成员。但由于这种突兀的场面与广告想要传达的信息毫无关联，因此毫无价值。然而，如果广告宣传的产品是“防狼撕咬的乐队制服”，那么这则广告或许就能斩获大奖。

What was this advertisement for? We have no idea. There’s no question that this ad was surprising and memorable. To this day, we remember the tastelessly comic image of the wolves chasing the terrified band members. But because the surprise was utterly nongermane to the message that needed to be communicated, it was worthless. If the product being advertised had been “mauling-proof band uniforms,” on the other hand, the ad could have been an award winner.

从这个意义上讲，狼群广告与飞地广告正好相反。广告中常常包含令人惊喜的元素，但只有Enclave的广告巧妙地利用了这些惊喜来强化其核心信息。在第一章中，我们讨论了找到创意核心的重要性。运用惊喜来传递核心信息，效果可能非常显著。

In this sense, the wolves ad is the opposite of the Enclave ad. Both ads contain powerful surprises, but only the Enclave ad uses that surprise to reinforce its core message. In Chapter 1 we discussed the importance of finding the core in your ideas. Using surprise in the service of a core message can be extremely powerful.

亨森和弗劳格

Hension and Phraug

下面列出了四个单词。请阅读每个单词，并花一点时间判断它是否是真正的英语单词。

Below is a list of four words. Read each one and take a second to determine whether it’s a real English word.

亨森·

巴德尔

·弗劳格·

泰布尔

HENSION

BARDLE

PHRAUG

TAYBL

据开发这项任务的研究人员布鲁斯·惠特尔西和丽莎·威廉姆斯称，“PHRAUG 和 TAYBL 经常会引起人们的惊讶和‘哦！’的反应。HENSION 和 BARDLE 经常会引起人们的皱眉。”

According to Bruce Whittlesea and Lisa Williams, the researchers who developed this task, “PHRAUG and TAYBL often cause raised eyebrows, and an ‘Oh!’ reaction. HENSION and BARDLE often cause a frown.”

PHRAUG 和 TAYBL 这两个词让人感到意外，因为它们看起来陌生，但听起来却很熟悉。“哦！”的一声来自于我们意识到 PHRAUG 只是 FROG（青蛙）的一种有趣的拼写方式。

PHRAUG and TAYBL cause the surprise brow because they look unfamiliar but sound familiar. The “Oh!” reaction comes when we realize that PHRAUG is just a funny way to spell FROG.

HENSION 和 BARDLE 更让人困惑。它们看起来似曾相识，因为它们借用了常用词的字母组合。它们看起来像是 SAT 考试词汇——一些我们本应知道却不知道的生僻词。但 HENSION 和 BARDLE 却是自造词。当我们意识到自己一直在苦苦寻找一个根本不存在的答案时，就会感到沮丧。

HENSION and BARDLE are more troubling. They seem oddly familiar, because they borrow letter combinations from common words. They have the look of SAT words—fancy vocabulary that we should probably know but don’t. But HENSION and BARDLE are made-up words. When we realize that we’ve been struggling to find a nonexistent solution, we get frustrated.

HENSION 和 BARDLE 就是一个缺乏洞察力的惊喜例子。到目前为止，我们已经讨论了很多关于惊喜的力量，以及惊喜如何让我们的想法更具吸引力。但是，尽管 HENSION 和 BARDLE 令人惊讶，但它们并不具有吸引力；它们只会让人感到沮丧。我们现在看到的是，仅仅有惊喜是不够的。我们还需要洞察力。

HENSION and BARDLE provide an example of surprise without insight. So far, we’ve talked a lot about the power of surprise, and how surprise can make our ideas stickier. But although HENSION and BARDLE are surprising, they aren’t sticky; they’re just frustrating. What we see now is that surprise isn’t enough. We also need insight.

要想令人惊喜，事件就不能是可以预测的。惊喜与可预测性截然相反。但是，要想让人感到满足，惊喜必须是“后可预测的”。剧情反转在你思考之后会变得合情合理，但你事先是绝对想不到的。PHRAUG 的结局是后可预测的，而 HENSION 则不是。对比一下你从电视剧或电影（例如《第六感》）中感受到的那种震撼——那些结局巧妙地将你一直以来接触到的线索串联起来——与那些噱头十足、出人意料的结局（例如“一切都是一场梦”）所带来的感受。

To be surprising, an event can’t be predictable. Surprise is the opposite of predictability. But, to be satisfying, surprise must be “post-dictable.” The twist makes sense after you think about it, but it’s not something you would have seen coming. PHRAUG is post-dictable, but HENSION isn’t. Contrast the feeling you get from TV shows or films, such as The Sixth Sense, that have great surprise endings—endings that unite clues that you’ve been exposed to all along—with the feeling you get from gimmicky, unforeseeable endings (“It was all a dream”).

本章伊始，我们指出，当我们的预测机制失效时，就会产生意外。这种意外情绪旨在引导我们关注失败，从而改进未来的预测机制。随后，我们区分了噱头式的意外（例如互联网广告）和意义深远的、超越可预测性的意外。

We started the chapter by pointing out that surprise happens when our guessing machines fail. The emotion of surprise is designed to focus our attention on the failure, so that we can improve our guessing machines for the future. Then we drew a distinction between gimmicky surprise, like dot-com ads, and meaningful post-dictable surprise.

就我们的日常实践而言，关键在于：如果你想让你的想法更深入人心，就必须打破人们的固有认知，然后再加以改进。但是，在给人带来惊喜、打破他们固有认知的过程中，我们如何避免像狼群那样，用花哨的噱头来制造惊喜呢？避免噱头式惊喜并确保你的出人意料的想法能够带来洞见的最简单方法，就是确保你针对的是受众固有认知中与你的核心信息相关的某个方面。我们已经看到了一些运用这种策略的例子。

Here is the bottom line for our everyday purposes: If you want your ideas to be stickier, you’ve got to break someone’s guessing machine and then fix it. But in surprising people, in breaking their guessing machines, how do we avoid gimmicky surprise, like the wolves? The easiest way to avoid gimmicky surprise and ensure that your unexpected ideas produce insight is to make sure you target an aspect of your audience’s guessing machines that relates to your core message. We’ve already seen a few examples of this strategy.

在第一章中，我们讨论了胡佛·亚当斯，这位报业出版商的座右铭是“人名、人名、还是人名”。对大多数地方报纸记者来说，这句座右铭似乎是常识。当然，他们眼中的“好的本地新闻”也包含以社区为中心的故事。

In Chapter 1, we discussed Hoover Adams, the newspaper publisher whose mantra is “Names, names, and names.” To most local newspaper reporters, this mantra will seem like common sense. Certainly, their schemas of “good local news” involve community-focused stories.

但这并非亚当斯的本意。他另有更激进的想法。于是他打破了他们的常规，说道：“如果可以，我会把电话簿上的页码都刊登出来收集名字。事实上，如果我能收集到足够多的名字，我会雇佣更多的排版工人来排版更多页面，以便把它们全部放进去。” 记者们突然意识到……“名字、名字、还是名字”与他们之前的理念不符。他们之前的理念可能是“尽可能强调本地视角”，而亚当斯则将其替换为“名字高于一切，甚至高于我个人的盈利能力”。这种信息的力量恰恰来自于它的出人意料。

But that wasn’t Adams’s point. He had something much more radical in mind. So he broke their schema by saying, essentially, “If I could, I’d publish pages from the phone book to get names. In fact, if I could gather up enough names I’d hire more typesetters to lay out more pages so they’d fit.” Suddenly the reporters realized that “Names, names, and names” was not consistent with their schemas. Whereas their previous schema might have been “Try to emphasize local angles when you can,” Adams had replaced that with “Names come before everything else, even my own profitability.” That’s a message that draws power from its unexpectedness.

我们在第一章讨论过的另一个例子是西南航空公司的口号“低成本航空公司”。同样，大多数西南航空的员工和乘客都知道西南航空是一家廉价航空公司。在这种情况下，这句口号似乎很符合直觉。但直到凯莱赫让这句口号真正发挥作用——即使乘客非常想要鸡肉沙拉，他也拒绝提供——它的含义才被人们理解。在凯莱赫之前，普通员工可能会猜测：“我们想以低成本的方式取悦顾客。” 而凯莱赫之后，这种猜测被修正为：“我们将成为‘低成本航空公司’，即使这意味着故意忽略一些顾客的喜好。”

Another example we discussed in Chapter 1 was Southwest Airlines’ proverb “THE low-cost airline.” Again, most Southwest staffers and customers know that Southwest is a discount airline. In that context, the proverb seems intuitive. It was only when Kelleher put teeth in the proverb—refusing to offer chicken salad to customers even if they really wanted it—that its meaning sank in. Before Kelleher, an average staffer’s guessing machine might have predicted, “We want to please our customers in a low-cost way.” After Kelleher, the guessing machine was refined to “We will be THE low-cost airline, even if it means intentionally disregarding some of our customers’ preferences.”

因此，让你的想法更深入人心的一个好方法是：（1）确定你需要传达的核心信息——找到核心要点；（2）找出信息中反直觉的部分——也就是说，你的核心信息有哪些意想不到的含义？为什么它没有自然而然地发生？（3）以一种能够打破受众固有认知的方式传达你的信息，这种方式要从关键的、反直觉的角度出发。然后，一旦他们的认知机制失效，就帮助他们改进认知机制。

So, a good process for making your ideas stickier is: (1) Identify the central message you need to communicate—find the core; (2) Figure out what is counterintuitive about the message—i.e., What are the unexpected implications of your core message? Why isn’t it already happening naturally? (3) Communicate your message in a way that breaks your audience’s guessing machines along the critical, counterintuitive dimension. Then, once their guessing machines have failed, help them refine their machines.

常识是有效信息的敌人。当信息听起来像是常识时，它们就像耳边风一样，左耳进右耳出。这又有什么奇怪的呢？如果我已经凭直觉“理解”了你想表达的意思，我又何必费心去记住它呢？当然，危险在于，那些听起来像是常识的东西往往并非如此，就像胡佛、亚当斯和西南航空的例子一样。作为沟通者，你的职责就是指出信息中那些不符合常识的部分。

Common sense is the enemy of sticky messages. When messages sound like common sense, they float gently in one ear and out the other. And why shouldn’t they? If I already intuitively “get” what you’re trying to tell me, why should I obsess about remembering it? The danger, of course, is that what sounds like common sense often isn’t, as with the Hoover Adams and Southwest examples. It’s your job, as a communicator, to expose the parts of your message that are uncommon sense.

Nordstrom 出售轮胎防滑链

Tire Chains at Nordstrom

诺德斯特龙百货公司以其卓越的客户服务而闻名。这种额外的服务自然价格不菲：在诺德斯特龙购物可能价格不菲。然而，许多人愿意支付更高的价格，正是因为诺德斯特龙让购物变得更加愉悦。

Nordstrom is a department store known for outstanding customer service. That extra service comes at a price: Nordstrom can be an expensive place to shop. Yet many people are willing to pay higher prices precisely because Nordstrom makes shopping so much more pleasant.

诺德斯特龙的战略要想奏效，就必须将一线员工转变为客户服务的狂热拥护者。但他们并非天生如此。大多数有服务经验的人都来自这样的环境：管理者将大部分精力都放在如何最大限度地降低人工成本上。他们普遍采用的客户服务模式大致是：“尽快让顾客进出，并尽量保持微笑。”

For Nordstrom’s strategy to work, it must transform its frontline employees into customer-service zealots. And they do not walk in the door that way. Most people with service experience come from environments where managers spend much of their energy trying to minimize labor costs. The prevailing schema of customer service might be, roughly, “Get customers in and out the door as fast as possible, and try to smile.”

诺德斯特龙的求职者很可能拥有多年遵循这种模式的工作经验。但诺德斯特龙的理念却截然不同：即使牺牲效率也在所不惜，也要让顾客满意。那么，诺德斯特龙是如何打破旧模式，代之以新模式的呢？

Job applicants at Nordstrom will likely have years of experience acting on this schema. But Nordstrom has a different philosophy: Make customers happy even at the expense of efficiency. How does Nordstrom break down one schema and replace it with another?

该公司部分地通过一些出人意料的故事解决了这个问题。吉姆·柯林斯和杰里·波拉斯在他们的著作《基业长青》中描述了诺德斯特龙公司员工提供的出人意料的服务故事，这些员工在公司内部被称为“诺迪”（Nordies）：

The company solves this problem, in part, through unexpected stories. Jim Collins and Jerry Porras, in their book Built to Last, describe stories told at Nordstrom about unexpected service by employees, who are known within the firm as “Nordies”:

那位北欧人帮一位顾客熨烫了一件新衬衫，因为顾客下午要开会，所以需要这件衬衫；

The Nordie who ironed a new shirt for a customer who needed it for a meeting that afternoon;

那位热情地为顾客在梅西百货购买的商品进行礼品包装的诺德人；

the Nordie who cheerfully gift wrapped products a customer bought at Macy’s;

冬天，当顾客购物结束后，他们会帮忙暖车。

the Nordie who warmed customers’ cars in winter while they finished shopping;

一位北欧人在最后一刻为焦急的女主人送去了派对服装；

the Nordie who made a last-minute delivery of party clothes to a frantic hostess;

甚至还有一位 Nordstrom 员工退还了一套防滑链的款项——尽管 Nordstrom 并不出售防滑链。

and even the Nordie who refunded money for a set of tire chains—although Nordstrom doesn’t sell tire chains.

你可以想象，这些故事会让诺德斯特龙的新员工感到多么惊讶，甚至震惊。“包装梅西百货的礼物！我不明白。这对我们有什么好处？”这些故事挑战了人们对客户服务的一些不成文的假设，例如：服务止步于店门口。不要把时间浪费在不购物的顾客身上。一旦完成一笔交易，就赶紧去接待下一个潜在客户。

You can imagine the surprise, if not shock, that these stories provoke in new Nordstrom employees. “Wrap a gift from Macy’s! I don’t get it. What’s in it for us?” These stories attack the unspoken assumptions of customer service, such as: Service stops at the door of the store. Don’t waste your time on someone who’s not buying. Once you close a sale, move on to the next prospect.

对新员工来说，为在竞争对手商店购买的礼物进行包装的想法简直荒谬至极，远远超出了他们原有的“服务”概念，以至于这个故事让他们愣住了。他们的认知机制彻底崩溃了。他们原有的“优质服务”认知机制绝不会让他们想到无私地包装礼物这种事。这些故事是帮助新员工用诺德斯特龙的服务模式取代原有“优质服务”模式的第一步。

To new employees, the idea of wrapping a gift bought at a competitor’s store is so absurd, so far outside the bounds of their existing notion of “service,” that the story stops them in their tracks. Their guessing machines have been broken. Their old “good service” guessing machine would never have produced the idea of altruistic gift-wrapping. The stories provide the first step toward replacing a new employee’s schema of “good service” with the Nordstrom service schema.

这样一来，诺德斯特龙就打破了常识的束缚。与其大肆宣传“诺迪人”，诺德斯特龙完全可以告诉员工，其使命是提供“业内最佳客户服务”。这句话或许没错，但可惜的是，听起来像是彭尼百货或西尔斯百货也会对员工说的话。要想让信息深入人心，就必须超越常识，走向非同寻常。“优质的客户服务”是常识，而冬天为顾客的车子预热则是非同寻常的做法。

In this way, Nordstrom breaks through the complacency of common sense. Instead of spreading stories about “Nordies,” Nordstrom could simply tell its employees that its mission is to provide “the best customer service in the industry.” This statement may be true, but, unfortunately, it sounds like something that JCPenney or Sears might also tell its employees. To make a message stick, you’ve got to push it beyond common sense to uncommon sense. “Great customer service” is common sense. Warming customers’ cars in the winter is uncommon sense.

请注意，如果这些故事的主角是7-Eleven的员工，那它们会更加出人意料，也更加不合常理。“是啊，我进去买包烟，结果收银员帮我熨了衬衫！”这些故事的价值并不在于其出人意料本身，而在于诺德斯特龙的目标与故事内容完美契合。在其他情况下，这些故事同样可能造成负面影响。7-Eleven的管理层可不想看到大量员工都去包装礼品。

Note that these stories would be even more unexpected—and even less commonsensical—if they were told about a 7-Eleven employee. “Yeah, I went in to get a pack of smokes and the counter clerk ironed my shirt!” The value of the stories does not come from unexpectedness in and of itself. The value comes from the perfect alignment between Nordstrom’s goals and the content of the stories. These stories could just as easily be destructive in another context. The 7-Eleven management does not want to face an epidemic of gift-wrapping clerks.

Nordstrom的故事是出其不意的力量的经典例证。这些故事绝不会让人觉得哗众取宠，因为惊喜之后往往蕴含着深刻的见解——它们告诉我们，成为一名优秀的诺德斯特龙员工意味着什么。这是以非常规的视角传递核心信息。

Nordstrom’s stories are a classic example of the power of unexpectedness. There’s no danger that the stories will feel gimmicky, because the surprise is followed by insight—the stories tell us what it means to be a good Nordstrom employee. It’s uncommon sense in the service of a core message.

新闻学入门

Journalism 101

诺拉·艾芙隆是一位编剧，她创作的《丝克伍德》、《当哈利遇到莎莉》和《西雅图夜未眠》的剧本都曾获得奥斯卡金像奖提名。艾芙隆的职业生涯始于《纽约邮报》和《时尚先生》的记者工作。她之所以成为一名记者，是因为她的高中新闻学老师。

Nora Ephron is a screenwriter whose scripts for Silkwood, When Harry Met Sally, and Sleepless in Seattle have all been nominated for Academy Awards. Ephron started her career as a journalist for the New York Post and Esquire. She became a journalist because of her high school journalism teacher.

埃夫隆至今仍记得她新闻课的第一天。尽管学生们之前没有任何新闻从业经验，但他们走进教室时对记者的工作内容却已经有了大致的了解：记者获取事实并进行报道。而要获取事实，就需要追溯五个“W”——谁、什么、哪里、何时、为什么。

Ephron still remembers the first day of her journalism class. Although the students had no journalism experience, they walked into their first class with a sense of what a journalist does: A journalists gets the facts and reports them. To get the facts, you track down the five Ws—who, what, where, when, and why.

学生们坐在各自的手动打字机前，埃夫隆的老师宣布了第一个作业：撰写一篇新闻报道的导语。老师滔滔不绝地陈述着：“贝弗利山高中的校长肯尼斯·L·彼得斯今天宣布，全校教职工将于下周四前往萨克拉门托参加一个关于新教学方法的研讨会。演讲嘉宾包括人类学家玛格丽特·米德、大学校长罗伯特·梅纳德·哈钦斯博士以及加州州长埃德蒙·‘帕特’·布朗。”

As students sat in front of their manual typewriters, Ephron’s teacher announced the first assignment. They would write the lead of a newspaper story. The teacher reeled off the facts: “Kenneth L. Peters, the principal of Beverly Hills High School, announced today that the entire high school faculty will travel to Sacramento next Thursday for a colloquium in new teaching methods. Among the speakers will be anthropologist Margaret Mead, college president Dr. Robert Maynard Hutchins, and California governor Edmund ‘Pat’ Brown.”

这些初出茅庐的记者们坐在打字机前，敲打着键盘，写下了他们职业生涯中的第一条新闻导语。据埃夫隆回忆，她和其他大多数学生写出的导语都是将事实重新排序，并浓缩成一句话：“州长帕特·布朗、玛格丽特·米德和罗伯特·梅纳德·哈钦斯将于周四在萨克拉门托向比佛利山庄高中的教职工发表讲话……等等等等。”

The budding journalists sat at their typewriters and pecked away at the first lead of their careers. According to Ephron, she and most of the other students produced leads that reordered the facts and condensed them into a single sentence: “Governor Pat Brown, Margaret Mead, and Robert Maynard Hutchins will address the Beverly Hills High School faculty Thursday in Sacramento … blah, blah, blah.”

老师收集了铅芯，快速扫描了一遍。然后他把铅芯放在一边，停顿了一会儿。

The teacher collected the leads and scanned them rapidly. Then he laid them aside and paused for a moment.

最后，他说：“新闻的开头是‘下周四停课’。”

Finally, he said, “The lead to the story is ‘There will be no school next Thursday.’”

“那真是令人震撼的时刻，”埃夫隆回忆道，“那一刻我意识到，新闻报道不仅仅是复述事实，更重要的是理解其背后的意义。仅仅知道人、事、时、地是不够的；你必须理解它的意义，以及它为何重要。” 她说，在接下来的那一年里，每一项作业都隐藏着一个秘密——一个需要学生们去解开才能写出好报道的隐秘要点。

“It was a breathtaking moment,” Ephron recalls. “In that instant I realized that journalism was not just about regurgitating the facts but about figuring out the point. It wasn’t enough to know the who, what, when, and where; you had to understand what it meant. And why it mattered.” For the rest of the year, she says, every assignment had a secret—a hidden point that the students had to figure out in order to produce a good story.

T他的创意应该载入史册。这位老师的影响力巨大，并非因为他是一位充满活力的演说家或一位关怀备至的导师——尽管他或许兼具两者——而是因为他构思了一个绝妙的创意。这个创意在短短几秒钟内，就彻底颠覆了学生们对新闻业的认知。它改变了一位学生的职业规划，并让她在三十年后依然受益匪浅。

This idea should be in the Sticky Hall of Fame. This teacher had a huge impact not because he was a dynamic speaker or a caring mentor—though he may have been both—but because he crafted a brilliant idea. It was an idea that, in a matter of seconds, rewrote the schema of journalism in the minds of his students. An idea that changed a student’s career plans and stuck with her thirty years later.

是什么让这个方法奏效？首先，这位老师知道学生们对新闻写作的理解存在缺陷，而且他清楚地知道这些缺陷在哪里。其次，他通过布置“撰写导语”的作业，让学生们公开地接受这些错误的认知模式。然后，他精心设计了一个出其不意的环节，彻底颠覆了他们的认知。通过揭示正确的导语——“下周四停课”——他打破了学生们原有的思维模式，让他们重新审视并改进了这些模式。

What made this idea work? First, the teacher knew that the students had a defective schema of journalism, and he knew how it was defective. Second, he made them publicly commit to their defective models with the “write the lead” assignment. Then he pulled the rug out from under them with a well-structured surprise. By revealing the right lead—“There will be no school next Thursday”—he took their mental models, gave them a swift kick, and made them work better.

诊所

CLINIC



美国在对外援助上的支出是否过多？

Does America Spend Too Much

on Foreign Aid?

现状： 多年来的民意调查显示，大多数美国人认为联邦政府在对外援助上的支出过多。自9/11事件以来，这一比例已接近五五开，但仍有半数美国人认为我们支出过高。让我们来看看两种试图说服人们我们支出过少而非过多的论点。

THE SITUATION: Over the years, polls have shown that the majority of Americans think the federal government spends too much on foreign aid. The ratio has dropped toward fifty/fifty since 9/11, but half of Americans still think we overspend. Let’s look at two arguments that try to persuade people that we spend too little, not too much.

• • •

• • •

信息 1：以下信息来自天主教倡导组织——跨社区和平与正义中心：

MESSAGE 1: Here is a message from the Intercommunity Peace and Justice Center, a Catholic advocacy group:

尽管国务院和其他政府机构已尽力向公众解释情况，但美国民众仍然坚持认为我们在对外援助上的支出过多。即使是布什总统提出的增加援助计划，虽然值得欢迎，但并不能使美国在对外援助方面变得慷慨。2003财年，布什政府将支出约150亿美元用于对外援助，但其中超过70亿美元——几乎占一半——是军事援助，而非经济援助。根据国会预算办公室最近的估计，这80亿美元的对外经济援助甚至不及伊拉克一个月战争的成本。在所有工业化国家中，美国对外援助支出占比最低，这种情况已经持续多年。整个撒哈拉以南非洲地区仅获得略高于10亿美元的经济援助，这大约相当于一架B-2轰炸机的价格。我们的对外援助项目与我们自诩的“以善行闻名于世”的形象并不相符。

Americans persist in thinking we spend too much on foreign aid despite honest efforts to inform the public by the State Department and other government agencies. Even President Bush’s proposed increases, though welcome, will not make the United States generous in its foreign assistance. In fiscal year 2003, the Bush administration will spend about $15-billion in foreign aid, but over $7-billion of this amount—almost half—will be military, not economic assistance. The $8-billion in foreign economic assistance is, according to a recent estimate by the Congressional Budget Office, less than the cost of one month of war with Iraq. Of all the industrialized nations, the U.S. spends proportionally the least amount on foreign aid, and has for many years. All of sub-Saharan Africa receives just over $1-billion of economic assistance, about the cost of a B-2 bomber. Our foreign aid programs do not support our belief that we are a nation known for its good works around the world.

对第一条信息的评论：首先，请注意，关键信息被埋没了。最后一句话才是最有力的论点。美国人的思维模式美国的形象往往是慷慨仁慈、关爱他人的国家——“以其在世界各地的善行而闻名”。打破这种刻板印象的方法是指出一个残酷的事实：美国“在对外援助上的支出比例最低，而且这种情况已经持续多年”。

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 1: First, notice that the lead has been buried. The last sentence is the most effective argument. Americans’ schema of the United States is that it is a generous, caring country—“known for its good works around the world.” The way to break that schema is to lay out the blunt fact that the United States “spends proportionally the least amount on foreign aid, and has for many years.”

以数十亿为单位的数字很难让人记住——庞大的数字难以理解，也难以记忆。为了克服这种“大数字难题”，我们采用了一种有效的方法，将我们对撒哈拉以南非洲的援助比作一架B-2轰炸机的成本。我们非常喜欢这种比喻，因为它能让读者做出决策：“我愿意用一架B-2轰炸机来换取将对撒哈拉以南非洲的援助翻倍的机会吗？”

The numbers in billions are unlikely to stick—huge numbers are difficult to grasp and hard to remember. One effective part of the message, in combating this “big-number problem,” is the analogy comparing our sub-Saharan Africa aid to the cost of a single B-2 bomber. We really like this comparison, because it puts the reader in a decision-making mode: “Would I trade one B-2 bomber for the chance to double aid to sub-Saharan Africa?”

为了让这个信息更深入人心，我们不妨尝试两件事。首先，我们只需重新排列现有的优质素材，同时淡化数十亿的数字。其次，我们选择一个更能引起情感共鸣的具体对比。有些人可能认为B-2轰炸机的开支是合理的。那么，我们不妨尝试创造一个更出人意料的对比，因为它显然是无关紧要的。

To make this message stickier, let’s try two things. First, let’s just reshuffle the great raw materials that are already there while downplaying the numbers in the billions. Second, let’s choose a concrete comparison that has a better emotional resonance. Some people might think B-2 bombers are a reasonable expense. Let’s try to create a comparison that would be more unexpected because it’s clearly frivolous.

• • •

• • •

信息二：我们的对外援助项目与我们自诩为以善行闻名于世的国家形象并不相符。公众普遍认为我们在援助其他国家上的花费远超实际数额。民调显示，大多数美国人认为联邦政府将预算的10%到15%用于对外援助。而事实是，我们的对外援助支出不到1%，是所有工业化国家中最低的。

MESSAGE 2: Our foreign-aid programs do not support our belief that we are a nation known for its good works around the world. The public believes we spend a great deal more money helping other countries than we actually do. Polls suggest that most Americans think the federal government spends about 10 to 15 percent of its budget on foreign aid. The truth is that we spend less than 1 percent, the lowest of any industrialized nation.

整个撒哈拉以南非洲地区每年获得的援助总额略高于10亿美元。如果美国每个人每月少喝一瓶软饮料，我们就能将目前对非洲的援助翻一番。如果每个人每年少看一部电影，我们就能将目前对非洲和亚洲的援助翻一番。

All of sub-Saharan Africa receives just over $1 billion in economic aid. If everyone in the United States gave up one soft drink a month, we could double our current aid to Africa. If everyone gave up one movie a year, we could double our current aid to Africa and Asia.

关于第二条信息的评论：为了让这条信息更深入人心，我们尝试了以下方法：首先，我们迅速而直接地打破了“慷慨的美国”这一固有观念，以此来吸引人们的兴趣。我们还将讨论的焦点转移到百分比上，这比数十亿更容易理解。其次，我们试图用软饮料和电影来代替B-2轰炸机，使之更加具体。软饮料和电影更直观——谁又能真正“凭直觉”判断一架B-2轰炸机的成本或价值呢？软饮料和电影作为一种无关紧要的开支，也与非洲迫切的人道主义需求形成了鲜明的对比。

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 2: Here’s what we tried to do to make this message stickier: First, we built interest by quickly and directly breaking our schema of a “generous America.” We also shifted the conversation to percentages, which are easier to understand than billions. Second, we tried to make the B-2 analogy more concrete by replacing it with soft drinks and movies. Soft drinks and movies are more tangible—does anyone really have a “gut feel” for what a B-2 bomber costs, or what it’s worth? Soft drinks and movies, because they are frivolous expenses, also provide an emotional contrast to the critical human needs present in Africa.

记分卡
检查清单 消息 1 消息 2
简单的 -
意外    （B-2 对比）    （引言与比较）
具体的
可信的
情绪 -
故事 - -

妙语：吸引人们注意力的最佳方法是直接打破他们现有的认知模式。

PUNCH LINE: The best way to get people’s attention is to break their existing schemas directly.

保持人们的注意力

KEEPING PEOPLE’S ATTENTION

指环王之谜

The Mystery of the Rings

本章伊始，我们提出了两个问题：如何吸引人们的注意力？以及如何保持他们的注意力？到目前为止，我们大多数意想不到的想法都只是对现有模式进行相对简单、快速的调整。它们或许意义深远——就像诺拉·艾芙隆笔下的新闻老师那样——但它们出现得很快，所以只需要在短时间内吸引人们的注意力即可。然而，有时我们的信息会更加复杂。我们如何才能让人们在理解更复杂的信息时保持专注？我们如何才能持续吸引人们的注意力？

We began this chapter with two questions: How do we get people’s attention? And how do we keep it? So far, most of our unexpected ideas represent relatively simple, quick adjustments to a model. They may be profound—as with Nora Ephron’s journalism teacher—but they happen rapidly, so they only need to get people’s attention for a short time. Sometimes, though, our messages are more complex. How do we get people to stick with us through a more complex message? How do we keep people’s attention?

几年前，亚利桑那州立大学的社会心理学家罗伯特·西奥迪尼（Robert Cialdini）决心改进他在写作和课堂上谈论科学的方式。为了寻找灵感，他去了图书馆。他把所有能找到的科学家为非科学读者撰写的书籍都借了出来，并复印了其中他喜欢的段落。之后，他翻阅着这些复印的段落，寻找其中的共同点。

A few years ago, Robert Cialdini, a social psychologist at Arizona State University, set out to improve the way he talked about science in his writing and in his classes. For inspiration, he went to the library. He pulled down every book he could find in which scientists were writing for an audience of nonscientists. He photocopied sections of prose that he liked. Later, flipping through his stack of copied passages, he hunted for consistencies.

在那些乏味的段落中，他发现的大多是意料之中的。文章的目的不明确，文风过于正式，充斥着晦涩难懂的术语。而在优秀的段落中，他也发现了许多显而易见的优点：结构清晰，例证生动，语言流畅。“但是，”西奥迪尼说道，“我还发现了一些意想不到的东西——这些作品中最成功的作品都以一个悬疑故事开头。作者们描述了一种看似毫无道理的情境，然后引导读者进入其中，共同解开谜团。”

In passages that weren’t interesting, he found mostly what he expected. The purpose wasn’t clear, and the prose was too formal and riddled with jargon. He also found a lot of predictable virtues in the good passages: The structure was clear, the examples vivid, and the language fluid. “But,” says Cialdini, “I also found something I had not expected—the most successful of these pieces all began with a mystery story. The authors described a state of affairs that seemed to make no sense and then invited the reader into the material as a way of solving the mystery.”

他印象最深刻的一个例子是一位天文学家写的，这个例子是从一道谜题开始的：

One example that stuck in his mind was written by an astronomer, who began with a puzzle:

我们该如何解释太阳系中最壮观的行星特征——土星环呢？世上没有任何其他天体可以与之媲美。土星环究竟是由什么构成的呢？

How can we account for what is perhaps the most spectacular planetary feature in our solar system, the rings of Saturn? There’s nothing else like them. What are the rings of Saturn made of anyway?

然后，他进一步加深了谜团，问道：“三个国际知名的科学家团队怎么会对这个问题得出截然不同的结论？”剑桥大学的团队宣称它们是气体；麻省理工学院的团队坚信它们是由尘埃颗粒组成的；而加州理工学院的团队则坚持认为它们是由冰晶构成的。这怎么可能呢？毕竟，每个团队研究的都是同一件事，对吧？那么，答案究竟是什么？

And then he deepened the mystery further by asking, “How could three internationally acclaimed groups of scientists come to wholly different conclusions on the answer?” One, at Cambridge University, proclaimed they were gas; another group, at MIT, was convinced they were made up of dust particles; while the third, at Cal Tech, insisted they were comprised of ice crystals. How could this be, after all, each group was looking at the same thing, right? So, what was the answer?

答案的揭晓如同悬疑小说的情节。科学家们追踪着看似有希望的线索，却屡屡碰壁，最终，经过数月的努力，他们取得了突破。西奥迪尼说：“你知道二十页纸的答案是什么吗？尘埃。尘埃。确切地说，是冰覆盖的尘埃，这也解释了为什么会有人感到困惑。说实话，我对尘埃并不感兴趣，土星环的组成也与我的生活毫无关系。但那位作者的文笔让我读得津津有味，简直像个速读读者。”

The answer unfolded like the plot of a mystery. The teams of scientists pursued promising leads, they hit dead ends, they chased clues. Eventually, after many months of effort, there was a breakthrough. Cialdini says, “Do you know what the answer was at the end of twenty pages? Dust. Dust. Actually, ice-covered dust, which accounts for some of the confusion. Now, I don’t care about dust, and the makeup of the rings of Saturn is entirely irrelevant to my life. But that writer had me turning pages like a speed-reader.”

西奥迪尼说，悬疑之所以强大，是因为它能激发人们对真相的渴望。“你听说过著名的‘啊哈！ ’体验吧？”他说道，“嗯，如果‘啊哈！ ’体验之前先有‘嗯？’的困惑，那它就会更加令人满足。”

Mysteries are powerful, Cialdini says, because they create a need for closure. “You’ve heard of the famous Aha! experience, right?” he says. “Well, the Aha! experience is much more satisfying when it is preceded by the Huh? experience.”

这位作家兼天文学家通过制造悬念，使尘埃变得引人入胜。他不仅在结尾的妙语连珠中牢牢抓住了读者的注意力，更在长达二十页、信息丰富的科学理论和实验文章中，持续吸引着读者的目光。

By creating a mystery, the writer-astronomer made dust interesting. He sustained attention, not just for the span of a punch line but for the span of a twenty-page article dense with information on scientific theories and experimentation.

西奥迪尼开始在自己的课堂上设置谜题，这种方法的威力很快就显现出来。他会在课前提出谜题，在讲解过程中反复提及，最后揭晓答案。然而，有一次，下课铃响了，他还没来得及揭晓答案。他说：“通常情况下，在预定下课时间前五到十分钟，一些学生就开始准备离开。你知道那些信号——铅笔收起来了，笔记本叠好了，书包拉上了拉链。” 但这一次，全班同学都……一片寂静。“铃声响起后，没有人动。事实上，当我试图在不揭晓谜底的情况下结束讲座时，我遭到了抗议。” 他说，他感觉自己就像发现了炸药。

Cialdini began to create mysteries in his own classroom, and the power of the approach quickly became clear. He would introduce the mystery at the start of class, return to it during the lecture, and reveal the answer at the end. In one lecture, though, the end-of-class bell rang before he had time to reveal the solution. He says, “Normally five to ten minutes before the scheduled end time, some students start preparing to leave. You know the signals—pencils are put away, notebooks folded, backpacks zipped up.” This time, though, the class was silent. “After the bell rang, no one moved. In fact, when I tried to end the lecture without revealing the mystery, I was pelted with protests.” He said he felt as if he’d discovered dynamite.

西奥迪尼认为，利用谜题进行教学的一大好处是“解谜的过程与科学过程惊人地相似”。因此，通过运用谜题，教师不仅可以提高学生对当天学习内容的兴趣，还可以训练他们像科学家一样思考。

Cialdini believes that a major benefit of teaching using mysteries is that “the process of resolving mysteries is remarkably similar to the process of science.” So, by using mysteries, teachers don’t just heighten students’ interest in the day’s material; they train them to think like scientists.

科学并非谜团的唯一来源。凡是有疑问且没有明显答案的地方，谜团就存在。为什么动物园里的熊猫繁殖如此困难？为什么顾客不喜欢我们的新产品？教孩子分数最好的方法是什么？

Science doesn’t have a monopoly on mysteries. Mysteries exist wherever there are questions without obvious answers. Why is it so hard to get pandas at the zoo to breed? Why don’t customers like our new product? What’s the best way to teach kids about fractions?

注意这里发生了什么：我们现在进入了一个更高层次的意外性阶段。以诺德斯特龙百货为例，诺德斯特龙员工的故事极具冲击力：诺德斯特龙员工会帮顾客预热汽车！当你听到这句话时，你过去对客户服务的认知模式会被迅速唤醒、颠覆和修正。而谜团的作用则不同。谜团并非源于意料之外的瞬间，而是源于意料之外的旅程。我们知道自己要去哪里——我们想要解开谜团——但我们并不确定该如何到达那里。

Notice what is happening here: We have now moved to a higher level of unexpectedness. In the Nordstrom example, the Nordie stories had a punchy immediacy: Nordies warm up customers’ cars! When you hear this, your past schema of customer service is called up, contradicted, and refined, all in a short period of time. Mysteries act differently. Mystery is created not from an unexpected moment but from an unexpected journey. We know where we’re headed—we want to solve the mystery—but we’re not sure how we’ll get there.

违反模式是一次性的事件。砰！一切都变了。如果我们被告知土星环是由烘干机里的绒毛组成的，那么就违反了模式。我们可以称之为“第一层”意外性。但真正的“土星环之谜”更加持久和微妙。我们被告知科学家们不知道土星环是由什么构成的，并且被邀请踏上一段结局不可预测的旅程。这就是第二层意外性。通过这种方式，我们从转瞬即逝的惊讶跃升至持久的兴趣。

A schema violation is a onetime transaction. Boom, something has changed. If we were told that the rings of Saturn were made of dryer lint, a schema would be violated. We could call it “first-level” unexpectedness. But the actual “rings of Saturn mystery” is more extended and subtle. We are told that scientists do not know what Saturn’s rings are made of, and we’re asked to follow on a journey whose ending is unpredictable. That’s second-level unexpectedness. In this way, we jump from fleeting surprise to enduring interest.

好莱坞剧本中的好奇心

Curiosity in Hollywood Screenplays

在电影《颠倒乾坤》的开头，比利·雷·瓦伦丁（艾迪·墨菲饰演）是一位显然没有双腿的乞丐，他正用双臂……他踩着类似滑板的装置在公园里滑来滑去。他向路人乞讨，还骚扰一位漂亮女士想约她出去。几个警察走了过来。他们把他拽起来时，他那双完全正常的腿露了出来。瓦伦丁是个骗子。

Early in the movie Trading Places, Billy Ray Valentine (played by Eddie Murphy), an apparently legless beggar, is using his arms to push himself around on a skateboardish contraption in a public park. He begs for money from passersby and harasses an attractive woman for a date. A couple of cops approach. As they jerk him up, his legs—perfectly normal legs—are exposed. Valentine is a con artist.

影片后半段，两位年长的商人杜克兄弟出手相助，将瓦伦丁从监狱中解救出来，说服警察将他移交给他们。几个场景之后，瓦伦丁身着一套三件套西装，出现在一间镶着木板的办公室里。杜克兄弟已经把他培养成了一名商品交易员。

Later in the movie, the Duke brothers—two elderly businessmen—intervene to get Valentine out of jail, persuading the cops to release him into their custody. A couple of scenes later, Valentine appears, dressed in a three-piece suit, in a wood-paneled office. The Duke brothers have turned him into a commodities trader.

编剧大师罗伯特·麦基用这个例子来阐释“转折点”的概念。麦基深谙如何吸引观众的注意力。他的编剧研讨会场场爆满，满是怀揣编剧梦想的学员，他们每人支付五百美元来聆听他的见解。《乡村之声》杂志曾评价他的课程“不仅对编剧，而且对演员、导演、影评人和普通影迷来说，都几乎是不可或缺的”。他的学生们曾参与编剧、导演或制作过《急诊室的故事》、《希尔街蓝调》和《X档案》等电视剧，以及《紫色》、《阿甘正传》和《十三号星期五》等电影。

Robert McKee, the screenwriting guru, uses this example to illustrate the concept of a “Turning Point.” McKee knows something about how to hold an audience’s attention. His screenwriting seminars play to packed auditoriums of aspiring screenwriters, who pay five hundred dollars a head to listen to his thoughts. The Village Voice described his course as “damned near indispensable not only for writers, but also for actors, directors, reviewers, and garden-variety cinephiles.” His students have written, directed, or produced television shows such as E.R., Hill Street Blues, and The X-Files, and movies ranging from The Color Purple to Forrest Gump and Friday the 13th.

麦基说：“好奇心是求知欲，它驱使我们去解答问题，填补空白。故事恰恰迎合了这种普遍的渴望，它反其道而行之，提出问题，开启新的情境。”在《颠倒乾坤》中，比利·雷和杜克兄弟的转折点激起了观众的好奇心。街头老练的骗子瓦伦丁，作为一名交易员，最终会如何呢？

McKee says, “Curiosity is the intellectual need to answer questions and close open patterns. Story plays to this universal desire by doing the opposite, posing questions and opening situations.” In Trading Places, the Turning Point with Billy Ray and the Duke brothers makes the audience curious. How will Valentine, the street-smart con man, fare as a trader?

在麦基看来，一部优秀的剧本应该让每一幕都成为转折点。“每一个转折点都能激起观众的好奇心。观众会想，接下来会发生什么？结局会如何？答案要等到最后一幕的高潮才会揭晓，因此，观众会被这种好奇心牢牢抓住，继续观看下去。”麦基指出，“结局会如何？ ”这个问题本身就极具吸引力，即使我们明知结局并非如此，也依然会继续观看。“想想你曾经为了找到这个挥之不去的答案而硬着头皮看完的那些烂片吧。”

In McKee’s view, a great script is designed so that every scene is a Turning Point. “Each Turning Point hooks curiosity. The audience wonders, What will happen next? and How will it turn out? The answer to this will not arrive until the Climax of the last act, and so the audience, held by curiosity, stays put.” McKee notes that the How will it turn out? question is powerful enough to keep us watching even when we know better. “Think of all the bad films you’ve sat through just to get the answer to that nagging question.”

接下来会发生什么？结果会如何？我们渴望找到这些问题的答案，而这种渴望让我们保持着兴趣。它让我们乐于观看烂片——但也可能促使我们阅读冗长的科学论文。麦基和西奥迪尼针对截然不同的问题提出了类似的解决方案。

What will happen next? How will it turn out? We want to answer these questions, and that desire keeps us interested. It keeps us watching bad movies—but it might also keep us reading long scientific articles. McKee and Cialdini have come up with similar solutions to very different problems.

然而，在其他领域，人们也会对一些缺乏神秘感的事物表现出狂热的兴趣。孩子们痴迷地记住宝可梦角色及其特征，这其中必有缘由，但并非出于对“接下来会发生什么？”的好奇。同样，让汽车爱好者们每期都翻阅《Car & Driver》杂志的，也不是对悬念迭起的剧情的好奇。但是，正如我们将要发现的，宝可梦粉丝和汽车爱好者与电影观众以及在引人入胜的讲座中学习的学生，其实有着某种共通之处。

Yet there are other domains where people can be rabidly interested in something that lacks this sense of mystery. Kids who obsessively memorize Pokémon characters and their traits are motivated by something, but it isn’t What will happen next? It isn’t a sense of an unfolding mystery that keeps car buffs plowing through every issue of Car & Driver. But, as we’ll discover, Pokémon fans and car buffs have something in common with movie viewers and students in an intriguing lecture.

心理学家几十年来一直在研究这个问题——是什么激发了人们的兴趣？。兴趣研究的终极目标是找到描述情境兴趣的方法。换句话说，情境的哪些特征能够激发并提升人们的兴趣？是什么让情境变得有趣？事实证明，西奥迪尼和麦基的答案已经非常接近真相了。

Psychologists have studied this question—What makes people interested?—for decades. The holy grail of research on interest is to find a way to describe situational interest. In other words, what features of a situation spark and elevate interest? What makes situations interesting? As it turns out, Cialdini and McKee were pretty close to the mark.

好奇心的“差距理论”

The “Gap Theory” of Curiosity

1994年，卡内基梅隆大学的行为经济学家乔治·洛温斯坦对情境兴趣给出了最全面的解释。他的解释出奇地简单。他认为，当我们感到自身知识存在空白时，好奇心就会产生。

In 1994, George Loewenstein, a behavioral economist at Carnegie Mellon University, provided the most comprehensive account of situational interest. It is surprisingly simple. Curiosity, he says, happens when we feel a gap in our knowledge.

洛温斯坦认为，知识空白会带来痛苦。当我们想知道某件事却一无所知时，就像身上痒得痒，非挠不可。为了消除这种痛苦，我们需要填补知识空白。我们耐心地看完烂片，即使它们可能让人痛苦不堪，因为不知道结局实在太痛苦了。

Loewenstein argues that gaps cause pain. When we want to know something but don’t, it’s like having an itch that we need to scratch. To take away the pain, we need to fill the knowledge gap. We sit patiently through bad movies, even though they may be painful to watch, because it’s too painful not to know how they end.

这种关于兴趣的“差距理论”似乎可以解释为什么某些领域会引发狂热的兴趣：它们自然而然地造成了知识鸿沟。例如电影。麦基的语言与洛温斯坦的语言相似：麦基说，“故事的运作方式是通过提出问题和展开情境。”电影让我们问：接下来会发生什么？悬疑小说让我们问：谁是凶手？体育比赛让我们问：谁会赢？填字游戏让我们问：用六个字母表示“精神科医生”的单词是什么？宝可梦卡牌让孩子们好奇：我还缺哪些角色？

This “gap theory” of interest seems to explain why some domains create fanatical interest: They naturally create knowledge gaps. Take movies, for instance. McKee’s language is similar to Loewenstein’s: McKee says, “Story works by posing questions and opening situations.” Movies cause us to ask, What will happen? Mystery novels cause us to ask, Who did it? Sports contests cause us to ask, Who will win? Crossword puzzles cause us to ask, What is a six-letter word for “psychiatrist”? Pokémon cards cause kids to wonder, Which characters am I missing?

差距理论的一个重要启示是，我们需要先打开差距，才能弥合差距。我们倾向于直接告诉人们事实。然而，首先，他们必须意识到自己需要这些事实。洛温斯坦认为，说服人们接受我们信息的关键在于，首先强调他们缺失的某些具体知识。我们可以提出问题或难题，让人们意识到自身知识的不足。我们可以指出其他人掌握着他们所不知道的信息。我们可以向他们展示一些结果未知的情境，例如选举、体育赛事或悬案。我们可以挑战他们预测结果（这会造成两个知识缺口——“将会发生什么？ ”和“我的预测对吗？ ”）。

One important implication of the gap theory is that we need to open gaps before we close them. Our tendency is to tell people the facts. First, though, they must realize that they need these facts. The trick to convincing people that they need our message, according to Loewenstein, is to first highlight some specific knowledge that they’re missing. We can pose a question or puzzle that confronts people with a gap in their knowledge. We can point out that someone else knows something they don’t. We can present them with situations that have unknown resolutions, such as elections, sports events, or mysteries. We can challenge them to predict an outcome (which creates two knowledge gaps—What will happen? and Was I right?).

例如，大多数地方新闻节目都会在即将播出的节目前播放预告广告。这些预告广告会概括当晚的头条新闻，通常用夸张到令人发笑的语言：“一种新型毒品正在青少年群体中肆虐——而且它可能就在你家的药柜里！”“哪家当地知名餐厅刚刚因为制冰机里有黏液而被点名批评？” “你家有一种看不见的化学物质——它可能正在慢慢杀死你！”

As an example, most local news programs run teaser ads for upcoming broadcasts. The teasers preview the lead story of the evening, usually in laughably hyperbolic terms: “There’s a new drug sweeping the teenage community—and it may be in your own medicine cabinet!” “Which famous local restaurant was just cited—for slime in the ice machine?” “There’s an invisible chemical in your home—and it may be killing you right now!”

这些都是耸人听闻的差距理论例子。它们之所以有效，是因为它们用你不知道的事情来挑逗你——事实上，是你原本毫不在意的事情，直到你发现自己对此一无所知。“我女儿是不是还在服用我以前开的某种处方药？我怀疑我是否去过那家有黏液的餐厅吃饭？”

These are sensationalist examples of the gap theory. They work because they tease you with something that you don’t know—in fact, something that you didn’t care about at all, until you found out that you didn’t know it. “Is my daughter strung out on one of my old prescriptions? I wonder if I ate at the restaurant with the slime?”

稍微运用一下新闻预告的方式，就能让我们的沟通变得更加有趣，正如我们将在诊所中看到的那样。

A little dollop of the news-teaser approach can make our communications a lot more interesting, as we’ll see in the Clinic.

诊所

CLINIC

内部筹款报告

An Internal Presentation on Fund-raising

情境： 假设你是一家当地剧院公司的筹款经理。你的工作是帮助剧院筹集捐款。现在是年底，你正在为剧院董事会准备一份总结报告。

THE SITUATION: Imagine that you’re the fund-raising manager for a local theater company. Your job is to help raise donations to support the theater. It’s now the end of the year, and you’re preparing a summary presentation for the theater’s board of directors.

• • •

• • •

消息 1：（本诊所中的两条消息均为虚构。）

MESSAGE 1: (Both messages in this Clinic are made up.)

今年，我们将筹款重点放在了35岁以下的观众身上。我们的目标是增加年轻观众的捐款，因为他们在观众中所占的比例历来远高于捐赠者的比例。为了联系到他们，我们实施了一项电话筹款计划。该计划实施六个月以来，回复率接近20%，我们认为这是一个成功的数字。

This year we targeted support from theatergoers under thirty-five. Our goal is to increase donations from younger patrons, who have traditionally composed a much greater percentage of our audience than of our donor base. To reach them, we implemented a phone-based fund-raising program. Six months into the program, the response rate has been almost 20 percent, which we consider a success.

关于第一条信息的评论：这条信息采用的是经典的总结式方法。我掌握了事实。我已经将事实按逻辑顺序排列，并会一一为你讲解。作为一种演示形式，它安全、正常，而且完全不会让人感到困扰。

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 1: This message is a classic summary approach. I know the facts. I’ve put the facts in a logical order and I will spoon-feed them to you. As a presentation format, it’s safe and normal and thoroughly nonsticky.

为了改进这条信息，我们需要思考如何激发读者的兴趣，而不是生硬地灌输事实。我们会尝试融入一些新闻预告的元素。

In improving this message, we need to think about how to elicit interest rather than force-feeding facts. We’ll try to add a dash of the news-teaser approach.

信息二：今年我们着手解答一个问题：为什么占我们受众40%的35岁以下人群只贡献了我们10%的捐款？我们的推测是，他们没有意识到我们开展工作对慈善捐款的依赖程度有多高。我们决定尝试给他们打电话，简要介绍一下我们的业务和即将举办的演出。在为期六个月的测试开始前，我们认为10%的回复率就算成功了。在我告诉你结果之前，让我先回顾一下我们是如何开展这个项目的。

MESSAGE 2: This year we set out to answer a question: Why do people under thirty-five, who make up 40 percent of our audience, provide only 10 percent of our donations? Our theory was that they didn’t realize how much we rely on charitable donations to do our work, so we decided to try calling them with a short overview of our business and our upcoming shows. Going into the six-month test, we thought a 10 percent response rate would be a success. Before I tell you what happened, let me remind you of how we set up the program.

关于第二条信息的评论：这种方法的灵感来源于差距理论。我们的目标不是总结，而是激发你对某些事物的好奇心，然后告诉你你想知道的答案。就像土星环之谜一样，它始于一个谜题：为什么年轻人捐款不多？然后我们提出一个理论以及验证该理论的方法。这个谜题能够吸引受众，让他们思考究竟发生了什么，以及我们的理论是否正确。

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 2: This approach is inspired by the gap theory. The goal is not to summarize; it’s to make you care about knowing something, and then to tell you what you want to know. Like the Saturn rings mystery, it starts with a puzzle: Why don’t young people donate more? Then we present a theory and a way of testing it. The mystery engages the members of our audience, causing them to wonder what happened and whether our theory was right.

这部剧的改进之处在于结构，而非内容。说实话，这并非一个特别引人入胜的谜团，它绝对不会出现在《法律与秩序》这样的剧集中。但我们对谜团题材的接受度很高——这种形式本身就很有吸引力。

The improvement here is driven by structure, not content. Let’s face it, this is not a particularly interesting mystery. It would never make an episode of Law & Order. But our minds are extremely generous when it comes to mysteries—the format is inherently appealing.

记分卡
检查清单 消息 1    消息 2
简单的 -    -
意外    -   
具体的 -    -
可信的    
情绪 -    -
故事 -    -

妙语：为了吸引人们的兴趣，我们可以利用好奇心的差距理论。一点点神秘感就能起到很大的作用。

PUNCH LINE: To hold people’s interest, we can use the gap theory of curiosity to our advantage. A little bit of mystery goes a long way.

新闻预告式方法可以应用于各种情境下的各种想法。为了使我们的沟通更有效，我们需要转变思维方式，从“我需要传达什么信息？”转变为“我希望我的受众提出什么问题？”

The news-teaser approach can be used with all sorts of ideas in all sorts of contexts. To make our communications more effective, we need to shift our thinking from “What information do I need to convey?” to “What questions do I want my audience to ask?”

克服过度自信

Battling Overconfidence

知识差距理论依赖于我们指出他人未知事物的能力。但问题在于，人们往往高估了自己的知识储备。研究表明，我们通常对自己掌握的知识量过于自信。

The gap theory relies on our ability to point out things that people don’t know. One complication is that people tend to think they know a lot. Research has shown that we are typically overconfident about how much we know.

在一项研究中，研究人员请参与者思考他们所在大学面临的严峻停车问题。参与者被给予时间尽可能多地提出解决方案。最终，参与者共提出了约300个解决方案，这些方案被归为七大类。其中一类提出了减少停车需求的方法（例如，提高停车费），另一类则提出了更有效地利用停车位的方法（例如，设立“仅限小型车停放”的停车位）。

In one study, researchers asked people to consider the serious parking problem faced by their university. Participants were given time to generate as many solutions as they could. The participants generated, in total, about 300 solutions, which were classified into seven major categories. One category suggested ways to reduce demand for parking (e.g., by raising parking fees), and another suggested ways to use parking space more efficiently (e.g., by creating spaces for “Compact Cars Only”).

平均而言，参与者未能识别出专家小组评选出的最佳解决方案的70%以上。这种不足是可以理解的；我们不会指望任何人能够生成一个包含所有解决方案的数据库。然而，当被要求评估自身表现时，参与者预测自己识别出了75%的解决方案。他们认为自己掌握了大部分，但实际上却遗漏了大部分。

The average participant failed to identify more than 70 percent of the best solutions identified by an expert panel. This failure is understandable; we wouldn’t expect any one person to be able to generate a database worth of solutions. However, when the individuals were asked to assess their own performance, they predicted that they had identified 75 percent. They thought they got the majority, but in reality they’d missed them.

如果人们自认为无所不知，那么差距理论就很难发挥作用。幸运的是，有一些策略可以对抗过度自信。例如，诺拉·艾芙隆的新闻学老师就通过打破学生们对新闻的固有认知来防止他们过度自信。他先让他们坚持自己先入为主的观念，然后突然推翻这些观念。

If people believe they know everything, it’s hard to make the gap theory work. Fortunately, there are strategies for combating overconfidence. For instance, Nora Ephron’s journalism teacher prevented overconfidence by causing the students’ schemas of journalism to fail. He made them commit to their preconceived ideas and then pulled the rug out from under them.

让人们对预测做出承诺有助于防止过度自信。哈佛大学物理学教授埃里克·马祖尔提出了一种名为“概念测试”的教学创新方法。在他的课堂上，马祖尔会不时提出一个概念性问题，然后让学生公开投票选出答案。这种简单的选择答案的方式，能够让学生更积极地参与课堂，并对结果产生更大的好奇心。

Making people commit to a prediction can help prevent overconfidence. Eric Mazur, a physics professor at Harvard, came up with a pedagogical innovation known as “concept testing.” Every so often in his classes, Mazur will pose a conceptual question and then ask his students to vote publicly on the answer. The simple act of committing to an answer makes the students more engaged and more curious about the outcome.

过于自信的人在意识到他人与自己观点相左时，更容易意识到自身的知识不足。南希·洛瑞和戴维·约翰逊研究了一个教学环境，其中五六年级的学生被安排就某个主题进行互动。一组学生的讨论旨在达成共识，而另一组学生的讨论则被设计成引发关于正确答案的分歧。

Overconfident people are more likely to recognize a knowledge gap when they realize that others disagree with them. Nancy Lowry and David Johnson studied a teaching environment where fifth and sixth graders were assigned to interact on a topic. With one group, the discussion was led in a way that fostered a consensus. With the second group, the discussion was designed to produce disagreements about the right answer.

那些轻易达成共识的学生对讨论主题的兴趣较低，学习投入也较少，更不愿意去图书馆查阅资料。然而，最能说明问题的差异，是在老师们利用课间休息时间播放一部关于讨论主题的影片之后才显现出来的！只有18%达成共识的学生为了看影片而错过了课间休息，而45%存在分歧的学生则留下来看影片。填补知识空白的渴望——想要知道谁对谁错——有时比对滑梯和攀爬架的渴望更为强烈。

Students who achieved easy consensus were less interested in the topic, studied less, and were less likely to visit the library to get additional information. The most telling difference, though, was revealed when teachers showed a special film about the discussion topic—during recess! Only 18 percent of the consensus students missed recess to see the film, but 45 percent of the students from the disagreement group stayed for the film. The thirst to fill a knowledge gap—to find out who was right—can be more powerful than the thirst for slides and jungle gyms.

知识差距始于知识。

Gaps Start with Knowledge

如果好奇心源于知识空白，我们可能会认为，当我们掌握更多信息时，好奇心就会降低，因为知识空白减少了。但洛温斯坦认为事实恰恰相反。他指出，随着我们获得的信息越来越多，我们反而会越来越关注自己不知道的东西。一个知道美国50个州中17个州首府的人可能会为自己的博学感到自豪。但一个知道47个州首府的人，则更有可能觉得自己还不知道3个州的首府。

If curiosity arises from knowledge gaps, we might assume that when we know more, we’ll become less curious because there are fewer gaps in our knowledge. But Loewenstein argues that the opposite is true. He says that as we gain information we are more and more likely to focus on what we don’t know. Someone who knows the state capitals of 17 of 50 states may be proud of her knowledge. But someone who knows 47 may be more likely to think of herself as not knowing 3 capitals.

有些话题自然而然地会凸显我们知识的空白。人物故事之所以引人入胜，是因为我们了解身为人类的感受——但我们却不了解经历某些惊心动魄的事情是什么滋味。赢得奥运奖牌是什么感觉？中了彩票大奖是什么感觉？连体双胞胎昌和恩·邦克（他们不仅各自结婚，还生了十个孩子……这又引发了一系列其他问题）的生活又是怎样的呢？

Some topics naturally highlight gaps in our knowledge. Human-interest stories are fascinating because we know what it’s like to be human—but we don’t know what it’s like to have certain dramatic experiences. How does it feel to win an Olympic medal? How does it feel to win the Lotto? How did it feel to be conjoined twins Chang and Eng Bunker (each of whom not only married but had ten children … which sparks several additional lines of questioning)?

八卦之所以流行，是因为我们对某些人了解颇多，但总有一些信息我们并不了解。我们不会去议论泛泛之交。名人八卦尤其引人入胜。我们对泰格·伍兹和茱莉亚·罗伯茨大概有所了解，但我们渴望知道那些缺失的部分——他们的怪癖、感情纠葛、不为人知的恶习。

Gossip is popular because we know a lot about some people but there’s some information that we lack. We don’t gossip about passing acquaintances. Celebrity gossip is particularly tantalizing. We have a sense of who Tiger Woods and Julia Roberts are, but we crave the missing pieces—their quirks, their romantic struggles, their secret vices.

好奇心源于知识的空白。但如果一开始就缺乏足够的知识呢？上世纪60年代，一家新兴的电视网——美国广播公司（ABC）——签下了一份转播NCAA橄榄球比赛的合同。大学体育历来都是圈内人才懂的话题。除了少数铁杆体育迷之外，大多数球迷通常只关心自己学校的球队。但ABC每周在每个地区只能转播几场比赛。为了让ABC的这笔投资获得回报，它必须让观众关注那些与自己主队无关的比赛。

Curiosity comes from gaps in our knowledge. But what if there’s not much knowledge there to begin with? In the 1960s, an upstart television network, American Broadcasting Corporation, signed a contract to televise NCAA football games. College sports is a classic insiders’ topic. With the exception of a fringe of die-hard sports junkies, most fans usually care only about their own schools’ teams. But ABC could show only a few games each week in each region. For ABC’s bet to pay off, it needed to make viewers care about games that didn’t involve their home teams.

如何才能让德克萨斯州大学城（College Station）的观众关注密歇根大学对阵俄亥俄州立大学的比赛？一位名叫鲁恩·阿利奇（Roone Arledge）的29岁年轻人，此前主要负责安排团队报道棒球、拳击和橄榄球比赛，他写了一份备忘录，提出了改进大学橄榄球比赛报道的建议。

How do you go about making viewers in College Station, Texas, care about the Michigan vs. Ohio State matchup? A twenty-nine-year-old named Roone Arledge, whose previous responsibilities primarily involved assigning crews to cover baseball, boxing, and football games, wrote a memo suggesting ways to improve the coverage of college football games.

阿莱奇认为还有很大的改进空间。体育解说员通常架好摄像机，对准赛场，然后等待场上发生什么。他们忽略了其他一切——球迷、色彩、盛大的场面。“这就像透过门上的窥视孔看大峡谷一样，”阿莱奇说道。

Arledge saw ample room for improvement. Sportscasters typically set up their cameras, focused on the field, and waited for something to happen in front of them. They ignored everything else—the fans, the color, the pageantry. “It was like looking out on the Grand Canyon through a peephole in a door,” Arledge said.

一个星期六下午，他拖延了一上午后，坐下来开始给老板们写一份提案：

One Saturday afternoon, after procrastinating all morning, he sat down to type out a proposal to his bosses:

此前，电视在将比赛带给观众方面做得非常出色——现在，我们将把观众带到比赛现场！

Heretofore, television has done a remarkable job of bringing the game to the viewer—now we are going to take the viewer to the game! …

在开场广告牌之后，我们不会像往常那样切换到球场全景镜头，而是播放预先拍摄的校园和体育场的画面，以便引导观众了解比赛地点。观众必须知道自己身处俄亥俄州哥伦布市，这座城市对橄榄球无比狂热；或者，他身处俄勒冈州科瓦利斯市，置身于一小群却热情似火的球迷之中。他必须了解周围的乡村景色和校园风貌，有多少人和他一起观看这场比赛，这个地区的人们在橄榄球比赛时通常的着装，以及这场比赛对两所参赛学校的意义。

After our opening commercial billboards, instead of dissolving to the usual pan shots of the field we will have pre-shot film of the campus and the stadium so we can orient the viewer. He must know he is in Columbus, Ohio, where the town is football mad; or that he is part of a small but wildly enthusiastic crowd in Corvallis, Oregon. He must know what the surrounding country and campus look like, how many other people are watching this game with him, how the people dress at football games in this part of the country, and what the game means to the two schools involved.

这份备忘录长达三页，讨论了镜头角度、精彩瞬间和开场画面。但备忘录的核心在于，它提供了一种吸引观众的新方法，让那些原本可能对俄勒冈州科瓦利斯市的大学比赛不感兴趣的人也能关注到这场比赛。阿莱奇表示，诀窍在于要提供足够的比赛背景信息，让人们开始关注这场比赛。

The memo was three pages long. It discussed camera angles, impact shots, opening graphics. The heart of the memo, though, was a new way of engaging viewers who might not ordinarily care about a college game in Corvallis, Oregon. The trick, Arledge said, was to give people enough context about the game so that they’d start to care.

ABC电视台的其他人对阿莱奇的备忘录内容感到兴奋。两天后，年仅29岁、履历单薄的他被要求根据备忘录中的指导方针制作一场大学橄榄球比赛。

Other people at ABC were excited by what Arledge had written. Two days later, he was asked—at age twenty-nine, with a skimpy résumé—to produce a college-football game using the guidelines in his memo.

阿莱奇巧妙地运用了洛温斯坦的差距理论。如何才能让人们对某个话题感兴趣？指出他们知识上的空白。但如果他们对佐治亚斗牛犬队之类的球队了解甚少，以至于他们面临的与其说是差距，不如说是深渊呢？在这种情况下，你必须填补足够的知识空白，才能将深渊变成差距。阿莱奇营造了氛围，展现了当地球迷，镜头扫过校园。他渲染了人们的情感、竞争和历史。比赛开始时，一些观众开始关心谁赢了，而另一些观众则看得津津有味。

Arledge intuitively made use of Loewenstein’s gap theory. How do you get people interested in a topic? You point out a gap in their knowledge. But what if they lack so much knowledge about, say, the Georgia Bulldogs, that they’ve got more of an abyss than a gap? In that case, you have to fill in enough knowledge to make the abyss into a gap. Arledge set the scene, showed the local fans, panned across the campus. He talked up the emotions, the rivalries, the histories. By the time the game started, some viewers had begun to care who won. Others were riveted.

阿莱奇的下一个任务是接手一档后来更名为《体育世界》（Wide World of Sports）的系列节目。这档节目向美国观众介绍了一系列他们可能从未见过的体育赛事：环法自行车赛、勒芒24小时耐力赛、牛仔竞技锦标赛、滑雪比赛和足球比赛。在报道这些赛事时，阿莱奇沿用了他之前在NCAA（美国大学体育协会）节目中开创的理念：先交代赛事背景，提供足够的故事脉络，让观众开始关注自身知识的不足之处。谁会在艰苦卓绝的勒芒24小时耐力赛中掉队？那位从教师转型为桶滚赛车手的选手能否赢得冠军？黄牌到底是什么？

Arledge’s next assignment was to take over a series that was eventually renamed Wide World of Sports. The show introduced Americans to a variety of sports events they may never have seen before: the Tour de France, the Le Mans auto races, rodeo championships, ski races, and soccer matches. In covering these events, Arledge used the same philosophy he’d pioneered for the NCAA: Set the context and give people enough backstory that they start to care about the gaps in their knowledge. Who’s going to falter during the grueling twenty-four-hour Le Mans? Will the teacher turned barrel racer win the championship? What the heck is a yellow card?

阿莱奇于2002年去世。在他的职业生涯中，他曾担任ABC体育频道负责人，后又担任ABC新闻部负责人。他创办了《体育世界》（Wide World of Sports）、《周一橄榄球之夜》（Monday Night Football）、《20/20》和《夜线》（Nightline）等节目。他荣获36项艾美奖。他为NCAA橄榄球开发的工具包经受住了时间的考验。吸引人们关注的关键在于提供背景信息。如今，这似乎显而易见，因为这些技巧已经无处不在。但这一切背景信息的普及，源于一位29岁的年轻人撰写的一份备忘录，内容是如何让大学橄榄球比赛更有趣。

Arledge died in 2002. During his career, he became the head of ABC Sports and later ABC News. He founded the Wide World of Sports, Monday Night Football, 20/20, and Nightline. He won thirty-six Emmys. The tool kit he developed for NCAA football stood the test of time. The way to get people to care is to provide context. Today that seems obvious, because these techniques have become ubiquitous. But this avalanche of context started because a twenty-nine-year-old wrote a memo about how to make college football more interesting.

许多教师会使用某种形式的阿莱奇工具包来激发学生的兴趣。有些人将这种策略称为“高级组织者”。其理念是，为了让学生对新主题感兴趣，应该先强调他们已知的一些知识。例如，一位地球科学老师可能会让学生带来地震破坏的照片，以此引出板块构造的讨论。或者，老师也可以像阿莱奇那样设定背景，让学生开始产生兴趣。一位化学老师可能会通过讨论门捷列夫及其对元素周期表的长期而充满热情的探索来引入元素周期表。这样，元素周期表就如同一个侦探故事般浮现出来。

Many teachers use some version of the Arledge tool kit to prime their students’ interest. Some label the strategy “advanced organizers.” The idea is that to engage students in a new topic you should start by highlighting some things they already know. An earth-science teacher might ask her students to bring in pictures of an earthquake’s devastation, as a way of leading up to a discussion of plate tectonics. Alternatively, the teacher can set the context, à la Arledge, so that students start to become interested. A chemistry teacher might lead into the periodic table of elements by discussing Mendeleyev and his long, passionate quest to organize the elements. In this way, the periodic table emerges from within the context of a sort of detective story.

知识空白会激发兴趣。但要证明这些知识……如果存在知识缺口，可能需要先强调一些已知信息。“这是你已知的知识。现在，这是你尚未掌握的知识。” 或者，你可以设置一些背景信息，让人们关心接下来会发生什么。悬疑小说家和填字游戏作者给我们提供线索并非偶然。当我们感觉自己即将解开谜题时，好奇心就会驱使我们最终找到答案。

Knowledge gaps create interest. But to prove that the knowledge gaps exist, it may be necessary to highlight some knowledge first. “Here’s what you know. Now here’s what you’re missing.” Alternatively, you can set context so people care what comes next. It’s no accident that mystery novelists and crossword-puzzle writers give us clues. When we feel that we’re close to the solution of a puzzle, curiosity takes over and propels us to the finish.

电影里展现的藏宝图往往很模糊，只标出几个关键地标，并在宝藏所在的地方画个大大的“X”。通常，冒险者只掌​​握足够的信息找到第一个地标，而这便是漫长寻宝之旅的第一步。如果藏宝图像MapQuest.com那样制作，提供精确到家的路线指引，那冒险电影类型恐怕就彻底没戏了。信息传递的顺序很重要——不要一下子把所有信息都一股脑儿地塞给别人，而是先抛出一条线索，再抛出另一条，如此反复。这种沟通方式与其说是说教，不如说是调情。

Treasure maps, as shown in the movies, are vague. They show a few key landmarks and a big X where the treasure is. Usually the adventurer knows just enough to find the first landmark, which becomes the first step in a long journey toward the treasure. If treasure maps were produced on MapQuest.com, with door-to-door directions, it would kill the adventure-movie genre. There is value in sequencing information—not dumping a stack of information on someone at once but dropping a clue, then another clue, then another. This method of communication resembles flirting more than lecturing.

出乎意料的想法，通过填补知识空白，引人遐想，令人心驰神往。它们如同在亟待探索的领域打上一个醒目的红叉，却未必会告诉你如何抵达彼岸。而正如我们将看到的，一个意义非凡的红叉，最终可能会在未来数年里，左右成千上万人的行动。

Unexpected ideas, by opening a knowledge gap, tease and flirt. They mark a big red X on something that needs to be discovered but don’t necessarily tell you how to get there. And, as we’ll see, a red X of spectacular size can end up driving the actions of thousands of people for many years.

月球漫步和口袋里的收音机

Walking on the Moon and Radios in Pockets

二战后，东京满目疮痍，一家后来名为索尼的年轻公司艰难求生。它吸引了一批才华横溢的科学家和工程师，但其首款创新产品——电饭煲——却以失败告终。起初，索尼靠修理短波收音机勉强维持生计。

In the rubble of Tokyo after World War II, a young company, later named Sony, struggled to stay in business. It attracted a handful of smart scientists and engineers, but its first innovation, an electric rice cooker, was a failure. Initially, Sony survived by repairing shortwave radios.

大约在同一时期，索尼首席技术官井深大对晶体管产生了浓厚的兴趣，这种技术是贝尔实验室的一个团队近期发明的。井深大渴望一个“实质性”的项目来激励他麾下由五十名科学家和工程师组成的团队，而他认为晶体管具有巨大的潜力。但是，当他竞标从……获得这项技术的授权时，却遇到了阻碍。贝尔实验室遭到日本产业通商资源部拒绝颁发许可证。该部门对这家年轻公司管理如此尖端技术的能力持怀疑态度。

Around this time, Masaru Ibuka, Sony’s lead technologist, became intrigued by transistors, which had recently been invented by a team at Bell Laboratories. Ibuka craved a “substantial” project to motivate his team of fifty scientists and engineers, and he saw tremendous promise in transistors. But when he bid to license the technology from Bell Labs, the Japanese Ministry of Trade and Industry denied the license. It was skeptical of the young company’s ability to manage such a cutting-edge technology.

1953年，井深大获得了晶体管的授权许可。他设想制造一款基于晶体管的收音机。对于工程师来说，晶体管收音机的优势显而易见：它将使收音机摆脱笨重且不可靠的真空管。贝尔实验室告诉井深大，他们认为“晶体管收音机”不可能实现。但他的工程师们仍然开始着手实现这个设想。

In 1953, Ibuka secured permission to license transistors. He had a vision for a radio that would be based on transistors. The advantage of a transistor radio was obvious to engineers; it would free radios from the big vacuum tubes that made them so bulky and unreliable. Bell Labs told Ibuka that it didn’t think a “transistor radio” was possible. His engineers began to pursue the vision anyway.

让我们稍作停顿，设身处地地站在井深大的角度思考。你的公司一直举步维艰，你手下有一群才华横溢的人才，你需要激励他们。你有潜力带领他们朝着一百个不同的方向发展——电饭煲、收音机、电话，或者研发部门能想到的任何其他产品。但你坚信，基于晶体管的收音机是最有前途的方向。

Let’s pause here for a moment to put ourselves in Ibuka’s shoes. Your company has been struggling, and you’ve got a team of brilliant people whom you need to inspire. You have the potential to lead them in one hundred different directions—rice cookers or radios or telephones or whatever else R & D could dream up. But you’re convinced that the idea of a transistor-based radio is the most promising path.

那么，你的核心信息就是晶体管收音机的梦想。你如何让这个信息出人意料？如何激发团队的好奇心和兴趣？“晶体管收音机”这个概念本身可能不足以激励你的团队。它更侧重于技术而非价值。一台晶体管收音机——那又怎样呢？

Your core message, then, is the dream of a transistor radio. How do you make this message unexpected? How do you engage the curiosity and interest of your team? The concept of a “transistor radio” is probably not enough, in and of itself, to motivate your team. It’s focused more on technology than on value. A transistor radio—so what?

不妨借鉴一些经典的管理理念？竞争：“索尼将在晶体管收音机领域击败贝尔实验室。”质量：“索尼将成为全球最受尊敬的收音机制造商。”创新：“索尼将打造世界上最先进的收音机。”

What about tapping into some of the classic managerial themes? Competition: “Sony will beat Bell Labs in making a transistor radio work.” Quality: “Sony will be the world’s most respected manufacturer of radios.” Innovation: “Sony will create the most advanced radios in the world.”

这是井深大向他的团队提出的想法：“口袋收音机”。

Here’s the idea Ibuka proposed to his team: a “pocketable radio.”

现在回想起来，很难理解这个想法的狂妄——索尼的工程师第一次听到这个想法时，该是多么出乎意料、多么荒谬啊。收音机可不是可以放进口袋的东西；它们是家具。当时，收音机工厂还雇佣了全职的木工师傅。

It’s hard, in retrospect, to comprehend the hubris of that idea—how utterly unexpected, how preposterous, it must have seemed the first time a Sony engineer heard it. Radios were not things you put into your pocket; they were pieces of furniture. At the time, radio factories employed full-time cabinetmakers.

此外，贝尔实验室的精英们都认为不可能，而一家新兴的日本公司却能取得这样的创新，这种想法本身就令人难以置信。毕竟，在20世纪50年代，“日本制造”几乎就是粗制滥造的代名词。

Furthermore, the idea that an upstart Japanese company would deliver such an innovation, when the brilliant minds at Bell Labs thought it impossible, was not credible. After all, the 1950s were a decade when “Made in Japan” was synonymous with shoddy workmanship.

但索尼的工程师们才华横溢，雄心勃勃。井深大提出的袖珍收音机构想在公司内部迅速获得认可，并推动索尼进入了一段令人瞩目的增长期。到1957年，索尼的员工人数已增至1200人。1957年3月，在索尼勉强获准尝试晶体管技术仅仅四年后，该公司发布了TR-55，世界上第一款袖珍晶体管收音机。TR-55的销量高达150万台，使索尼在世界范围内声名鹊起。

But Sony engineers were talented and hungry. Ibuka’s idea of a pocketable radio caught on internally and drove Sony through an incredible period of growth. By 1957, Sony had grown to 1,200 employees. In March 1957, just four years after Sony was grudgingly granted permission to tinker with transistors, the company released the TR-55, the world’s first pocketable transistor radio. The TR-55 sold 1.5 million units and put Sony on the world map.

“口袋收音机”——这难道不只是一个绝妙的产品创意，而非一个绝妙的“吸睛点子”吗？不，两者兼具，而且两者都不可或缺。毫无疑问，即便井深大当初决定制造世界上最精致的电饭煲，世界上也总会有人发明出晶体管收音机。晶体管收音机是技术发展的必然趋势。但最初的晶体管收音机远非口袋大小，如果没有井深大出人意料的想法，他的工程师们或许会在技术发展到足够小巧实用之前就放弃研发。井深大激发了数百名工程师多年来的潜心钻研，正是因为他提出了一个出人意料的想法，激励他们竭尽全力。

A “pocketable radio”—isn’t this simply a brilliant product idea, rather than a brilliant “sticky idea”? No, it is both, and both elements are indispensable. There’s no question that someone in the world would have invented a transistor radio, even if Ibuka had decided to build the world’s fanciest rice cooker. Transistor radios were an inevitable technological progression. But the first transistor radios were nowhere near pocket-sized, and without Ibuka’s unexpected idea his engineers might have stopped pursuing the technology long before it became small enough to be useful. Ibuka inspired years of effort because he came up with an unexpected idea that challenged hundreds of engineers to do their best work.

1961年5月，约翰·F·肯尼迪在国会特别会议上发表了讲话。当时，冷战主导着全球政治。冷战时期，衡量成功——记录得失——的方法寥寥无几，但在一个备受瞩目的领域，美国显然落后了。这个领域就是太空。

In May 1961, John F. Kennedy gave a speech to a special session of Congress. It was a time when the Cold War dominated global politics. The Cold War allowed for few ways to measure success—to record gains and losses—but, in one highly visible field, the United States was clearly lagging behind. That field was space.

四年前，美国——这个曾自诩为科技最先进国家的国家——被苏联发射的第一颗人造卫星“斯普特尼克”震惊了。美国最终也发射了自己的卫星作为回应，但苏联始终保持领先，不断斩获第一。1961年4月，

Four years earlier, the United States—which had prided itself as the most technologically advanced nation—was stunned when the Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the first satellite. The United States eventually responded with its own satellite launches, but the Soviet Union maintained its lead, racking up first after first. In April 1961,

苏联宇航员尤里·加加林成为第一个进入太空的人类。一个月后，美国宇航员艾伦·谢泼德也进入了太空。

Soviet cosmonaut Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space. U.S. astronaut Alan Shepard followed a month later.

肯尼迪在向国会发表的演说中，提出了一系列请求，以帮助美国在冷战期间保持其领导地位。他要求提供资金以实现若干战略目标：建立国际发展援助计划（AID），扩大北约联盟，在拉丁美洲和东南亚建设广播电视台，以及加强民防。

In Kennedy’s address to Congress, he outlined a series of requests to help the United States maintain its leadership during the Cold War. He asked for funds to achieve a number of strategic goals: to establish the AID program for international development, to expand the NATO alliance, to build radio and television stations in Latin America and Southeast Asia, and to shore up civil defense.

但他的演讲结尾却出人意料。他最后的提议与国际援助或民防毫无关系，而是：“我相信，在这个十年结束之前，我们国家应该致力于实现将人类送上月球并安全返回地球的目标……如果我们做出肯定的决定，那么登上月球的就不是一个人，而是一个国家。因为我们所有人都必须为此努力。”

But he ended the speech on a curious note. His final proposal had nothing to do with international aid or civil defense. It was this: “I believe that this nation should commit itself to achieving the goal, before this decade is out, of landing a man on the moon and returning him safely to the earth … if we make this judgment affirmatively, it will not be one man going to the moon, it will be an entire nation. For all of us must work to put him there.”

两个出人意料的想法，都令人感到意外。收音机是家具，不是可以塞进口袋的东西。人类不会在月球上行走，因为那里地势高，空气稀薄。

Two unexpected ideas. Both create surprise. Radios are pieces of furniture, not something to slip into a pocket. Men don’t walk on the moon. It’s a long way up. The air is thin.

两者都能带来深刻的洞见。这些理念并非引导我们循序渐进地走下去，而是让我们突然而深刻地领略到世界可能的发展方向。不仅如此，它们还揭示了世界发展的原因。

Both create insight. Rather than leading us along a plodding route from one incremental step to the next, the ideas give us a sudden, dramatic glimpse of how the world might unfold. And not just how but why.

两者都会造成知识鸿沟。鸿沟理论的提出者洛温斯坦指出，我们必须记住，知识鸿沟会带来痛苦。“如果人们喜欢好奇心，为什么他们还要努力去填补它呢？”他问道，“为什么他们不会在读完悬疑小说最后一章之前就放下书，或者在精彩的棒球比赛进入最后一局之前就关掉电视呢？”

Both create knowledge gaps. Loewenstein, the author of the gap theory, says it’s important to remember that knowledge gaps are painful. “If people like curiosity, why do they work to resolve it?” he asks. “Why don’t they put mystery novels down before the last chapter, or turn off the television before the final inning of a close ball game?”

这两个出乎意料的想法都造成了巨大的知识空白——但并非大到无法逾越。肯尼迪并没有提出“载人登月”的设想，井深大也没有提出“植入式无线电”的设想。每个目标都大胆而富有启发性，但并非令人束手无策。任何听过“载人登月”演讲的工程师都必然会……立即开始集思广益：“嗯，首先我们需要解决这个问题，然后我们需要开发这项技术，然后……”

Both of these unexpected ideas set up big knowledge gaps—but not so big that they seemed insurmountable. Kennedy didn’t propose a “man on Mercury,” and Ibuka didn’t propose an “implantable radio.” Each goal was audacious and provocative, but not paralyzing. Any engineer who heard the “man on the moon” speech must have begun brainstorming immediately: “Well, first we’d need to solve this problem, then we’d need to develop this technology, then …”

便携式收音机的愿景支撑着一家公司度过了艰难的增长期，并最终使其成为国际公认的科技巨头。登月计划的愿景激励着数十个组织、数万名员工，持续了近十年之久。这些都是宏大、强大且具有深远影响的理念。

The vision of a pocketable radio sustained a company through a tricky period of growth and led it to become an internationally recognized player in technology. The vision of a man on the moon sustained tens of thousands of separate individuals, in dozens of organizations, for almost a decade. These are big, powerful, sticky ideas.

当我们怀疑自己能否吸引或留住人们的注意力时，不妨从肯尼迪和井深大身上汲取灵感。此外，我们还可以借鉴诺拉·艾芙隆的新闻学老师和诺德斯特龙百货公司的管理者们的做法。出其不意，只要服务于核心原则，就能带来意想不到的持久效果。

When we’re skeptical about our ability to get people’s attention, or our ability to keep people’s attention, we should draw inspiration from Kennedy and Ibuka. And, on a smaller scale, from Nora Ephron’s journalism teacher and Nordstrom’s managers. Unexpectedness, in the service of core principles, can have surprising longevity.



具体的



CONCRETE

一个炎热的夏日，一只狐狸在果园里漫步。他看到一串葡萄挂在高高的藤蔓上，已经成熟了。“这正是我解渴的好东西！”他心想。他后退几步，然后助跑跳向葡萄，却扑了个空。他又转身，跑得更快，再次跳跃。还是没够到。他一次又一次地跳，直到最后筋疲力尽，放弃了。他昂着头走开了，说道：“我敢肯定这些葡萄是酸的。”得不到的东西，总是容易被人轻视。

One hot summer day a Fox was strolling through an orchard. He saw a bunch of Grapes ripening high on a grape vine. “Just the thing to quench my thirst,” he said. Backing up a few paces, he took a run and jumped at the grapes, just missing. Turning around again, he ran faster and jumped again. Still a miss. Again and again he jumped, until at last he gave up out of exhaustion. Walking away with his nose in the air, he said: “I am sure they are sour.” It is easy to despise what you can’t get.

T上面提到的寓言故事《狐狸和葡萄》出自伊索之手。据希罗多德记载，伊索曾是奴隶（后来获得自由）。伊索创作了许多脍炙人口的故事，堪称世界历史上最经典的寓言之一。我们都听过他的代表作：《龟兔赛跑》、《狼来了》、《下金蛋的鹅》、《披着羊皮的狼》等等。如果这本书里的故事能在几千年后依然流传，那很可能就是《狐狸和葡萄》。

The fable above, “The Fox and the Grapes,” was written by Aesop. According to Herodotus, he was a slave (though he was later freed). Aesop authored some of the stickiest stories in world history. We’ve all heard his greatest hits: “The Tortoise and the Hare,” “The Boy Who Cried Wolf,” “The Goose That Laid the Golden Eggs,” “The Wolf in Sheep’s Clothing,” and many more. If any story told in this book is still circulating a few millennia from now, odds are it will be “The Fox and the Grapes.”

即使从未听过《狐狸与葡萄》这首童话故事的英语使用者，也会认出“酸葡萄”这个短语，它概括了……这个故事的寓意。伊索寓言传遍了世界。在匈牙利，人们说“酸葡萄”（ savanyu a szolo）。在中国，人们说“葡萄酸，因为够不着”。在瑞典，这句话又增添了一些地方特色；瑞典语谚语“Surt sa räven om rönnbären ”的意思是“狐狸说，花楸果真酸”。

Even English speakers who’ve never heard “The Fox and the Grapes” will recognize the phrase “sour grapes,” which encapsulates the moral of the story. Aesop’s lesson has traveled the world. In Hungary, people say savanyu a szolo—“sour grapes” in Hungarian. In China, they say, “Grapes are sour because you cannot reach them.” In Sweden, a little local color was added; the Swedish expression Surt sa räven om rönnbären means “Sour, the fox said, about the rowanberries.”

显然，伊索寓言揭示了人类普遍存在的缺陷。如果这则寓言没有反映出深刻的人性真理，它不可能流传两千五百多年。然而，许多深刻的真理并未渗透到数十种文化的日常语言和思维中。这条真理之所以如此难以流传，正是因为它独特的表达方式。寓言中生动的意象——葡萄、狐狸、以及对“酸葡萄”的轻蔑之语——使其信息得以流传至今。人们不禁会想，如果伊索的思想仅仅被包装成“伊索的实用建议”——比如“失败时别那么愤世嫉俗”，那么它的寿命或许会更短。

Clearly, Aesop was illustrating a universal human shortcoming. The fable would not have survived for more than 2,500 years if it didn’t reflect some profound truth about human nature. But there are many profound truths that have not seeped into the day-to-day language and thinking of dozens of cultures. This truth is especially sticky because of the way it was encoded. The concrete images evoked by the fable—the grapes, the fox, the dismissive comment about sour grapes—allowed its message to persist. One suspects that the life span of Aesop’s ideas would have been shorter if they’d been encoded as Aesop’s Helpful Suggestions—“Don’t be such a bitter jerk when you fail.”

世界需要更多寓言故事。在网络上，一个讽刺网站推出了一款“商业流行语生成器”。读者可以从三个栏目中各选一个词组合，生成自己的商业流行语，例如“基于成本的互惠重组”、“以客户为中心的远见卓识范式”和“战略物流价值观”。（顺便说一句，所有这些词听起来都像是很贴切的流行语。）教师也有自己的流行语：元认知技能、内在动机、作品集评估、适龄发展、主题式学习。如果你曾经和医生交谈过，我们甚至无需举例子。我们最喜欢的医学术语是：“特发性心肌病”。“心肌病”意味着你的心脏出了问题，“特发性”则意味着“我们不知道你的心脏为什么出了问题”。

What the world needs is a lot more fables. On the Web, a satirical site features a “Business Buzzword Generator.” Readers can produce their own business buzzwords by combining one word each from three columns, which yields phrases like “reciprocal cost-based reengineering,” “customer-oriented visionary paradigm,” and “strategic logistical values.” (All of these sound eerily plausible as buzzwords, by the way.) Teachers have their own buzzwords: metacognitive skills, intrinsic motivation, portfolio assessment, developmentally appropriate, thematic learning. And if you’ve ever talked to a doctor, we don’t even have to provide examples. Our favorite from medicine: “idiopathic cardiomyopathy.” “Cardiomyopathy” means something is wrong with your heart, and “idiopathic” means “we have no idea why yours isn’t working.”

语言往往是抽象的，但生活并非抽象的。教师教学生关于战争、动物和书籍的知识。医生修复疾病。我们用胃、用背、用心去体验。公司开发软件、制造飞机、发行报纸；他们制造的汽车比去年的更便宜、更快或更豪华。即使是最抽象的商业战略，最终也必须体现在人们的实际行动中。理解这些实际行动比理解抽象的战略宣言要容易得多——正如理解一只狐狸对葡萄的嘲弄比理解关于人类心理的抽象评论要容易得多一样。

Language is often abstract, but life is not abstract. Teachers teach students about battles and animals and books. Doctors repair problems with our stomachs, backs, and hearts. Companies create software, build planes, distribute newspapers; they build cars that are cheaper, faster, or fancier than last year’s. Even the most abstract business strategy must eventually show up in the tangible actions of human beings. It’s easier to understand those tangible actions than to understand an abstract strategy statement—just as it’s easier to understand a fox dissing some grapes than an abstract commentary about the human psyche.

抽象概念会使理解和记忆变得更加困难。它也使我们与他人协调行动变得更加困难，因为不同的人对抽象概念的理解可能截然不同。具体性则有助于我们避免这些问题。这或许是伊索寓言能教给我们的最重要的一课。

Abstraction makes it harder to understand an idea and to remember it. It also makes it harder to coordinate our activities with others, who may interpret the abstraction in very different ways. Concreteness helps us avoid these problems. This is perhaps the most important lesson that Aesop can teach us.

自然保护协会

The Nature Conservancy

五十年来，大自然保护协会（TNC）一直采用最简单的方法保护世界各地环境珍贵的地区：购买土地。它以市场价格购买土地，使其免受开发或伐木等破坏环境的用途。这种策略在TNC内部被称为“资金与土地”。它对捐赠者和捐助者极具吸引力，因为他们的捐赠成果显而易见。大额捐赠可以购买大片土地，小额捐赠可以购买小片土地。正如一位捐赠者所说，TNC的成果“触手可及”。

For fifty years, The Nature Conservancy (TNC) has helped protect environmentally precious areas in the world using the simplest possible method: It buys them. It buys land at market prices, making it offlimits to environmentally damaging uses, such as development or logging. This strategy has come to be known within TNC as “bucks and acres.” It had appeal to donors and benefactors, because the results of their gifts were so clear. A big gift bought a big piece of land. A small gift bought a small piece of land. As one donor commented, TNC produced “results you could walk around on.”

2002年，大自然保护协会加州分会的首席运营官迈克·斯威尼面临着一项巨大的挑战。加州对大自然保护协会来说尤为重要，因为它拥有众多环境极其重要的区域。加州是世界上仅有的五个地中海气候区之一。（其他三个分别是南非的芬博斯、智利的马托拉尔、澳大利亚的昆根，当然还有地中海本身。）这些地中海气候区仅占世界陆地面积的2%，却承载着极其重要的生态环境。超过20%的植物物种都生长在地中海气候区。如果您想购买环境优美的土地，地中海气候区绝对是物超所值的选择。

In 2002, Mike Sweeney, the COO of TNC California, was facing a big challenge. California is particularly important to TNC, because it contains so many environmentally critical areas. California is one of only five Mediterranean climate regions in the world. (The others are the fynbos of South Africa, the matorral of Chile, the kwongan of Australia, and, of course, the Mediterranean.) These Mediterranean climate zones occupy only 2 percent of the world’s landmass but host more than 20 percent of its plant species. If you want to buy environmentally precious land, Mediterranean climates give you a lot of bang for your buck.

2002年，斯威尼和他的团队拿了一张加州地图，把环境最敏感、最值得保护的区域都标了出来。令人惊讶的是，地图上40%的区域都被涂上了颜色。但这根本行不通：根本没有足够的资金来购买那么多土地。

In 2002, Sweeney and his staff had taken a map of California and colored in the most environmentally sensitive areas, the areas worth preserving. Astonishingly, 40 percent of the map was colored. This was a non-starter: There weren’t enough bucks out there to buy that many acres.

然而，加州有9%的地区被列为“极度危险”地区。9%的加州面积仍然远远超出收购范围，但这些地区对环境至关重要；大自然保护协会不能就此放弃。

Yet 9 percent of the state was classified as being in “critical danger.” Nine percent of California was still far too much to contemplate purchasing, but these regions were environmentally essential; TNC couldn’t simply give up on them.

大自然保护协会（TNC）决定采取一些新方法。“资金和土地”模式无法应对如此广袤的土地。因此，TNC不再直接拥有土地所有权，而是确保关键区域免受破坏。该组织会向土地所有者支付费用，以阻止他们开发土地，购买所谓的“保护地役权”。TNC将与地方和州政府合作，改变政策，鼓励保护私人和公共土地。TNC还将重点关注重要的海洋区域，因为这些区域没有土地可供购买。

TNC decided to implement some new approaches. “Bucks and acres” couldn’t succeed with this vast quantity of land. So instead of owning the land outright, TNC would ensure that the critical areas were protected against damage. The organization would pay landowners not to develop their land, buying what’s known as a “conservation easement.” It would work with local and state governments to change policies and encourage conservation of private and public land. It would focus on important marine areas, where there was no land to buy.

这些新策略合情合理——TNC 可以用这些策略保护比“资金和土地”所能覆盖的更多区域。但它们也有缺点。首先，这些策略对捐赠者来说缺乏实际意义。捐赠者无法“坐享其成”，毕竟政府的优惠政策无法兑现。其次，这些策略也可能打击员工的士气——让进展变得不那么直观。斯威尼说，当 TNC 专注于土地交易时，“很容易庆祝交易达成，告诉所有人，‘约翰和玛丽拿到了这块地’，然后拍拍他们的肩膀表扬他们。”这些对士气大有裨益的“里程碑时刻”，在新模式下却很难找到。TNC 该如何让新策略更加具体呢？
