These new strategies made sense—TNC could protect more areas than it could reach through “bucks and acres.” But they also had drawbacks. First, they were much less concrete to donors. Donors can’t “walk around on” a favorable government regulation. Second, they were also potentially demoralizing for employees—they made progress less tangible. When TNC was focused on land deals, Sweeney said, “it was easy to celebrate a deal closing, to tell everyone, ‘John and Mary got this land,’ and to pat them on the back.” These “milestone moments,” so great for morale, were harder to find in the new model. How could TNC make the new strategy more concrete?

在这种情况下你会怎么做？有没有办法重新夺回控制权？在形势必然更加模糊的情况下，“资金和土地”策略的宝贵可观性又体现在哪里呢？你需要保护该州40%（或至少9%）的土地，而这些土地是买不到的。你该如何向捐助者和合作伙伴解释你的策略？

What would you do in this situation? Is there a way to recapture the invaluable tangibility of the “bucks and acres” strategy in a context that was necessarily more ambiguous? You’ve got 40 percent (or at least 9 percent) of the state to protect, and you can’t buy it. How do you explain yourself to donors and partners?

Chip曾与他在斯坦福大学的学生们讨论过这个案例。在努力寻找具体化方案的过程中，一些学生试图将这项挑战——加州40%的土地！其中9%亟需保护！——分解成更具体的“子目标”。例如：“我们将在未来二十年里，每年保护加州2%的土地。” 另一些学生则试图引入一个我们能够理解的计量单位，例如英亩。大多数人都能想象出一英亩的样子。但规模实在太大了：加州2%的土地大约是两百万英亩。没有人能想象出两百万英亩是什么样子。

Chip has discussed this case with his students at Stanford, and in grappling with the need for concreteness some students respond by breaking up the impossibly large scale of the challenge—40 percent of California! 9 percent in critical need!—by subdividing it into more tangible “subgoals.” For example: “We will protect a 2 percent chunk of California every year for twenty years.” Others try to invoke a unit of measurement that we can understand, such as the acre. Most people can visualize an acre. But the scale is too big: 2 percent of California is about two million acres. No one can picture two million acres.

学生们明智地尝试将一个庞大而抽象的目标分解成更小、更具体的子目标。这个想法是对的。但在这个例子中，数字实在太大了。“面积”未必是最佳的思考方式。有些1500英亩的土地比另一些9万英亩的土地更具环境价值。考虑“每年的面积”就好比博物馆馆长只考虑“每年的画作数量”，而不考虑时期、风格或画家。

The students are wisely trying to find a way to break up a big, abstract goal into smaller, more concrete subgoals. This is the right idea. But in this case the numbers are just too big. And “acreage” is not necessarily the best way to think. There are 1,500-acre plots of land that are more environmentally precious than other 90,000-acre plots. Thinking about “acreage per year” is akin to a museum curator thinking about “canvases per year,” without regard to period, style, or painter.

以下是大自然保护协会（TNC）的做法：他们不再以土地面积来衡量保护目标，而是以“景观”为概念。景观指的是一块连片的土地，其特征独特且具有珍贵的生态价值。大自然保护协会设定了一个目标，即在十年内保护五十个景观——其中二十五个是当务之急。每年保护五个景观听起来比每年保护两百万英亩土地更现实，也更具体。

Here’s what TNC did: Instead of talking in terms of land area, it talked about a “landscape.” A landscape is a contiguous plot of land with unique, environmentally precious features. The TNC set a goal of preserving fifty landscapes—of which twenty-five were an immediate priority—over a ten-year period. Five landscapes per year sounds more realistic than 2 million acres per year, and it’s much more concrete.

硅谷东部有一片褐色的山丘，那是面积堪比优胜美地国家公园的荒野的起点。这些褐色的山丘是旧金山湾的重要分水岭，但它们……硅谷的扩张正迅速蚕食着这片土地。尽管这片区域在生态上很重要，但它不像红杉林或海岸那样拥有令人叹为观止的美景。山丘上覆盖着草地，间或点缀着几棵橡树。一年中的大部分时间，草地都是枯黄的。斯威尼承认，这景色并不吸引人。即使是硅谷地区那些致力于保护开放空间的当地团体，也没有注意到这些枯黄的山丘。但是，斯威尼说：“我们保护自然并非因为它的美观，而是因为它是生态系统中不可或缺的一部分。”

To the east of Silicon Valley there is a set of brown hills that are the beginnings of a wilderness the size of Yosemite. The brown hills are an important watershed for the San Francisco Bay, but they are quickly being chipped away by Silicon Valley sprawl. Although the area is important ecologically, it is not like the redwoods or the coast, with beautiful visuals that engage people’s imaginations. The hills are covered with grass interspersed with a few oak trees. Most of the year, the grass is brown. Sweeney admits that it’s not very sexy. Even local groups in the Silicon Valley area that were interested in protecting open spaces weren’t paying attention to the brown hills. But, says Sweeney, “We don’t go after stuff because it’s pretty. We go after it because it’s an ecologically important part of creation.”

大自然保护协会（TNC）将这片橡树稀树草原命名为汉密尔顿山荒野区（以其最高峰命名，该山峰也是当地天文台的所在地）。将这片区域认定为一个完整的景观并为其命名，使其进入了当地团体和政策制定者的视野。斯威尼表示，此前，硅谷的团体也希望保护靠近家园的重要区域，但他们却不知从何入手。“如果你说，‘硅谷东部有一片非常重要的区域’，这根本提不起他们的兴趣，因为它不够具体。但如果你说，‘汉密尔顿山荒野区’，他们的兴趣就会立刻被激发出来。”

TNC named the oak savanna the Mount Hamilton Wilderness (based on its highest peak, the site of a local observatory). Identifying the area as a coherent landscape and naming it put it on the map for local groups and policymakers. Before, Sweeney says, Silicon Valley groups wanted to protect important areas close to their homes, but they didn’t know where to start. “If you say, ‘There’s a really important area to the east of Silicon Valley,’ it’s just not exciting, because it’s not tangible. But when you say, ‘The Mount Hamilton Wilderness,’ their interest perks up.”

由惠普公司创始人之一创立的硅谷机构帕卡德基金会提供了一笔巨额捐款，用于保护汉密尔顿山荒野地区。湾区的其他环保组织也开始发起保护该地区的运动。斯威尼说：“我们现在总是笑，因为我们看到其他人的文件里提到汉密尔顿山荒野地区时，我们会说，‘你知道，那是我们编造的。’”

The Packard Foundation, a Silicon Valley institution created by one of the founders of the Hewlett-Packard Company, provided a large grant to protect the Mount Hamilton Wilderness. Other environmental groups in the Bay Area started campaigning to preserve the area. Sweeney says, “We’re always laughing now, because we see other people’s documents and they’re talking about the Mount Hamilton Wilderness. We say, ‘You know we made that up.’”

城市居民往往会给自己的街区命名，例如“卡斯特罗区”、“苏豪区”、“林肯公园”等等。这些名称逐渐定义了一个区域及其特征。街区都有其独特的个性。大自然保护协会也通过其景观设计达到了同样的效果。汉密尔顿山荒野保护区不仅仅是一片土地，它本身就是生态界的明星。

People who live in cities tend to name and define their neighborhoods: “the Castro,” “SoHo,” “Lincoln Park,” and so forth. These names come to define an area and its traits. Neighborhoods have personalities. The Nature Conservancy created the same effect with its landscapes. The Mount Hamilton Wilderness is not a set of acres; it’s an eco-celebrity.

这并非一个关于土地的故事，而是一个关于抽象的故事。——TNC通过将地图上抽象的色块转化为触手可及的景观，大自然保护协会避免了陷入抽象的陷阱，每年节省了200万英亩土地。他们明智地意识到，背景和解决方案都变得越来越模糊，但他们的信息绝不能也变得模糊不清。具体性是让理念深入人心的必要组成部分。

This is not a story about land; it’s a story about abstraction. TNC avoided the trap of abstraction—saving 2 million acres per year—by converting abstract blobs on a map into tangible landscapes. TNC realized, wisely, that the context had grown more ambiguous, and the solutions had grown more ambiguous, but that their messages could not be allowed to grow more ambiguous. Concreteness is an indispensable component of sticky ideas.

理解减法

Understanding Subtraction

什么使某物“具体”？如果你能用感官去感知某物，它就是具体的。V8发动机就是具体的。“高性能”是抽象的。大多数情况下，具体性归根结底在于特定的人在做特定的事情。在“意想不到”那一章中，我们讨论了诺德斯特龙百货公司世界一流的客户服务。“世界一流的客户服务”是抽象的。而一位诺德斯特龙的员工正在为顾客熨烫衬衫，这才是具体的。

What makes something “concrete”? If you can examine something with your senses, it’s concrete. A V8 engine is concrete. “Highperformance” is abstract. Most of the time, concreteness boils down to specific people doing specific things. In the “Unexpected” chapter, we talked about Nordstrom’s world-class customer service. “World-class customer service” is abstract. A Nordie ironing a customer’s shirt is concrete.

具体语言有助于人们，尤其是初学者，理解新概念。抽象是专家的特权。如果你需要向满屋子的人讲解某个概念，而你又不确定他们掌握了多少知识，那么具体语言是唯一安全的选择。

Concrete language helps people, especially novices, understand new concepts. Abstraction is the luxury of the expert. If you’ve got to teach an idea to a room full of people, and you aren’t certain what they know, concreteness is the only safe language.

为了了解这一点，我们可以从研究亚洲的数学课堂入手。多年来的新闻报道告诉我们，东亚儿童在几乎所有方面都优于美国儿童（除了高脂肪食物的摄入量）。这一点在数学方面尤为明显。美国人的数学能力很早就落后于亚洲人——这种差距在一年级就已显现，并在整个小学阶段不断扩大。

To see this, we can start by studying math classrooms in Asia. We know, from the news over the years, that East Asian children outperform American children in, well, just about everything (except the consumption of fatty foods). This is especially evident in math. The math skills of Americans fall behind those of Asians early—the gap is apparent in the first grade, and it widens throughout elementary school.

亚洲学校究竟有何不同？我们通常认为这些学校运作高效得近乎机械：课时长，纪律严明。我们觉得东亚学生在某种程度上缺乏“创造力”；我们倾向于认为他们凭借死记硬背和机械学习就能胜过我们的学生。但事实却恰恰相反。

What are Asian schools doing differently? Our stereotype is that these schools operate with almost robotic efficiency: Hours are long and discipline is strict. We think of East Asian students as being less “creative” somehow; we like to think they outperform our students through rote mechanics and memorization. The truth, it turns out, is almost exactly the opposite.

1993年，一组研究人员对日本的十所学校、台湾的十所学校和美国的二十所学校进行了研究。在每所学校，他们挑选了两位不同的数学老师进行观察，每位老师观察了四节课。研究人员发现，所有老师都大量使用死记硬背；在每个国家，至少有一半的课程都采用了这种方法。但其他教学方法在三个国家之间差异很大。

In 1993, a group of researchers studied ten schools in Japan, ten in Taiwan, and twenty in the United States. In each school, they picked two different math teachers to observe, and they observed four lessons with each teacher. The researchers found that all the teachers used rote recall quite a bit; it was standard procedure in at least half the lessons observed in every country. But other techniques varied greatly among the three countries.

例如，考虑一下日本老师提出的这个问题：“你有100日元，后来你又花了70日元买了一个笔记本。你还剩下多少钱？”或者台湾一位老师提出的这个问题：“一开始有三个孩子在玩球。后来又来了两个，之后又有一个孩子加入。现在有多少个孩子在玩球？”她一边讲解，一边在黑板上画出简笔画小人，并写下了等式3 + 2 + 1。

For instance, consider this question by a Japanese teacher: “You had 100 yen but then you bought a notebook for 70 yen. How much money do you still have?” Or this question, posed by a teacher in Taiwan: “Originally there are three kids playing ball. Two more came later, and then one more joined them. How many are playing now?” As she talked, she drew stick figures on the board and wrote down the equation 3 + 2 + 1.

请注意，这些老师通过强调具体且熟悉的事物——例如购买学习用品和打球——来解释抽象的数学概念。他们的解释利用了已有的认知图式，我们在“简单”一章中探讨过这种策略。老师们选取一个已有的认知图式——例如六人球赛的动态——并在此基础上叠加一层新的抽象概念。

Notice that these teachers are explaining abstract mathematical concepts by emphasizing things that are concrete and familiar—buying school supplies and playing ball. Their explanations take advantage of preexisting schemas, a tactic we explored in the “Simple” chapter. Teachers take an existing schema—the dynamics of a six-person ball game—and overlay a new layer of abstraction.

研究人员将这种提问方式称为“情境计算”。它与“死记硬背”几乎完全相反。而且，与我们的刻板印象相反，这种方式在亚洲的使用频率大约是美国的两倍（61%的课程对31%）。

The researchers called this style of questioning Computing in Context. It is pretty much the opposite of “rote recall.” And, contrary to our stereotypes, it occurred about twice as much in Asia as it did in the United States (61 percent of lessons versus 31 percent).

在另一个案例中，一位日本老师在桌子上放了5排，每排10块瓷砖。然后她拿走了3排，每排10块瓷砖。她问一个学生还剩下多少块瓷砖，学生给出了正确的答案：20块。老师接着问学生们是如何知道这是一个减法题的。这位老师为学生们提供了一个减法的直观图像。学生们可以将“减法”这个抽象概念建立在一个具体的基础上：从最初的50块瓷砖中抽走30块。研究人员将这类问题归类为概念知识题。这类问题是在日本，37% 的课程中​​会问到这个问题；在台湾，这一比例为 20%；但在美国，只有 2% 的课程中​​会问到这个问题。

In another case, a Japanese teacher placed on a desk 5 rows of 10 tiles each. Then she took away 3 rows of 10 tiles. She asked a student how many tiles were left, and he gave the correct answer: 20. The teacher then asked the students how they knew that this was a subtraction problem. This teacher provided her students with a visual image of subtraction. Students could build an abstract concept—“subtraction”—on a concrete foundation: 30 tiles being yanked away from an original set of 50. The researchers coded questions like this one as Conceptual Knowledge questions. This type of question was asked in 37 percent of lessons in Japan, 20 percent in Taiwan, but only 2 percent in the United States.

以具体事物为基础进行抽象不仅有利于数学教学，更是理解的基本原则。初学者渴望具体事物。你是否曾经读过学术论文、技术文章，甚至是备忘录，却被其中晦涩难懂的抽象语言搞得晕头转向，迫切需要一个例子？

Using concreteness as a foundation for abstraction is not just good for mathematical instruction; it is a basic principle of understanding. Novices crave concreteness. Have you ever read an academic paper or a technical article or even a memo and found yourself so flummoxed by the fancy abstract language that you were crying out for an example?

或许你也曾有过这样的经历：面对过于抽象的食谱，你束手无策：“煮至混合物达到浓稠的程度。” 咦？直接告诉我搅拌多少分钟不就好了吗？给我看看成品图！等我们做过几次之后，“浓稠的程度”这句话或许就能理解了。我们会逐渐构建出它所代表的感官图像。但第一次听到这句话时，它就像3+2+1对三岁小孩来说一样毫无意义。

Or maybe you’ve experienced the frustration of cooking from a recipe that was too abstract: “Cook until the mixture reaches a hearty consistency.” Huh? Just tell me how many minutes to stir! Show me a picture of what it looks like! After we’ve cooked the dish a few times, then the phrase “hearty consistency” might start to make sense. We build a sensory image of what that phrase represents. But the first time it’s as meaningless as 3 + 2 + 1 would be to a three-year-old.

这就是具体性如何帮助我们理解的——它帮助我们基于现有知识和认知的基石，构建更高层次、更抽象的洞见。抽象需要具体的基础。试图在没有具体基础的情况下教授抽象原理，就像试图在空中建造屋顶一样。

This is how concreteness helps us understand—it helps us construct higher, more abstract insights on the building blocks of our existing knowledge and perceptions. Abstraction demands some concrete foundation. Trying to teach an abstract principle without concrete foundations is like trying to start a house by building a roof in the air.

混凝土令人难忘

Concrete Is Memorable

具体的概念更容易记忆。以单个词语为例。人类记忆实验表明，人们更容易记住具体、易于想象的名词（例如“自行车”或“鳄梨”），而不是抽象的名词（例如“正义”或“个性”）。

Concrete ideas are easier to remember. Take individual words, for instance. Experiments in human memory have shown that people are better at remembering concrete, easily visualized nouns (“bicycle” or “avocado”) than abstract ones (“justice” or “personality”).

那些令人津津乐道的传说，自然充满了具体的词语和画面——想想肯德基炸鼠，或是肾脏劫案中装满冰块的浴缸。如果肾脏劫案的主人公醒来后发现有人偷走了他的自尊，那么这个传说就不会那么令人津津乐道了。

Naturally sticky ideas are stuffed full of concrete words and images—think of the Kentucky Fried Rat or the Kidney Heist’s ice-filled bathtub. The Kidney Heist legend would have been far less sticky if the man had woken up and found that someone had absconded with his self-esteem.

耶鲁大学研究员埃里克·哈夫洛克研究口口相传的故事，例如《伊利亚特》和《奥德赛》。他指出，这些故事的特点是包含大量具体情节，而抽象概念则很少。为什么呢？古希腊人显然并不排斥抽象概念——毕竟，柏拉图和亚里士多德就诞生于这样的社会。哈夫洛克认为，这些故事随着时间的推移逐渐摆脱了抽象概念。在代代相传的过程中，那些更令人难忘的具体细节得以保留，而抽象概念则逐渐消失。

Yale researcher Eric Havelock studies tales that have been passed down by word of mouth, such as the Iliad and the Odyssey. He notes that these tales are characterized by lots of concrete actions, with few abstractions. Why? The ancient Greeks certainly had no problem with abstraction—this was the society that produced Plato and Aristotle, after all. Havelock believes that the stories evolved away from abstraction over time. When they were passed along from generation to generation, the more memorable concrete details survived and the abstractions evaporated.

让我们跳到现代世界，看看另一个永恒而美好的表达领域：会计。试想一下，如果你是一位会计学教授，需要向大学生介绍会计原理，你会作何感想？对于一个初学者来说，会计似乎抽象得令人费解——损益表、资产负债表、T型账户、应收账款、库存股。看不到任何具体的人或感官数据。

Let’s skip to the modern world and another timeless and beautiful domain of expression: accounting. Put yourself in the shoes of an accounting professor who has to introduce accounting principles to college students. To a new student, accounting can seem bewilderingly abstract—the income statement, the balance sheet, T-accounts, accounts receivable, treasury stock. No people or sensory data in sight.

作为教师，如何才能让会计概念生动形象？佐治亚州立大学的两位教授卡罗尔·斯普林格和费伊·博西克决定尝试一种截然不同的方法。2000年秋季，斯普林格和博西克开设了一学期的会计课程，并以一个贯穿整个学期的案例研究为核心。该案例研究讲述了两位虚构的大学二年级学生克里斯和桑迪在勒格兰德州立大学创办一家新公司的故事。

As the teacher, how do you make accounting concepts vivid? Two professors from Georgia State University, Carol Springer and Faye Borthick, decided to try something radically different. In the fall of 2000, Springer and Borthick taught a semester of accounting using, as a centerpiece, a semester-long case study. The case study followed a new business launched by two imaginary college sophomores, Kris and Sandy, at LeGrande State University.

克里斯和桑迪构思了一款名为“安全夜间出行”（Safe Night Out，简称SNO）的新产品，这是一款面向家有已达驾驶年龄的青少年的家长的设备。该设备安装在青少年的车上，可以记录车辆的行驶路线和速度。这样一来，家长们就能首次确认孩子是否在安全驾驶。

Kris and Sandy had an idea for a new product called Safe Night Out (SNO), a device targeted at parents with teenagers who were old enough to drive. Installed in the teenager’s car, the device would record the route and speed of the car. For the first time, parents could confirm whether their car was being driven responsibly.

此时，作为一名会计入门课程的学生，你也成为了故事的一部分。克里斯和桑迪是你的朋友，他们听说你在上会计课，需要你的帮助。他们问你：“我们的商业想法可行吗？我们需要卖出多少单位的产品才能支付学费？” 你会得到关于如何追踪销售情况的指导。降低相关材料（GPS接收器、存储硬件）和合作关系（在eBay上出售的成本）的成本。

At this point you, as a student in introductory accounting, become part of the story. Kris and Sandy are your friends, and they’ve heard that you’re taking an accounting class. They need your help. They ask, Is our business idea feasible? How many units would we have to sell in order to pay for our tuition? You are given guidance on how to track down the costs of the relevant materials (GPS receivers, storage hardware) and partnerships (how much it would cost to sell it on eBay).

克里斯和桑迪这段持续一个学期的“肥皂剧”揭示了会计在商业生活中扮演的角色。每门会计课程都会讲解固定成本和可变成本的区别，但在这部肥皂剧中，这种区别与其说是被定义，不如说是被揭示。克里斯和桑迪无论如何都必须支付一些费用，例如产品开发所需的编程费用。这些是固定成本。而另一些成本则只有在产品生产或销售时才会产生——例如材料成本或eBay的佣金。这些是可变成本。如果你的朋友们正把学费投入到创业中，那么这些区别就至关重要了。

The semester-long Kris and Sandy soap opera revealed the role that accounting plays in business life. Every accounting course defines the distinction between fixed and variable costs, but in the soap opera this distinction wasn’t so much defined as discovered. Kris and Sandy have to pay some costs no matter what, such as the programming expense for developing the product. Those are fixed costs. Other costs are incurred only when products are made or sold—the cost of the materials or eBay’s commission, for example. Those are variable costs. If your friends are pouring their tuition money into a start-up business, those distinctions matter.

这个案例研究体现了情境式学习的理念，类似于亚洲数学课堂上的教学方式。但在数学课堂上，学生一个学期可能会接触到300个不同的例子。而在会计课上，学生们只有一个例子，但这个例子却足以涵盖一个学期的内容。

The case study is an example of learning in context, similar to the teachers in the Asian math classrooms. But in the math classrooms a student might encounter 300 different examples over the course of a semester. In the accounting class, students had one example that was sufficiently rich to encompass a semester’s worth of material.

随着学期的推进，作为克里斯和桑迪的会计，你亲眼见证了他们生意的起伏。当地法院找到克里斯和桑迪，想为假释犯使用SNO设备，但希望租赁而不是购买。克里斯和桑迪该如何应对？之后，生意开始飞速发展，但克里斯和桑迪突然惊慌失措地打电话给你，说他们有一张支票跳票了。他们的销量比以往任何时候都高，但银行账户里却空空如也。这怎么可能？（许多初创企业都会遇到这个问题，它引出了盈利能力和现金流之间的区别。）只有在你仔细研究了一个月的付款单和eBay收据之后，答案才逐渐清晰起来。

As the semester progresses, you witness, from your hot seat as Kris and Sandy’s accountant, the evolution of their business. A local court approaches Kris and Sandy wanting to use the SNO device for its parolees, but it wants to lease the device rather than buy it. How should Kris and Sandy respond? Later, the business begins to grow rapidly, but suddenly Kris and Sandy make a panicked call to you, having bounced a check. They’ve been selling more units than ever, yet there’s no cash in the bank. How is that possible? (This problem is faced by many startup businesses, and it introduces the difference between profitability and cash flow.) The answer becomes clear to you only after you’ve worked through a month of payment slips and eBay receipts.

那么，学生们的学习效果是否更好了呢？起初很难说。课程的调整使得期末考试很难与往年直接比较。一些学生似乎对新课程更加热情，但也有一些学生抱怨案例分析耗时过长。然而，随着时间的推移，具体措施带来的好处逐渐显现出来。案例研究的重要性日益凸显。在体验过案例研究后，高GPA学生更有可能选择会计专业。这种具体化的教学方式实际上激发了最优秀学生成为会计师的愿望。

So, did the students learn better? At first it was hard to say. The changes to the course made it hard to compare final exams directly with those of previous years. Some students seemed more enthusiastic about the new course, but others groused because the case study demanded a lot of time. Over time, however, the benefits of the concrete case study became increasingly obvious. After experiencing the case study, students with high GPAs were more likely to major in accounting. The concreteness actually made the most capable students want to become accountants.

但案例研究对普通学生也产生了积极影响。在平均两年后开设的下一门会计课程中，第一部分大量运用了学生在入门会计课程中应该掌握的概念。完成案例研究的学生在第一次考试中的得分明显更高。事实上，对于总平均成绩为C的学生来说，分数差距尤为显著。一般来说，他们的得分高出12分。请记住，这是在案例研究结束两年后得出的结果。具体知识确实有效。

But the case study also had positive effects for regular students. In the next accounting course—taken an average of two years later—the first section of the course built heavily on the concepts that students were supposed to have learned in introductory accounting. Students who had worked through the case study scored noticeably higher on this first exam. In fact, the difference in scores was particularly dramatic for students with a C average overall. Generally speaking, they scored twelve points higher. And remember, this is two years after the case study ended. Concreteness sticks.

记忆的粘扣理论

The Velcro Theory of Memory

具体事物为何能让想法深入人心？答案在于我们记忆的本质。

What is it about concreteness that makes ideas stick? The answer lies with the nature of our memories.

我们很多人都觉得，记住某件事有点像把它存放起来。记住一个故事，就像把它归档到我们大脑的“档案柜”里。这种比喻无可厚非。但令人惊讶的是，不同类型的记忆可能对应着完全不同的“档案柜”。

Many of us have a sense that remembering something is a bit like putting it in storage. To remember a story is to file it away in our cerebral filing cabinets. There’s nothing wrong with that analogy. But the surprising thing is that there may be completely different filing cabinets for different kinds of memories.

你其实可以亲自验证一下这个方法。接下来的句子会要求你记住各种不同的想法。每个句子花五到十秒钟仔细思考，不要匆匆略过。当你从一个句子过渡到另一个句子时，你会发现记住不同类型的事物感觉截然不同。

You can actually test this idea for yourself. The following set of sentences will ask you to remember various ideas. Spend five or ten seconds lingering on each one—don’t rush through them. As you move from one sentence to another, you’ll notice that it feels different to remember different kinds of things.

  • 记住堪萨斯州的首府。

  • Remember the capital of Kansas.

  • 记住《Hey Jude》的第一句歌词（或者其他你熟悉的歌曲）。

  • Remember the first line of “Hey Jude” (or some other song that you know well).

  • 记住蒙娜丽莎。

  • Remember the Mona Lisa.

  • 记住你童年时期度过大部分时光的那栋房子。

  • Remember the house where you spent most of your childhood.

  • 记住“真理”的定义。

  • Remember the definition of “truth.”

  • 记住“西瓜”的定义。

  • Remember the definition of “watermelon.”

杜克大学认知心理学家大卫·鲁宾用这个练习来阐释记忆的本质。每个需要记住的指令似乎都会触发不同的心理活动。记住堪萨斯州的首府是一个抽象的练习，除非你恰好住在托皮卡。相比之下，当你想到《Hey Jude》这首歌时，你可能会听到保罗·麦卡特尼的歌声和钢琴声。（如果你对《Hey Jude》这首歌完全没有印象，请把这本书换成一张披头士乐队的专辑。你会更开心的。）

David Rubin, a cognitive psychologist at Duke University, uses this exercise to illustrate the nature of memory. Each command to remember seems to trigger a different mental activity. Remembering the capital of Kansas is an abstract exercise, unless you happen to live in Topeka. By contrast, when you think about “Hey Jude,” you may hear Paul McCartney’s voice and piano playing. (If the phrase “Hey Jude” drew a blank, please exchange this book for a Beatles album. You’ll be happier.)

毫无疑问，蒙娜丽莎的形象唤起了你对那神秘微笑的视觉记忆。回忆童年的家或许会勾起你许多记忆——气味、声音、景象。你甚至可能感觉自己仿佛在家中奔跑，或者想起父母过去常坐的地方。

No doubt the Mona Lisa memory conjured a visual image of that famously enigmatic smile. Remembering your childhood home might have evoked a host of memories—smells, sounds, sights. You might even have felt yourself running through your home, or remembering where your parents used to sit.

“真理”的定义或许更难把握——你当然对“真理”的含义有所了解，但你可能不像解读《蒙娜丽莎》那样，能从记忆中提取出一个预先设定的定义。你或许需要即兴创造一个似乎符合你对“真理”理解的定义。

The definition of “truth” may have been a bit harder to summon—you certainly have a sense of what “truth” means, but you probably had no preformulated definition to pluck out of memory, as with the Mona Lisa. You might have had to create a definition on the fly that seemed to fit with your sense of what “truth” means.

“西瓜”的定义或许也需要一番思索。“西瓜”这个词立刻唤起感官记忆——条纹状的绿色瓜皮和鲜红的果肉，甜美的香气和味道，以及整个西瓜沉甸甸的重量。然后，当你试图将这些感官记忆提炼成一个定义时，你的思维或许会发生变化。

The definition of “watermelon” might also have involved some mental gyrations. The word “watermelon” immediately evoked sense memories—the striped green rind and red fruit, the sweet smell and taste, the heft of a whole watermelon. Then you might have felt your gears switch as you tried to encapsulate these sense memories into a definition.

因此，记忆不像一个单独的文件柜，它更像是魔术贴。如果你观察魔术贴的两面，你会发现一面布满了成千上万个细小的钩子，而另一面则没有。它由成千上万个微小的环组成。当你把两边压在一起时，大量的钩子会被钩住，从而形成魔术贴的粘合效果。

Memory, then, is not like a single filing cabinet. It is more like Velcro. If you look at the two sides of Velcro material, you’ll see that one is covered with thousands of tiny hooks and the other is covered with thousands of tiny loops. When you press the two sides together, a huge number of hooks get snagged inside the loops, and that’s what causes Velcro to seal.

你的大脑中存在着数量惊人的神经回路。一个想法拥有的“钩子”越多，就越容易被记住。你童年时的家在你脑海中留下了无数个“钩子”。一张新的信用卡号码，如果幸运的话，也只会留下一个。

Your brain hosts a truly staggering number of loops. The more hooks an idea has, the better it will cling to memory. Your childhood home has a gazillion hooks in your brain. A new credit card number has one, if it’s lucky.

优秀的教师善于拓展某个特定理念的吸引力。爱荷华州一位名叫简·艾略特的教师曾设计过一个极具感染力的教学理念——它触动了人们情感和记忆的诸多层面——以至于二十年后，她的学生仍然记忆犹新。

Great teachers have a knack for multiplying the hooks in a particular idea. A teacher from Iowa named Jane Elliott once designed a message so powerful—tapping into so many different aspects of emotion and memory—that, twenty years later, her students still remember it vividly.

棕色眼睛，蓝色眼睛

Brown Eyes, Blue Eyes

1968年4月4日，马丁·路德·金遇刺身亡。第二天，爱荷华州的一位小学教师简·艾略特不得不向她班上的三年级学生解释他的死讯。在爱荷华州赖斯维尔这个全是白人的小镇上，学生们虽然熟悉金，但却无法理解是谁想要他的命，以及背后的原因。

Martin Luther King, Jr., was assassinated on April 4, 1968. The next day, Jane Elliott, an elementary-school teacher in Iowa, found herself trying to explain his death to her classroom of third-graders. In the all-white town of Riceville, Iowa, students were familiar with King but could not understand who would want him dead, or why.

埃利奥特说：“我知道是时候以具体的方式来解决这个问题了，因为我们从开学第一天就开始谈论歧视问题。但是，两个月前马丁·路德·金——我们‘每月英雄’之一——被枪杀的事件，无法向爱荷华州赖斯维尔的小三年级学生解释清楚。”

Elliott said, “I knew it was time to deal with this in a concrete way, because we’d talked about discrimination since the first day of school. But the shooting of Martin Luther King, one of our ‘Heroes of the Month’ two months earlier, couldn’t be explained to little third-graders in Riceville, Iowa.”

第二天，她带着计划来到课堂：她要让学生们真切地感受到偏见的存在。上课伊始，她把学生分成两组：棕色眼睛的孩子和蓝色眼睛的孩子。然后，她宣布了一个令人震惊的消息：棕色眼睛的孩子比蓝色眼睛的孩子更优秀——“他们是这间教室里更优秀的人。” 随后，两组学生被分开：蓝色眼睛的孩子被迫坐在教室后排，而棕色眼睛的孩子则被告知他们更聪明。他们获得了额外的课间休息时间。蓝眼睛的孩子必须佩戴特殊的项圈，以便人们能从远处一眼认出他们的眼睛颜色。课间休息时，两组孩子不允许混在一起。

She came to class the next day with a plan: She aimed to make prejudice tangible to her students. At the start of class, she divided the students into two groups: brown-eyed kids and blue-eyed kids. She then made a shocking announcement: Brown-eyed kids were superior to blue-eyed kids—“They’re the better people in this room.” The groups were separated: Blue-eyed kids were forced to sit at the back of the classroom. Brown-eyed kids were told that they were smarter. They were given extra time at recess. The blue-eyed kids had to wear special collars, so that everyone would know their eye color from a distance. The two groups were not allowed to mix at recess.

埃利奥特对班级的变化之快感到震惊。“我眼睁睁地看着那些孩子变成了令人讨厌、恶毒、充满歧视的三年级学生……真是太可怕了，”她说。“友谊似乎瞬间瓦解，棕色眼睛的孩子嘲笑他们以前是蓝眼睛的朋友。一个棕色眼睛的学生问埃利奥特，‘你长着蓝眼睛，怎么能当老师呢？’”

Elliott was shocked at how quickly the class was transformed. “I watched those kids turn into nasty, vicious, discriminating third-graders … it was ghastly,” she said. “Friendships seemed to dissolve instantly, as brown-eyed kids taunted their blue-eyed former friends. One brown-eyed student asked Elliott how she could be the teacher “if you’ve got dem blue eyes.”

第二天上课伊始，艾略特走进教室，宣布她错了。其实低人一等的是那些棕色眼睛的孩子。这番逆转立刻赢得了大家的热烈欢呼。蓝眼睛的孩子们欢呼雀跃，跑去给那些低人一等的棕色眼睛的同伴戴上项圈。

At the start of class the following day, Elliott walked in and announced that she had been wrong. It was actually the brown-eyed children who were inferior. This reversal of fortune was embraced instantly. A shout of glee went up from the blue-eyed kids as they ran to place their collars on their lesser, brown-eyed counterparts.

在被分到低分组的那天，学生们形容自己很悲伤、很糟糕、很笨、很刻薄。“当我们处于低分的时候，”一个男孩哽咽着说，“感觉所有不好的事情都发生在我们身上。”而当他们处于高分组的时候，学生们则感到快乐、优秀和聪明。

On the day when they were in the inferior group, students described themselves as sad, bad, stupid, and mean. “When we were down,” one boy said, his voice cracking, “it felt like everything bad was happening to us.” When they were on top, the students felt happy, good, and smart.

就连他们在学习任务上的表现也发生了变化。其中一项阅读练习是一套拼读卡片，孩子们需要尽可能快地完成。第一天，蓝眼睛的孩子们排名垫底，他们用了5.5分钟。第二天，他们排名靠前，只用了2.5分钟。“你们昨天为什么没这么快？”埃利奥特问道。一个蓝眼睛的女孩说：“我们戴着项圈……”另一个学生插话说：“我们满脑子都是那些项圈。”

Even their performance on academic tasks changed. One of the reading exercises was a phonics card pack that the kids were supposed to go through as quickly as possible. The first day, when the blue-eyed kids were on the bottom, it took them 5.5 minutes. On the second day, when they were on top, it took 2.5 minutes. “Why couldn’t you go this fast yesterday?” Elliott asked. One blue-eyed girl said, “We had those collars on….” Another student chimed in, “We couldn’t stop thinking about those collars.”

埃利奥特的模拟实验使偏见变得具体——残酷而具体。它也对学生的生活产生了持久的影响。十年后和二十年后进行的研究表明，埃利奥特的学生比没有参加过这项实验的同龄人偏见程度要低得多。

Elliott’s simulation made prejudice concrete—brutally concrete. It also had an enduring impact on the students’ lives. Studies conducted ten and twenty years later showed that Elliott’s students were significantly less prejudiced than their peers who had not been through the exercise.

学生们至今仍对那次模拟记忆犹新。十五年PBS系列纪录片《前线》播出的埃利奥特学生们的重聚节目，揭示了这段经历对他们的深刻影响。雷·汉森回忆起自己对这段经历的理解是如何在一天之内发生转变的，他说：“这是我经历过的最深刻的学习体验之一。”苏·金德·罗兰说：“偏见必须在年幼时消除，否则它将伴随你一生。有时我会发现自己带有歧视的倾向，我会立刻停下来，回想起三年级时被人轻视的感受。”

Students still remember the simulation vividly. A fifteen-year reunion of Elliott’s students broadcast on the PBS series Frontline revealed how deeply it had moved them. Ray Hansen, remembering the way his understanding changed from one day to the next, said, “It was one of the most profound learning experiences I’ve ever had.” Sue Ginder Rolland said, “Prejudice has to be worked out young or it will be with you all your life. Sometimes I catch myself [discriminating], stop myself, think back to the third grade, and remember what it was like to be put down.”

简·艾略特巧妙地将偏见这一概念融入教学之中。她本可以像对待其他课堂概念一样，将偏见视为一个重要但抽象的知识点，比如堪萨斯州的首府或“真理”的定义。她本可以像讲述二战战役的故事一样，将偏见当作需要学习的东西。然而，艾略特却将偏见变成了一种体验。想想其中的“钩子”：朋友突然对你冷笑；领子勒住脖子的感觉；感到自卑的绝望；照镜子时看到自己眼睛的震惊。这种体验在学生的记忆中留下了如此深刻的印象，以至于几十年后，他们仍然难以忘怀。

Jane Elliott put hooks into the idea of prejudice. It would have been easy for her to treat the idea of prejudice the way other classroom ideas are treated—as an important but abstract bit of knowledge, like the capital of Kansas or the definition of “truth.” She could have treated prejudice as something to be learned, like the story of a World War II battle. Instead, Elliott turned prejudice into an experience. Think of the “hooks” involved: The sight of a friend suddenly sneering at you. The feel of a collar around your neck. The despair at feeling inferior. The shock you get when you look at your own eyes in the mirror. This experience put so many hooks into the students’ memories that, decades later, it could not be forgotten.

通往抽象之路：

蓝图与机器

The Path to Abstraction:

The Blueprint and the Machine

简·艾略特对偏见的模拟有力地证明了具体性的力量。但如果具体性如此强大，为什么我们又如此轻易地陷入抽象呢？

Jane Elliott’s simulation of prejudice is compelling evidence of the power of concreteness. But if concreteness is so powerful, why do we slip so easily into abstraction?

原因很简单：专家和新手的区别在于抽象思维能力。新晋陪审员会被律师的个性、事实细节和法庭仪式所吸引。而法官则会将当前的案件与过往案例和法律先例中的抽象经验进行权衡。生物课的学生努力记住爬行动物是否产卵，而生物课的老师则着眼于动物分类学的宏大体系。

The reason is simple: because the difference between an expert and a novice is the ability to think abstractly. New jurors are struck by lawyers’ personalities and factual details and courtroom rituals. Meanwhile, judges weigh the current case against the abstract lessons of past cases and legal precedent. Biology students try to remember whether reptiles lay eggs or not. Biology teachers think in terms of the grand system of animal taxonomy.

新手将具体细节视为具体细节，而专家则将具体细节视为他们多年经验积累而成的模式和洞见的象征。正因为他们能够洞察更高层次的本质，所以他们自然而然地想要进行更高层次的讨论。他们想谈论的是国际象棋策略，而不是主教斜线移动的细节。

Novices perceive concrete details as concrete details. Experts perceive concrete details as symbols of patterns and insights that they have learned through years of experience. And, because they are capable of seeing a higher level of insight, they naturally want to talk on a higher level. They want to talk about chess strategies, not about bishops moving diagonally.

而我们经典的“知识诅咒”也正是在这里登场。一位名叫贝丝·贝奇基的研究员研究了一家制造公司，该公司设计并制造用于生产硅芯片的复杂机器。为了制造这些机器，该公司需要两类技能：能够创造出精妙设计的工程师，以及能够将这些设计转化为复杂物理机器的熟练制造人员。

And here is where our classic villain, the Curse of Knowledge, inserts itself. A researcher named Beth Bechky studied a manufacturing firm that designed and built the complicated machinery used to produce silicon chips. To build such machinery, the firm needed two sets of skills: engineers who could create brilliant designs, and skilled manufacturing people who could transform those designs into complex physical machines.

公司要想成功，这两类人必须能够顺畅沟通。但不出所料，他们说着不同的语言。工程师们倾向于抽象思考——他们整天埋头研究图纸和蓝图。而制造团队则倾向于实际操作——他们每天忙于制造机器。

If the firm was to succeed, these two sets of people had to be able to communicate smoothly. But, not surprisingly, they spoke different languages. The engineers tended to think abstractly—they spent their day agonizing over drawings and blueprints. The manufacturing team, on the other hand, tended to think on a physical level—they spent their day building machines.

最能揭示“知识诅咒”本质的，是生产车间出现问题时的情形。生产人员有时会遇到一些问题——比如零件不匹配，或者供电不足。他们会把问题反馈给工程师，而工程师则会立即着手解决。具体来说，他们会着手修改图纸。

What’s most revealing for the Curse of Knowledge is what happened when something went wrong on the manufacturing floor. The manufacturing folks would sometimes run into a problem—something didn’t fit or perhaps wasn’t receiving enough power. The manufacturers would bring the problem to the engineers, and the engineers would immediately get to work. Specifically, they’d get to work fixing their drawings.

例如，制造团队可能发现某个零件无法安装到机器上。当他们把这个零件展示给工程师时，工程师们就想拿出蓝图，在图纸上调整零件的位置。换句话说，工程师们本能地想要跳到更高层次的抽象思维中去。

For example, the manufacturing team might find a part that didn’t fit on the machine. When the team showed the part to the engineers, they wanted to pull out the blueprints and move things around on the drawing. In other words, the engineers instinctively wanted to jump to a higher level of abstraction.

贝奇基发现，工程师们绘制的图纸“越来越”最初的想法是“详尽地绘制图纸”，希望这些改进后的图纸能够帮助制造商更好地理解工艺流程。但随着时间的推移，图纸变得越来越抽象，这反而进一步阻碍了沟通。

The engineers, Bechky found, made their drawings “increasingly elaborate” in the hope that the enhanced drawings would clarify the process for the manufacturers. Over time, the drawings became more abstract, which further hampered communication.

这些工程师的行为就像去国外旅游的美国游客，为了让别人听懂，故意放慢语速、提高音量说英语。他们患上了“知识诅咒”，完全丧失了从非专业人士的角度看待技术图纸的能力。

The engineers were behaving like American tourists who travel to foreign countries and try to make themselves understood by speaking English more slowly and loudly. They were suffering from the Curse of Knowledge. They had lost the ability to imagine what it was like to look at a technical drawing from the perspective of a nonexpert.

生产部门的人心想：你为什么不亲自到车间来，给我演示一下零件应该安装在哪里呢？而工程师们则在想：我需要做些什么才能改进图纸呢？

The manufacturing people were thinking, Why don’t you just come down to the factory floor and show me where the part should go? And the engineering people were thinking, What do I need to do to make the drawings better?

这种沟通不畅的特质，对于许多不从事硅芯片制造的读者来说，无疑并不陌生。那么，该如何解决呢？双方是否应该学会更多地体谅对方，最终达成妥协？实际上并非如此。解决方案在于工程师们改变自身的行为。为什么呢？正如贝奇基所指出的，物理机器才是最有效、最相关的沟通领域。每个人都能流畅地理解机器。因此，问题应该在机器层面解决。

The miscommunication has a quality that is familiar, no doubt, to many readers who don’t work on silicon chip—making machinery. So how do you fix it? Should both parties learn greater empathy for the other and, in essence, meet in the middle? Actually, no. The solution is for the engineers to change their behavior. Why? As Bechky notes, the physical machine was the most effective and relevant domain of communication. Everyone understands the machines fluently. Therefore problems should be solved at the level of the machine.

我们很容易忽略自己说话时像个专家一样。我们会开始受到“知识诅咒”的影响，就像“敲击者和聆听者”游戏中的敲击者一样。用具体的语言谈论我们多年来熟稔的话题可能会感觉很不自然。但如果我们愿意付出努力，就会看到回报：我们的听众会理解我们所说的话，并且记住它们。

It’s easy to lose awareness that we’re talking like an expert. We start to suffer from the Curse of Knowledge, like the tappers in the “tappers and listeners” game. It can feel unnatural to speak concretely about subject matter we’ve known intimately for years. But if we’re willing to make the effort we’ll see the rewards: Our audience will understand what we’re saying and remember it.

这个故事的寓意并非要“简化问题”。制造业人士面临着复杂的问题，他们需要的是巧妙的解决方案。相反，这个故事的寓意在于找到一种“通用语言”，一种人人都能流利使用的语言。而这种通用语言必然是具体的。

The moral of this story is not to “dumb things down.” The manufacturing people faced complex problems and they needed smart answers. Rather, the moral of the story is to find a “universal language,” one that everyone speaks fluently. Inevitably, that universal language will be concrete.

混凝土促进协调

Concrete Allows Coordination

上一章结尾，我们提到了两个出人意料的口号，它们被用来激励和协调一大群聪明人。这两个口号分别是“制造袖珍收音机”和“十年内将人送上月球”。值得注意的是，这些口号都非常具体。我们很难想象日本工程师会因为对任务的不确定性而束手无策，或者美国宇航局会花费大量时间纠结于“人”、“月球”或“十年”的具体含义。

In the last chapter, we closed with two unexpected slogans that were used to motivate and coordinate large groups of smart people. The slogans were challenges to build a “pocketable radio” and to “put a man on the moon within the decade.” Notice that these slogans are also pleasingly concrete. It is doubtful that Japanese engineers were paralyzed with uncertainty about their mission, or that much time was spent at NASA quibbling about the meaning of “man,” “moon,” or “decade.”

具体化使目标清晰可见。即使是专家也需要透明度。设想一家软件创业公司，其目标是打造“下一个伟大的搜索引擎”。公司里有两位知识水平几乎相同的程序员，在相邻的工位上工作。对其中一位来说，“下一个伟大的搜索引擎”意味着完备性，确保搜索引擎能够返回网络上所有可能相关的内容，无论多么冷门。对另一位来说，则意味着速度，确保能够快速获得相当不错的搜索结果。只有当目标具体化之后，他们的努力才能真正协同一致。

Concreteness makes targets transparent. Even experts need transparency. Consider a software start-up whose goal is to build “the next great search engine.” Within the start-up are two programmers with nearly identical knowledge, working in neighboring cubes. To one “the next great search engine” means completeness, ensuring that the search engine returns everything on the Web that might be relevant, no matter how obscure. To the other it means speed, ensuring pretty good results very fast. Their efforts will not be fully aligned until the goal is made concrete.

上世纪60年代，波音公司准备启动727客机的设计工作时，其管理层设定了一个非常具体的目标：727必须能容纳131名乘客，能够从迈阿密直飞纽约，并且能够在拉瓜迪亚机场的4-22跑道降落。（之所以选择4-22跑道，是因为它的长度——不到一英里，对于当时任何一款客机来说都太短了。）有了如此明确的目标，波音公司才能有效地协调数千名来自工程或制造各个领域的专家。试想一下，如果727的目标是“成为世界上最好的客机”，那将会是多么困难。

When Boeing prepared to launch the design of the 727 passenger plane in the 1960s, its managers set a goal that was deliberately concrete: The 727 must seat 131 passengers, fly nonstop from Miami to New York City, and land on Runway 4-22 at La Guardia. (The 4-22 runway was chosen for its length—less than a mile, which was much too short for any of the existing passenger jets.) With a goal this concrete, Boeing effectively coordinated the actions of thousands of experts in various aspects of engineering or manufacturing. Imagine how much harder it would have been to build a 727 whose goal was to be “the best passenger plane in the world.”

法拉利在研发实验室里去了迪士尼乐园

The Ferraris Go to Disney World in the R & D Lab

Stone Yamashita Partners 是一家位于旧金山的小型咨询公司，由前苹果公司创意人员 Robert Stone 和 Keith Yamashita 创立。Stone Yamashita 擅长运用具体方法帮助组织实现变革。“我们所做的几乎所有事情都注重直观性和视觉效果，”Keith Yamashita 说。大多数咨询公司的“成果”往往是一份 PowerPoint 演示文稿。而在 Stone Yamashita，成果更可能是模拟演练、活动或创意装置。

Stone Yamashita Partners, a small consulting firm in San Francisco, was founded by Robert Stone and Keith Yamashita, former Apple creatives. Stone Yamashita is a master of using concrete techniques to help organizations create change. “Almost everything we do is visceral and visual,” Keith Yamashita says. The “product” of most consulting firms is often a PowerPoint presentation. At Stone Yamashita, it’s much more likely to be a simulation, an event, or a creative installation.

大约在2002年，惠普公司（HP）联系了山下石。惠普的高层管理团队希望与迪士尼建立合作关系，他们请山下石帮忙准备一份提案，重点介绍惠普的研究成果，并说明这些成果如何帮助迪士尼运营其主题公园。

Around 2002, Stone Yamashita was approached by Hewlett-Packard (HP). HP’s top management team hoped to win a partnership with Disney, and they asked Stone Yamashita to help prepare a proposal that would highlight HP research, and show how it could help Disney run its theme parks.

与许多科技公司一样，惠普在实验室里产出了大量的研究成果，但这些成果并非总能转化为实际的产品。研究人员热衷于突破技术边界，打造复杂精密的精密产品，而消费者通常更倾向于简单易用、性能可靠的产品。研究人员和消费者的需求并非总能完全契合。

HP, like many technology firms, generates great research in its laboratories, but that research isn’t always translated into tangible physical products. Researchers get excited about pushing the boundaries of a technology, making products that are complex and sophisticated, while customers generally seek out products that are easy and reliable. The desires of researchers and customers don’t always dovetail.

斯通·山下设计的“展示”是一个占地6000平方英尺的展览。山下描述了展览的要点：“我们虚构了一个名为法拉利家族的三代人，并围绕他们的生活和他们游览迪士尼乐园的故事，搭建了一个展览。”

The “presentation” that Stone Yamashita designed was an exhibit that filled 6,000 square feet. Yamashita describes the gist: “We invented a fictitious family called the Ferraris, three generations of them, and built an exhibit about their life and their visit to Disney World.”

走进展览，首先映入眼帘的是费拉里一家的客厅，里面摆满了家庭照片。接下来的每个房间都带领观众重温费拉里一家在迪士尼乐园度假的精彩瞬间。惠普的技术帮助他们购买门票、加快入园速度，并预订了晚餐。另一项技术则帮助他们尽情享受心仪的游乐设施，同时最大限度地减少等待时间。一天结束时，回到酒店房间，还有一个惊喜：一个数码相框自动下载了一张他们乘坐迪士尼乐园过山车的照片。

Walking into the exhibit, you began in the Ferraris’ living room, furnished with family photos. Each subsequent room followed the Ferraris through various scenes of their Disney World vacation. HP technology helped them buy tickets, sped their entry into the park, and scheduled their reservations for dinner. Another bit of technology helped them enjoy their favorite rides while minimizing waiting time. Back inside their hotel room at the end of the day, there was a final twist: A digital picture frame had automatically downloaded a picture of them as they rode a Disney World roller coaster.

斯通·山下与惠普的工程师合作，将原本可能只是一份 PowerPoint 演示文稿的内容，变成了一个生动逼真的模拟演示，旨在阐述协作的益处。他们将电子服务的概念融入其中。他们将一个抽象的概念具体化，并营造出强烈的感官体验。

Stone Yamashita, working with HP’s engineers, turned a message about the benefits of collaboration—what could have been a Power-Point presentation—into a living, breathing simulation. Stone Yamashita put hooks into the idea of e-services. They took an abstract idea and made it concrete with an intense sensory experience.

值得注意的是，展览面向两个不同的受众群体。第一个受众群体是迪士尼。迪士尼的高管们是“新手”——他们需要以切实可行的方式了解惠普的技术能为他们带来什么。第二个受众群体是惠普的员工，尤其是工程师们。他们远非新手。许多工程师此前对山下先生的演示价值持怀疑态度。然而，展览开幕后，在惠普内部引发了极大的热情。展览最初的计划是只展出到迪士尼的提案阶段，但由于反响热烈，之后又持续展出了三四个月。一位观察人士表示：“展览迅速传播开来，其他人开始询问：‘你看到实验室团队做的那个了不起的东西了吗？你知道我们也能做到吗？你知道他们只用了28天就完成了吗？’”

Note that there were two different audiences for the exhibit. The first audience was Disney. Disney’s execs were the “novices”—they needed to be shown, in tangible terms, what HP’s technology could do for them. Then there were HP’s employees, particularly the engineers. They were far from novices. Many engineers had been skeptical about the value of Yamashita’s demos. Once the exhibit opened, however, it produced tremendous enthusiasm within HP. It was initially intended to stay up long enough to make the Disney pitch, but, because it was so popular, it remained for three or four months afterward. One observer said, “It became very viral in that others began to ask, ‘Did you see that great thing that the labs team did? Did you know that we could do this? Did you know that they did it in only twenty-eight days?’”

具体性帮助这支专家团队协调合作。这群背景各异的工程师，习惯于思考棘手的技术难题，突然间与法拉利一家面对面。通过应对这个家庭的具体需求——他们的车票、预订和照片——他们完成了一项非凡的壮举：他们将实验室里抽象的概念转化为一张全家福，记录了他们乘坐过山车的场景。

Concreteness helped this team of experts coordinate. A diverse group of engineers, accustomed to contemplating difficult technology problems, suddenly came face-to-face with the Ferrari family. By grappling with one family’s concrete needs—their tickets and reservations and photos—they did something remarkable: They took abstract ideas from their research labs and turned them into a family picture on a roller-coaster ride.

混凝土带来知识：

白色事物

Concrete Brings Knowledge to Bear:

White Things

拿一支铅笔和一张纸，并想办法计时（比如用手表计时，或者让喜欢数数的伴侣帮忙）。这里有一个关于具体性的自测题。你需要完成两个简短的十五秒练习。准备好材料后，将计时器设置为十五秒，然后按照下面的步骤1操作。

Grab a pencil and a piece of paper and find a way to time yourself (a watch, a spouse who likes to count, etc.). Here is a do-it-yourself test on concreteness. You’ll do two brief fifteen-second exercises. When you’ve got your supplies ready, set your timer for fifteen seconds, then follow the instructions for Step 1 below.

步骤 1 说明：

STEP 1 INSTRUCTIONS:

把你所能想到的所有白色物品都写下来。

Write down as many things that are white in color as you can think of.

停。将计时器重置为十五秒。

STOP. Reset your timer for fifteen seconds.

步骤 2 说明：

STEP 2 INSTRUCTIONS:

把你冰箱里所有你能想到的白色物品都写下来。

Write down as many white things in your refrigerator as you can think of.

令人惊讶的是，大多数人能列举出的冰箱里白色物品的数量，几乎和任何白色物品的数量一样多。这个结果令人震惊，因为，嗯，我们的冰箱里的东西毕竟只占宇宙中很小的一部分。即使是那些能列举出更多白色物品的人，也常常觉得冰箱测试“更容易”。

Most people, remarkably, can list about as many white things from their refrigerators as white anythings. This result is stunning because, well, our fridges don’t include a particularly large part of the universe. Even people who list more white anythings often feel that the refrigerator test is “easier.”

为什么会这样呢？因为具体化能够调动和集中你的大脑。为了更好地理解这种现象，请思考以下两个问题：（1）想想过去十年里人们在世界上做过的五件蠢事。（2）想想过去十年里你的孩子做过的五件蠢事。

Why does this happen? Because concreteness is a way of mobilizing and focusing your brain. For another example of this phenomenon, consider these two statements: (1) Think of five silly things that people have done in the world in the past ten years. (2) Think about five silly things your child has done in the past ten years.

没错，这的确是个巧妙的思维技巧。但它有什么价值呢？试想一下，如果一位企业家运用这种巧妙的思维技巧，从一群精明老练的投资者那里获得了 450 万美元的投资，会是什么情况？

Sure, this is a neat brain trick. But what value does it have? Consider a situation where an entrepreneur used this neat brain trick to earn a $4.5 million investment from a savvy and sophisticated group of investors.

Kaplan 和 Go Computers

Kaplan and Go Computers

对于创业者来说，有机会向当地的风险投资家推介商业计划意义非凡，就像一个崭露头角的演员获得了与独立电影导演的试镜机会。但有机会向硅谷最负盛名的公司——凯鹏华盈（Kleiner Perkins）推介，则更像是与史蒂文·斯皮尔伯格进行一对一的私人试镜。你可能一举成名，也可能错失人生中最重要的一次机会。

For an entrepreneur, having the chance to pitch a business idea to local venture capitalists is a big deal, like a budding actor getting an audition with an independent film director. But having a chance to pitch an idea to Kleiner Perkins—the most prestigious firm in Silicon Valley—is more like a private one-on-one audition with Steven Spielberg. You could walk out a star, or you could walk out having blown the biggest chance of your life.

这就是为什么1987年初，29岁的杰瑞·卡普兰站在凯鹏华盈办公室里时会感到紧张的原因。他的演讲还有大约30分钟就要开始了。卡普兰曾是斯坦福大学的研究员，后来辞职加入了莲花公司，参与了该公司早期的发展。莲花公司凭借其畅销的Lotus 1-2-3电子表格软件，成为了股市的宠儿。现在，卡普兰已经准备好迎接下一个挑战。他设想开发出更小巧、更便携的新一代个人电脑。

And that’s why twenty-nine-year-old Jerry Kaplan was nervous as he stood in the Kleiner Perkins office in early 1987. His presentation would start in about thirty minutes. Kaplan was a former researcher at Stanford who had quit to work at Lotus in its early days. Lotus, with its bestselling Lotus 1-2-3 spreadsheet, became a stock market darling. Now Kaplan was ready for the next challenge. He had a vision for a smaller, more portable generation of personal computers.

上一位企业家结束演讲时，他一直在会议室外徘徊。看着对方，他觉得自己准备不足。随着观察的深入，他的紧张感逐渐演变成恐慌。对方穿着深色细条纹西装，系着一条红色领带。卡普兰则穿着运动夹克，内搭一件敞领衬衫。对方正在白板上投影一张令人印象深刻的彩色图表。卡普兰手里拿着一个酒红色文件夹，里面装着一本空白的记事本。这预示着情况不妙。

He hung around outside the conference room as the previous entrepreneur finished his presentation. Watching the other entrepreneur, he felt underprepared. As he observed, his nervousness advanced toward panic. The other entrepreneur wore a dark pin-striped suit with a red power tie. Kaplan had on a sport jacket with an open-collared shirt. The other entrepreneur was projecting an impressive color graph onto the whiteboard. Kaplan was carrying a maroon portfolio with a blank pad of paper inside. This did not bode well.

卡普兰原本以为自己只是来参加一个非正式的“互相了解”的会议，但站在那里，他才意识到自己有多么天真。他“没有商业计划，没有幻灯片，没有图表，没有财务预测，也没有原型”。更糟糕的是，这位在会议室里准备得天衣无缝的创业者，面对的是一群充满怀疑的听众，他们正向他抛出一连串尖锐的问题。

Kaplan had thought that he was showing up for an informal “get to know you” session, but, standing there, he realized how naive he’d been. He had “no business plan, no slides, no charts, no financial projections, no prototypes.” Worst of all, the überprepared entrepreneur in the boardroom was facing a skeptical audience that now peppered him with tough questions.

轮到卡普兰发言时，一位合伙人向他做了介绍。卡普兰深吸一口气，开始说道：“我相信，一种新型电脑，更像笔记本而不是打字机，用笔而不是键盘操作，将满足我们这些专业人士在离开办公桌时的需求。我们将用它来记笔记，通过手机连接收发信息；查找地址、电话号码、价目表和库存；进行电子表格计算；以及填写订单。”

When Kaplan’s turn arrived, one of the partners introduced him. Kaplan took a deep breath and started: “I believe that a new type of computer, more like a notebook than a typewriter, and operated by a pen rather than a keyboard, will serve the needs of professionals like ourselves when we are away from our desks. We will use them to take notes, send and receive messages through cellular telephone links; look up addresses, phone numbers, price lists, and inventories; do spreadsheet calculations; and fill out order forms.”

他介绍了所需的技术，并着重强调了最大的未知数：机器能否可靠地识别手写内容并将其转换为指令。卡普兰随后讲述了接下来发生的事情：

He covered the required technology, highlighting the major unknown: whether a machine could reliably recognize handwriting and convert it into commands. Kaplan recounts what happened next:

我的听众似乎很紧张。我分不清他们是对我准备不足感到恼火，还是仅仅在认真听我讲话……心想反正已经搞砸了，也没什么可失去的了，于是我决定冒险表演一下。

My audience seemed tense. I couldn’t tell whether they were annoyed by my lack of preparation or merely concentrating on what I was saying…. Thinking I had already blown it, and therefore had little to lose, I decided to risk some theatrics.

“如果我现在带着一台便携式电脑，你肯定会……”他当然知道。你可能没意识到，我手里拿着的正是未来计算技术的模型。

“If I were carrying a portable PC right now, you would sure as hell know it. You probably didn’t realize that I am holding a model of the future of computing right here in my hands.”

我把酒红色皮套抛向空中。它飞到桌子中央，重重地摔在地上，发出清脆的啪嗒声。

I tossed my maroon leather case in the air. It sailed to the center of the table where it landed with a loud clap.

“先生们，这就是计算机革命下一步发展的一个模型。”

“Gentlemen, here is a model of the next step in the computer revolution.”

那一刻，我心想这最后的举动会不会让我被赶出房间。他们呆呆地坐在那里，一言不发，盯着我那平放在桌上的普通皮质文件夹——仿佛它随时都会活过来似的。布鲁克·拜尔斯，这位看起来很年轻但资历很老的合伙人，缓缓伸出手，轻轻触碰着文件夹，仿佛那是什么护身符。他问出了第一个问题。

For a moment, I thought this final act of drama might get me thrown out of the room. They were sitting in stunned silence, staring at my plain leather folder—which lay motionless on the table—as though it were suddenly going to come to life. Brook Byers, the youthful-looking but long-time partner in the firm, slowly reached out and touched the portfolio as if it were some sort of talisman. He asked the first question.

“像这样的东西到底能存储多少信息？”

“Just how much information could you store in something like this?”

约翰·多尔（另一位合伙人）在我回答之前就接话了。“这无关紧要。内存芯片每年都在变得更小更便宜，而且在尺寸和价格不变的情况下，容量可能会每年翻一番。”

John Doerr [another partner] answered before I could respond. “It doesn’t matter. Memory chips are getting smaller and cheaper each year and the capacity will probably double for the same size and price annually.”

另一个人插话道：“但约翰，请记住，除非你能高效地翻译手写文本，否则它很可能会占用更多空间。” 说话的是Sun Microsystems的创始首席执行官维诺德·科斯拉，他曾帮助该合作项目评估技术交易。

Someone else chimed in. “But bear in mind, John, that unless you translate the handwriting efficiently, it’s likely to take up a lot more room.” The speaker was Vinod Khosla, the founding CEO of Sun Microsystems, who helped the partnership evaluate technology deals.

卡普兰说，从那时起，他几乎不用说话了，合伙人和同事们就互相提问、分享见解，完善了他的提案。他表示，时不时会有人伸手触摸或查看他的作品集。“它神奇地从文具店里的一个小摆设变成了科技未来的象征。”

Kaplan said that from that point on he hardly had to speak, as partners and associates traded questions and insights that fleshed out his proposal. Periodically, he said, someone would reach out to touch or examine his portfolio. “It had been magically transformed from a stationery-store accessory into a symbol of the future of technology.”

几天后，卡普兰接到了凯鹏华盈的电话。合伙人决定支持他的想法。他们的投资使卡普兰这家当时还不存在的公司估值450万美元。

A few days later, Kaplan got a call from Kleiner Perkins. The partners had decided to back the idea. Their investment valued Kaplan’s nonexistent company at $4.5 million.

是什么让这场会议从一场“拷问式”的质询——一位焦虑的创业者坐在“热座”上——转变为一场头脑风暴？答案是那份深红色的作品集。作品集给与会者提出了挑战——它促使他们集中精力，并将已有的知识运用到实际工作中。它改变了他们的态度，从被动和批判转变为主动和创新。

What transformed this meeting from a grill session—with an anxious entrepreneur in the hot seat—to a brainstorming session? The maroon portfolio. The portfolio presented a challenge to the boardroom participants—a way of focusing their thoughts and bringing their existing knowledge to bear. It changed their attitude from reactive and critical to active and creative.

投资组合的存在让风险投资家们更容易进行头脑风暴，就像我们专注于“冰箱里的白色食物”更容易进行头脑风暴一样。当他们看到投资组合的规模时，一些问题随之而来：这东西能装下多少内存？未来几年哪些电脑组件会缩小，哪些不会？需要发明哪些新技术才能使其可行？索尼日本工程团队也因为“口袋收音机”的概念而产生了同样的思考过程。

The presence of the portfolio made it easier for the venture capitalists to brainstorm, in the same way that focusing on “white things in our refrigerator” made it easier for us to brainstorm. When they saw the size of the portfolio, it sparked certain questions: How much memory could you fit in that thing? Which PC components will shrink in the next few years, and which won’t? What new technology would have to be invented to make it feasible? This same process was sparked in Sony’s Japanese engineering team by the concept of a “pocketable radio.”

具体性创造了一个共享的“空间”，人们可以在此基础上进行协作。房间里的每个人都感到安心，因为他们正在应对同一个挑战。即使是专家——即使是像凯鹏华盈这样的风险投资家，科技界的明星人物——也能从具体的讨论中受益，因为这让他们找到了共同点。

Concreteness creates a shared “turf” on which people can collaborate. Everybody in the room feels comfortable that they’re tackling the same challenge. Even experts—even the Kleiner Perkins venture capitalists, the rock stars of the technology world—benefit from concrete talk that puts them on common ground.

诊所

CLINIC

口服补液疗法拯救儿童生命！

Oral Rehydration Therapy Saves Children’s Lives!

现状： 每年全球有超过一百万儿童因腹泻导致脱水而死亡。其实，只要给孩子们补充合适的液体，就能以极低的成本预防这个问题。如何才能让人们接受并支持这一理念呢？

THE SITUATION: Each year more than a million children in countries around the world die from dehydration caused by diarrhea. This problem can be prevented, at very low cost, by getting kids the right kind of fluids. How do you get people invested in this idea?

• • •

• • •

信息 1：以下是 PSI（一家致力于解决发展中国家健康问题的非营利组织）的解释：

MESSAGE 1: Here’s an explanation from PSI, a nonprofit group that addresses health problems in developing countries:

腹泻是发展中国家幼儿死亡的主要原因之一，每年导致超过150万儿童死亡。腹泻本身并非致死原因，而是脱水，即体液流失。人体约四分之三由水组成，如果体液流失超过体液总量的百分之十，重要器官就会衰竭，最终导致死亡。如果病情严重，例如霍乱，甚至可能在短短八小时内死亡。

Diarrhea is one of the leading killers of young children in developing countries, causing over 1.5 million child deaths annually. Diarrhea itself is not the cause of death, but rather dehydration, the loss of body fluid. Approximately three quarters of the body is composed of water, and if fluid loss exceeds ten percent of total body fluid, vital organs collapse, followed by death. If an episode is severe, as with cholera, death can occur within just eight hours.

为防止危及生命的脱水，必须增加液体摄入量，以补​​充腹泻流失的体液和电解质。最佳的补充液体是电解质、糖和水的混合物，称为口服补液盐（ORS）。与其他任何液体相比，ORS 能更快地恢复体液和电解质，即使肠壁因疾病受损也能发挥作用。

To prevent life-threatening dehydration it is necessary to increase liquid intake in quantities sufficient to replenish the fluids and electrolytes lost with diarrhea. The best liquid for this purpose is a blend of electrolytes, sugar, and water, known as oral rehydration salts. ORS restores body fluid and electrolytes more rapidly than any other liquid, and does so even when the intestinal wall is compromised by disease.

对第一条信息的评论：快速回答：这个问题有多容易解决？假设你是一个发展中国家的卫生官员，你明天会采取什么措施来拯救儿童？

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 1: Quick: How solvable is this problem? Suppose you were a health official in a developing nation. What would you do tomorrow to start saving kids?

平心而论，这条信息出现在一个介绍PSI为解决这个问题所做工作的网页上。但这段文字并不一定反映出该组织会如何与决策者沟通并说服他们采取行动。信息的语言力求可信，运用了大量的科学术语和阐述。然而，如果问题听起来过于复杂，反而可能会阻碍人们尝试解决它。

To be fair, this message appears on a Web page that describes what PSI has been doing to solve this problem. The text doesn’t necessarily reflect how the organization might approach decision-makers to persuade them to act. The information is written in language that creates credibility; there is lots of scientific language and exposition. If the problem sounds too complex, however, that could deter people from trying to solve it.

• • •

• • •

信息2：这条信息来自詹姆斯·格兰特，他曾担任联合国儿童基金会主任多年。格兰特出行时总是带着一个装满资料的信封。一茶匙盐和八茶匙糖——这是口服补液疗法（ORT）的配方，与一升水混合即可。当他会见发展中国家的总理时，他会拿出这包盐和糖，说道：“你们知道吗？这比一杯茶还便宜，却能拯救你们国家成千上万儿童的生命！”

MESSAGE 2: This message is from James Grant, who was the director of UNICEF for many years. Grant always traveled with a packet filled with one teaspoon of salt and eight teaspoons of sugar—the ingredients for Oral Rehydration Therapy (ORT) when mixed with a liter of water. When he met with the prime ministers of developing countries, he would take out his packet of salt and sugar and say, “Do you know that this costs less than a cup of tea and it can save hundreds of thousands of children’s lives in your country?”

关于第二条信息的评论：快速思考：这个问题有多容易解决？明天你打算做些什么来拯救这些孩子的生命？格兰特的信息让你参与进来，帮助你运用你的知识。也许你正在集思广益，想办法把盐/糖包送到学校。也许你正在考虑开展宣传活动，教妈妈们正确的盐糖比例。

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 2: Quick: How solvable is this problem? What are you going to do tomorrow to start saving these children’s lives? Grant’s message brings you to the table, helps you bring your knowledge to bear. Maybe you’re brainstorming ways of getting salt/sugar packets to schools. Maybe you’re thinking about publicity campaigns to teach mothers the right ratio of salt and sugar.

格兰特显然是一位让观点深入人心的大师。他拿出一个具体的道具，并以一个引人注目的反差开场：这包东西比一杯茶还便宜，却能产生真正的影响。首相们花费大量时间思考复杂精细的社会问题——建设基础设施、建造医院、维护健康的环境——而现在，一包盐和糖就能拯救成千上万的孩子。

Grant is clearly a master of making ideas stick. He brings out a concrete prop and starts with an attention-grabbing unexpected contrast: This packet costs less than a cup of tea, but it can have a real impact. Prime ministers spend their time thinking about elaborate, complex social problems—building infrastructure, constructing hospitals, maintaining a healthy environment—and suddenly here’s a bag of salt and sugar that can save hundreds of thousands of children.

格兰特的演讲确实牺牲了那些能增强PSI信息可信度的统计数据和科学描述。但是，作为联合国儿童基金会的负责人，他拥有足够的威望，足以让人们对他所陈述的事实不加质疑。因此，格兰特放弃了（毫无争议的）事实之争，转而投入到激励之战中。他那包“盐和糖”就如同卡普兰在风险投资演讲中展示的深红色投资组合：它能帮助听众运用自身的专业知识来分析问题。你不可能看到它而不开始集思广益，探讨各种可能性。

Grant’s message does sacrifice the statistics and the scientific description that add credibility to the PSI message. But, as the director of UNICEF, he had enough credibility to keep people from questioning his facts. So Grant left the (uncontested) factual battle behind and fought the motivational battle. His bag of salt and sugar is the equivalent of Kaplan’s maroon portfolio in the venture-capital presentation: It helps the members of the audience bring their expertise to the problem. You can’t see it and not start brainstorming about the possibilities.

记分卡
检查清单 消息 1    消息 2
简单的 -   
意外    -   
具体的 -   
可信的     -
情绪    
故事 -    -

重点：这家诊所是我们书中最喜欢的改造前后对比案例之一，因为它展现了一个具体想法的力量。其寓意在于，要找到某种方式邀请人们参与进来，帮助他们运用自身的知识。在这里，一个实物比科学描述更有效。

PUNCH LINE: This Clinic is one of our favorite before-and-after examples in the book, because it shows how powerful a concrete idea can be. The moral is to find some way to invite people to the table, to help them bring their knowledge to bear. Here, a prop works better than a scientific description.

将想法具体化

Making Ideas Concrete

我们如何才能构思出更具体的宣传信息？如果我们能以特定人群的需求为导向——例如我们的读者、学生和客户——我们或许会发现，做出决策会更容易。

How do we move toward concrete ideas for our own messages? We might find our own decisions easier to make if they are guided by the needs of specific people: our readers, our students, our customers.

通用磨坊是全球最大的消费品制造商之一。旗下品牌包括 Pillsbury、Cheerios、Green Giant、Betty Crocker、Chex 等众多知名品牌。从销售额来看，汉堡助手（Hamburger Helper）是该公司最大的品牌之一。来自密歇根州的 28 岁女孩梅丽莎·斯图津斯基 (Melissa Studzinski) 于 2004 年加入通用磨坊，担任汉堡助手品牌经理。

General Mills is one of the world’s largest manufacturers of consumer products. Its brands include Pillsbury, Cheerios, Green Giant, Betty Crocker, Chex, and many others. One of the largest brands in the company, from a sales perspective, is Hamburger Helper. Melissa Studzinski, a twenty-eight-year-old from Michigan, joined General Mills in 2004 as Hamburger Helper’s brand manager.

她加入团队时，汉堡助手（Hamburger Helper）已经陷入了长达十年的低迷期。首席执行官对这种下滑趋势感到沮丧，并宣布他2005年的首要目标是扭转局面并发展壮大汉堡助手。Helper品牌。团队新成员斯图津斯基渴望迎接挑战。

When she joined the team, Hamburger Helper had been in a decade-long slump. The CEO, frustrated by the decline, announced that his number one goal for 2005 was to fix and grow the Hamburger Helper brand. Studzinski, the newest person on the team, was eager to tackle the challenge.

她刚入职时，拿到了三大本厚厚的活页夹，里面全是数据和统计资料：销售和销量数据、广告策略简报、产品信息以及品牌客户市场调研报告。这些活页夹厚得让人难以翻阅，更别提记住了。她管它们叫“死亡活页夹”。

When she started the job, she was given three huge binders full of data and stats: sales and volume data, advertising-strategy briefs, product information, and market research on the brand’s customers. The binders were difficult to pick up, let alone absorb into memory. She called them the “death binders.”

几个月后，斯图津斯基的团队决定暂时搁置数据，尝试一些新方法。他们计划派遣汉堡助手团队的成员——包括市场营销、广告和研发人员——走进汉堡助手顾客的家中。这个想法被非正式地称为“指尖追踪”，因为通用磨坊的员工需要随时掌握品牌顾客的状况。

A few months later, Studzinski’s team decided to put the data aside and try something new. They made plans to send members of the Hamburger Helper team—marketing, advertising, and R & D staffers—out into the homes of Hamburger Helper customers. The idea was known informally as “Fingertips,” because the General Mills employees needed to have a picture of the brand’s customers at their fingertips.

他们向妈妈们发出邀请（汉堡助手的主要客户群体），希望她们愿意让陌生人进屋，在她们做饭时旁观。团队走访了二三十户人家。斯图津斯基去了三户，这段经历让她印象深刻。“我读过关于我们客户的所有数据，而且能背诵出来，”她说，“我对他们的背景信息了如指掌。但走进顾客的家，亲身感受她们的生活，却是截然不同的体验。我永远不会忘记一位女士，她一边抱着蹒跚学步的孩子，一边在炉子上准备晚餐。我们知道‘便捷’是我们产品的重要属性，但亲眼目睹人们对便捷的需求，又是另一回事。”

A call went out for mothers (the predominant customers of Hamburger Helper) who were willing to let strangers come into their homes and gawk at them while they cooked. The team visited two to three dozen homes. Studzinski visited three homes, and the experience stuck with her. “I had read and I could recite all the data about our customers,” she says. “I knew their demographics by heart. But it was a very different experience to walk into a customer’s home and experience a little bit of her life. I’ll never forget one woman, who had a toddler on her hip while she was mixing up dinner on the stove. We know that ‘convenience’ is an important attribute of our product, but it’s a different thing to see the need for convenience firsthand.”

最重要的是，斯图津斯基发现妈妈和孩子们都非常重视可预测性。汉堡助手（Hamburger Helper）有十一种不同的意面形状，但孩子们并不在意形状。他们真正关心的是口味，而妈妈们只想买到孩子们不会拒绝的、口味固定的产品。然而，汉堡助手却有三十多种口味，妈妈们很难在琳琅满目的超市货架上找到自己喜欢的口味。食品饮料公司不断推出新口味和新包装，但斯图津斯基必须抵制这种趋势。“妈妈们认为新口味是有风险的，”她说。

Most of all, Studzinski learned that moms and their kids really valued predictability. Hamburger Helper had eleven different pasta shapes, but kids didn’t care about different shapes. What they did care about was flavor, and moms just wanted to buy the same predictable flavor their kids wouldn’t reject. But Hamburger Helper had more than thirty different flavors, and moms struggled to find their favorites among the massive grocery-store displays. Food and beverage companies constantly push to develop new flavors and packages, but Studzinski needed to resist this push. “Moms saw new flavors as risky,” she says.

利用这些关于妈妈和孩子的具体信息，团队说服了公司内部各个部门——从供应链和生产制造到财务——的众多人员，共同简化产品线。据斯图津斯基称，成本节省“非常可观”，而且妈妈们也更开心了，因为她们更容易在超市货架上找到家人喜爱的产品。简化产品线的洞察，以及其他关于定价和广告的关键洞察，促成了该品牌的扭亏为盈。2005财年末，汉堡助手（Hamburger Helper）的销售额增长了11%。

Using this concrete information about moms and kids, the team convinced a diverse collection of people across the organization—in groups ranging from supply chain and manufacturing to finance—to simplify the product line. According to Studzinski, the cost savings were “huge,” yet moms were happier because it was easier to find their families’ favorites on grocery stores shelves. The insight to simplify the product line—along with other key insights concerning pricing and advertising—sparked a turnaround for the brand. At the end of fiscal year 2005, Hamburger Helper’s sales had increased 11 percent.

斯图津斯基说：“现在，每当我要对品牌做出决定时，我都会想起我遇到的那些女性。我会想，如果她们处在我的位置，她们会怎么做。这种思考方式真的很有帮助。”

Studzinski says, “Now when I’ve got a decision to make about the brand, I think of the women I met. I wonder what they would do if they were in my shoes. And it’s amazing how helpful it is to think that way.”

T同样的哲学理念也适用于更超越性的思想。马鞍峰教会是加州尔湾郊区一间非常成功的教会，拥有超过5万名成员。多年来，教会的领袖们对他们想要接触的人群进行了细致的描绘。他们称他为“马鞍峰山姆”。以下是马鞍峰教会牧师里克·沃伦对他的描述：

The same philosophy is just as useful for ideas that are more transcendent. The Saddleback Church is a very successful church in a suburb of Irvine, California, that has grown to more than 50,000 members. Over the years, the church’s leaders have created a detailed picture of the kind of person they’re trying to reach. They call him “Saddleback Sam.” Here’s how Rick Warren, the minister of the Saddleback Church, describes him:

萨德尔贝克·萨姆是我们这片地区典型的非教会人士。他三十多岁或四十出头，拥有大学学位，或许还有更高学位……他与萨德尔贝克·萨曼莎结婚，育有两个孩子，史蒂夫和萨莉。

Saddleback Sam is the typical unchurched man who lives in our area. His age is late thirties or early forties. He has a college degree and may have an advanced degree…. He is married to Saddleback Samantha, and they have two kids, Steve and Sally.

调查显示，萨姆喜欢他的工作，喜欢他居住的地方，而且他认为自己现在的生活比五年前更幸福。他对自己的生活状态感到满意，甚至有些自满。他要么是专业人士，要么是经理，要么是成功的企业家。

Surveys show that Sam likes his job, he likes where he lives, and he thinks he’s enjoying life more now than he was five years ago. He’s self-satisfied, even smug, about his station in life. He’s either a professional, a manager, or a successful entrepreneur.

山姆的另一个重要特点是他对所谓的“有组织的”宗教持怀疑态度。他可能会说：“我信耶稣，但我就是不喜欢有组织的宗教。”

… Another important characteristic of Sam is that he’s skeptical of what he calls “organized” religion. He’s likely to say, “I believe in Jesus. I just don’t like organized religion.”

这份人物简介更加深入地介绍了：萨姆和萨曼莎对流行文化的品味、他们对社交活动的偏好等等。

The profile goes into much greater depth: Sam and Samantha’s tastes in pop culture, their preferences about social events, and so on.

“马鞍峰山姆”对教会领袖有何意义？山姆迫使他们以不同的视角看待自己的决策。假设有人提议向当地社区居民开展电话营销活动。这听起来似乎很有潜力接触到新信徒。但领袖们通过调查得知，山姆非常讨厌电话推销员，于是这个想法就被否决了。

What does “Saddleback Sam” accomplish for church leaders? Sam forces them to view their decisions through a different lens. Say someone proposes a telemarketing campaign to local community members. It sounds as if it has great potential to reach new people. But the leaders know from their research that Sam hates telemarketers, so the idea is scratched.

想到马鞍峰教会的萨姆和萨曼莎，并非只有教会领袖才会想到。马鞍峰教会里有数百个小型事工：小学课程、母亲节外出活动、男子篮球联赛等等。所有这些事工都由志愿者成员带领，他们不接受教会工作人员的日常指导。但这些不同的事工之所以能够协同运作，是因为教会里的每个人都知道他们的目标群体。“我们大多数成员都能轻松地描述出萨姆的形象，”沃伦说。

And thinking about Saddleback Sam and Samantha isn’t limited to church leaders. There are hundreds of small ministries at the Saddleback Church: grade school classes, Mother’s Day Out programs, a men’s basketball league. All are led by volunteer members who don’t receive day-to-day direction from paid church staff. But these diverse programs work together because people throughout the church know whom they’re trying to reach. “Most of our members would have no trouble describing Sam,” Warren says.

通过让马鞍峰教会的萨姆和萨曼莎成为教会成员心中鲜活、具体、具体的形象，教会成功地接触到了 50,000 个真实的萨姆和萨曼莎。

By making Saddleback Sam and Samantha a living, breathing, concrete presence in the minds of the members of the Saddleback Church, the church has managed to reach 50,000 real Sams and Samanthas.

本书探讨了粘性的六个特征，其中具体性或许是最容易理解和接受的，也可能是最有效的。

Of the six traits of stickiness that we review in this book, concreteness is perhaps the easiest to embrace. It may also be the most effective of the traits.

化繁为简——找到我们的核心信息——其实相当困难。（当然，这值得付出努力，但我们也不要自欺欺人地认为这很容易。）以出人意料的方式构思我们的想法需要相当多的精力和创造力。但具体化并不难，也不需要花费太多精力。真正的障碍在于遗忘——我们忘记了自己正在滑入抽象的语言。我们忘记了其他人并不了解我们所知道的。我们就像那些不断翻看图纸的工程师，却没注意到装配工人只是想让我们跟着他们下到生产车间。

To be simple—to find our core message—is quite difficult. (It’s certainly worth the effort, but let’s not kid ourselves that it’s easy.) Crafting our ideas in an unexpected way takes a fair amount of effort and applied creativity. But being concrete isn’t hard, and it doesn’t require a lot of effort. The barrier is simply forgetfulness—we forget that we’re slipping into abstractspeak. We forget that other people don’t know what we know. We’re the engineers who keep flipping back to our drawings, not noticing that the assemblers just want us to follow them down to the factory floor.



可信的



CREDIBLE

哦人一生中，十分之一的人会患上溃疡。十二指肠溃疡是最常见的溃疡类型，几乎不会致命，但会非常疼痛。长期以来，溃疡的病因一直是个谜。传统观点认为，溃疡是由于胃酸过多积聚，腐蚀胃壁而形成的。人们认为，压力、辛辣食物或大量饮酒都可能导致胃酸过多。由于没有明确的“治愈”溃疡的方法，传统的溃疡治疗主要侧重于缓解疼痛症状。

Over the course of a lifetime, one person in ten will develop an ulcer. Duodenal ulcers, the most common type, are almost never fatal, but they are extremely painful. For a long time, the cause of ulcers was a mystery. Conventional wisdom held that ulcers developed when surplus acid built up in the stomach, eating through the stomach wall. Such surplus acid could be caused, it was thought, by stress, spicy foods, or lots of alcohol. Ulcer treatments traditionally focused on mitigating the painful symptoms, since there was no clear way to “cure” an ulcer.

20世纪80年代初，两位来自澳大利亚珀斯的医学研究人员做出了一个惊人的发现：溃疡是由细菌引起的。这两位研究人员巴里·马歇尔和罗宾·沃伦发现，罪魁祸首是一种微小的螺旋状细菌。（后来它被命名为幽门螺杆菌，简称H. pylori。）这项发现意义重大：如果溃疡是由细菌引起的，那么它们就可以治愈。事实上，只需使用抗生素进行简单的治疗，几天之内就能治愈溃疡。

In the early 1980s, two medical researchers from Perth, Australia, made an astonishing discovery: Ulcers are caused by bacteria. The researchers, Barry Marshall and Robin Warren, identified a tiny spiral-shaped type of bacteria as the culprit. (It would later be named Helicobacter pylori, or H. pylori.) The significance of this discovery was enormous: If ulcers were caused by bacteria, they could be cured. In fact, they could be cured within a matter of days by a simple treatment with antibiotics.

然而，医学界却并未为此欢欣鼓舞。马歇尔和沃伦几乎凭借一己之力取得了突破性进展，但医学界却并未为此庆祝。改善了数亿人的健康状况。之所以没有获得赞誉，原因很简单：没人相信他们。

The medical world, however, did not rejoice. There were no celebrations for Marshall and Warren, who had almost single-handedly improved the health prospects of several hundred million human beings. The reason for the lack of acclaim was simple: No one believed them.

关于细菌在胃里生存的说法存在几个问题。首先，这违背了常识。胃酸的腐蚀性极强——它显然能腐蚀厚牛排，而且（虽然不太明显）它甚至能溶解钉子。认为细菌能在这种环境下生存简直荒谬至极。这就像在撒哈拉沙漠里偶然发现一个冰屋一样荒唐。

There were several problems with the bacteria story. The first problem was common sense. The acid in the stomach is potent stuff—it can, obviously, eat through a thick steak, and it’s (less obviously) strong enough to dissolve a nail. It was ludicrous to think that bacteria could survive in such an environment. It would be like stumbling across an igloo in the Sahara.

第二个问题在于发现者。发现之时，罗宾·沃伦是珀斯一家医院的病理学家；巴里·马歇尔当时是一位三十岁的内科住院医师，甚至还不是医生。医学界普遍认为，重要的发现应该来自研究型大学的博士或世界一流大型医疗中心的教授。内科医生无法治愈那些影响全球10%人口的疾病。

The second problem was the source. At the time of the discovery, Robin Warren was a staff pathologist at a hospital in Perth; Barry Marshall was a thirty-year-old internist in training, not even a doctor yet. The medical community expects important discoveries to come from Ph.D.s at research universities or professors at large, world-class medical centers. Internists do not cure diseases that affect 10 percent of the world’s population.

最后一个问题是地点。珀斯的医学研究人员就像密西西比州的物理学家一样。科学就是科学，但由于人类根深蒂固的势利眼，我们往往认为科学只会诞生于某些地方，而不会诞生于其他地方。

The final problem was the location. A medical researcher in Perth is like a physicist from Mississippi. Science is science, but, thanks to basic human snobbery, we tend to think it will emerge from some places but not others.

马歇尔和沃伦的研究论文甚至都没能被医学期刊接受。当马歇尔在一次专业会议上展示他们的研究成果时，科学家们都嗤之以鼻。一位听过他报告的研究人员评论说，他“根本没有科学家的风范”。

Marshall and Warren could not even get their research paper accepted by a medical journal. When Marshall presented their findings at a professional conference, the scientists snickered. One of the researchers who heard one of his presentations commented that he “simply didn’t have the demeanor of a scientist.”

平心而论，怀疑论者的论点也并非毫无道理：马歇尔和沃伦的证据是基于相关性而非因果关系。几乎所有溃疡患者似乎都感染了幽门螺杆菌。然而，不幸的是，也有一些人感染了幽门螺杆菌却没有患溃疡。至于证明因果关系，研究人员总不能给一群无辜的人注射细菌，然后观察他们是否会患上溃疡吧。

To be fair to the skeptics, they had a reasonable argument: Marshall and Warren’s evidence was based on correlation, not causation. Almost all of the ulcer patients seemed to have H. pylori. Unfortunately, there were also people who had H. pylori but no ulcer. And, as for proving causation, the researchers couldn’t very well dose a bunch of innocent people with bacteria to see whether they sprouted ulcers.

到了1984年，马歇尔的耐心已经耗尽。一天早上，他他没吃早饭，叫同事们到实验室来。在他们惊恐的注视下，他咕咚咕咚喝下了一杯装有大约十亿个幽门螺杆菌的杯子。“味道就像沼泽水，”他说。

By 1984, Marshall’s patience had run out. One morning he skipped breakfast and asked his colleagues to meet him in the lab. While they watched in horror, he chugged a glass filled with about a billion H. pylori. “It tasted like swamp water,” he said.

几天之内，马歇尔就出现了疼痛、恶心和呕吐——这是胃炎的典型症状，也是溃疡的早期阶段。他的同事们用内窥镜发现，他原本粉嫩健康的胃黏膜现在变得红肿发炎。神奇的是，马歇尔服用了一个疗程的抗生素和铋剂（必妥-比斯莫尔的活性成分）后，竟然痊愈了。

Within a few days, Marshall was experiencing pain, nausea, and vomiting—the classic symptoms of gastritis, the early stage of an ulcer. Using an endoscope, his colleagues found that his stomach lining, previously pink and healthy, was now red and inflamed. Like a magician, Marshall then cured himself with a course of antibiotics and bismuth (the active ingredient in Pepto-Bismol).

即便在这次引人注目的演示之后，争论也并未结束。其他科学家对演示结果提出了质疑。他们认为，马歇尔在溃疡完全形成之前就已经治愈了自己，所以他可能只是产生了溃疡症状，而非真正的溃疡。但马歇尔的演示为细菌理论的支持者们注入了新的活力，随后的研究也积累了越来越多的证据支持这一理论。

Even after this dramatic demonstration, the battle wasn’t over. Other scientists quibbled with the demonstration. Marshall had cured himself before he developed a full-blown ulcer, they argued, so maybe he had just generated ulcer symptoms rather than a genuine ulcer. But Marshall’s demonstration gave a second wind to supporters of the bacteria theory, and subsequent research amassed more and more evidence in its favor.

1994年，十年之后，美国国立卫生研究院最终认可了抗生素是治疗溃疡的首选方法。马歇尔和沃伦的研究为现代医学的一个重要主题做出了贡献：细菌和病毒引起的疾病比我们想象的要多得多。现在我们知道，宫颈癌是由传染性极强的人乳头瘤病毒（HPV）引起的。某些类型的心脏病与巨细胞病毒有关，这是一种常见的病毒，感染了大约三分之二的人口。

In 1994, ten years later, the National Institutes of Health finally endorsed the idea that antibiotics were the preferred treatment for ulcers. Marshall and Warren’s research contributed to an important theme in modern medicine: that bacteria and viruses cause more diseases than we would think. It is now known that cervical cancer is caused by the contagious human papillomavirus, or HPV. Certain types of heart disease have been linked to cytomegalovirus, a common virus that infects about two thirds of the population.

2005年秋天，马歇尔和沃伦因其研究成果荣获诺贝尔生理学或医学奖。这两位科学家拥有卓越的、足以获得诺贝尔奖的、改变世界的洞见。那么，马歇尔为何要通过毒害自己来让人们相信他呢？

In the fall of 2005, Marshall and Warren received the Nobel Prize in medicine for their work. These two men had a brilliant, Nobel-worthy, world-changing insight. So why did Marshall have to poison himself to get people to believe him?

寻找可信度

Finding Credibility

让我们用最宽泛的方式来提出这个问题：是什么让人们相信某些想法？这个问题够有深度吧？让我们开始吧。显而易见的答案是：我们相信是因为我们的父母或朋友相信；我们相信是因为我们有过一些经历，这些经历引导我们形成了现在的信仰；我们相信是因为我们有宗教信仰；我们相信是因为我们信任权威。

Let’s pose the question in the broadest possible terms: What makes people believe ideas? How’s that for an ambitious question? Let’s start with the obvious answers. We believe because our parents or our friends believe. We believe because we’ve had experiences that led us to our beliefs. We believe because of our religious faith. We believe because we trust authorities.

这些都是强大的力量——家庭、个人经历、信仰。值得庆幸的是，我们无法控制这些力量如何影响人们。我们不能通过别人的母亲来传递备忘录以增加可信度，也不能制作一份能够动摇人们核心信仰的PPT演示文稿。

These are powerful forces—family, personal experience, faith. And, thankfully, we have no control over the way these forces affect people. We can’t route our memos through people’s mothers to add credibility. We can’t construct a PowerPoint presentation that will nullify people’s core beliefs.

如果我们试图说服持怀疑态度的听众接受新的信息，现实情况是，我们将面临一场艰苦的战斗，因为人们终其一生都在学习和建立社会关系。似乎我们很难左右人们的信仰。但如果我们对影响他人信仰的能力持怀疑态度，只需关注那些天生具有强大影响力的观念，因为其中一些观念确实能让我们相信一些不可思议的事情。

If we’re trying to persuade a skeptical audience to believe a new message, the reality is that we’re fighting an uphill battle against a lifetime of personal learning and social relationships. It would seem that there’s nothing much we can do to affect what people believe. But if we’re skeptical about our ability to affect belief, we merely have to look at naturally sticky ideas, because some of them persuade us to believe some pretty incredible things.

大约在1999年，一封电子邮件在互联网上广泛传播，被人们转发，声称来自哥斯达黎加的香蕉感染了坏死性筋膜炎，也就是俗称的“食肉菌”。邮件警告人们在接下来的三周内不要购买香蕉，并敦促人们如果在食用香蕉后出现皮疹，立即就医！邮件还警告说：“坏死性筋膜炎引起的皮肤感染非常痛苦，每小时会侵蚀两到三厘米的果肉。截肢的可能性很大，甚至可能导致死亡。”邮件声称，美国食品药品监督管理局（FDA）不愿发布普遍警告，因为它担心会引发全国恐慌。（人们可能会认为，即使没有FDA的回应，果肉的消失也足以引起恐慌。）这则令人震惊的消息被认为是曼海姆研究所发布的。

Around 1999, an e-mail message spread over the Internet, forwarded from person to person, claiming that shipments of bananas from Costa Rica were infected with necrotizing fasciitis, otherwise known as flesh-eating bacteria. People were warned not to purchase bananas for the next three weeks, and urged to SEEK MEDICAL ATTENTION!!! if they contracted a rash after eating a banana. The e-mail also warned, “The skin infection from necrotizing fasciitis is very painful and eats two to three centimeters of flesh per hour. Amputation is likely, death is possible.” It claimed that the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) was reluctant to issue a general warning because it feared a nationwide panic. (One would think that disappearing centimeters of flesh might be sufficient to cause a panic, even in the absence of the FDA’s response.) This surprising message was attributed to the Manheim Research Institute.

这个离奇的谣言之所以传播开来，至少部分原因是它给人一种权威的感觉。它竟然是由曼海姆研究所散布的！而且美国食品药品监督管理局也知道这个问题！曼海姆研究所和美国食品药品监督管理局（FDA）被用来增强可信度。它们的权威性让我们对一些原本听起来相当不可思议的说法重新思考：坏死性筋膜炎每小时会吞噬三厘米的组织？如果这是真的，为什么晚间新闻没有报道？

This bizarre rumor spread at least in part because it had an air of authority. It was circulated by the Manheim Research Institute! And the Food and Drug Administration knew about the problem! The Manheim Research Institute and the FDA are invoked as credibility-boosters. Their authority makes us think twice about what would otherwise be some pretty incredible statements: Necrotizing fasciitis consumes three centimeters of flesh per hour? If that’s true, why isn’t the story on the evening news?

显然，有人意识到这个谣言的可信度还可以提高。后来的版本加上了“此消息已由疾病控制中心核实”。如果这个谣言流传足够长的时间，毫无疑问，它最终会“得到达赖喇嘛的认可”和“得到安理会的热烈支持”。

Evidently, someone realized that the rumor’s credibility could be improved. Later versions added, “This message has been verified by the Centers for Disease Control.” If the rumor circulated long enough, no doubt it would eventually be “approved by the Dalai Lama” and “heartily endorsed by the Security Council.”

一个正如受污染香蕉事件所表明的那样，权威机构是我们观点可信度的可靠来源。当我们想到能够提升可信度的权威人士时，我们往往会想到两类人。第一类是专家——那种墙上挂满证书的人：比如神经科学领域的奥利弗·萨克斯、经济学领域的艾伦·格林斯潘或物理学领域的斯蒂芬·霍金。

As the contaminated bananas show, authorities are a reliable source of credibility for our ideas. When we think of authorities who can add credibility, we tend to think of two kinds of people. The first kind is the expert—the kind of person whose wall is covered with framed credentials: Oliver Sachs for neuroscience, Alan Greenspan for economics, or Stephen Hawking for physics.

名人和其他具有启发意义的人物构成了第二类“权威”。我们为什么会在意迈克尔·乔丹喜欢麦当劳？他当然不是注册营养师，也不是世界级美食家。我们之所以在意，是因为我们想成为像乔丹那样的人，如果乔丹喜欢麦当劳，我们也喜欢。如果奥普拉喜欢一本书，我们就会对这本书更感兴趣。我们信任那些我们想要效仿的人的推荐。

Celebrities and other aspirational figures make up the second class of “authorities.” Why do we care that Michael Jordan likes McDonald’s? Certainly he is not a certified nutritionist or a world-class gourmet. We care because we want to be like Mike, and if Mike likes McDonald’s, so do we.
