If Oprah likes a book, it makes us more interested in that book. We trust the recommendations of people whom we want to be like.

如果你能获得斯蒂芬·霍金或迈克尔·乔丹——这些知名专家或名人——的认可，可以跳过本章的这一部分。至于我们其他人，我们还能求助于谁呢？我们能否找到不涉及名人或专家的外部可信度来源？

If you have access to the endorsement of Stephen Hawking or Michael Jordan—renowned experts or celebrities—skip this part of the chapter. As for the rest of us, whom can we call on? Can we find external sources of credibility that don’t involve celebrities or experts?

令人惊讶的是，答案是肯定的。我们可以利用反权威人士的信誉。帕姆·拉芬就是这样一位反权威人士。

The answer, surprisingly, is yes. We can tap the credibility of anti-authorities. One antiauthority was a woman named Pam Laffin.

帕姆·拉芬，反权威人士

Pam Laffin, the Antiauthority

帕姆·拉芬曾是上世纪90年代中期一系列反吸烟电视广告的代言人。拉芬既不是名人，也不是健康专家。她本人就是一名吸烟者。

Pam Laffin was the star of a series of antismoking TV ads that were broadcast in the mid-1990s. Laffin is not a celebrity and she’s not a health expert. She’s a smoker.

当时，拉芬是一位29岁的两个孩子的母亲。她从10岁开始吸烟，24岁时患上了肺气肿。她的肺移植手术失败了。

At the time, Laffin was a twenty-nine-year-old mother of two. She had started smoking at age ten and had developed emphysema by age twenty-four. She’d suffered a failed lung transplant.

马萨诸塞州公共卫生部（MDPH）烟草控制主任格雷格·康诺利负责设计一项反对吸烟的公益宣传活动。他了解到帕姆·拉芬的故事，并邀请她向公众分享自己的经历。她同意了。

Greg Connolly, the director of tobacco control for the Massachusetts Department of Public Health (MDPH), was in charge of designing a public-service campaign against smoking. He became aware of Pam Laffin and asked her to share her story with the public. She agreed.

康诺利说：“我们从以往的宣传活动中了解到，用真实人物讲述故事是最引人入胜的方式。” 马萨诸塞州公共卫生部拍摄了一系列30秒的短片，在《艾莉的异想世界》和《道森溪》等热门剧集播出期间播放。这些短片令人触目惊心。它们展现了拉芬因肺功能衰竭而窒息挣扎求生的画面。电视观众目睹了她接受侵入性支气管镜检查的过程——这项检查需要将一根末端带有摄像头的管子从口腔插入肺部。短片还展示了她背上触目惊心的手术疤痕。

Connolly said, “What we’ve learned from previous campaigns is that telling stories using real people is the most compelling way.” The MDPH filmed a series of thirty-second spots, broadcast during hip shows such as Ally McBeal and Dawson’s Creek. The spots were brutal. They showed Laffin battling to live while slowly suffocating because of her failing lungs. The TV audience watched her enduring an invasive bronchoscopy—a procedure in which a tube with a camera at the end is inserted through the mouth and pushed into the lungs. The spots showed the nasty surgical scars on her back.

在另一则广告中，展示了拉芬童年和成年时的照片，她谈到肺气肿如何让她“脸变胖”和“脖子上长了个驼峰”。她说：“我开始抽烟是为了让自己看起来更成熟，很遗憾，这招奏效了。”

In another spot, featuring photos of Laffin as a child and as an adult, she talks about how her emphysema left her with a “fat face” and “a hump on my neck.” She said, “I started smoking to look older and I’m sorry to say it worked.”

这些广告令人难以观看，与《道森溪》等轻松的肥皂剧风格形成了鲜明的对比。“我们毫不犹豫地用这种方式唤醒吸烟者，”康诺利说道。

The spots were difficult to watch, and contrasted jarringly with the light soap-opera fare of shows like Dawson’s Creek. “We have no compunction at all about shocking smokers into waking up,” Connolly said.

拉芬成为了反吸烟运动的英雄人物。MTV曾为她拍摄了一部纪录片。美国疾病控制与预防中心在反吸烟网络宣传活动和一部名为《我无法呼吸》的20分钟教育视频中讲述了她的故事。

Laffin became a heroine of the antismoking movement. She was the subject of an MTV documentary. The Centers for Disease Control features her story in an antismoking Web campaign and a twenty-minute educational video titled I Can’t Breathe.

她在 2000 年 11 月去世，年仅 31 岁，距离她第二次肺移植手术还有三周。

She died in November 2000 at the age of thirty-one, three weeks before she was scheduled for a second lung transplant.

一个听完拉芬的故事，你大概不会对她是一位优秀的代言人感到惊讶。毫无疑问，她凭借亲身经历，深知自己所言非虚。她有一个震撼人心的故事要讲述。

After hearing Laffin’s story you’re probably not surprised that she was an effective spokeswoman. There’s no question that she knew from personal experience what she was talking about. She had a powerful tale to tell.

另一个利用反权威来博取信任的例子来自纽约市的“多伊基金会”（Doe Fund）。该组织收容无家可归的男性——我们社会中的“无名氏”——并通过心理咨询、戒毒康复以及最重要的职业培训，将他们转变为对社会有贡献的公民。几年前，一些来自资助机构（潜在的资助方）的代表计划拜访“多伊基金会”的办公室。基金会派了一位名叫丹尼斯的司机去接他们，并将他们送到总部。

Another example of drawing credibility from antiauthorities comes from the Doe Fund in New York City, an organization that takes homeless men—the John Does of our society—and turns them into productive citizens through counseling, drug rehabilitation, and, most important, job training. A few years ago, some representatives from a grant organization—potential financial supporters—were going to visit the offices of the Doe Fund. The Doe Fund sent a driver, Dennis, to pick them up and drive them to the home office.

在向“多伊基金会”寻求帮助之前，丹尼斯曾无家可归。在45分钟的车程中，丹尼斯向基金会代表讲述了自己的故事。其中一位代表评论道：“我们并非只是坐在那里听一群负责人吹嘘他们的服务有多么有效；丹尼斯是‘多伊基金会’能提供的最佳代言人——他就是活生生的例子。” “多伊基金会”在内部也运用了这一原则。每一位加入该项目的无家可归者都会被安排一位导师，这位导师两年前也曾身处同样的困境。

Dennis had been homeless before he turned to the Doe Fund for help. During the forty-five-minute car trip, Dennis shared his story with the grant representatives. One commented, “We weren’t just sitting around listening to a bunch of directors telling us how effective their services are; Dennis was the best ambassador that the Doe Fund could provide—he was living proof.” The Doe Fund also uses this principle internally. Every homeless man who enters the program is matched with a mentor who, two years before, was in the same situation.

值得我们记住的是，拉芬和丹尼斯能否成为有效的权威人士，这一点并非显而易见。三十年前，像拉芬这样的反吸烟运动或许根本不会出现。取而代之的是，卫生局局长会严厉训斥吸烟的危害，或者伯特·雷诺兹会大力宣扬无烟生活的种种好处。

It’s worth reminding ourselves that it wasn’t obvious that Laffin or Dennis would be effective authorities. Thirty years ago, an antismoking campaign like Laffin’s would probably not have happened. Instead, the Surgeon General would have given us a stern lecture on the dangers of smoking. Or Burt Reynolds would have extolled the virtues of a smoke-free life.

现代社会的公民不断被各种信息淹没，逐渐学会对信息的来源抱持怀疑态度。

A citizen of the modern world, constantly inundated with messages, learns to develop skepticism about the sources of those messages.

这些信息是谁发的？我应该相信他们吗？如果我相信他们，他们能得到什么好处？

Who’s behind these messages? Should I trust them? What do they have to gain if I believe them?

一则广告声称某款新洗发水能让你的头发更有弹性，其可信度远不如你闺蜜滔滔不绝地讲述某款新洗发水如何让她的头发更蓬松。这还用说吗？广告商想卖给你洗发水，而你的朋友并不想，所以她更值得信赖。由此可见，信息来源的诚实和可信度，而非其地位，才是他们能否成为权威的关键。有时，反权威人士甚至比权威人士更有价值。

A commercial claiming that a new shampoo makes your hair bouncier has less credibility than hearing your best friend rave about how a new shampoo made her own hair bouncier. Well, duh. The company wants to sell you shampoo. Your friend doesn’t, so she gets more trust points. The takeaway is that it can be the honesty and trustworthiness of our sources, not their status, that allows them to act as authorities. Sometimes antiauthorities are even better than authorities.

细节的力量

The Power of Details

我们并非总能找到外部权威来为我们的信息作证；大多数时候，信息本身就必须经得起推敲。它们必须具备“内部可信度”。当然，内部可信度往往取决于我们讨论的主题：一个可信的数学证明与一篇可信的影评截然不同。但令人惊讶的是，建立内部可信度有一些通用原则。为了更好地理解这些原则，我们可以再次从都市传说入手。

We don’t always have an external authority who can vouch for our message; most of the time our messages have to vouch for themselves. They must have “internal credibility.” Of course, internal credibility frequently depends on what topic we’re discussing: A credible math proof looks different from a credible movie review. But, surprisingly, there are some general principles for establishing internal credibility. To see these principles in action, we can again turn to urban legends.

《男友之死》是一个著名的都市传说，故事讲述的是一对情侣开着男友的车去约会。车子在一条荒凉的路上抛锚，停在一棵树下。女孩怀疑男友是为了和她亲热才装的，但很快她就​​意识到他们真的被困住了。男友决定步行去最近的房子寻求帮助，女孩则留在车里。他走了很久——感觉像是几个小时——女孩被车顶传来的令人毛骨悚然的刮擦声吓坏了，那声音可能是低垂的树枝刮蹭发出的。几个小时焦急的等待后，女孩下车，发现——恐怖音乐响起！——她的男友被谋杀了，吊死在车顶的树上。他的脚趾在风中摇晃，擦着车顶。

The Boyfriend’s Death is a famous urban legend that begins with a couple heading out on a date in the boyfriend’s car. The car runs out of gas under a tree on a deserted road. The girl suspects that the guy is faking in order to make out with her, but soon she realizes they’re really stuck. The boyfriend decides to walk to the nearest house for help, and the girl stays behind. He has been gone for a long time—it feels like hours—and the girl is frightened by a creepy scratching coming from the roof of the car, possibly the scrapings of a low-hanging tree branch. After several hours of anxious waiting, the girl gets out of the car to discover—cue the horror music!—her boyfriend, murdered and hanging from the tree above the car. His toes scrape the roof as he swings in the wind.

人们口口相传这个传说时，总会添加一些细节。它总是发生在一个特定的地点，而这个地点会随着时间而变化。在全国各地流传着这样的说法：“就发生在121号农场公路旁；”“就发生在特拉维斯湖畔的悬崖顶上。”民间传说专家简·布伦万德表示，传说的“可信度和影响力很大程度上来自于其地域性的细节”。

When people pass this legend along, they always add particular details. It’s always set in a specific location, which varies when it is told in different parts of the country: “It happened right off Farm Road 121;” “It happened right on top of that bluff over Lake Travis.” An expert on folk legends, Jan Brunvand, says that legends “acquire a good deal of their credibility and effect from their localized details.”

一个人对细节的了解程度往往能很好地反映其专业水平。想想看，一个历史爱好者只要讲述一个有趣的南北战争轶事，就能迅速建立起自己的可信度。但具体的细节不仅能增强提供这些细节的权威人士的可信度，也能增强故事本身的可信度。这个包含大量有趣细节的南北战争轶事，无论由谁讲述都更具说服力。细节使一个论断变得具体而具体，从而使其显得更加真实可信。

A person’s knowledge of details is often a good proxy for her expertise. Think of how a history buff can quickly establish her credibility by telling an interesting Civil War anecdote. But concrete details don’t just lend credibility to the authorities who provide them; they lend credibility to the idea itself. The Civil War anecdote, with lots of interesting details, is credible in anyone’s telling. By making a claim tangible and concrete, details make it seem more real, more believable.

陪审员和达斯·维达牙刷

Jurors and the Darth Vader Toothbrush

1986年，密歇根大学的研究人员乔纳森·谢德勒和梅尔文·马尼斯设计了一项模拟审判的实验。受试者被要求扮演陪审员的角色，并阅读一份（虚构的）审判记录。陪审员们需要评估一位母亲约翰逊夫人的适任性，并决定她七岁的儿子是否应该继续由她抚养。

In 1986, Jonathan Shedler and Melvin Manis, researchers at the University of Michigan, created an experiment to simulate a trial. Subjects were asked to play the role of jurors and were given the transcript of a (fictitious) trial to read. The jurors were asked to assess the fitness of a mother, Mrs. Johnson, and to decide whether her seven-year-old son should remain in her care.

庭审记录经过精心设计，力求保持平衡：针对约翰逊夫人的论点有八条，支持约翰逊夫人的论点也有八条。所有陪审员听到的论点都相同。唯一的区别在于这些论点的细节程度。在一个实验组中，所有支持约翰逊夫人的论点都包含生动的细节，而反对她的论点则没有任何额外的细节；相比之下，后者显得苍白无力。另一个实验组听到的则是相反的情况。

The transcript was constructed to be closely balanced: There were eight arguments against Mrs. Johnson and eight arguments for Mrs. Johnson. All the jurors heard the same arguments. The only difference was the level of detail in those arguments. In one experimental group, all the arguments that supported Mrs. Johnson had some vivid detail, whereas the arguments against her had no extra details; they were pallid by comparison. The other group heard the opposite combination.

例如，支持约翰逊太太的一个论点是：“约翰逊太太确保她的孩子在睡前洗漱刷牙。” 更生动的版本还补充了一个细节：“他用的是一把星战主题的牙刷，看起来像达斯·维达。”

As an example, one argument in Mrs. Johnson’s favor said: “Mrs. Johnson sees to it that her child washes and brushes his teeth before bedtime.” In the vivid form, the argument added a detail: “He uses a Star Wars toothbrush that looks like Darth Vader.”

针对约翰逊太太的一个指控是：“孩子手臂严重擦伤，约翰逊太太既没有清洗也没有处理，就去上学了。”校医不得不清理擦伤。”生动的描写还补充了一个细节：当校医清理擦伤时，她不小心把红汞洒在了自己身上，把制服染成了红色。

An argument against Mrs. Johnson was: “The child went to school with a badly scraped arm which Mrs. Johnson had not cleaned or at tended to. The school nurse had to clean the scrape.” The vivid form added the detail that, as the nurse was cleaning the scrape, she spilled Mercurochrome on herself, staining her uniform red.

研究人员仔细测试了包含和不包含生动细节的论点，以确保它们在感知上具有相同的重要性——这些细节被设计成与对约翰逊太太品行的判断无关。约翰逊太太没有处理擦伤的手臂很重要；护士制服因此沾上污渍则无关紧要。

The researchers carefully tested the arguments with and without vivid details to ensure that they had the same perceived importance—the details were designed to be irrelevant to the judgment of Mrs. Johnson’s worthiness. It mattered that Mrs. Johnson didn’t attend to the scraped arm; it didn’t matter that the nurse’s uniform got stained in the process.

尽管细节本不该重要，但它们却产生了影响。听过对约翰逊夫人有利的陈述（细节生动）的陪审员，认为她更适合做母亲（5.8分，满分10分），而听过对约翰逊夫人不利的陈述（细节4.3分，满分10分）的陪审员则认为她更适合做母亲。细节产生了巨大的影响。

But even though the details shouldn’t have mattered, they did. Jurors who heard the favorable arguments with vivid details judged Mrs. Johnson to be a more suitable parent (5.8 out of 10) than did jurors who heard the unfavorable arguments with vivid details (4.3 out of 10). The details had a big impact.

或许我们可以感到欣慰的是，结果的波动并没有更剧烈。（如果母亲的体能评分从八分降到两分，我们可能就得对司法系统感到担忧了。）但陪审员们确实基于一些无关紧要的细节做出了不同的判断。那么，这些细节为何会产生影响呢？它们增强了论点的可信度。如果我能想象出达斯·维达的牙刷，我就更容易想象出男孩在浴室里认真刷牙的画面，这反过来又强化了约翰逊太太是一位好母亲的印象。

We can take comfort, perhaps, in the fact that the swing wasn’t more dramatic. (If the mother’s fitness had dropped from eight to two, we might have had to worry a bit about our justice system.) But the jurors did make different judgments based on irrelevant vivid details. So why did the details make a difference? They boosted the credibility of the argument. If I can mentally see the Darth Vader toothbrush, it’s easier for me to picture the boy diligently brushing his teeth in the bathroom, which in turn reinforces the notion that Mrs. Johnson is a good mother.

W我们应该从都市传说和约翰逊夫人案中学到的是，生动的细节能增强可信度。但更重要的是，我们需要运用真实、核心的细节。我们需要找到那些像“达斯·维达牙刷”一样引人入胜、充满人性化的细节，但这些细节更具意义——它们能够象征并支撑我们的核心观点。

What we should learn from urban legends and the Mrs. Johnson trial is that vivid details boost credibility. But what should also be added is that we need to make use of truthful, core details. We need to identify details that are as compelling and human as the “Darth Vader toothbrush” but more meaningful—details that symbolize and support our core idea.

2004年，两位斯坦福商学院教授在华盛顿特区与艺术机构举办了一场研讨会。其中一项练习是……此次研讨会的目的是让艺术界领袖们专注于各自机构的永恒原则，这些原则在任何情况下都绝不妥协。参加研讨会的机构之一是莉兹·勒曼舞蹈交流中心（LLDE），这是一个“由舞蹈艺术家组成的团体，致力于创作、表演、教学，并鼓励人们参与艺术创作”。在研讨会上，LLDE的领导者们强调，他们的核心价值观之一是“多元化”。

In 2004, two Stanford Business School professors held a workshop with arts organizations in Washington, D.C. One exercise was de signed to make the arts leaders focus on the enduring principles of their organizations, the principles they would not compromise under any circumstances. One organization at the workshop was the Liz Lerman Dance Exchange (LLDE), “a company of dance artists that creates, performs, teaches, and engages people in making art.” At the workshop, the leaders from the LLDE maintained that one of their core values was “diversity.”

“别开玩笑了，”一位教授嗤之以鼻，怀疑其中有些夸张。“每个人都声称重视多元化，但你们只是个舞蹈团。你们的舞者大概都是二十五岁左右的，个个身材高挑纤瘦。他们当中或许有一些是有色人种，但这能算是多元化吗？” 台下其他不了解LLDE的人，对这种质疑的回应点头表示赞同。

“Come on,” scoffed one of the professors, suspecting an exaggeration. “Everyone claims that they value diversity, but you’re a dance company. You’re probably filled with a bunch of twenty-five-year-old dancers, all of them tall and thin. Some of them are probably people of color, but is that diversity?” Other people in the audience, unfamiliar with the LLDE, nodded at this skeptical response.

LLDE的艺术总监彼得·迪穆罗举例回应道：“事实上，”他说，“我们公司资历最老的成员是一位名叫托马斯·德怀尔的73岁老人。他在美国政府工作了一辈子，1988年退休后加入了LLDE，此前没有任何舞蹈经验。他现在已经在LLDE工作了17年。”

Peter DiMuro, the artistic director of the LLDE, responded with an example. “As a matter of fact,” he said, “the longest-term member of our company is a seventy-three-year-old man named Thomas Dwyer. He came to the LLDE after a full career working for the U.S. government when he retired in 1988, and had no previous dance experience. He has now been with the LLDE for seventeen years.”

这个细节——73岁的托马斯·德怀尔——打消了房间里所有人的疑虑。教授们罕见地哑口无言。

This detail—seventy-three-year-old Thomas Dwyer—silenced the skepticism in the room. The professors experienced a rare moment of speechlessness.

迪穆罗能够迅速举出生动的例子是有充分理由的。原因在于，多元化确实是LLDE的核心价值观，它已融入LLDE的组织基因之中。

And there was a good reason that DiMuro could respond quickly with a vivid example. The reason is that diversity truly is a core value at the LLDE. It’s part of the LLDE’s organizational DNA.

2002年，莉兹·勒曼凭借其在美国各地社区创作的现代舞作品，荣获麦克阿瑟“天才奖”。在名为《哈利路亚/美国》的舞蹈项目中，勒曼走访了全国各地的社区，询问居民们感恩的事情。随后，她围绕这些赞美的主题编排了舞蹈。最终的演出阵容包括当地社区的成员：明尼阿波利斯的苗族少女舞者、弗吉尼亚州的边境牧羊犬主人，以及六位正在玩纸牌的……来自佛蒙特州伯灵顿的几位女士，四十年来只缺席过两次每周的纸牌游戏。

In 2002, Liz Lerman won a MacArthur “genius grant” for her work creating modern dance involving communities throughout the United States. In a dance project called Hallelujah/U.S.A., Lerman visited communities across the country and asked residents what made them thankful. Then she choreographed dances around those themes of praise. The final performances featured members of the local community: teenage female Hmong dancers in Minneapolis, Border collie owners in Virginia, and a group of six card-playing ladies from Burlington, Vermont, who’d missed only two of their weekly card games in forty years.

现在，我要对那些翻白眼的怀疑论者们说几句，在他们看来，现代舞表演听起来就像活埋一样令人反感：不管你是否愿意在周末观看边境牧羊犬主人的扭动身姿，你都不得不承认，LLDE（可能是指某个组织或机构）确实具有多样性。这是真正的多样性，而不是那种空洞的、矫揉造作的多样性。

Now, a brief aside to the eye-rolling skeptics out there, to whom a modern dance performance sounds as appealing as being buried alive: Whether or not you’d like to spend your weekends watching the gyrations of Border collie owners, you’ve got to admit that the LLDE is diverse. It’s real diversity, not workspeak diversity.

托马斯·德怀尔（Thomas Dwyer）——这位73岁的前政府雇员——的例子生动具体地象征着该组织的核心价值观。这不仅对支持者意义重大，对舞者本身也具有象征意义。没有人愿意参与一个“舞蹈项目”，却发现自己是舞台上唯一一个秃顶的中年男人，而台下却挤满了像崔姬（Twiggy）一样的年轻人。LLDE声称多元化是其核心价值观，而德怀尔的例子本身就赋予了这一说法以可信度，而非来自外部来源。

The example of Thomas Dwyer—the seventy-three-year-old former government employee—is a vivid, concrete symbol of a core organization value. It’s a symbol both to supporters and to the dancers themselves. No one wants to participate in a “dance project” and be the only balding, middle-aged guy on a stage full of Twiggys. The LLDE’s claim that diversity was a core value gained credibility from the details of Dwyer’s example, rather than from an external source.

超越战争

Beyond War

运用生动的细节是建立内在可信度的一种方法——将可信度来源融入到观点本身。另一种方法是运用统计数据。从小学开始，我们就被教导要用统计证据来支持自己的论点。但统计数据往往枯燥乏味。我们如何在运用统计数据的同时，还能吸引听众呢？

The use of vivid details is one way to create internal credibility—to weave sources of credibility into the idea itself. Another way is to use statistics. Since grade school, we’ve been taught to support our arguments with statistical evidence. But statistics tend to be eye-glazing. How can we use them while still managing to engage our audience?

20世纪80年代，杰夫·安斯科和其他“超越战争”运动的领导人决心找到一种方法来解决以下悖论：当我们看到一个孩子拿着剪刀奔跑时，我们会感到害怕，并大声喝止她。然而，当我们读到关于核武器——这种武器有能力摧毁数百万儿童——的新闻报道时，充其量只会让我们感到一丝震惊。

Geoff Ainscow and other leaders of the Beyond War movement in the 1980s were determined to find a way to address the following paradox: When we see a child running with scissors, we wince. We shout at her to stop. Yet when we read newspaper articles about nuclear weapons—which have the power to destroy millions of children—it provokes, at best, only a moment of dismay.

“超越战争”运动是由一群对美苏军备竞赛感到震惊的公民发起的。当时，苏美两国的核武库总和足以毁灭世界数次。“超越战争”的参与者挨家挨户走访社区，希望能够动员民众……公众对军备竞赛的强烈抗议。他们苦苦思索如何才能让人们相信军备竞赛已经失控。如何才能让人们清楚地认识到世界核武库惊人的破坏力？它如此难以捉摸，如此隐形。然而，仅仅讲述故事或提供细节似乎远远不够：要应对核军备竞赛，我们必须正视它的规模。而规模则取决于数字。

Beyond War was started by a group of citizens who were alarmed by the arms race between the United States and the Soviet Union. At this point, the combined Soviet and American nuclear arsenals were sufficient to destroy the world multiple times. The Beyond War participants went door-to-door in their neighborhoods, hoping to galvanize a public outcry against the arms race. They struggled with the problem of how to make credible their belief that the arms race was out of control. How do you make clear to people the staggering destructive capability of the world’s nuclear stockpile? It’s so intangible, so invisible. And yet telling stories, or providing details, seems inadequate: Grappling with the nuclear arms race requires us to grapple with the scale of it. Scale relies on numbers.

“超越战争”组织会举办“家庭聚会”，由一个家庭邀请一群朋友和邻居到家中做客，并邀请“超越战争”的代表进行演讲。艾恩斯科回忆起该组织在演讲中使用的一个简单的演示。他总是带着一个金属桶去聚会。在演讲的适当环节，他会从口袋里掏出一颗钢珠，扔进空桶里。钢珠在桶里弹跳，发出响亮的撞击声。艾恩斯科会说：“这就是广岛原子弹。”然后，他会花几分钟时间描述广岛原子弹造成的破坏——绵延数英里的建筑物被夷为平地，数万人当场死亡，以及更多人遭受烧伤或其他长期健康问题。

Beyond War would arrange “house parties,” in which a host family invited a group of friends and neighbors over, along with a Beyond War representative to speak to them. Ainscow recounts a simple demonstration that the group used in its presentations. He always carried a metal bucket to the gatherings. At the appropriate point in the presentation, he’d take a BB out of his pocket and drop it into the empty bucket. The BB made a loud clatter as it ricocheted and settled. Ainscow would say, “This is the Hiroshima bomb.” He then spent a few minutes describing the devastation of the Hiroshima bomb—the miles of flattened buildings, the tens of thousands killed immediately, the larger number of people with burns or other long-term health problems.

接着，他往桶里扔了十颗钢珠。撞击声更大更嘈杂。“这相当于一艘美国或苏联核潜艇上导弹的火力，”他会这样说。

Next, he’d drop ten BBs into the bucket. The clatter was louder and more chaotic. “This is the firepower of the missiles on one U.S. or Soviet nuclear submarine,” he’d say.

最后，他让在场的人闭上眼睛。他会说：“这就是世界现有的核武器库。”然后他往桶里倒了5000颗钢珠（一颗代表世界上每一枚核弹头）。那声音震耳欲聋，甚至令人恐惧。“钢珠的轰鸣声持续不断，”艾恩斯科说，“之后总是死一般的寂静。”

Finally, he asked the attendees to close their eyes. He’d say, “This is the world’s current arsenal of nuclear weapons.” Then he poured 5,000 BBs into the bucket (one for every nuclear warhead in the world). The noise was startling, even terrifying. “The roar of the BBs went on and on,” said Ainscow. “Afterward there was always dead silence.”

这种方法巧妙地传达了一个统计数据。让我们来仔细分析一下。首先，“超越战争”组织的核心信念是：“公众需要觉醒，并采取行动应对军备竞赛。”其次，该组织的成员确定了信息中出人意料之处：每个人都知道二战后世界核武库有所增长，但没有人意识到增长的规模如此之大。第三，他们他们用一个统计数据来佐证自己的观点——比如，世界上有5000枚核弹头，而实际上一枚就足以摧毁一座城市。但问题在于，5000这个数字对人们来说意义不大。诀窍在于如何让这个庞大的数字变得有意义。

This approach is an ingenious way to convey a statistic. Let’s unpack it a bit. First, Beyond War had a core belief: “The public needs to wake up and do something about the arms race.” Second, the group’s members determined what was unexpected about the message: Everyone knew that the world’s nuclear arsenal had grown since World War II, but no one realized the scale of the growth. Third, they had a statistic to make their belief credible—i.e., that the world had 5,000 nuclear warheads when a single one was enough to decimate a city. But the problem was that the number 5,000 means very little to people. The trick was to make this large number meaningful.

最后的高潮是演示——水桶和BB弹，这为原本抽象的概念增添了感官上的冲击。此外，演示材料的选择也经过精心考量——BB弹是武器，BB弹击中水桶的声音恰如其分地营造出一种威胁感。

The final twist was the demonstration—the bucket and the BBs, which added a sensory dimension to an otherwise abstract concept. Furthermore, the demonstration was carefully chosen—BBs are weapons, and the sound of the BBs hitting the bucket was fittingly threatening.

注意一点可能与直觉相悖：这个统计数据并没有被人们记住。它根本不可能被记住。一周后，任何看过那次示威的人都不会记得世界上有5000枚核弹头。

Notice something that may be counterintuitive: The statistic didn’t stick. It couldn’t possibly stick. No one who saw the demonstration would remember, a week later, that there were 5,000 nuclear warheads in the world.

真正让人印象深刻的是那种突如其来的、发自内心的巨大危险感——二战时期有限的原子武器规模与如今遍布全球的核武库相比，简直是天壤之别。究竟是4135枚核弹头还是9437枚并不重要，关键在于要让人们真切地感受到，这个问题已经失控了。

What did stick was the sudden, visceral awareness of a huge danger—the massive scale-up from World War II’s limited atomic weaponry to the present worldwide arsenal. It was irrelevant whether there were 4,135 nuclear warheads or 9,437. The point was to hit people in the gut with the realization that this was a problem that was out of control.

这是有效运用统计数据时最重要的一点：统计数据本身很少具有实际意义。统计数据几乎总是（也应该）用来阐明某种关系。对人们来说，记住这种关系比记住具体的数字更重要。

This is the most important thing to remember about using statistics effectively. Statistics are rarely meaningful in and of themselves. Statistics will, and should, almost always be used to illustrate a relationship. It’s more important for people to remember the relationship than the number.

人性化尺度原则

The Human-Scale Principle

另一种使统计数据生动起来的方法是，用更人性化、更贴近日常生活的语言来解释它们。举个科学方面的例子，对比以下两个陈述：

Another way to bring statistics to life is to contextualize them in terms that are more human, more everyday. As a scientific example, contrast the following two statements:

  1. 科学家最近以惊人的精度计算出了一个重要的物理约束。为了更好地理解这种精度，想象一下从太阳向……扔一块石头。击中地球，误差在正中心三分之一英里以内。

  2. Scientists recently computed an important physical constraint to an extraordinary accuracy. To put the accuracy in perspective, imagine throwing a rock from the sun to the earth and hitting the target within one third of a mile of dead center.

  3. 科学家们最近计算出了一个对极高精度至关重要的物理限制。为了更直观地理解这种精度，想象一下从纽约向洛杉矶扔一块石头，并且击中目标的精度控制在正中心三分之二英寸以内。

  4. Scientists recently computed an important physical constraint to an extraordinary accuracy. To put the accuracy in perspective, imagine throwing a rock from New York to Los Angeles and hitting the target within two thirds of an inch of dead center.

哪个说法更准确？

Which statement seems more accurate?

正如你可能已经猜到的，这两个问题的准确度水平完全相同，但当不同群体评估这两个陈述时，58%的受访者认为关于太阳到地球距离的统计数据“非常令人印象深刻”。而对于关于纽约到洛杉矶距离的统计数据，这一比例跃升至83%。我们对太阳和地球之间的距离没有任何经验或直觉。纽约到洛杉矶的距离则要具体得多。（不过坦白说，它仍然远非具体可感。问题在于，如果你把距离设定得更具体——比如一个足球场的距离——那么准确度就变得难以捉摸了。“以3.4微米的精度把一块石头扔出一个足球场的距离”这种说法毫无意义。）

As you may have guessed, the accuracy levels in both questions are exactly the same, but when different groups evaluated the two statements, 58 percent of respondents ranked the statistic about the sun to the earth as “very impressive.” That jumped to 83 percent for the statistic about New York to Los Angeles. We have no human experience, no intuition, about the distance between the sun and the earth. The distance from New York to Los Angeles is much more tangible. (Though, frankly, it’s still far from tangible. The problem is that if you make the distance more tangible—like a football field—then the accuracy becomes intangible. “Throwing a rock the distance of a football field to an accuracy of 3.4 microns” doesn’t help.)

史蒂芬·柯维在他的著作《第八个习惯》中描述了一项针对来自多家公司和行业的23000名员工的调查。他报告了调查结果：

Stephen Covey, in his book The 8th Habit, describes a poll of 23,000 employees drawn from a number of companies and industries. He reports the poll’s findings:

  • 只有 37% 的人表示他们清楚自己所在的组织想要实现什么目标以及为什么。

  • Only 37 percent said they have a clear understanding of what their organization is trying to achieve and why.

  • 只有五分之一的人对他们团队和组织的目标充满热情。

  • Only one in five was enthusiastic about their team’s and their organization’s goals.

  • 只有五分之一的人表示，他们对自己工作的任务与团队和组织的目标之间有清晰的“联系”。

  • Only one in five said they had a clear “line of sight” between their tasks and their team’s and organization’s goals.

  • 只有 15% 的人认为他们的组织能够充分支持他们实现关键目标。

  • Only 15 percent felt that their organization fully enables them to execute key goals.

  • 只有 20% 的人完全信任他们所在的公司。

  • Only 20 percent fully trusted the organization they work for.

这些数据相当令人警醒，也相当抽象。你看完这些数据后可能会想：“大多数公司里都存在很多不满和困惑。”

Pretty sobering stuff. It’s also pretty abstract. You probably walk away from these stats thinking something like “There’s a lot of dissatisfaction and confusion in most companies.”

然后，科维用一个非常人性化的比喻来解释这些统计数据。他说：“比如说，如果一支足球队也出现同样的比分，那么场上11名球员中只有4人知道哪个球门是自己的。只有2人会在意。只有2人知道自己踢什么位置，也知道自己应该做什么。而且，除了这2名球员之外，其他所有球员在某种程度上都是在和自己的队友竞争，而不是和对手竞争。”

Then Covey superimposes a very human metaphor over the statistics. He says, “If, say, a soccer team had these same scores, only 4 of the 11 players on the field would know which goal is theirs. Only 2 of the 11 would care. Only 2 of the 11 would know what position they play and know exactly what they are supposed to do. And all but 2 players would, in some way, be competing against their own team members rather than the opponent.”

足球的比喻为统计数据赋予了人性化的意义，营造出一种戏剧性和动感。我们不禁会想象两名球员在试图进球时，如何一步步被对方队友阻挠的场景。

The soccer analogy generates a human context for the statistics. It creates a sense of drama and a sense of movement. We can’t help but imagine the actions of the two players trying to score a goal, being opposed at every stage by the rest of their team.

为什么这个类比有效？它依赖于我们对足球队的固有认知，以及这种认知比我们对组织的认知更加清晰明确。想象一支以团队合作为核心的足球队缺乏合作，比想象一家公司缺乏合作要生动得多。而这正是科维的观点：公司应该像团队一样运作，但事实并非如此。将统计数据人性化，能使论点更有说服力。

Why does the analogy work? It relies on our schema of soccer teams and the fact that this schema is somehow cleaner, more well-defined, than our schemas of organizations. It’s more vivid to think of a lack of cooperation on a soccer team—where teamwork is paramount—than in a corporation. And this is exactly Covey’s point: Corporations should operate like teams, but they don’t. Humanizing the statistics gives the argument greater wallop.

再举一个体现“以人为本”原则的例子，我们来看一个日常情境：评估某项技术升级是否物有所值。思科就曾面临过这样的抉择：是否要为员工增设无线网络。维护无线网络的成本估计为每位员工每年 500 美元。这笔费用听起来不菲——相当于为所有员工购买牙科或视力保险。但这并非福利，而是一项投资。那么，如何计算这项投资的价值呢？你需要判断的是，增设网络后，每位员工每年能否为你带来价值 501 美元的额外收益。

As another example of the human-scale principle, take a mundane situation: figuring out whether a particular technological upgrade is worth the money. One example comes from Cisco, when it had to decide whether to add a wireless network for its employees. The cost of maintaining a wireless network was estimated at $500 per year per employee. That price sounds hefty—on the order of adding dental or vision insurance for all employees. But it’s not a benefit, it’s an investment. So how do you compute the value of an investment? You’ve got to decide whether you can get $501 worth of additional value from each employee each year after adding the network.

一位思科员工想出了一个更好的投资思考方式：“如果你相信你能提高员工的生产力。”每天节省一到两分钟，就能收回无线网络的成本。” 在这个规模下，投资评估就容易多了。我们的直觉在这个规模下也适用。我们可以轻松模拟员工如何利用无线网络节省几分钟的场景——例如，在重要会议期间，向他人发送请求，索取遗忘的文件。

One Cisco employee figured out a better way to think about the investment: “If you believe you can increase an employee’s productivity by one to two minutes a day, you’ve paid back the cost of wireless.” On this scale, the investment is much easier to assess. Our intuition works at this scale. We can easily simulate scenarios where employees can save a few minutes from wireless access—for instance, sending someone a request for a forgotten document during a critical meeting.

统计数据本身并无益处；关键在于其规模和背景。很少有人能凭直觉判断无线网络能否为每位员工每年创造 500 美元的边际价值。合适的规模决定一切。我们看到，具体性使人们能够运用已有的知识——还记得惠普公司模拟一家人在迪士尼乐园游玩的案例吗？同样，人性化尺度原则使我们能够运用直觉来评估信息内容的可信度。

Statistics aren’t inherently helpful; it’s the scale and context that make them so. Not many people have an intuition about whether wireless networking can generate $500 worth of marginal value per employee per year. The right scale changes everything. We saw that Concreteness allows people to bring their knowledge to bear—remember HP’s simulation of a family at Disney World? Similarly, the human-scale principle allows us to bring our intuition to bear in assessing whether the content of a message is credible.

S当统计数据用于阐明各种关系时，它们是增强内部可信度的良好来源。本书引言中，我们讨论了公共利益科学中心（CSPI）反对高饱和脂肪爆米花的运动的例子。相关的统计数据是，一袋中等大小的爆米花含有37克饱和脂肪。那么，这又意味着什么呢？是好事还是坏事？

Statistics are a good source of internal credibility when they are used to illustrate relationships. In the introduction of this book, we discussed the example of the CSPI’s campaign against saturated-fat-loaded movie popcorn. The relevant statistic was that a medium-sized bag of popcorn had 37 grams of saturated fat. So what? Is that good or bad?

公共利益科学中心（CSPI）的阿特·西尔弗曼巧妙地将爆米花的饱和脂肪含量置于一个相关的比较背景下。他指出，一袋爆米花的饱和脂肪含量相当于一整天的不健康饮食。西尔弗曼知道，大多数人听到这个发现都会感到震惊。

Art Silverman, of the CSPI, cleverly placed the popcorn’s saturated-fat content in a relevant context for comparison. He said that one bag of popcorn was equivalent to a whole day’s worth of unhealthy eating. Silverman knew that most people would be appalled by this finding.

如果西尔弗曼是个卑鄙小人呢？他可能会选择一种臭名昭著的不健康食品，但饱和脂肪含量相对较低，比如棒棒糖。“一袋爆米花的脂肪含量相当于71.2万根棒棒糖！”（或者说是无限多根棒棒糖，因为它们不含脂肪。）这个统计数据之所以卑鄙，是因为它的影响力来源于……这是一种利用人们对不健康食物的不同认知来误导消费者的伎俩。一个卑鄙的电影院高管为了报复，可能会把比较的重点从饱和脂肪转移到玉米的某些积极属性上：“一袋爆米花所含的维生素J相当于71磅西兰花！”（这是我们编的。）

What if Silverman had been a sleazebag? He could have picked a food item that was notoriously unhealthy but relatively low in saturated fat, such as lollipops. “One bag of popcorn has the fat equivalent of 712,000 lollipops!” (Or an infinite number of lollipops, since they’re fat-free.) This statistic is sleazy because it draws its power from sleight of hand involving different senses of unhealthy food. A sleazy movie-theater executive, to retaliate, might have changed the domain of comparison from saturated fat to some positive attribute of corn: “A bag of popcorn has as much Vitamin J as 71 pounds of broccoli!” (We made this up.)

这些可能性正是我们为何对统计学写作感到焦虑的原因之一。尤其是在政治领域，操纵统计数据为无数的议题倡导者提供了有利可图的就业机会。一些道德败坏但分析能力极强的人，只要稍加曲解，就能利用一组既定的统计数据，提出几乎任何论点。

These possibilities are examples of why writing about statistics filled us with anxiety. Particularly in the realm of politics, tinkering with statistics provides lucrative employment for untold numbers of issue advocates. Ethically challenged people with lots of analytical smarts can, with enough contortions, make almost any case from a given set of statistics.

当然，我们也要记住，没有统计数据比有统计数据更容易撒谎。数据能划定界限。除非有人不择手段到捏造数据，否则数据的真实性会约束他们。这固然是好事，但仍然留下了很大的回旋余地。

Of course, let’s also remember that it’s easier to lie without statistics than with them. Data enforces boundaries. Unless people are unethical enough to make up data, the reality of the data constrains them. That’s a good thing, but it still leaves a lot of wiggle room.

那么我们这些不擅长粉饰数据的人该怎么办呢？我们依然会忍不住想要美化自己的数据。我们都会这么做。“我今晚在教会篮球队得了16分！”（没提的是：投丢了22个球，输掉了比赛。）“我身高1米68。”（没提的是：我穿了7.6厘米的高跟鞋。）“今年的收入增长了10%，所以我认为我应该拿到奖金。”（没提的是：利润暴跌。）

So what about the rest of us, who aren’t spinmeisters? What do we do? We will still be tempted to put the best possible spin on our statistics. All of us do it. “I scored sixteen points for the church basketball team tonight!” (Not mentioned: twenty-two missed shots and the loss of the game.) “I’m five feet six.” (Not mentioned: The three-inch heels.) “Revenue was up 10 percent this year, so I think I deserve a bonus.” (Not mentioned: Profits tanked.)

谈到统计数据，我们最好的建议是将其作为输入，而非输出。用它们来帮助你对某个问题做出判断。不要先下结论，然后再去找数据来支持自己的观点——那样只会自找麻烦。但如果我们利用统计数据来帮助我们做出判断，我们就能更好地与他人分享关键数据，就像杰夫·安斯科和“超越战争”的支持者们所做的那样。

When it comes to statistics, our best advice is to use them as input, not output. Use them to make up your mind on an issue. Don’t make up your mind and then go looking for the numbers to support yourself—that’s asking for temptation and trouble. But if we use statistics to help us make up our minds, we’ll be in a great position to share the pivotal numbers with others, as did Geoff Ainscow and the Beyond War supporters.

诊所

CLINIC

应对鲨鱼袭击恐慌

Dealing with Shark Attack Hysteria

情况： 每隔几年，媒体就会大肆报道鲨鱼袭击事件。然而，鲨鱼袭击事件仍然极其罕见，而且每年发生的事件数量变化不大。那么，为什么它们会吸引如此多的媒体和公众关注呢？答案是，鲨鱼袭击事件会催生出一些令人毛骨悚然、噩梦连连的故事，例如以下来自 奥普拉·温弗瑞秀的案例：

THE SITUATION: Every few years the media go frothy over shark attacks. Shark attacks, however, remain extremely rare and do not vary much from one year to the next. So why do they consume so much media and public attention? The answer is that shark attacks spawn terrifying, dream-haunting stories like the following, from The Oprah Winfrey Show:

奥普拉：贝瑟妮·汉密尔顿热爱冲浪。她从8岁起就每天冲浪，天赋异禀，人们都说她天生就与海浪结缘。13岁时，贝瑟妮已是冲浪界冉冉升起的新星，成为当地名人，但接下来发生的事情却让她登上了世界各地的头条新闻。

OPRAH: Bethany Hamilton loved to ride the waves. Surfing daily since she was 8 years old, Bethany was such a phenom, people said she had salt water running through her veins. At the young age of 13, Bethany was a rising star on the surfing circuit and had become a local celebrity, but what happened next landed Bethany in headlines around the world.

清晨，贝瑟妮躺在冲浪板上，手臂悬在海里。突然，一条十五英尺长的凶猛虎鲨咬住了她的手臂。它猛烈地拽扯，直到她的手臂从她娇小的身体上被硬生生地扯了下来。几秒钟后，鲨鱼和她的整条手臂都消失了，贝瑟妮独自一人躺在冲浪板上，周围是血淋淋的海水。

It was early morning. Bethany was in the ocean lying on her board with her arm dangling in the water. Suddenly, a deadly fifteen-foot tiger shark seized her arm. Violently, he jerked and yanked it until her arm was ripped right off of her small body. Seconds later the shark and her entire arm were gone, and Bethany was left alone on her board surrounded by bloody water.

想象一下，你被迫应对这些生动的报道。也许你是拯救鲨鱼基金会的宣传主管，也许你正试图说服你的初中女儿去海滩玩。你会怎么做？你掌握着事实——鲨鱼袭击事件非常罕见——但这并不能保证人们会相信你。那么，你该如何建立可信度才能赢得人们的信任呢？

Imagine that you are forced to combat these vivid stories. Maybe you’re the publicity director of the Save the Sharks Foundation, or maybe you’re trying to convince your junior high school daughter that it’s okay to go to the beach. How do you do it? You’ve got the truth on your side—attacks are very rare—but that’s no guarantee that people will believe you. So what source of credibility do you tap to get people to believe you?

• • •

• • •

信息 1：本信息基于佛罗里达自然历史博物馆公布的统计数据：

MESSAGE 1: We based this message on statistics published by the Florida Museum of Natural History:

在有救生员值守的海滩上，你溺水的概率远高于被鲨鱼袭击的概率，更遑论被鲨鱼咬死。2000年，美国有12人在有救生员值守的海滩上溺水身亡，没有鲨鱼致死的案例。（通常情况下，每年因鲨鱼袭击致死的案例只有0.4例。）

You’re more likely to drown on a beach in an area protected by a lifeguard than you are to be attacked by a shark, much less killed by one. In the United States in 2000, twelve people died in lifeguard-protected areas. There were no fatalities from sharks. (In a typical year there are only 0.4 fatalities.)

关于第一条信息的评论：这条信息还可以，但并不出色。它利用了内部可信度——也就是冷冰冰的统计数据。我们有两点评论：首先，溺水似乎不是一个合适的比较对象，因为很多人可能认为溺水是常见的死因。“溺水比鲨鱼袭击更常见”这种说法并不让人感到意外。（或许我们过于怀疑，但大学生救生员的存在从来都不能保证绝对安全。）其次，统计上的比较——12例死亡对0.4例——虽然不错，但从人的角度来看，却不够生动形象，也缺乏实际意义。一周后，恐怕没人会记得这些数字。

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 1: This is okay but not great. This message taps internal credibility—the credibility of hard statistics. We have two comments: First, drowning does not seem like the right comparison to make, because many people may think drowning is a common cause of death. “Drowning is more common than shark attacks” does not feel particularly unexpected. (And maybe we’re too skeptical, but the presence of the college-student lifeguard never struck us as an ironclad guarantee of safety.) Second, the statistical comparison—12 deaths versus 0.4—is good, but it isn’t particularly vivid or meaningful on a human scale. It’s unlikely that anyone would remember these numbers a week later.

• • •

• • •

信息 2：本信息也基于佛罗里达自然历史博物馆公布的统计数据：

MESSAGE 2: This message is also based on statistics published by the Florida Museum of Natural History:

以下哪种动物更有可能杀死你？

Which of these animals is more likely to kill you?

一条鲨鱼     一只鹿

答案：鹿更有可能杀死你。事实上，它撞上你的车致你死亡的可能性是你的300倍。

ANSWER: The deer is more likely to kill you. In fact, it’s 300 times more likely to kill you (via a collision with your car).

关于第二条信息的评论：我们喜欢这个出人意料的想法——小鹿斑比比凶猛的鲨鱼更危险，以及随后更加出人意料的统计数据——斑比的危险程度远超鲨鱼（致命性是鲨鱼的300倍！）。这荒诞到令人发笑，而幽默正是缓解鲨鱼袭击故事带来的恐惧的良药。从某种意义上说，我们是在用情感联想对抗情感联想（详见下一章）。

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 2: We like the unexpected idea that Bambi is more dangerous than the evil shark, followed by the doubly unexpected statistic that Bambi is wildly more dangerous (300 times more deadly!). It’s absurd to the point of being funny, and humor is a nice antidote to the fear generated by shark-attack stories. In a sense, we’re fighting emotional associations with emotional associations (see the next chapter).

这条信息利用统计数据增强了内部可信度，同时也利用了受众的信任。受众知道自己开车时对鹿的恐惧程度——也就是说，并不怎么害怕。我们当中很少有人会因为担心鹿出没而不敢在晚上出门。我们知道自己不怕鹿，那为什么还要怕鲨鱼呢？（这比把鲨鱼袭击和溺水相提并论更有效——毕竟，我们大多数人至少都对溺水有些许恐惧。）

This message taps internal credibility with the statistic, but it also taps into the audience as a source of credibility. People in the audience know how much they fear deer when they’re driving around—i.e., not much. Few of us are afraid to go out in the evening on account of lurking deer. We know that we don’t fear deer, so why should we fear sharks? (This is more effective than comparing shark attacks with drowning—after all, most of us have at least a mild fear of drowning.)

记分卡
检查清单 消息 1    消息 2
简单的    
意外    -   
具体的    
可信的    
情绪 -   
故事 -    -

妙语：当我们使用统计数据时，越少依赖具体数字越好。数字固然能告诉我们潜在的关系，但还有比数字本身更好的方式来阐释这种潜在关系。将鹿和鲨鱼并置，就好比艾因斯科用桶里的BB弹来做比喻一样。

PUNCH LINE: When we use statistics, the less we rely on the actual numbers the better. The numbers inform us about the underlying relationship, but there are better ways to illustrate the underlying relationship than the numbers themselves. Juxtaposing the deer and the shark is similar to Ainscow’s use of BBs in a bucket.

辛纳屈测试和 Safexpress

The Sinatra Test and Safexpress

我们已经看到，通过运用令人信服的细节或统计数据，我们可以使我们的想法本身更具可信度。建立内部可信度的第三种方法是使用特定类型的例子，即能够通过我们称之为“辛纳屈测试”的例子。

We’ve seen that we can make our ideas more credible, on their own merits, by using compelling details or by using statistics. A third way to develop internal credibility is to use a particular type of example, an example that passes what we call the Sinatra Test.

在弗兰克·辛纳屈的经典歌曲《纽约，纽约》中，他唱到在纽约开启新生活，副歌唱道：“如果我能在那里成功，我就能在任何地方成功。” 当一个例子足以在特定领域建立信誉时，它就通过了“辛纳屈测试”。例如，如果你拿到了诺克斯堡的安保合同，你就有资格竞标任何安保合同（即使你没有其他客户）。如果你承办过白宫的宴会，你就可以参与任何餐饮合同的竞争。这就是“辛纳屈测试”：如果你能在那里成功，你就能在任何地方成功。

In Frank Sinatra’s classic “New York, New York,” he sings about starting a new life in New York City, and the chorus declares, “If I can make it there, I’ll make it anywhere.” An example passes the Sinatra Test when one example alone is enough to establish credibility in a given domain. For instance, if you’ve got the security contract for Fort Knox, you’re in the running for any security contract (even if you have no other clients). If you catered a White House function, you can compete for any catering contract. It’s the Sinatra Test: If you can make it there, you can make it anywhere.

Safexpress是一家总部位于印度的家族企业，它巧妙地利用了“辛纳特拉测试”（Sinatra Test）。Safexpress身处竞争激烈的航运业。虽然竞争带来了低价，但也存在一个问题：大多数航运公司无法保证安全准时送达。有些公司甚至根本无法保证货物能够送达。

Safexpress, a family-owned business based in India, used the Sinatra Test to its advantage. Safexpress competes in the shipping business, where competition is fierce. And, while the competition led to low prices, there was a catch: Most shipping firms would not guarantee safe, on-time deliveries. With some firms, you couldn’t be guaranteed that deliveries would be made, ever.

为了在竞争中脱颖而出，Safexpress 向客户保证安全准时的递送服务。在印度运营的国际公司——这些公司早已习惯了 FedEx 的可靠性——立即接受了 Safexpress。但 Safexpress 难以吸引那些不习惯支付更高运费的印度公司。Safexpress 创始家族成员之一的鲁巴尔·贾恩决心要打入印度市场。

To distinguish itself from the competition, Safexpress assured its customers of safe, on-time delivery. International companies operating in India—companies accustomed to the reliability of FedEx—embraced Safexpress immediately. But Safexpress struggled to attract business from Indian companies that weren’t accustomed to paying higher rates. Rubal Jain, a member of the founding family of Safexpress, was determined to make inroads with Indian companies.

为此，贾恩将目光投向了赢得一家大型宝莱坞电影公司的业务。当贾恩提议由Safexpress发行该电影公司的电影时，对方的反应是“你在开玩笑吗？”

To do so, Jain set his sights on winning the account of a major Bollywood studio. When Jain proposed that Safexpress distribute the studio’s films, the reaction was “Are you kidding?”

这种怀疑是可以预见的，也是合情合理的：盗版在印度是一个主要问题，就像在世界其他地方一样，因此发行渠道也面临着挑战。至关重要。如果影片在运输途中“丢失”，几周后街头就会出现盗版版本。电影公司无法承担这种风险。

The skepticism was predictable and plausible: Piracy is a major concern in India, as it is in the rest of the world, so distribution is mission-critical. If films end up “misplaced” en route, bootlegged versions show up a few weeks later on street corners. This risk wasn’t one that the movie studio could take.

幸运的是，贾恩早已准备好了一份强有力的凭证。Safexpress曾负责第五部《哈利·波特》的发行——印度所有书店里的《哈利·波特》系列书籍都是由Safexpress运送的，这是一项极其复杂的配送工作：所有书籍必须在发售当天早上8点前送达书店。不能太早，否则书店老板可能会提前售卖，导致秘密泄露；也不能太晚，否则书店老板会因为销量损失而大发雷霆。此外，《哈利·波特》系列书籍需要像电影一样采取防盗版措施——绝对不能有任何泄露。

Fortunately, Jain had a powerful credential ready. Safexpress had handled the release of the fifth Harry Potter book—every Potter book in every bookstore in India had been delivered there by Safexpress, an insanely complicated delivery: All the books had to arrive in stores by 8 A.M. on the morning of the release. Not too early, or the bookstore owners might try to sell them early and the secret would be blown; and not too late, or the bookstore owners would be irate at lost sales. Also, the Potter books needed the same piracy protections as the studio’s films—there could be no leaks.

贾恩还有第二个故事。他从之前的谈话中得知，这位宝莱坞制片厂高管有个弟弟最近刚参加完高中毕业会考。讲完哈利·波特的故事后，贾恩补充道：“对了，我们也安全运送了您弟弟的考卷和答题卡。” Safexpress 负责所有高中和大学入学考试的试卷分发工作。

And Jain had a second story. He knew from an earlier conversation that the Bollywood studio executive had a brother who had recently taken his high school board exams. After telling the Harry Potter story, Jain mentioned, “By the way, we also safely delivered the examination papers for your brother’s boards and carried the return answer sheets.” Safexpress handles the distribution of all the central examinations for high school and university admissions.

两个月后，协议签署完毕。

Two months later, the deal was signed.

贾恩的两个故事都经受住了“辛纳屈测试”。他本可以用统计数据代替故事——“我们98.84%的包裹都能准时送达。”或者，他也可以引用外部可信度来源，例如一家跨国公司首席执行官的证词：“我们在印度的所有包裹都使用Safexpress，我们发现他们是一家非常优秀的服务提供商。”这两种方式都能有效提升可信度。但是，能够运送已完成的考试试卷和最新版的《哈利·波特》系列书籍，这本身就非同寻常。他们的力量源于这些具体事例，而非数字或权威。这些故事让人不禁思考：“如果Safexpress能做到这些，他们就能做到任何地方。”

Both of Jain’s stories passed the Sinatra Test. Jain could have used statistics instead of stories—“98.84 percent of our deliveries arrive on time.” Or he could have drawn on an external source of credibility, such as a testimonial from the CEO of a multinational company: “We’ve used Safexpress for all our deliveries in India and we’ve found them to be an excellent service provider.” Both of these are good credibility-boosters. But there is something extraordinary about being the company that carries completed board exams and the latest Harry Potter book. Their power comes from their concreteness rather than from numbers or authority. These stories make you think, “If Safexpress can make it there, they can make it anywhere.”

可食用织物

Edible Fabrics

要举一个能将“内部可信度”这三个来源——细节、统计数据和辛纳屈测试——结合起来的例子，我们可以看看比尔·麦克唐纳，他是一位环保主义者，以帮助公司改善环境和盈利而闻名。

For an example that unites all three of the “internal credibility” sources—details, statistics, and the Sinatra Test—we can turn to Bill McDonough, an environmentalist known for helping companies improve both the environment and the bottom line.

大多数高管在面对环保主义者时，往往抱持怀疑态度，即便像麦克唐纳这样“亲商”的环保主义者也不例外。为了消除这种疑虑——为了证明商业目标和环境目标可以完美契合——麦克唐纳讲述了一个经得起“辛纳屈测试”的故事。

Most executives tend to be skeptical and suspicious when approached by an environmentalist, even a “business-friendly” environmentalist like McDonough. To overcome such skepticism—to prove that there can be perfect consistency between business goals and environmental goals—McDonough tells a story that passes the Sinatra Test.

故事是这样的。1993年，麦克唐纳和化学家迈克尔·布朗加特受聘于瑞士纺织品制造商罗纳纺织公司，该公司为Steelcase椅子生产面料。他们的任务在纺织业大多数人看来是不可能完成的：创造一种不使用有毒化学物质的生产工艺。

The story goes as follows. In 1993, McDonough and a chemist, Michael Braungart, were hired by the Swiss textile manufacturer Rohner Textil, which produces the fabrics for Steelcase chairs. Their mission was one that most people in the textile industry considered impossible: Create a manufacturing process without using toxic chemicals.

纺织业经常接触危险化学品。大多数染料都含有有毒元素。事实上，罗纳纺织厂的边角料——也就是椅子生产过程中未使用的剩余布料——含有大量可疑化学物质，以至于瑞士政府将其列为危险废物。此外，这些边角料在瑞士既不能掩埋也不能焚烧——为了遵守政府规定，它们必须出口——运往监管较为宽松的国家，例如西班牙。（请注意这个生动具体的细节。）麦克唐纳说：“如果你的边角料被认定为危险废物，但你却可以出售中间的布料，那么你不需要是火箭科学家也知道你是在出售危险废物。”

The textile industry routinely deals with hazardous chemicals. Most dye colors contain toxic elements. In fact, the trimmings from Rohner Textil’s factory—the excess cloth not used on the chairs—contained so many questionable chemicals that the Swiss government classified them as hazardous waste. Furthermore, the trimmings couldn’t be buried or burned in Switzerland—to comply with government regulations, they had to be exported—shipped to a country with laxer regulations, such as Spain. (Note the vivid, concrete detail.) McDonough said, “If your trimmings are declared hazardous waste but you can sell what’s in the middle, you don’t need to be a rocket scientist to know you’re selling hazardous waste.”

为了解决这个问题——即消除家具制造过程中的有毒化学物质——麦克唐纳需要在化工行业找到一位愿意合作的伙伴。他必须为罗纳纺织公司提供符合其生产需求的清洁化学品。需求。于是他和布朗加特开始接触化工行业的管理人员。他们说：“我们希望未来所有产品都能像儿科药品一样安全。我们希望我们的婴儿能够吮吸这些产品，获得健康而不是疾病。”

To tackle this problem—eliminating toxic chemicals from the furniture-manufacturing process—McDonough needed to find a willing partner in the chemical industry. He had to provide Rohner Textil with a source for clean chemicals that would fit the company’s production needs. So he and Braungart started approaching executives in the chemical industry. They said, “We’d like to see all products in the future be as safe as pediatric pharmaceuticals. We’d like our babies to be able to suck on them and get health and not sickness.”

他们要求化工厂公开账目，并说明化学品的生产过程。麦克唐纳告诉这些公司：“别跟我们说‘这是专有技术，合法’。如果我们不知道它是什么，我们就不会用。”六十家化工厂拒绝了他们的要求。最终，汽巴-嘉基公司的董事长同意了。

They asked chemical factories to open their books and talk about how the chemicals were manufactured. McDonough told the companies, “Don’t tell us ‘it’s proprietary and legal.’ If we don’t know what it is, we’re not using it.” Sixty chemical companies turned them down. Finally, the chairman of one firm, Ciba-Geigy, said okay.

麦克唐纳和布朗加特研究了纺织工业中常用的8000种化学品。他们根据一系列安全标准对每种化学品进行了评估。在测试的化学品中，7962种不合格。最终只剩下38种化学品——但根据麦克唐纳的说法，这38种化学品“安全到可以食用”。（注意这个具体的细节——“安全到可以食用”——以及一个建立起关联性的统计数据——在大量有毒化学品中，只有极少数是安全的。）

McDonough and Braungart studied 8,000 chemicals commonly used in the textile industry. They measured each chemical against a set of safety criteria. Of the chemicals they tested, 7,962 failed. They were left with 38 chemicals—but those 38 were “safe enough to eat,” according to McDonough. (Note the concrete detail—“safe enough to eat”—plus a statistic that establishes a relationship—a tiny number of good chemicals out of a larger number of toxic chemicals.)

令人惊奇的是，他们仅用这38种化学物质就生产出了一系列颜色齐全的面料，除了黑色以外，几乎涵盖了所有颜色。他们选择的面料由天然材料制成——羊毛和一种名为苎麻的植物纤维。生产线上线后，瑞士政府的检查人员前来检测工厂流出的水，以确保化学物质排放量符合法律规定。“起初，检查人员以为是他们的设备出了故障，”麦克唐纳说。仪器在水中检测不到任何物质。随后，检查人员检测了流入工厂的水——瑞士的饮用水——发现设备运转正常。麦克唐纳解释说：“生产过程中，面料本身就起到了进一步过滤水的作用。”

Amazingly, using just those 38 chemicals, they were able to create a complete line of fabrics, containing every color but black. The fabric they chose was made from natural materials—wool and a plant fiber called ramie. When the production process went online, inspectors from the Swiss government came to check the water flowing out of the plant to make sure chemical emissions were within legal limits. “At first, the inspectors thought their equipment had broken,” McDonough says. The instruments were detecting nothing in the water. Then the inspectors tested the water flowing into the factory, which was Swiss drinking water, and found that the equipment was fine. McDonough says, “The fabrics during the production process were further filtering the water.”

麦克唐纳的新工艺不仅更安全，而且更便宜。制造成本降低了20%。节省下来的成本部分来自于处理有毒化学品的麻烦和费用的减少。工人们不再需要穿防护服。而废料——不再被运往西班牙掩埋——而是被转化成了……这种毛毡被卖给瑞士的农民和园丁，用于农作物隔热。

McDonough’s new process wasn’t just safer, it was cheaper. Manufacturing costs shrank 20 percent. The savings came, in part, from the reduced hassle and expense of dealing with toxic chemicals. Workers no longer had to wear protective clothing. And the scraps—instead of being shipped off to Spain for burial—were converted into felt, which was sold to Swiss farmers and gardeners for crop insulation.

这个故事令人叹为观止。想想那些令人印象深刻的元素：看似不可能完成的任务；8000种化学品中仅剩38种被清除；工厂的水变得如此洁净，以至于瑞士的检验人员都以为他们的仪器坏了；废弃的有害废料被转化为农作物隔热材料；这种织物“安全到可以食用”；以及最终带来的令人满意的商业成果——工人的工作更安全，成本降低了20%。

This story is remarkable. Think about all the memorable elements: The impossible mission. The elimination of all but 38 of 8,000 chemicals. The factory’s water turned so clean that Swiss inspectors thought their instruments were broken. The scraps were transformed from hazardous waste into crop insulation. The idea that this fabric was “safe enough to eat.” And the happy business result—workers made safer and costs down 20 percent.

如果麦克唐纳向任何行业的任何企业提出更环保的工艺建议，这个故事将为他赢得极大的信誉。它轻松通过了“辛纳屈测试”的考验。

If McDonough approaches any business, in any industry, with a suggestion for a more environment-friendly process, this story will give him enormous credibility. It easily clears the bar set by the Sinatra Test.

到目前为止，我们已经讨论了如何通过引用外部信息来源——权威人士和反权威人士——来建立可信度。我们也讨论了如何通过引用信息本身的信息来源来建立可信度——使用细节、统计数据和能够通过“辛纳屈测试”的例子。但是，还有一种可信度来源我们尚未讨论。而它或许是所有来源中最强大的。

So far we’ve talked about creating credibility by drawing on external sources—authorities and antiauthorities. And we’ve talked about creating credibility by drawing on sources inside the message itself—by using details and statistics and examples that pass the Sinatra Test. But there’s one remaining source of credibility that we haven’t discussed. And it may be the most powerful source of all.

牛肉在哪儿？

Where’s the Beef?

温蒂汉堡在1984年推出了一项堪称史上最精彩的电视广告宣传活动。第一支广告以三位老妇人站在柜台前开场。柜台上放着一个汉堡包，她们目不转睛地盯着它，因为它太大了——直径大约有一英尺。

One of the most brilliant television ad campaigns of all time was launched by Wendy’s in 1984. The first commercial opens on three elderly women standing together at a counter. On the counter there’s a hamburger on a plate, and they’re gawking at it, because it’s huge—about a foot in diameter.

“这确实是个大面包，”左边的女士说道。

“It certainly is a big bun,” says the woman on the left.

“一个很大的面包，”中间的面包说道。

“A very big bun,” echoes the one in the center.

“一个又大又蓬松的面包，”第一个人说道。

“A big, fluffy bun,” says the first.

“一个非常大、毛茸茸的……”

“A very big fluffy …”

镜头一转，中间的女人掀开面包的上半部分，露出一块可怜的、烤过头的牛肉饼和一根酸黄瓜。牛肉饼在面包的映衬下显得格外渺小。

There’s a pause as the woman in the middle lifts the top half of the bun and reveals a meager, overcooked beef patty and a single pickle. The patty is dwarfed by the bun.

我们第一次听到右边那位女士的声音，她由八十岁的克拉拉·佩勒饰演。她眯着眼睛透过眼镜，不耐烦地问道：“牛肉在哪儿？”

For the first time, we hear from the woman on the right, played by eighty-year-old Clara Peller. She squints through her glasses and says, cantankerously, “Where’s the beef?”

播音员说：“有些汉堡店给的牛肉少得可怜，面包却松软无比……”

The announcer says, “Some hamburger places give you a lot less beef on a fluffy bun….”

佩勒：“牛肉在哪儿？”

Peller: “Where’s the beef?”

旁白：“温蒂汉堡的单层汉堡比皇堡或巨无霸汉堡的牛肉更多。在温蒂汉堡，您能吃到更多牛肉，更少面包。”

Announcer: “The Wendy’s Single has more beef than the Whopper or the Big Mac. At Wendy’s you get more beef and less bun.”

佩勒：“嘿！牛肉在哪儿？”她探头看向柜台。“我觉得后面没人。”

Peller: “Hey! Where’s the beef?” She peers over the counter. “I don’t think there’s anybody back there.”

这些广告有很多值得称道之处。它们既幽默又制作精良。克拉拉·佩勒也因此小有名气。更值得一提的是，这些广告突出了温蒂汉堡的真正优势：牛肉确实更多。这些广告摆脱了传统广告商惯用的伎俩，不再试图在消费品上强加一些无关紧要的情感​​——例如，将母亲对孩子的爱与某个品牌的衣物柔顺剂联系起来。温蒂汉堡的做法更令人钦佩：它突出了产品真正的优势，并以一种轻松愉快的方式呈现出来。

There’s a lot to love about these commercials. They’re funny and well produced. Clara Peller became a minor celebrity. More remarkably, the ads highlighted a true advantage of Wendy’s hamburgers: They really did have more beef. The ads were a refreshing departure from the standard advertiser tool kit that attempts to paint powerful but irrelevant emotions on consumer goods—for instance, associating a mother’s love of her children with a particular brand of fabric softener. Wendy’s did something more admirable: It highlighted a genuine advantage of its product and presented it in an enjoyable way.

这些广告产生了巨大影响。根据温蒂汉堡进行的民意调查，在广告播出后的两个月内，认为温蒂单层汉堡比皇堡或巨无霸更大的顾客人数增加了47%。在广告播出后的第一个完整年度，温蒂汉堡的营收增长了31%。

The ads had a big impact. According to polls taken by Wendy’s, the number of customers who believed that Wendy’s Single was larger than the Whopper or the Big Mac increased by 47 percent in the two months after the commercial aired. During the first full year after the ads ran, Wendy’s revenues rose 31 percent.

温蒂汉堡宣称其汉堡牛肉含量更高。在此之前，大多数人可能不会过多关注这一信息。当然，这在当时也并非常识。那么，温蒂汉堡是如何使这一说法令人信服的呢？

The claim Wendy’s had made was that its burgers had more beef. This information was probably not something most people would have given much thought to before. Certainly it was not common sense at the time. So how did Wendy’s make this claim credible?

请注意，这里的情况有所不同。这条信息并没有诉诸外部权威——温蒂汉堡并没有邀请拉里·伯德来评价汉堡的尺寸。（它也没有使用反权威的形象，比如一个肥胖的汉堡狂人。）它也没有诉诸内部权威，比如引用“牛肉含量增加11%！”之类的统计数据。相反，广告开辟了一种全新的信誉来源：观众。温蒂汉堡将信誉外包给了顾客。

Notice that something different is going on here. This message doesn’t draw on external credibility—Wendy’s didn’t invite Larry Bird to weigh in on burger sizes. (Nor did it use an antiauthority, like an obese burger-eating giant.) It doesn’t draw on internal credibility, either, quoting a statistic like “11 percent more beef!” Instead, the commercials developed a brand-new source of credibility: the audience. Wendy’s outsourced its credibility to its customers.

这些广告巧妙地向顾客发出挑战，让他们验证温蒂汉堡的说法：眼见为实——看看我们的汉堡和麦当劳的汉堡。你会发现尺寸上的差异！用科学术语来说，温蒂汉堡的说法是可证伪的。任何顾客只要有尺子和秤就能验证这一说法的真假。（不过，温蒂汉堡的优势非常显著，即使目测也能看出差别。）

The spots implicitly challenged customers to verify Wendy’s claims: See for yourself—look at our burgers versus McDonald’s burgers. You’ll notice the size difference! To use scientific language, Wendy’s made a falsifiable claim. Any customer with a ruler and a scale could have verified the claim’s truth value. (Though Wendy’s advantage was sufficiently substantial that just eyeballing the difference was enough.)

这种挑战——要求客户亲自验证某种说法——是一种“可验证的资质”。可验证的资质可以极大地提升信誉度，因为它本质上允许你的受众“先试后买”。

This challenge—asking customers to test a claim for them-selves—is a “testable credential.” Testable credentials can provide an enormous credibility boost, since they essentially allow your audience members to “try before they buy.”

可测试凭证

Testable Credentials

可验证的资质认证在都市传说中有着一段丰富多彩的历史。上世纪90年代，Snapple公司就曾苦苦挣扎于支持三K党的谣言之中。造谣者自认为掌握了一些“证据”：“看看任何一瓶Snapple——瓶身上都印着一艘奴隶船！” 怀疑者还被怂恿去寻找一个奇怪的符号：一个圆圈里有个字母“ K” ——据说这是三K党拥有Snapple的证据。

Testable credentials have a colorful history in urban legends. In the 1990s, Snapple struggled to shake rumors that it supported the Ku Klux Klan. Rumormongers thought they had a few pieces of “evidence” on their side: “Look on any bottle of Snapple—there’s a picture of a slave ship on the front!” Doubters were also encouraged to look for the strange symbol showing a K inside a circle—allegedly, evidence of the Klan’s ownership.

果不其然，Snapple的标签上确实印着一艘船和一个圆圈里的字母K。但这与三K党毫无关系。船的图案取自波士顿倾茶事件的版画。圆圈里的K是“犹太洁食”的标志。但一些不明真相的人看到这些符号后，却信以为真，相信了谣言。

Sure enough, Snapple’s labels did feature a picture of a ship and a K in a circle. They just had nothing to do with the Klan. The ship was from an engraving of the Boston Tea Party. The circled K is a symbol for “kosher.” But some uninformed people saw these symbols and bought into the rumors.

注意，Snapple 的谣言提供了一种类似“牛肉在哪儿？”的诱饵式反驳。温蒂汉堡说：“你自己看看——我们的汉堡牛肉更多。” 谣言散布者说：“你自己看看——上面有个圈起来的K。因此，Snapple 支持三K党。” 这种“你自己看看”的说法是否属实，让一些人信以为真。不合逻辑地就得出谣言散布者的结论。这就是可验证的资质为何会适得其反——“亲自验证”这一步骤本身或许有效，但最终得出的结论却可能完全无效。

Notice that the Snapple rumor provides a kind of bait-and-switch version of “Where’s the Beef?” Wendy’s says, “See for yourself—our burgers have more beef.” The Rumormongers say, “See for yourself—there’s a circled K. Therefore Snapple supports the Ku Klux Klan.” The validity of the see-for-yourself claim causes some people to leap, illogically, to the rumormongers’ conclusion. This is how testable credentials can backfire—the “see for yourself” step can be valid, while the resulting conclusion can be entirely invalid.

可验证的资质在许多领域都非常有用。例如，罗纳德·里根在1980年与吉米·卡特的总统辩论中，就曾向观众提出过这样一个著名的问题：“你现在的生活比四年前更好吗？”里根本可以着重列举统计数据——高通胀率、失业率、不断上涨的利率。但他并没有试图说服观众，而是将问题留给了他们。

Testable credentials are useful in many domains. For example, take the question “Are you better off now than you were four years ago?” Ronald Reagan famously posed this question to the audience during his 1980 presidential debate with Jimmy Carter. Reagan could have focused on statistics—the high inflation rate, the loss of jobs, the rising interest rates. But instead of selling his case he deferred to his audience.

另一个可验证资质的例子来自积极教练联盟（PCA）的创始人吉姆·汤普森。PCA的使命是强调青少年体育运动不应以不惜一切代价赢得比赛为目标，而应侧重于学习人生经验。

Another example of testable credentials comes from Jim Thompson, the founder of the Positive Coaching Alliance (PCA). The mission of the PCA is to emphasize that youth sports should not be about winning at all costs; it should be about learning life lessons.

PCA为青少年体育教练举办积极教练研讨会。在研讨会上，培训师会用“情绪油箱”这个比喻，引导教练思考表扬、支持和批评性反馈之间的恰当比例。“情绪油箱就像汽车的油箱。如果汽车的油箱空了，你就开不了多远。同样，如果你的情绪油箱空了，你就无法发挥出最佳水平。”

The PCA holds positive-coaching seminars for youth sports coaches. At the seminars, trainers use the analogy of an “Emotional Tank” to get coaches to think about the right ratio of praise, support, and critical feedback. “The Emotional Tank is like the gas tank of an automobile. If your car’s tank is empty, you can’t drive very far. If your Emotional Tank is empty, you are not going to be able to perform at your best.”

在介绍完“情绪罐”的比喻后，培训师们开始进行一项练习。首先，他们要求教练们想象自己旁边的人刚刚在比赛中犯了一个关键错误。教练们的任务是想办法对这个人说些什么，以消耗他的“情绪罐”。由于巧妙的挖苦是许多体育互动中常见的技巧，这项练习受到了教练们的热烈欢迎。汤普森说：“当教练们投入到练习中时，房间里充满了笑声，有时他们还会展现出极富创意的表达方式。”

After the Emotional Tank analogy is introduced, the trainers begin an exercise. First, they ask the coaches to imagine that the person next to them has just flubbed a key play in the game. The coaches are challenged to say something to the person to drain his Emotional Tank. Since clever put-downs are a staple of many sports interactions, this exercise is embraced with noticeable enthusiasm. Thompson says, “The room fills with laughter as coaches get into the exercise, sometimes with great creativity.”

然后，教练们被要求想象另一个人犯了同样的错误，但现在他们的任务是帮助这个人重拾信心，而不是打击他的积极性。这会产生一种更为温和的反应。汤普森说：“房间里常常变得非常安静，最后你会听到一声微弱的‘不错！’”

Then the coaches are asked to imagine that someone else has made the same mistake, but now they’re in charge of filling that person’s Emotional Tank instead of draining it. This generates a more muted response. Thompson says, “The room often gets very quiet, and you finally hear a feeble, ‘Nice try!’”

教练们反思自己的行为，从中吸取了教训——他们发现批评比支持更容易，想出十句巧妙的讽刺比一句安慰更容易。汤普森找到了一种方法，将他的观点转化为可验证的论证，让教练们可以亲身验证。

Observing their own behavior, the coaches learn the lesson—how they found it easier to criticize than to support, to think of ten clever insults rather than a single consolation. Thompson found a way to transform his point into a testable credential, something the coaches could experience for themselves.

诊所

CLINIC

我们的直觉是有缺陷的，

但谁愿意相信这一点呢？

Our Intuition Is Flawed,

but Who Wants to Believe That?

现状： 人们常常相信自己的直觉，但我们的直觉却受到一些显而易见的偏见的影响。尽管如此，大多数人仍然对自己的直觉充满信心，很难让他们改变看法。这就是研究决策的心理学家们面临的艰巨挑战。假设你是一位心理学入门教材的编辑，你正在研究两种对“可得性偏见”概念的解释方式。

THE SITUATION: People often trust their intuition, but our intuition is flawed by identifiable biases. Still, most people feel pretty good about their intuition, and it’s hard to convince them otherwise. This is the uphill battle faced by psychologists who study decision-making. Pretend that you’re the editor of an introductory psychology textbook, and you’re looking at two competing ways of explaining the concept of “availability bias.”

• • •

• • •

信息1：准备好做出一些预测。以下哪项事件造成的死亡人数更多：他杀还是自杀？洪水还是肺结核？龙卷风还是哮喘？请花点时间思考你的答案。

MESSAGE 1: Get ready to make a few predictions. Which of the following events kill more people: Homicide or suicide? Floods or tuberculosis? Tornadoes or asthma? Take a second to think about your answers.

你可能认为凶杀、洪水和龙卷风更常见。人们通常也这么认为。但在美国，自杀死亡人数比凶杀死亡人数多50%，肺结核死亡人数是洪水死亡人数的九倍，哮喘死亡人数是龙卷风死亡人数的八十倍。

You might have thought that homicide, floods, and tornadoes are more common. People generally do. But in the United States there are 50 percent more deaths from suicide than from homicide, nine times more deaths from tuberculosis than from floods, and eighty times more deaths from asthma than from tornadoes.

那么，为什么人们的预测会出错呢？原因在于可得性偏差。可得性偏差是一种自然倾向，它导致我们在估计某个事件发生的概率时，会根据该事件在我们记忆中的可得性来判断其概率。我们直觉地认为，越容易记住的事件发生的可能性就越大。但通常情况下，我们记住的事物并不能准确地概括世界。

So why do people predict badly? Because of the availability bias. The availability bias is a natural tendency that causes us, when estimating the probability of a particular event, to judge the event’s probability by its availability in our memory. We intuitively think that events are more likely when they are easier to remember. But often the things we remember are not an accurate summary of the world.

我们之所以对某些事物记忆更深刻，可能是因为它们更能唤起情感，而非因为它们更常见。我们之所以对某些事物记忆更深刻，可能是因为媒体花费更多时间报道它们（或许是因为媒体提供了更生动的画面），而非因为它们更普遍。可得性偏差可能会误导我们的直觉，让我们把不寻常的事物当作常见，把不太可能的事物当作很可能。

We may remember things better because they evoke more emotion, not because they are more frequent. We may remember things better because the media spend more time covering them (perhaps because they provide more vivid images), not because they are more common. The availability bias may lead our intuition astray, prompting us to treat unusual things as common and unlikely things as probable.

• • •

• • •

对信息 1 的评论：这段文字使用了一个简单但有效的可检验的验证问题：你认为哪个问题导致的死亡人数更多？如果运气好的话，读者至少会猜错一个答案，从而亲眼目睹可得性偏差的现实。

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 1: This passage uses a simple but effective testable credential: Which problem do you think kills more people? With any luck, readers will botch at least one of the predictions, thus illustrating for themselves the reality of the availability bias.

信息 2：以下是另一段文字，说明了入门教材中更常见的可得性偏差：

MESSAGE 2: Here’s an alternative passage illustrating the availability bias that is more typical of introductory textbooks:

可得性偏差是一种自然倾向，它导致我们在估计特定事件的概率时，会根据事件在我们记忆中的可得性来判断其概率。我们直觉地认为，越容易记住的事件发生的可能性就越大。但通常情况下，我们记住的事情并不能准确概括世界。例如，俄勒冈大学决策研究人员进行的一项研究中，实验参与者认为死于他杀的人数比自杀的人数多20%，而事实是自杀死亡人数比他杀多50%。受试者认为……死于洪水的人数多于死于肺结核的人数，但死于肺结核的人数是洪水的九倍。受访者认为死于龙卷风的人数与死于哮喘的人数大致相同，但死于哮喘的人数是龙卷风的八十倍。

The availability bias is a natural tendency that causes us, when estimating the probability of a particular event, to judge the event’s probability by its availability in our memory. We intuitively think that events are more likely when they’re easier to remember. But often the things we remember are not an accurate summary of the world. For example, in a study by decision researchers at the University of Oregon, experimental participants thought that 20 percent more people died in homicides than in suicides, when the truth is that there are 50 percent more deaths from suicides. Subjects thought that more people were killed by floods than tuberculosis, but nine times more people are killed by tuberculosis. Subjects believed that approximately as many people were killed by tornadoes as by asthma, but there are eighty times more deaths from asthma.

人们更容易记住某些事物，是因为它们能唤起更多情感，而不是因为它们更常见。人们更容易记住某些事物，是因为媒体花更多时间报道它们（或许是因为媒体提供了更生动的画面），而不是因为它们更普遍。可得性偏差可能会误导我们的直觉，让我们把不寻常的事物当作常见，把不太可能的事物当作很可能。

People remember things better because they evoke more emotion, not because they are more frequent. People remember things better because the media spend more time covering them (perhaps because they provide more vivid images), not because they are more common. The availability bias may lead our intuition astray, prompting us to treat unusual things as common and unlikely things as probable.

关于第二条信息的评论：这条信息缺乏吸引力。你可以想象一个学生读到第二段——这段话剧透了哮喘造成的死亡人数是龙卷风的八十倍——然后心想：哇，这些研究参与者真蠢。亲身体验的效果要震撼得多。

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 2: This is less involving. You could imagine a student reading the second paragraph—which gives away the punch line that asthma kills eighty times more people than tornadoes—and thinking, Wow, those research participants were dumb. It’s much more powerful to experience the effect for yourself.

记分卡
检查清单 消息 1    消息 2
简单的    
意外        -
具体的    
可信的    
情绪 -    -
故事 -    -

重点：使用可测试的凭证可以让人们亲自尝试某个想法。

PUNCH LINE: Using testable credentials allows people to try out an idea for themselves.

新兵入职培训

Rookie Orientation

让我们换个体育领域：美国国家篮球协会（NBA）。想象一下，你的工作是向即将加入NBA的新秀普及艾滋病的危害。NBA球员都是年轻人——新秀通常不到21岁。他们一夜成名，承受着随之而来的各种关注。他们从小就听说过艾滋病，所以风险不在于他们对艾滋病一无所知；而在于，他们的生活环境可能会让他们在某个夜晚放松警惕。

Let’s shift to a different sports domain: the National Basketball Association. Imagine that it’s your job to educate incoming NBA rookies about the danger of AIDS. NBA players are young men—rookies are often under twenty-one. And they are sudden celebrities, with all the attention that goes with this new fame. They’ve heard about AIDS their entire lives, so the risk is not that they’re unaware of AIDS; the risk is that the circumstances of their lives prompt them to drop their guard for a night.

如何让艾滋病的威胁显得真实可信且迫在眉睫？仔细思考一下可能的可信度来源。你可以借助外部权威——比如像魔术师约翰逊这样的名人/专家，或者像身患艾滋病晚期的运动员这样的“反权威人士”。你可以使用贴近实际的统计数据（比如与陌生人一次性接触感染艾滋病的概率）。你可以运用生动的细节——比如运动员讲述自己如何因为一次狂欢之夜而放松了对安全性行为的警惕。这些方法都可能非常有效。但如果你想把可信度的来源转移到球员内部，让他们自己意识到这一点呢？NBA就想出了一个巧妙的办法。

How do you make the threat of AIDS credible and immediate? Think through the possible sources of credibility. You could draw on external credibility—a celebrity/expert like Magic Johnson, or an antiauthority, such as an athlete in the terminal stages of AIDS. You could use statistics on a human scale (perhaps the odds of contracting AIDS from a single encounter with a stranger). You could use vivid details—an athlete could recount how his normal safe-sex vigilance was eroded by a particularly wild night of partying. Any of these could be quite effective. But what if you wanted to move the source of credibility inward, inside the heads of the players? The NBA came up with an ingenious way to do just that.

一个NBA赛季开始前几周，所有新秀球员都必须前往纽约州塔里敦参加强制性的岗前培训。他们基本上会被“关”在酒店里六天：没有传呼机，没有手机。新秀们要学习进入职业联赛的方方面面——从如何应对媒体到如何明智地投资理财，无所不包。

A few weeks before the NBA season begins, all the rookie players are required to meet in Tarrytown, New York, for a mandatory orientation session. They’re essentially locked in a hotel for six days: no pagers, no cell phones. The rookies are taught about life in the big leagues—everything from how to deal with the media to how to make sensible investments with their new riches.

有一年，尽管迎新活动保密工作做得滴水不漏，还是有一群女粉丝提前埋伏在活动地点。迎新活动的第一晚，她们就在酒店的酒吧和餐厅里闲逛。他们精心打扮，力求引人注目。球员们对这种关注感到很开心。现场气氛活跃，球员们互相调情，并计划在之后的迎新会上与一些女球员见面。

One year, despite the secrecy surrounding the orientation, a group of female fans staked out the location. On the first night of the orientation, they were hanging out in the hotel bar and restaurant, dressed to be noticed. The players were pleased by the attention. There was a lot of flirting, and the players made plans to meet up with some of the women later in the orientation.

第二天早上，新人们按时来到训练场。他们惊讶地发现房间门口站着一群女粉丝。女粉丝们一个接一个地做了自我介绍。“大家好，我是希拉，我是艾滋病毒携带者。”“大家好，我是唐娜，我是艾滋病毒携带者。”

The next morning, the rookies dutifully showed up for their session. They were surprised to see the female fans in front of the room. The women introduced themselves again, one by one. “Hi, I’m Sheila and I’m HIV positive.” “Hi, I’m Donna and I’m HIV positive.”

突然间，这些新兵们对艾滋病有了更深的理解。他们明白了生活是如何失控的，一夜之间又会如何酿成终生的悔恨。

Suddenly the talk about AIDS clicked for the rookies. They saw how life could get out of control, how a single night could cause a lifetime of regret.

对比一下NBA和NFL的做法。有一年NFL新秀训练营，联盟工作人员让每个新秀都把避孕套套在香蕉上。毫无疑问，现场肯定一片哗然。之后，两位女性——她们以前是橄榄球队的球迷——讲述了她们如何勾引球员，希望能让他们怀孕。女性的那场训练很有冲击力——信息传递得非常巧妙。但是，究竟是听到别人欺骗别人的故事更容易让人记住，还是自己被欺骗的经历更容易让人记住呢？

Contrast the NBA’s approach with the NFL’s approach. At the NFL’s orientation one year, league personnel had every rookie put a condom on a banana. No doubt eye-rolling was epidemic. Later, two women—former football groupies—talked about how they would try to seduce players, hoping to get pregnant. The women’s session was powerful—it was a well-designed message. But what’s more likely to stick with someone: hearing about someone who fooled someone else, or being fooled yourself?

H我们如何才能让人们相信我们的想法？我们必须找到一个可以依靠的信誉来源。有时，信誉的源泉会枯竭，巴里·马歇尔在寻求治愈胃溃疡的过程中就发现了这一点。依靠外部信誉行不通——他上司和珀斯所在机构的认可似乎还不够。依靠内部信誉也行不通——他精心收集的数据和细节仍然无法让他通过考验。最终，他只能依靠观众的信誉——他通过吞下一杯细菌，实际上“模拟”了一种可验证的证明。其隐含的挑战是：你自己看看——如果你喝下这杯污秽物，你就会得胃溃疡，就像我一样。

How do we get people to believe our ideas? We’ve got to find a source of credibility to draw on. Sometimes the wellsprings are dry, as Barry Marshall discovered in his quest to cure the ulcer. Drawing on external credibility didn’t work—the endorsement of his supervisors and his institution in Perth didn’t seem to be enough. Drawing on internal credibility didn’t work—his careful marshaling of data and detail still didn’t help him clear the bar. In the end, what he did was draw on the audience’s credibility—he essentially “modeled” a testable credential by gulping a glass of bacteria. The implicit challenge was: See for yourself—if you drink this gunk, you’ll get an ulcer, just like I did.

我们应该从哪个渠道获取信誉，这一点并不总是显而易见的。从中汲取灵感。马歇尔最精彩的成就莫过于展现出的毅力——他懂得何时该另辟蹊径。在本章中，我们看到，最显而易见的信誉来源——外部验证和统计数据——并非总是最佳选择。几个生动的细节或许比铺天盖地的统计数据更具说服力。反权威人士或许比权威人士更有影响力。一个经得起“辛纳屈测试”的故事或许就能克服重重质疑。令人鼓舞的是，像马歇尔这样的医学天才，为了实现他的想法，也必须克服与我们如今为了实现自己的想法所面临的同样的重重障碍——而他最终取得了成功，造福了我们所有人。

It’s not always obvious which wellspring of credibility we should draw from. What Marshall showed so brilliantly was perseverance—knowing when it was time to draw on a different well. In this chapter we’ve seen that the most obvious sources of credibility—external validation and statistics—aren’t always the best. A few vivid details might be more persuasive than a barrage of statistics. An antiauthority might work better than an authority. A single story that passes the Sinatra Test might overcome a mountain of skepticism. It’s inspirational to know that a medical genius like Marshall had to climb over the same hurdles with his idea as we’ll have to climb with ours—and to see that he eventually prevailed, to the benefit of us all.



情绪化的



EMOTIONAL

M另一位特蕾莎修女曾说过：“如果我只关注群体，我永远不会行动；如果我关注个体，我就会行动。” 2004年，卡内基梅隆大学的一些研究人员决定探究大多数人是否像特蕾莎修女那样行事。

Mother Teresa once said, “If I look at the mass, I will never act. If I look at the one, I will.” In 2004, some researchers at Carnegie Mellon University decided to see whether most people act like Mother Teresa.

研究人员想了解人们对向一个抽象的慈善事业捐款和向某个特定个人捐款这两种情况的反应。他们向参与者提供五美元，让他们完成一份关于使用各种科技产品的调查问卷。（这份调查问卷与慈善捐款无关；目的是为了确保参与者手头有一些现金，以便考虑捐款。）

The researchers wanted to see how people responded to an opportunity to make a charitable contribution to an abstract cause versus a charitable contribution to a single person. They offered participants five dollars to complete a survey about their usage of various technology products. (The survey was irrelevant; the point was to ensure that the participants would have some cash on hand to consider donating to charity.)

人们完成调查后，收到了五张一美元的钞票作为报酬。此外，他们还意外地收到一个信封和一封慈善募捐信，让他们有机会将部分款项捐赠给救助儿童会，这是一个致力于改善全球儿童福祉的慈善机构。

When people finished the survey, they received their payment in five one-dollar bills. They also received, unexpectedly, an envelope and a charity-request letter giving them an opportunity to donate some of their money to Save the Children, a charity that focuses on the well-being of children worldwide.

研究人员测试了两种版本的请求信。第一种版本列举了非洲儿童面临的诸多问题的严重程度，例如：

The researchers tested two versions of the request letter. The first version featured statistics about the magnitude of the problems facing children in Africa, such as the following:

  • 马拉维粮食短缺影响了超过300万儿童。

  • Food shortages in Malawi are affecting more than 3 million children.

  • 赞比亚严重缺雨导致玉米产量自2000年以来下降了42%。据估计，约有300万赞比亚人因此面临饥饿。

  • In Zambia, severe rainfall deficits have resulted in a 42 percent drop in maize production from 2000. As a result, an estimated 3 million Zambians face hunger.

  • 四百万安哥拉人——占该国人口的三分之一——被迫逃离家园。

  • Four million Angolans—one third of the population—have been forced to flee their homes.

  • 埃塞俄比亚有超过1100万人急需粮食援助。

  • More than 11 million people in Ethiopia need immediate food assistance.

信件的另一个版本提供了关于一位年轻女孩的信息：

The other version of the letter gave information about a single young girl:

  • 您捐赠的每一分钱都将用于帮助来自非洲马里的七岁女孩罗基娅。罗基娅极度贫困，面临着严重的饥饿甚至饿死的威胁。您的慷慨捐助将改善她的生活。在您和其他爱心捐助者的支持下，救助儿童会将与罗基娅的家人和社区其他成员携手合作，帮助她解决温饱问题，接受教育，并提供基本的医疗保健和卫生知识教育。

  • Any money that you donate will go to Rokia, a seven-year-old girl from Mali, Africa. Rokia is desperately poor and faces the threat of severe hunger or even starvation. Her life will be changed for the better as a result of your financial gift. With your support, and the support of other caring sponsors, Save the Children will work with Rokia’s family and other members of the community to help feed and educate her and provide basic medical care and hygiene education.

研究人员给参与者两封信中的一封，然后让他们自己决定是否放钱，以及放多少钱，然后封好信封并交给研究人员。

The researchers gave participants one of the two different letters, then left them alone. They chose how much money, if any, to put back into the envelope, then they sealed the envelope and handed it back to a researcher.

平均而言，阅读统计数据的人捐款1.14美元，而阅读罗基娅故事的人捐款2.38美元——是前者的两倍多。看来大多数人和特蕾莎修女有一点共通之处：在人心面前，个人的力量胜过大众。

On average, the people who read the statistics contributed $1.14. The people who read about Rokia contributed $2.38—more than twice as much. It seems that most people have something in common with Mother Teresa: When it comes to our hearts, one individual trumps the masses.

研究人员认为，统计信函捐款减少可能是他们所谓的“捐款下降”造成的。“桶效应”。如果人们感到问题规模庞大，他们的小额捐款可能显得微不足道。但事情远不止于此。研究人员决定向第三组人提供两组信息——统计数据和罗基亚的故事。研究人员想知道，获得所有信息的人平均捐款额是否会超过罗基亚组捐赠的2.38美元。或许，统计数据和故事的结合——个人需求的力量与问题的统计规模相结合——会激发人们更高层次的捐赠热情。

The researchers believed that the smaller donations for the statistical letter could be a result of what they called the “drop in the bucket effect.” If people felt overwhelmed by the scale of the problem, their small donations might have seemed meaningless. But here’s where things get even more interesting. The researchers decided to give a third group of people both sets of information—the statistics and the story about Rokia. The researchers wondered whether people who got all the information would give more, on average, than the $2.38 that had been given by the Rokia group. Perhaps the combination of statistics and stories—the power of individual need coupled with the statistical scale of the problem—would inspire a whole new level of giving.

不。同时收到两封信的人平均只捐了1.43美元，比只收到罗基亚故事的人少了将近1美元。不知为何，这些统计数据——非洲大规模人道主义灾难的证据——反而降低了人们的慈善意愿。这到底是怎么回事？

Nope. The people who received both letters gave $1.43, almost a dollar less than the people who got the Rokia story alone. Somehow the statistics—evidence of massive human suffering in Africa—actually made people less charitable. What was going on?

研究人员推测，思考统计数据能使人们进入更具分析性的思维模式。当人们进行分析性思考时，他们就不太可能受情绪左右。研究人员认为，正是人们对罗基亚困境的情感反应促使他们采取行动。

The researchers theorized that thinking about statistics shifts people into a more analytical frame of mind. When people think analytically, they’re less likely to think emotionally. And the researchers believed it was people’s emotional response to Rokia’s plight that led them to act.

为了验证这一论点，他们进行了第二项研究。在这项研究中，他们引导一些参与者进行分析性思考，例如：“如果一个物体以每分钟五英尺的速度运动，那么根据你的计算，它在360秒内会运动多少英尺？” 而另一些参与者则被引导进行情感性思考：“请用一个词来描述你听到‘婴儿’这个词时的感受。”

To prove this argument, they ran a second study. In this study they primed some people to think in an analytical way by asking questions such as, “If an object travels at five feet per minute, then by your calculations how many feet will it travel in 360 seconds?” Other people were primed to think in terms of feelings: “Please write down one word to describe how you feel when you hear the word ‘baby.’”

随后，两组参与者都收到了罗基亚的信。结果证实了研究人员的理论：接受过分析性引导的参与者捐款更少。当参与者在阅读罗基亚的信件之前被引导去感受某种情绪时，他们捐出了2.34美元，与之前基本持平。但当他们在阅读罗基亚的信件之前被引导去计算某种因素时，他们捐出了1.26美元。

Then both groups were given the Rokia letter. And, confirming the researchers’ theory, the analytically primed people gave less. When people were primed to feel before they read about Rokia, they gave $2.34, about the same as before. But when they were primed to calculate before they read about Rokia, they gave $1.26.

这些结果令人震惊。仅仅是计算这一行为就降低了人们的慈善意愿。一旦我们戴上分析的帽子，我们对情感诉求的反应就会截然不同。我们会抑制自己的感受能力。

These results are shocking. The mere act of calculation reduced people’s charity. Once we put on our analytical hat, we react to emotional appeals differently. We hinder our ability to feel.

• • •

• • •

我上一章我们讨论了如何让人们相信我们的想法是可信的，如何让他们相信。信念固然重要，但光有信念还不够。要让人们采取行动，他们必须关心。

In the last chapter, we discussed how to convince people that our ideas are credible, how to make them believe. Belief counts for a lot, but belief isn’t enough. For people to take action, they have to care.

每个人都相信非洲存在着巨大的人道主义灾难；这一点毋庸置疑。但这种信念并不一定能促使人们采取行动。每个人都相信食用大量高脂肪食物会导致健康问题；这一点也毋庸置疑。但这种信念并不一定能促使人们采取行动。

Everyone believes there is tremendous human suffering in Africa; there’s no doubt about the facts. But belief does not necessarily make people care enough to act. Everyone believes that eating lots of fatty food leads to health problems; there’s no doubt about the facts. But the belief does not make people care enough to act.

慈善机构早已洞悉了“特蕾莎修女效应”——他们知道，捐赠者更容易对具体的个人而非抽象的事业产生共鸣。你不会捐款给“非洲贫困”，而是会资助一个具体的孩子。（事实上，以资助儿童作为慈善捐赠方式的想法可以追溯到20世纪50年代，当时一位年轻的基督教牧师鼓励美国人资助贫困的韩国孤儿。）这个理念也适用于动物。在致力于减少农场动物虐待的非营利组织“农场庇护所”（Farm Sanctuary），捐赠者可以“认养”一只鸡（每月10美元）、一只山羊（25美元）或一头牛（50美元）。

Charities have long since figured out the Mother Teresa effect—they know that donors respond better to individuals than to abstract causes. You don’t give to “African poverty,” you sponsor a specific child.
