事实上，他们比世上任何人都更清楚双钢琴为何值得保存。但知识的诅咒阻碍了他们清晰地表达出来。“保存双钢琴音乐”的使命在默里·德拉诺夫内部卓有成效且意义非凡，但在组织之外却晦涩难懂。一些与会者后来表示，他们也曾思考过这个问题：“如果双钢琴音乐彻底消失，世界为何会因此而黯然失色？”双钢琴究竟有何特别之处？谁会在意呢？

The reality is that they did in fact know better than anyone on earth why the duo piano was worth preserving. But the Curse of Knowledge prevented them from expressing it well. The mission to “preserve duo piano music” was effective and meaningful inside Murray Dranoff, but outside the organization it was opaque. Several attendees later commented that they had sympathized with the question “Why would the world be a less rich place if duo piano music disappeared completely?” What’s so special about the duo piano? Who cares?

如果你年复一年地专注于双钢琴演奏，很容易忘记世界上还有很多人从未听说过双钢琴。很容易忘记你是演奏者，而世界是听众。双钢琴组合之所以能摆脱“知识的诅咒”，是因为满屋子的人不停地问他们“为什么？”。通过三次追问“为什么”，双钢琴组合的讨论重点从“做什么”转移到了“为什么做”。他们从一套毫无影响力的联想（除了对那些已经了解双钢琴音乐的人而言）转变为一套更深刻、更具体的联想，从而与局外人产生情感共鸣。

If you come to work every day for years, focused on duo piano issues, it’s easy to forget that a lot of the world has never heard of the duo piano. It’s easy to forget that you’re the tapper and the world is the listener. The duo piano group was rescued from the Curse of Knowledge by a roomful of people relentlessly asking them, “Why?” By asking “Why?” three times, the duo piano group moved from talking about what they were doing to why they were doing it. They moved from a set of associations that had no power (except to someone who already knew duo piano music) to a set of deeper, more concrete associations that connected emotionally with outsiders.

“三问法”策略有助于规避知识诅咒。（丰田实际上有一套“五问法”流程，用于深入探究生产线上的问题。你可以根据需要使用任意数量的“问号”。）不断追问“为什么？”有助于我们牢记那些支撑我们理念的核心价值观和核心原则。

This tactic of the “Three Whys” can be useful in bypassing the Curse of Knowledge. (Toyota actually has a “Five Whys” process for getting to the bottom of problems on its production line. Feel free to use as many “Whys” as you like.) Asking “Why?” helps to remind us of the core values, the core principles, that underlie our ideas.

一个几年前，一群医院管理人员邀请设计公司IDEO帮助改进医院的工作流程。IDEO团队知道，他们的建议可能会遭到内部的强烈反对。为了激励他们，第一步是……改变医院工作人员的方式是让他们意识到存在问题，并让他们关心这个问题。

A few years back, a group of hospital administrators asked the design firm IDEO to help improve the hospital’s workflow. The team at IDEO knew that they would probably face a lot of internal resistance to their recommendations. The first step in motivating the hospital staff to change was to get them to realize that there was a problem and get them to care about it.

IDEO制作了一段视频，以一位因腿部骨折前往急诊室的病人的视角拍摄。在视频中，我们看到了病人所看到的景象。我们就是这位病人。我们走进急诊室的大门——四处寻找登记指南，并与说着我们听不懂的医学术语的接待人员交流。最终，我们被放在担架上，推着穿过医院。我们看到医院高耸的天花板。我们听到的是飘忽不定的声音，因为我们看不到说话的人。偶尔，会有人把头探进我们的视野。很多时候，我们会长时间地沉默着，盯着天花板，不知道接下来会发生什么。

IDEO created a video, shot from the perspective of a patient who goes to the emergency room for a leg fracture. In the video, we see what the patient sees. We are the patient. We come in through the door to the ER—we hunt around for check-in instructions and interact with the admissions people, who are speaking in a foreign medical tongue. Eventually, we are laid on a gurney and wheeled through the hospital. We see long stretches of the hospital ceiling. We hear disembodied voices, because we can’t see the person addressing us. Every now and then, someone pokes his or her head into our field of view. Frequently, there are long pauses where we just sit idle, staring at the ceiling, unsure what’s coming next.

IDEO公司的心理学家简·富尔顿·苏里表示，医院工作人员观看这段视频后，立即产生了影响。“他们的第一反应总是类似‘哦，我以前从来没意识到……’”苏里说她很喜欢“意识到”这个词。在医院工作人员观看视频之前，这个问题并没有真正存在过。之后，她说：“他们立刻就有了解决问题的动力。这不再只是问题清单上的一个问题了。”

Jane Fulton Suri, a psychologist at IDEO, said that when the hospital staff was shown the video it had an immediate impact. “The first reaction was always something like ‘Oh, I never realized …’” Suri says she likes the word realized. Before the hospital workers saw the video, the problem wasn’t quite real. Afterward, she said, “There’s an immediate motivation to fix things. It’s no longer just some problem on a problem list.”

IDEO还设计了角色扮演练习，让员工设身处地地体验患者的感受。这些练习包括这样的任务：“想象一下，你是一位法国人，正在医院里寻找你的父亲，但你不会说英语。” IDEO因这类模拟而闻名——这些模拟旨在引导员工设身处地地为客户着想。在某些情况下，时间似乎会削弱同理心，而IDEO的模拟练习则有助于恢复我们与生俱来的同理心。“商业世界往往更注重模式而非具体细节，”苏里说道，“模式的理性层面阻碍了人们的关怀。”

IDEO also created role-playing exercises, putting the staffers in the patients’ shoes. The exercises included such tasks as, “Imagine that you are French and you are trying to locate your father in the hospital, but you don’t speak any English.” IDEO has become known for this type of simulation—simulations that drive employees to empathize with their customers. Time seems to erode empathy in some contexts, and IDEO’s simulations manage to restore the natural empathy that we have for others. “The world of business tends to emphasize the pattern over the particular,” Suri said. “The intellectual aspects of the pattern prevent people from caring.”

• • •

• • •

T他的领悟——同理心源于具体事例而非模式——让我们又回到了本章开头特蕾莎修女的那句话：“如果我只关注群体，我永远不会行动；如果我关注个体，我就会行动。”

This realization—that empathy emerges from the particular rather than the pattern—brings us back full circle to the Mother Teresa quote at the beginning of the chapter: “If I look at the mass, I will never act. If I look at the one, I will.”

如何才能让人们关心我们的想法？我们要让他们放下分析的思维模式。我们要让他们对特定的人产生共鸣。我们要展示我们的想法与人们已经关心的事情之间的联系。我们要诉诸他们的自身利益，也要诉诸他们的身份认同——不仅是他们现在的身份，还有他们想要成为的身份。

How can we make people care about our ideas? We get them to take off their Analytical Hats. We create empathy for specific individuals. We show how our ideas are associated with things that people already care about. We appeal to their self-interest, but we also appeal to their identities—not only to the people they are right now but also to the people they would like to be

而且，虽然我们应该始终考虑受众的“利益所在”，但我们也应该记住要远离马斯洛的需求层次理论。受众的“利益所在”可能是审美动机或对超越的渴望，而不是250美元的奖金。弗洛伊德·李曾说过：“在我看来，我不仅负责餐饮服务，还负责士气。” 谁不想要像弗洛伊德·李这样的领导者呢？

And, while we should always think about “what’s in it” for our audience, we should remember to stay clear of Maslow’s Basement. “What’s in it” for our audience might be aesthetic motivation or the desire for transcendence rather than a $250 bonus. Floyd Lee said, “As I see it, I am not just in charge of food service; I am in charge of morale.” Who wouldn’t want a leader like Floyd Lee?



故事



STORIES

T这位护士在新生儿重症监护室工作，那里负责治疗和监测患有严重健康问题的新生儿。她已经观察一个婴儿好几个小时了，但她看到的景象让她很不安。婴儿的肤色是判断潜在问题的关键指标，而他的肤色一直在波动——时而呈现健康的粉红色，时而又变得暗淡，令人担忧。

The nurse was working in the neonatal intensive-care unit, where newborns with serious health problems are treated and monitored. She’d been watching one baby in particular for several hours, and she didn’t like what she was seeing. His color, a key indicator of potential problems, had been fluctuating—wavering between a healthy shade of pink and a duller, more troublesome hue.

突然间，短短几秒钟内，婴儿的脸色变成了深青黑色。护士的心猛地一沉。重症监护室里的其他人大声呼喊着要X光技师和医生。

Suddenly, within a matter of seconds, the baby turned a deep blue-black. The nurse’s stomach fell. Others in the ICU yelled for an X-ray technician and a doctor.

赶到现场的医疗团队假定婴儿的肺部已经塌陷，这是使用呼吸机的婴儿常见的并发症。团队准备采取应对肺部塌陷的典型措施，即刺破胸腔，插入导管，将塌陷肺部周围的空气抽出，使其重新扩张。

The gathering medical team was operating on the assumption that the baby’s lung had collapsed, a common problem for babies on ventilators. The team prepared for the typical response to a collapsed lung, which involves piercing the chest and inserting a tube to suck the air from around the collapsed lung, allowing it to reinflate.

但护士认为这是心脏问题。她一看到婴儿的肤色——那种可怕的蓝黑色——就怀疑是气胸，也就是空气充满包裹心脏的囊袋，向内挤压，导致心脏无法跳动。护士吓坏了，因为她上次见到气胸时，婴儿还没来得及确诊就夭折了。

But the nurse thought it was a heart problem. As soon as she saw the baby’s color—that awful blue-black—she suspected a pneumopericardium, a condition in which air fills the sac surrounding the heart, pressing inward and preventing the heart from beating. The nurse was terrified, because the last time she witnessed a pneumopericardium the baby died before the problem could even be diagnosed.

护士试图阻止他们慌乱地准备治疗肺部。“是心脏！”她说。但其他医护人员却指着心电监护仪，上面显示婴儿的心脏一切正常；他的心率稳定地跳动着，是新生儿正常的每分钟130次。护士仍然坚持，她推开他们的手，大声喊着让他们安静，然后放下听诊器去听心跳。

The nurse tried to stop the frantic preparations to treat the lung. “It’s the heart!” she said. But in response the other medical personnel pointed to the heart monitor, which showed that the baby’s heart was fine; his heart rate was bouncing along steadily, at the normal newborn rate of 130 beats per minute. The nurse, still insistent, pushed their hands away and screamed for quiet as she lowered a stethoscope to check for a heartbeat.

没有声音——心脏停止了跳动。

There was no sound—the heart was not beating.

她开始按压婴儿的胸部。新生儿科主任冲进房间，护士把注射器塞到他手里。“是气胸，”她说，“扎心脏。”

She started doing compressions on the baby’s chest. The chief neonatologist burst into the room and the nurse slapped a syringe in his hand. “It’s a pneumopericardium,” she said. “Stick the heart.”

X光技师终于拿到了扫描结果，证实了护士的诊断。新生儿科医生将注射器送入心脏，缓缓释放了阻塞婴儿心脏的空气。婴儿的生命得以挽救。他的脸色也慢慢恢复了正常。

The X-ray technician, who was finally receiving results from his scan, confirmed the nurse’s diagnosis. The neonatologist guided the syringe into the heart and slowly released the air that had been strangling the baby’s heart. The baby’s life was saved. His color slowly returned to normal.

后来，他们才明白为什么心率监护仪会误导他们。它测量的是电活动，而不是实际的心跳。婴儿的心脏神经一直在放电——指挥心脏以适当的频率跳动——但包裹心脏的囊袋内的空气阻止了心脏的实际跳动。只有当护士使用听诊器——以便听出心脏是否正常跳动——时，他们才清楚地意识到婴儿的心脏已经停止跳动。

Later, the group realized why the heart monitor misled them. It is designed to measure electrical activity, not actual heartbeats. The baby’s heart nerves were firing—telling the heart to beat at the appropriate rate—but the air in the sac around the heart prevented the heart from actually beating. Only when the nurse used the stethoscope—so she could hear whether the heart was pumping correctly—did it become clear that his heart had stopped.

T这个故事是由心理学家加里·克莱因收集的，他专门研究人们在高压、高风险环境下如何做出决策。他与消防员、空中交通管制员、发电厂操作员和重症监护室工作人员密切接触。这个关于婴儿的故事收录在克莱因的著作《力量之源》中名为“故事的力量”的章节里。

This story was collected by Gary Klein, a psychologist who studies how people make decisions in high-pressure, high-stakes environments. He spends time with firefighters, air-traffic controllers, powerplant operators, and intensive-care workers. The story about the baby appears in a chapter called “The Power of Stories,” in Klein’s book Sources of Power.

克莱因说，在他研究的环境中，故事被反复讲述，是因为故事蕴含着智慧。故事是一种有效的教学方式。这些工具揭示了环境如何误导人们做出错误的决定。故事阐明了人们以前未曾意识到的因果关系，并突显了人们解决问题的出人意料的、富有创造性的方法。

Klein says that, in the environments he studies, stories are told and retold because they contain wisdom. Stories are effective teaching tools. They show how context can mislead people to make the wrong decisions. Stories illustrate causal relationships that people hadn’t recognized before and highlight unexpected, resourceful ways in which people have solved problems.

从医学角度来看，上述故事蕴含着重要的教训。它指导人们如何识别和治疗气胸心包这种疾病。更广泛地说，它警示医务人员不要过度依赖机器。心电监护仪虽然运转良好，但它无法取代人类用听诊器进行的细致观察。

Medically, the story related above teaches important lessons. It instructs people in how to spot and treat the specific condition pneumopericardium. More broadly, it warns medical personnel about relying too much on machines. The heart monitor was functioning perfectly well, but it couldn’t substitute for the insight of a human being with a simple stethoscope.

这些医学经验对非医护人员来说可能用处不大。但这个故事却能激励所有人。故事的主人公是一位女性，她顶住来自各方的压力，坚持自己的信念。这是一个弱者逆袭的故事——在等级森严的医院环境中，正是这位护士向新生儿科主任做出了正确的诊断。一条生命取决于她是否愿意走出自己“该在的位置”。

These medical lessons are not particularly useful to people who don’t work in health care. But the story is inspiring to everyone. It’s a story about a woman who stuck to her guns, despite implicit pressure to conform to the group’s opinion. It’s an underdog story—in the hierarchical hospital environment, it was the nurse who told the chief neonatologist the right diagnosis. A life hinged on her willingness to step out of her “proper place.”

因此，故事的力量体现在两个方面：它提供模拟（关于如何行动的知识）和启发（行动的动力）。需要注意的是，模拟和启发这两种益处都旨在促成行动。在前几章中，我们已经看到，一个可信的想法能让人相信，一个感人的想法能让人关心。而本章我们将看到，恰当的故事能促使人们采取行动。

The story’s power, then, is twofold: It provides simulation (knowledge about how to act) and inspiration (motivation to act). Note that both benefits, simulation and inspiration, are geared to generating action. In the last few chapters, we’ve seen that a credible idea makes people believe. An emotional idea makes people care. And in this chapter we’ll see that the right stories make people act.

施乐午餐室里的闲聊

Shop Talk in the Xerox Lunchroom

复印机或许是我们大多数人用过的最复杂的机器。还有哪种日常机器能将光学、机械、化学和电子技术结合在一起呢？复印机居然还能正常工作，这本身就是一个奇迹。而且，它们经常出故障。一旦出现问题——而且不是那种坐在办公室里的人只要开关几次纸盒就能解决的问题——就需要非常专业的维修人员来排查故障。

Photocopiers are perhaps the most complex machines that most of us will ever use. What other everyday machine combines optical, mechanical, chemical, and electrical technologies? It’s a wonder copiers work at all. And often they don’t. When there’s a problem—and it’s not one that a cubicle-dweller can fix by opening and shutting the paper tray a few times—it takes a very sophisticated repair person to troubleshoot the situation.

研究员朱利安·奥尔花了很多时间与施乐复印机维修人员交流，发现他们经常互相讲述各种故事。以下这个故事就来自一位施乐复印机销售员，他是在午餐时一边玩纸牌游戏一边讲述的。（括号内附有一些解释性说明。）这位销售员首先提到了复印机设计师最近进行的一项机械改进，旨在防止普通的电源浪涌烧毁多个组件：

Researcher Julian Orr spent a lot of time among Xerox copier repairmen and found that they spent a lot of time swapping stories. Take the story below, which was told by a Xerox copier salesperson over a game of cribbage at lunch. (We’ve provided some explanatory comments in brackets.) The salesperson starts with a reference to a recent mechanical change made by copier designers in an attempt to prevent an ordinary power surge from frying multiple components:

新的 XER 板配置不会因为二极管电弧放电而烧毁电路板。相反，它会触发低压电源上的 24 伏互锁装置，导致机器崩溃。但重启后，它会显示 E053 错误代码。[这是一个误导性的错误代码，它指向的机器区域与真正的问题无关。]

The new XER board configuration won’t cook the board if you had an arcing dicorotron. Instead, it now trips the 24-Volt interlock on the Low Voltage Power Supply, and the machine will crash. But when it comes back up it’ll give you an E053 error. [This is a misleading error code that refers to an area of the machine that is unrelated to the real problem.]

那玩意儿跟我当时在走廊尽头遇到的完全一样，我和韦伯追了四个小时才找到它。结果发现只是个坏掉的双极管。我们好不容易让它运转了足够长的时间，得到了一个E053和一个F066，结果我们刚检查完双极管，就发现其中一个彻底坏了……[奥尔说，他们停顿了很久玩纸牌游戏。] 是啊，那可真有意思。

That’s exactly what I had down there, at the end of the hall, and Weber and I ran for four hours trying to chase that thing. All it was was a bad dicorotron. We finally got it running long enough so that we got an E053 with an F066 and the minute we checked the dicorotrons we had one that was totally dead…. [Orr reports that there was a long pause for cribbage.] Yeah that was a fun one.

T这些在午餐室里玩扑克牌的家伙们只是在闲聊，就像我们所有人一样。一个误导性的E053错误在你们的世界里或许算不上什么大事，但毫无疑问，我们每个人都会遇到类似的情况。

These cribbage-playing guys in the lunchroom are simply talking shop, as we all do. A misleading E053 error may not constitute drama in your world, but no doubt we all have our equivalents.

为什么人们喜欢谈论工作？部分原因很简单，这是人之常情——我们都想和别人聊聊共同的话题。施乐维修工的工作就是修理复印机，所以他们自然会谈论复印机。但这并非唯一的原因。例如，上面提到的那位讲述者完全可以省略细节，只讲述故事的大致经过：“我今天遇到了一个棘手的问题——花了四个小时才解决。总算解决了。”或者他本来可以直接切入正题：“费了好几个小时，我才发现问题出在一个不起眼的烧毁的双极管上。你早上过得怎么样？”

Why do people talk shop? Part of the reason is simply Humanity 101—we want to talk to other people about the things that we have in common. Xerox repairmen work with photocopiers, so they talk about them. But that’s not the only factor at play here. For example, the storyteller above could have shared the general arc of the story without the details: “I had a real bear of a problem today—it took me four hours to get to the bottom of it. I’m glad that one’s over.” Or he could have leapt straight to the punch line: “After hours of hassle, I traced the problem back to a measly burned-out dicorotron. How was your morning?”

相反，他讲了一个更吸引午餐伙伴的故事。这个故事本身就充满戏剧性——一个误导性的故障代码让两个人白跑一趟，直到他们经过大量的思考和努力，才发现问题其实比他们最初想象的要简单得多。为什么这种讲故事的方式更有趣呢？因为它让他的午餐伙伴们能够参与其中。他提供了足够的信息，让他们可以设想自己会如何处理这种情况。那些之前不了解E053故障代码的人，现在他们的“E053思维模式”已经得到纠正。以前，面对E053故障代码只有一种应对方法。现在，维修人员都知道要警惕“误导性的E053”这种情况了。

Instead, he tells a story that’s much more interesting to his lunch partners. It has built-in drama—a misleading code leads two men on a wild goose chase until they uncover, through lots of work and thought, that the problem is simpler than they initially thought. Why is this story format more interesting? Because it allows his lunch partners to play along. He’s giving them enough information so that they can mentally test out how they would have handled the situation. The people in the room who weren’t aware of the misleading E053 code have now had their “E053 schema” fixed. Before, there was only one way to respond to an E053 code. Now, repairmen know to be aware of the “misleading E053” scenario.

换句话说，这个故事既有娱乐性，也有教育意义。行业内的闲聊传递着应对世界的重要线索。它告诫护士不要盲目相信心率监测仪。它提醒复印机维修人员要警惕误导性的E053故障码。

In other words, this story is part entertainment and part instruction. Shop talk conveys important clues about how to respond to the world. It teaches nurses not to have blind faith in heart monitors. It teaches copy repairmen to beware of the misleading E053 code.

但以上案例并非仅仅是传递零星信息。施乐公司的案例与公司内部一封包含“注意与双极管烧毁相关的虚假E053代码”的电子邮件在功能上并不相同。这里面蕴含着更深层次的意义。我们需要仔细分析才能发现这些案例的额外价值。

But the stories above aren’t simply transferring nuggets of information. The Xerox story is not functionally equivalent to an e-mail sent around the company that contains the line “Watch out for false E053 codes related to burned-out dicorotrons.” Something more profound is happening here. It will take a bit of unpacking to reveal the additional value that these stories bring.

不被动的观众

The Un-passive Audience

故事与娱乐紧密相连——电影、书籍、电视节目和杂志。当孩子们说“给我讲个故事”时，他们是在寻求娱乐，而不是学习。

Stories are strongly associated with entertainment—movies and books and TV shows and magazines. When children say “Tell me a story,” they’re begging for entertainment, not instruction.

作为故事的“观众”似乎是一个被动的角色——那些从电视上获取故事的观众被称为“沙发土豆”。毕竟如此。但用“被动”来形容可能有些夸张。我们读书时，会感觉自己被带入了作者的世界。朋友讲故事时，我们会本能地产生共鸣。看电影时，我们会认同主角。

Being the “audience” for a story seems like a passive role—audiences who get their stories from television are called “couch potatoes,” after all. But “passive” may be overstating the case. When we read books, we have the sensation of being drawn into the author’s world. When friends tell us stories, we instinctively empathize. When we watch movies, we identify with the protagonists.

但如果故事以一种不那么直观、更加戏剧化的方式吸引我们呢？一组研究人员提出了一些令人兴奋的证据，表明故事的“观众”和故事的“主角”之间的界限可能有点模糊。

But what if stories involve us in less intuitive, more dramatic ways? One team of researchers has produced some exciting evidence suggesting that the line between a story’s “audience” and a story’s “protagonist” may be a bit blurry.

T三位对人们如何理解故事感兴趣的心理学家创作了一些故事，供他们的研究参与者在电脑上阅读。他们将参与者分成两组。第一组阅读的故事中，一个关键对象与故事的主人公相关联——例如，“约翰在慢跑前穿上了运动衫”。第二组阅读的故事中，同一个关键对象与主人公分离：“约翰在慢跑前脱掉了运动衫”。

Three psychologists interested in how people come to understand stories created a few for their study participants to read on a computer. They divided the participants into two groups. The first group read a story in which a critical object was associated with the main character in the story—for instance, “John put on his sweatshirt before he went jogging.” The second group read a story in which the same critical object was separated from the main character: “John took off his sweatshirt before jogging.”

两句话之后，故事里提到了运动衫，电脑能够追踪人们阅读这句话所花费的时间。奇怪的是：那些认为约翰在慢跑前脱掉了运动衫的人，比那些认为约翰穿着运动衫的人读这句话所花费的时间更长。

Two sentences later, the story threw in a reference to the sweatshirt, and the computer was able to track how long it took people to read that sentence. Something strange happened: The people who thought John had taken off his sweatshirt before the jog took more time to read the sentence than the people who thought John had it on.

这个结果微妙却引人入胜。它暗示我们会对听到的故事进行某种地理模拟。说“阅读故事会让我们在脑海中浮现画面”是一回事，我们都会觉得这种说法很直观。但如果说约翰把运动衫落在家里，在我们脑海中，它其实是留在了房子里一个更遥远的地方，那就完全是另一回事了。要做到这一点，我们不能简单地在脑海中把故事画面投射到电影屏幕上；我们必须以某种方式模拟它，包括用某种（哪怕很松散的）方式来模拟故事中描述的空间关系。这些研究表明……根本不存在被动的听众。当我们听故事时，我们的思绪会从一个房间飘到另一个房间。当我们听故事时，我们会进行模拟。但模拟有什么用呢？

This result is subtle but fascinating. It implies that we create a kind of geographic simulation of the stories we hear. It’s one thing to say “Reading stories makes us see pictures in our head.” We’d all find that statement intuitive. It’s quite another thing to say that when John left his sweatshirt behind, he left it back at the house in a more remote place in our heads. For that to be true, we cannot simply visualize the story on a movie screen in our heads; we must somehow simulate it, complete with some analogue (however loose) to the spatial relationships described in the story. These studies suggest that there’s no such thing as a passive audience. When we hear a story, our minds move from room to room. When we hear a story, we simulate it. But what good is simulation?

一个一群加州大学洛杉矶分校的学生被要求思考他们生活中目前遇到的一个问题，这个问题“让他们感到压力很大”，但将来有可能得到解决，例如学业上的问题或人际关系上的问题。

A group of UCLA students were asked to think about a current problem in their lives, one that was “stressing them out” but was potentially solvable in the future, such as a problem with schoolwork or with a relationship.

学生们被告知，实验的目的是帮助他们有效地解决问题，并收到了一些关于解决问题的简要指导：“思考问题、了解更多信息、思考你能做些什么、采取措施来解决问题非常重要……解决问题可以减轻你的压力，让你对自己的处理方式感到满意，并帮助你从这次经历中成长。” 收到这些指导后，这组“对照组”学生被送回家，并被要求一周后返回实验室汇报情况。

The students were told that the goal of the experiment was to help them deal with the problem effectively, and they got some brief instructions on problem-solving: “It is important to think about the problem, learn more about it, think about what you can do, take steps to deal with it…. Resolving it could reduce your stress, make you feel pleased with how you dealt with it, and help you grow from the experience.” After receiving these instructions, this “control group” was sent home and asked to report back to the lab a week later.

第二组学生，即“事件模拟”组，被留在实验室里。他们被要求在脑海中模拟问题是如何发生的：

A second group of students, the “event-simulation” group, were kept in the lab. They were asked to mentally simulate how the problem had unfolded:

请您将问题是如何产生的，并进行形象化的描述。想象问题的起源，详细回顾第一个事件……一步一步地回顾事件的发生过程。想象您当时采取的行动。记住您说过的话，做过的事。想象当时的环境，周围有哪些人，您在哪里。

We would like you to visualize how this problem arose. Visualize the beginning of the problem, going over in detail the first incident…. Go over the incidents as they occurred step by step. Visualize the actions you took. Remember what you said, what you did. Visualize the environment, who was around, where you were.

事件模拟参与者需要逐步重现导致问题的事件经过。可以推测，回顾因果链有助于学生思考如何解决问题，就像程序员进行系统调试一样。

The event-simulation participants had to retrace, step by step, the events that led to their problem. Presumably, reviewing the chain of causation might help the students think about how to fix the problem, like programmers engaged in systematic debugging.

第三组，即“结果模拟”组，被要求在脑海中模拟问题最终带来的积极结果：

A third group, the “outcome-simulation” group, was asked to mentally simulate a positive outcome emerging from the problem:

想象一下问题开始解决，你正从压力重重的处境中走出来……想象一下你如释重负的感觉。想象一下你成功解决问题后所感受到的满足感。想象一下你对自己充满信心，因为你知道自己已经成功地解决了问题。

Picture this problem beginning to resolve, you are coming out of the stressful situation…. Picture the relief you feel. Visualize the satisfaction you would feel at having dealt with the problem. Picture the confidence you feel in yourself, knowing that you have dealt successfully with the problem.

结果模拟器始终关注期望的未来结果：当这个问题过去之后，会是什么样子？

The outcome-simulators kept their focus on the desired future outcome: What will it be like once this problem is behind me?

完成初步练习后，两个模拟小组都被送回家。他们被要求每天花五分钟时间重复模拟练习，并在一周后返回实验室汇报。

After this initial exercise, both of the simulation groups were sent home. Both groups were asked to spend five minutes every day repeating their simulations, and to report back to the lab a week later.

现在是居家游戏时间：快速预测一下哪组学生在应对问题方面表现最好。（提示：不是对照组。）

Now it’s play-at-home time: Make a quick prediction about which group of students fared best in coping with their problems. (Hint: It’s not the control group.)

答案是：事件模拟组——也就是模拟事件发展过程的那组人——在几乎所有方面都表现得更好。模拟过去的事件比模拟未来的结果更有帮助。事实上，两组之间的差距在实验室的第一次实验后就立即显现出来。到了第一晚，事件模拟组的参与者相比其他两组，情绪就已经明显好转。

Here’s the answer: The event-simulation group—the people who simulated how the events unfolded—did better on almost every dimension. Simulating past events is much more helpful than simulating future outcomes. In fact, the gap between the groups opened up immediately after the first session in the lab. By the first night, the event-simulation people were already experiencing a positive mood boost compared with the other two groups.

一周后，当各小组返回时，事件模拟器组的优势更加明显。他们更有可能采取具体行动来解决问题，更有可能寻求他人的建议和支持，也更有可能表示自己有所收获并有所成长。

When the groups returned a week later, the event simulators’ advantage had grown wider. They were more likely to have taken specific action to solve their problems. They were more likely to have sought advice and support from others. They were more likely to report that they had learned something and grown.

你可能会觉得这些结果有点违反直觉，因为大众心理学文献中充斥着各种大师敦促你想象成功的场景。但事实证明，积极的心态并不足以让你获得成功。任务完成。或许金融专家不应该告诉我们想象自己富可敌国；相反，他们应该告诉我们重现导致我们贫穷的步骤。

You may find these results a bit counterintuitive, because the pop-psychology literature is full of gurus urging you to visualize success. It turns out that a positive mental attitude isn’t quite enough to get the job done. Maybe financial gurus shouldn’t be telling us to imagine that we’re filthy rich; instead, they should be telling us to replay the steps that led to our being poor.

W为什么心理模拟有效？原因在于，我们无法在不激活大脑中与真实身体活动相同的区域的情况下想象事件或序列。脑部扫描显示，当人们想象闪光灯时，大脑的视觉区域会被激活；当他们想象有人轻拍皮肤时，大脑的触觉区域会被激活。心理模拟的活动并不局限于我们的大脑内部。想象以字母b或p开头的单词的人会不由自主地做出细微的嘴唇动作，想象自己正在欣赏埃菲尔铁塔的人会不由自主地向上移动视线。心理模拟甚至可以改变人的生理反应：当人们喝水但想象自己喝的是柠檬汁时，唾液分泌会更多。更令人惊讶的是，当人们喝柠檬汁但想象自己喝的是水时，唾液分泌反而会减少。

Why does mental simulation work? It works because we can’t imagine events or sequences without evoking the same modules of the brain that are evoked in real physical activity. Brain scans show that when people imagine a flashing light, they activate the visual area of the brain; when they imagine someone tapping on their skin, they activate tactile areas of the brain. The activity of mental simulation is not limited to the insides of our heads. People who imagine words that start with b or p can’t resist subtle lip movements, and people who imagine looking at the Eiffel Tower can’t resist moving their eyes upward. Mental simulation can even alter visceral physical responses: When people drink water but imagine that it’s lemon juice, they salivate more. Even more surprisingly, when people drink lemon juice but imagine that it’s water, they salivate less.

心理模拟有助于我们管理情绪。针对各种恐惧症——例如蜘蛛恐惧症、公开演讲恐惧症、飞机旅行恐惧症等等——都有标准的治疗方法。患者首先会接受一种能够抑制焦虑的放松技巧，然后被要求想象自己暴露在恐惧对象面前。最初的想象从恐惧的边缘开始。例如，害怕飞机旅行的人可以先想象开车去机场的情景。治疗师会引导患者进行一系列的想象，这些想象会逐渐逼近恐惧的核心（“现在飞机引擎在跑道上轰鸣，声音越来越大……”）。每次想象引发焦虑时，患者都会暂停片刻，并运用放松技巧来恢复内心的平静。

Mental simulations help us manage emotions. There is a standard treatment for phobias of various kinds—spiders, public speaking, airplane travel, and others. Patients are introduced to a relaxation procedure that inhibits anxiety, and then asked to visualize exposure to the thing they fear. The first visualizations start at the periphery of the fear. For example, someone who’s afraid of air travel might start by thinking about the drive to the airport. The therapist leads the patient through a series of visualizations that get closer and closer to the heart of the fear (“Now the airplanes’ engines are revving up on the runway, sounding louder and louder …”). Each time the visualizations create anxiety, the person pauses for a moment and uses the relaxation technique to restore equilibrium.

请注意，这些可视化图表关注的是事件本身——过程，而不是结果。没有人能通过想象恐惧症消失后自己会有多么快乐而治愈它。

Notice that these visualizations focus on the events themselves—the process, rather than the outcomes. No one has ever been cured of a phobia by imagining how happy they’ll be when it’s gone.

心理模拟有助于解决问题。即使在日常的计划情境中，心理模拟也能帮助我们想到一些原本可能被忽略的事情。例如，想象去超市购物会提醒我们，也可以在同一家购物中心的干洗店送洗衣服。心理模拟还能帮助我们预判未来情境的恰当应对方式。想象一下与老板可能发生的争吵，想象她会说什么，这或许能让我们在真正需要的时候找到合适的措辞（避免说错话）。研究表明，心理演练可以防止人们复发吸烟、酗酒或暴饮暴食等不良习惯。一个试图戒酒的人如果能在心理上演练一下自己将如何应对超级碗周日：当有人起身去拿啤酒时，他应该如何回应？

Mental simulation helps with problem-solving. Even in mundane planning situations, mentally simulating an event helps us think of things that we might otherwise have neglected. Imagining a trip to the grocery store reminds us that we could drop off the dry cleaning at the store in the same shopping center. Mental simulations help us anticipate appropriate responses to future situations. Picturing a potential argument with our boss, imagining what she will say, may lead us to have the right words available when the time comes (and avoid saying the wrong words). Research has suggested that mental rehearsal can prevent people from relapsing into bad habits such as smoking, excessive drinking, or overeating. A man trying to kick a drinking problem will be better off if he mentally rehearses how he will handle Super Bowl Sunday: How should he respond when someone gets up for beers?

或许最令人惊讶的是，心理模拟也能提升技能。一项对35项研究（共3214名参与者）的回顾表明，仅靠心理练习——安静地坐着，一动不动，想象自己从头到尾成功完成一项任务——就能显著提高表现。这一结果在大量任务中都得到了证实：心理模拟帮助人们更好地焊接和投掷飞镖；长号演奏者提高了演奏水平；竞技花样滑冰运动员提高了滑冰技巧。不出所料，当任务涉及更多脑力活动（例如演奏长号）而非体力活动（例如保持平衡）时，心理练习的效果更佳，但平均而言，心理练习带来的收益幅度很大：总体而言，仅靠心理练习就能产生实际体力练习约三分之二的益处。

Perhaps most surprisingly, mental simulation can also build skills. A review of thirty-five studies featuring 3,214 participants showed that mental practice alone—sitting quietly, without moving, and picturing yourself performing a task successfully from start to finish—improves performance significantly. The results were borne out over a large number of tasks: Mental simulation helped people weld better and throw darts better. Trombonists improved their playing, and competitive figure skaters improved their skating. Not surprisingly, mental practice is more effective when a task involves more mental activity (e.g., trombone playing) as opposed to physical activity (e.g., balancing), but the magnitude of gains from mental practice is large on average: Overall, mental practice alone produced about two thirds of the benefits of actual physical practice.

结论很简单：心理模拟虽然不如实际操作，但却是次佳选择。回到我们讨论的那些棘手问题，我们想表达的是，合适的故事实际上就是一种模拟。故事就像大脑的飞行模拟器。听护士讲述心电监护仪的故事虽然无法让你身临其境，但却是次佳选择。

The takeaway is simple: Mental simulation is not as good as actually doing something, but it’s the next best thing. And, to circle back to the world of sticky ideas, what we’re suggesting is that the right kind of story is, effectively, a simulation. Stories are like flight simulators for the brain. Hearing the nurse’s heart-monitor story isn’t like being there, but it’s the next best thing.

或者想想施乐E053故障码的故事。为什么听这个故事比在培训手册里警告“误导性的E053指示器”更好呢？原因就在于，飞行模拟器比一堆教学卡片更适合飞行员。培训越能模拟我们在现实世界中必须采取的行动，就越有效。

Or think about the Xerox E053 code story. Why is hearing this story better than a warning about “misleading E053 indicators” in the training manual? It’s better for precisely the reason that flight simulators are better for pilots than stacks of instructional flash cards. The more that training simulates the actions we must take in the world, the more effective it will be.

故事之所以有力，是因为它提供了抽象散文所缺乏的背景信息。这又回到了记忆的“魔术贴”理论：我们给想法添加的“钩子”越多，它们就越牢固。E053 的故事层层递进，营造出一种情感氛围——找不到问题所在以及被机器代码误导的挫败感。它还融入了历史背景——最近“XER 板配置”的变更导致了这一新错误。最后，它传递了一种更深层次的道德启示：你不应该完全相信错误代码。这种“代码怀疑论”是维修人员在未来每一项工作中都应该运用的。

A story is powerful because it provides the context missing from abstract prose. It’s back to the Velcro theory of memory, the idea that the more hooks we put into our ideas, the better they’ll stick. The E053 story builds in emotions—the frustration of failing to find the problem and being misled by the machine’s code. It builds in historical background—the idea that the recent change in the “XER board configuration” led to this new error. At the end, it delivers a kind of meta-level moral: You shouldn’t have complete faith in the error code. This “code skepticism” is something the repairmen can apply to every future job they undertake.

医生一旦确诊阑尾炎，治疗起来很容易，但问题在于如何区分阑尾炎、胃部不适、食物中毒或溃疡。再想想那些刚开始学代数的学生，他们能解复杂的方程式，但一遇到同样的简单数学应用题就束手无策了。问题X并不总是会明确地表明自己是问题X。

It’s easy for a doctor to treat appendicitis once it’s been diagnosed, but the problem is learning to distinguish an inflamed appendix from an upset stomach or food poisoning or an ulcer. Or think about beginning algebra students, who can solve complex equations but grind to a halt when they’re presented with a simple word problem that involves exactly the same math. Problem X doesn’t always identify itself as Problem X.

这就是故事的作用——将知识融入一个更贴近生活、更贴近我们日常生活的框架中，就像飞行模拟器一样。毕竟，作为故事的听众并非被动的。在内心深处，我们早已准备好行动。

This is the role that stories play—putting knowledge into a framework that is more lifelike, more true to our day-to-day existence. More like a flight simulator. Being the audience for a story isn’t so passive, after all. Inside, we’re getting ready to act.

诊所

CLINIC

处理问题学生

Dealing with Problem Students

情况： 教授们偶尔会在课堂上遇到一些令人头疼的学生——比如愤怒、好斗或爱挑衅的学生。许多教授对此措手不及，不知如何应对。在本期研讨会中，我们将对比两种不同的信息，旨在分享应对这类学生的策略。

THE SITUATION: Professors have to deal with the occasional nuisance in class—an angry, aggressive, or challenging student. Many professors are caught by surprise and aren’t sure how to deal with the situation. In this Clinic we’ll compare two different messages that were intended to share strategies for coping with these students.

• • •

• • •

消息 1：第一条消息由印第安纳大学制作，作为教师的资源。

MESSAGE 1: The first message was produced by Indiana University as a resource for instructors.

  • 保持冷静。放慢节奏，调整呼吸。不要表现出防御姿态。

  • Remain calm. Slow down and regularize your breathing. Don’t become defensive.

  • 不要忽视他们。尝试安抚他们的情绪。安排在课间休息或课后与他们见面。见面时，要理解学生的情绪并认真倾听。用专业且礼貌的方式交谈。

  • Don’t ignore them. Attempt to defuse their anger. Arrange to meet them during a break or after class. During the meeting, acknowledge the student’s emotions and listen. Talk in a professional and courteous manner.

对第一条信息的评论：请注意，这里没有什么出乎意料的内容——没有什么不合常理的。（如果应对难缠的学生是常识，那我们为什么还需要发布应对技巧呢？）大多数建议——“保持冷静”、“不要辩解”、“尝试化解他们的怒气”——既过于抽象，又过于显而易见，难以真正奏效。（很少有老师会认为面对问题学生应该惊慌失措。）

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 1: Notice that there’s nothing unexpected here—nothing that is uncommon sense. (And if dealing with difficult students is common sense, then why do we need to publish tips for dealing with them?) Most of the advice—”Remain calm;” “Don’t become defensive;” “Attempt to defuse their anger”—is both too abstract and too obvious to stick. (Few teachers believe that you should freak out in response to a problem student.)

• • •

• • •

消息 2：第二条消息是一位名叫艾莉森·巴克曼的教授非正式地发布到新闻组的，她想与组内的其他教师分享自己的经验：

MESSAGE 2: The second message was posted, informally, to a newsgroup by a professor named Alyson Buckman, who wanted to share her experience with other teachers in the group:

我有个学生……他经常在教室后排大声说话，尤其是在我讲课的时候。我在前面都能听到他的声音，其他同学也一样。而且，无论我说什么，他都反对。很快，同学们就开始在课堂笔记里抱怨他的行为，并提出各种应对方法，这些方法通常都是为了羞辱他。

I had a student … who talked loudly and often in the back of the class, generally when I was speaking. I could hear his comments at the front of the room, and so could everyone else. He also disagreed with me on every point I made, no matter what it was. Students began very quickly to complain about his behavior in their journals and suggest methods, generally designed to humiliate, of dealing with him.

我一开始就尝试了好几种方法，但最终还是在一天下课后，把他和他的同伙叫到讲台前，约他们到我办公室见面。我确保每次见面都有证人在场——这是共享办公空间的优势之一。我相信，那个同伙是被迫扮演这个角色的——而另一个学生只是利用他的存在来扰乱课堂秩序。

I tried several things from the beginning, but finally called he and his confidante in class up to the front at the end of class one day and scheduled appointments with me in my office. I made sure I had witnesses to these appointments as well—one of the perks of being in a shared office space. The confidante, I believe, had been trapped into that role—the other student just utilized his body as a means to disruption.

我见到那个霸凌者时，他戴着墨镜进来，一副完全挑衅的样子。我开口问道：“你为什么不告诉我房间后面发生了什么……”，他回答说：“我不同意你的说法。”我试图和他谈谈这件事，但他却沉默不语。

When I met with the bully, he came in with sunglasses on and a totally defiant behavior. I started with “Why don’t you tell me what’s going on in the back of the room …” and he responded, “I disagree with you.” I attempted to talk about this and met with silence.

直到我告诉他其他同学都在抱怨并提出解决办法，他才听进去。他的肢体语言和态度都完全变了。从那以后，我就没再和他有过节了。我对这件事的理解是，那些对老师表现出蔑视的学生，很可能会被其他同学制止。毕竟，他原本以为自己是在炫耀，结果发现同学们根本不想听也不想看。

It was not until I told him that other students were complaining and suggesting treatments for the situation that he listened. His body language totally changed as did his manner. I didn’t have a problem with him from then on. My basic understanding of this little teaching lesson was that students who display contempt for the teacher might very well be brought into check by other students. After all, he thought he was showing off for them and found that they didn’t want to hear or see it.

关于第二条信息的评论：这个故事让我们得以模拟处理问题学生的过程。我们跟随巴克曼的视角……她逐步解决了这个问题。请注意，第一条信息中的许多要点在故事中都是以展示而非叙述的方式呈现的。教授试图“化解”学生的怒气。她安排“在更私密的环境下与学生谈话”。她始终保持冷静。

COMMENTS ON MESSAGE 2: This story allows us to simulate the process of dealing with a problem student. We follow along with Buckman as she works her way through the problem. Notice that many of the bulleted points from the first message are shown, rather than told, in the story. The professor attempts to “defuse” the student’s anger. She arranges “to talk with the student in a more private setting.” She stays calm throughout.

解决方案——本质上是利用同伴压力来约束学生——既具体又出人意料。这其实很反常理。我们或许会认为问题学生不会在意同伴的看法。但我们对巴克曼感同身受，这让我们开始关心结果。比起一连串的指示，关心一个人更容易。

The solution—in essence, using peer pressure to get the student under control—is both concrete and unexpected. It’s uncommon sense. We might have expected a problem student not to care about what his peers thought. We empathize with Buckman, which makes us care about the outcome. It’s easier to care about a person than a list of bulleted instructions.

记分卡
检查清单 消息 1    消息 2
简单的 -    -
意外    -   
具体的    
可信的 -    -
情绪 -   
故事 -   

重点：几个像巴克曼教授那样的故事——比如用飞行模拟器来管教问题学生——比第一条信息中的要点列表更有趣、更有效，也更有利于教授培训。这个方案并非显而易见；十个培训部门中有九个会采用第一条信息。我们必须克制住直接跳到“技巧”而忽略故事的冲动。

PUNCH LINE: A few stories like Professor Buckman’s—flight simulators for reining in problem students—would be much more interesting and effective in training professors than the list of bullet points in Message 1. This solution is not intuitive; nine out of ten training departments would create Message 1. We must fight the temptation to skip directly to the “tips” and leave out the story.

故事的灵感：贾里德的故事

Stories as Inspiration: The Tale of Jared

上世纪90年代末，快餐巨头赛百味发起了一项宣传活动，旨在推广其新推出的一系列健康三明治。该活动以一项统计数据为核心：七款三明治的脂肪含量均低于6克。就统计数据而言，这确实不错——押韵的宣传语确实能让人更容易接受。但“7 Under 6”的宣传效果远不如赛百味接下来的宣传活动，后者聚焦于一位名叫贾里德·福格尔的大学生的非凡故事。

In the late 1990s, the fast-food giant Subway launched a campaign to tout the healthiness of a new line of sandwiches. The campaign was based on a statistic: Seven subs under six grams of fat. As far as statistics go, that’s pretty good—a spoonful of alliteration helps the medicine go down. But “7 Under 6” didn’t stick like Subway’s next campaign, which focused on the remarkable story of a college student named Jared Fogle.

贾里德的体重问题非常严重。大学三年级时，他的体重飙升至425磅（约193公斤）。他穿XXXXXXL码的衬衫，这是大码男装店里能买到的最大尺码。他的裤子腰围是60英寸（约152厘米）。

Jared had a serious weight problem. By his junior year in college, he had ballooned to 425 pounds. He wore size XXXXXXL shirts, the largest size available in big-and-tall clothing stores. His pants had a 60-inch waist.

贾里德的父亲是印第安纳波利斯的一名全科医生，多年来一直告诫儿子注意体重，但收效甚微。直到十二月的一天，贾里德的室友——一位医学预科生——注意到贾里德的脚踝肿胀。他正确地诊断出贾里德患有水肿，这是一种由于血液无法输送足够液体而导致体内水分滞留的疾病；水肿通常会导致糖尿病、心脏问题，甚至过早发生心脏病。贾里德的父亲告诉他，以他的体重和整体健康状况来看，他可能活不过三十五岁。

Jared’s father, a general practitioner in Indianapolis, had been warning his son about his weight for years without much success. Then, one day in December, Jared’s roommate, a premed major, noticed that Jared’s ankles were swollen. He correctly diagnosed edema, a condition in which the body retains fluid because the blood can’t transport enough liquid; it often leads to diabetes, heart problems, and even early heart attacks. Jared’s father told him that, given his weight and general health, he might not live past thirty-five.

在十二月住院治疗后的春假期间，贾里德下定决心减肥。受到“7 Under 6”活动的激励，他第一次尝试了火鸡俱乐部三明治。他很喜欢这种三明治，最终他制定了自己的全赛百味饮食计划：午餐吃一个一英尺长的蔬菜三明治，晚餐吃一个六英寸的火鸡三明治。

By the spring break following his December hospital visit, Jared had decided to slim down. Motivated by the “7 Under 6” campaign, he had his first turkey club. He liked the sandwich, and eventually he developed his own, all-Subway diet: a foot-long veggie sub for lunch and a six-inch turkey sub for dinner.

他把“赛百味减肥法”称为“赛百味减肥法”，三个月后，他站上了体重秤。结果显示330磅。仅仅三个月，他就靠吃赛百味减掉了近100磅。他又坚持了几个月，有时一天就能减掉一磅。一旦身体允许，他就开始尽可能多地步行——不再坐公交车去上课，甚至在百货商店里也选择走楼梯而不是乘扶梯。

After three months of the “Subway diet,” as he called it, he stepped on the scale. It read 330 pounds. He had dropped almost 100 pounds in three months by eating at Subway. He stuck with the diet for several more months, sometimes losing as much as a pound a day. As soon as his health permitted, he began walking as much as he could—not taking the bus to classes and even walking up stairs rather than taking the department-store escalator.

贾里德鼓舞人心的减肥故事如何成为全国瞩目的焦点，要从1999年4月《印第安纳日报学生版》上发表的一篇文章说起。这篇文章的作者是瑞恩·科尔曼，他是贾里德以前的宿舍室友。科尔曼在贾里德减肥成功后见到他，几乎认不出他来。他动情地描述了贾里德肥胖时的经历：

The story of how Jared’s inspiring weight-loss became a national phenomenon begins with an article that appeared in the Indiana Daily Student in April 1999. It was written by Ryan Coleman, a former dormmate of Jared’s. Coleman saw Jared after he had lost weight and almost didn’t recognize him. He wrote movingly about what it was like for Jared to be obese:

福格尔选课时，不像大多数学生那样以教授或上课时间为标准，而是以教室座位是否足够容纳自己为标准。

When Fogle registered for a class, he didn’t base his choice on professor or class time like most students. He based which classes to register on whether he could fit into the classroom seats.

当大多数人担心能否在校园附近找到停车位时，福格尔担心能否找到一个附近没有其他车辆停放的停车位——他需要额外的空间来打开驾驶员侧车门以便下车。

When most folks worried whether they could find a parking spot close to campus, Fogle worried whether he could find a parking spot without a car already parked nearby—he needed the extra room in order to open the driver’s side door so he could get out.

文章最后引用了贾里德的话：“赛百味拯救了我的生命，让我重新开始。我永远也无法报答这份恩情。”这或许是快餐连锁店首次被认为以如此积极的方式改变了一个人的生活。

The article ended with this quote from Jared: “Subway helped save my life and start over. I can’t ever repay that.” This may have been the first time that a fast-food chain was credited with transforming someone’s life in a profoundly positive way.

后来， 《男士健康》杂志的一位记者，当时正在撰写一篇题为“疯狂有效的减肥方法”的文章，碰巧看到了《印第安纳日报》关于贾里德的报道，于是他在文章中简要介绍了一种“赛百味三明治减肥法”。文章没有提到贾里德的名字，甚至没有提到他是在哪里买的三明治；只是笼统地提到了“赛百味三明治”。

Then a reporter at Men’s Health magazine, who was writing an article called “Crazy Diets That Work,” happened to see the Indiana Daily article about Jared, and he included a blurb about a “subway sandwich diet.” The article didn’t mention Jared’s name or even where he had bought the sandwiches; it simply referred generically to “subway sandwiches.”

这条线索的关键人物是一位名叫鲍勃·奥克维亚的赛百味加盟店老板，他偶然发现了这篇文章，觉得它很有潜力。他特意抽出时间联系了赛百味芝加哥广告公司的创意总监理查德·科德，建议他看看这篇文章。科德说：“我一开始觉得有点好笑，但我们还是跟进了这件事。”

The key link in the chain was a Subway franchise owner named Bob Ocwieja, who spotted the article and thought it had potential. He took time out of his schedule to track down the creative director at Subway’s Chicago ad agency, a man named Richard Coad, and suggested that he check out the article. Coad says, “I kind of laughed at first, but we followed up on it.”

贾里德是减肥故事中的英雄，但奥克维耶亚和科德才是我们创意故事中的英雄。奥克维耶亚的英雄之处在于他发现了潜力。在故事中，Coad 是一位英雄，因为他投入资源进行后续调查。

Jared is the hero of the weight-loss story, but Ocwieja and Coad are the heroes of our idea story. Ocwieja is a hero for spotting potential in a story, and Coad is a hero for spending the resources to follow up on it.

科德和广告公司哈尔·里尼的总裁巴里·克劳斯派了一名实习生前往印第安纳州布卢明顿，任务很模糊：找到那个神秘的“三明治减肥男”，还要查清楚他吃的究竟是哪家的三明治。结果很可能是，贾里德一直在弗洛的潜艇三明治店用餐。

Coad and Barry Krause, the president of the advertising agency called Hal Riney, sent an intern to Bloomington, Indiana, with vague instructions to find the mystery sandwich-diet guy—and also to find out whose sandwiches he had been eating. It could easily have turned out that Jared had been dining at Flo’s Sub Shop.

实习生不太确定自己该做什么。他暂定的计划是先到布卢明顿，翻翻黄页，然后挨家挨户拜访镇上的三明治店。幸运的是，事情并没有那么复杂。实习生拜访的第一家店是离校园很近的赛百味加盟店。他开始描述那位神秘食客，结果刚说了一句话，柜台店员就说：“哦，那是贾里德。他每天都来。”

The intern wasn’t exactly sure what he was supposed to do. His tentative plan was to show up in Bloomington, look through the Yellow Pages, and start dropping by the town’s sub shops. Fortunately for the intern, the operation never became that complex. The first sub shop the intern visited was a Subway franchise close to campus. He launched into his description of the mystery eater, and about one sentence into the description the counter worker said, “Oh, that’s Jared. He comes here every day.”

实习生凯旋而归。贾里德确有其人，而且他确实靠吃赛百味瘦了不少。公司心想：我们手里握着一个绝佳的新闻素材。

The intern returned victorious. Jared was real, and he’d shed pounds by eating at Subway. The agency thought, We’ve got a great story on our hands.

就在这时，杰瑞德的故事又遇到了新的阻碍。广告公司总裁克劳斯打电话给赛百味的营销总监，想跟他讲述杰瑞德的故事，但这位营销总监却不以为然。他刚到赛百味工作不久，之前在另一家快餐公司工作过。“这种事我见怪不怪了，”他说，“快餐做不出健康食品。”这位营销总监更倾向于发起一场以赛百味三明治口味为重点的营销活动。

And that’s when the Jared story hit another hurdle. Ad agency president Krause called Subway’s marketing director to unveil the tale of Jared, but the marketing director wasn’t impressed. He had just started his job at Subway, having previously worked for another fast-food company. “I’ve seen that before,” he said. “Fast foods can’t do healthy.” The marketing director preferred to launch a campaign focused on the taste of Subway’s sandwiches.

为了安抚克劳斯，这位导演还是把杰瑞德的营销方案告诉了赛百味的律师。不出所料，律师们表示杰瑞德的营销活动行不通。他们认为这似乎涉及医疗方面的宣传，可能会带来法律责任等等。避免承担任何责任的唯一办法就是发布免责声明，比如“我们不推荐这种饮食方式。请先咨询医生。”

To satisfy Krause, though, the director ran the Jared campaign idea by Subway’s lawyers. The lawyers, predictably, said a Jared campaign couldn’t be done. It would appear to be making a medical claim that might create a liability, blah blah blah. The only way to avoid any liability was to run disclaimers like “We don’t recommend this diet. See your doctor first.”

这个想法似乎已经胎死腹中。但克劳斯和科德还没准备好……放弃吧。像许多特许经营公司一样，赛百味的广告宣传分为两个层面：全国层面和区域层面。虽然赛百味全国总部否决了贾里德的提议，但一些区域加盟商对这个故事很感兴趣，并愿意用区域广告预算投放广告。

The idea seemed dead. But Krause and Coad weren’t ready to give up. Subway, like many franchise-based firms, runs ad campaigns at two levels: national and regional. While the national Subway office had vetoed Jared, some regional Subway franchisees expressed interest in the story and were willing to run the ads using regional advertising money.

接下来又出现了另一个难题：加盟商通常不负责制作广告本身，他们只负责在各自区域投放广告。广告费用一般由总部承担。那么，谁来支付贾里德的广告费用呢？

Then came another hurdle: Franchisees didn’t usually pay to make the actual commercials; they just paid to run the commercials in their regions. The commercials were generally funded by the national office. So who would pay for the Jared commercials?

克劳斯决定免费制作这些广告。他说：“这是我职业生涯中第一次也是唯一一次，我同意拍摄一个不收取任何费用的广告。”

Krause decided to make the spots for free. He said, “For the first and only time in my career, I gave the go-ahead to shoot an ad that we weren’t going to be paid for.”

这则广告于2000年1月1日首次播出——恰逢每年新年伊始，各种减肥计划层出不穷。广告中，贾里德站在自家门前。“这位是贾里德，”旁白说道，“他以前重达425磅（约193公斤）”——画面中出现了一张贾里德穿着他以前那条60英寸腰围裤子的照片——“但如今，他体重只有180磅（约82公斤），这都要归功于他所谓的‘赛百味减肥法’。”旁白描述了贾里德的饮食计划，最后总结道：“这种饮食计划，再加上大量的步行，对贾里德来说非常有效。我们并不是说这种方法适合所有人。在开始任何减肥计划之前，您都应该咨询医生。但这种方法对贾里德确实有效。”

The ad first ran on January 1, 2000—just in time for the annual epidemic of diet-related New Year’s resolutions. It showed Jared in front of his home. “This is Jared,” the announcer said. “He used to weigh 425 pounds”—we see a photo of Jared in his old 60-inch-waist pants—“but today he weighs 180 thanks to what he calls the Subway diet.” The announcer describes Jared’s meal plan, then concludes, “That, combined with a lot of walking, worked for Jared. We’re not saying this is for everyone. You should check with your doctor before starting any diet program. But it worked for Jared.”

克劳斯说，第二天早上电话就响个不停。《今日美国》、ABC和福克斯新闻都打来了电话。第三天，奥普拉也打来了电话。“这些年来，我接触过很多想博取媒体关注的营销人员，”克劳斯说，“但没人靠给奥普拉打电话就能成功。我职业生涯中唯一一次成功打动奥普拉，是和贾里德一起的时候，奥普拉主动联系了我们。”

The next day, Krause said, the phones started ringing in the morning and didn’t let up. USA Today called, ABC and Fox News called. On the third day, Oprah called. “I’ve talked to a lot of marketers over the years who wanted to get media attention” Krause said. “No one ever got anywhere by lavishing calls on Oprah. The only time I’ve succeeded in my career with Oprah was with Jared, and Oprah called us.”

几天后，赛百味全国总部致电克劳斯，询问这则广告是否可以在全国播出。1999年，赛百味的销售额持平。2000年，销售额增长了18%，2001年又增长了16%。当时，其他一些规模小得多的三明治连锁店，例如Schlotzsky's和Quiznos，年增长率约为7%。

A few days later, Subway’s national office called Krause, asking if the ad could be aired nationally. In 1999, Subway’s sales were flat. In 2000, sales jumped 18 percent, and they jumped another 16 percent in 2001. At the time, other (much smaller) sandwich chains such as Schlotzsky’s and Quiznos were growing at about 7 percent per year.

• • •

• • •

T贾里德的故事确实具有一定的模拟价值。它让人相对容易地想象出如果采用赛百味减肥法会是什么样子——午餐点餐、晚餐点餐，以及两餐之间的步行。但这个故事与其说是飞行模拟器，不如说是一则励志演讲。这个体型庞大的家伙靠着自己发明的减肥方法减掉了245磅！哇！这个故事能给那些还在为减掉最后10磅而苦苦挣扎的人带来莫大的鼓舞。

The Jared story has a morsel of simulation value. It makes it relatively easy to imagine what it would be like to embrace the Subway diet—the lunch order, the dinner order, the walking in between. But this story is not so much a flight simulator as it is a pep talk. This huge guy lost 245 pounds on a diet that he invented! Wow! The story provides a good kick in the pants for anyone who’s been struggling to lose that last 10 pounds.

就像本章开头的护士故事一样，这个故事也同样能引起情感共鸣。即使是那些对节食不感兴趣的瘦子，也会被贾里德的故事所激励。他克服了重重困难，凭借毅力最终取得了成功。而这正是故事带来的第二个重要价值：激励。激励能够驱动行动，正如模拟情境一样。

Like the nurse story that opened the chapter, this story, too, has emotional resonance. Even skinny people who aren’t interested in dieting will be inspired by Jared’s tale. He fought big odds and prevailed through perseverance. And this is the second major payoff that stories provide: inspiration. Inspiration drives action, as does simulation.

顺便一提，请注意这项活动的效果比“7 Under 6”活动好得多。两项活动的目标相同——都强调营养丰富、低脂三明治的普及，也都承诺可以帮助减肥。但一项活动只​​是小有成效，而另一项活动却引起了轰动。

By the way, note how much better this campaign functions than the “7 Under 6” campaign. Both campaigns are mining the same turf—they both highlight the availability of nutritious, low-fat sandwiches. They both hold out the promise of weight loss. But one campaign was a modest success and one was a sensation.

我们在本书中论证的观点——我们希望您现在已经相信了——是，您可以事先预测贾里德会在这两场竞选中获胜。

What we have argued in this book—and we hope we’ve made you a believer by now—is that you could have predicted in advance that Jared would be the winner in these two campaigns.

请注意 Jared 的故事在 SUCCESs 清单上的表现如何：

Note how well the Jared story does on the SUCCESs checklist:

  • 很简单：吃三明治就能减肥。（坦白说，这可能过于简单化了，因为加了额外蛋黄酱的肉丸三明治并不能帮助你减肥。）

  • It’s simple: Eat subs and lose weight. (It may be oversimplified, frankly, since the meatball sub with extra mayo won’t help you lose weight.)

  • 这太出乎意料了：一个男人竟然靠吃快餐瘦了很多！这个故事颠覆了我们对快餐的固有印象，这种印象更符合胖胖的杰瑞德，而不是瘦骨嶙峋的杰瑞德。

  • It’s unexpected: A guy lost a ton of weight by eating fast food! This story violates our schema of fast food, a schema that’s more consistent with the picture of a fat Jared than a skinny Jared.

  • 这是实实在在的：想想那些超大的裤子，想想巨大的损失身材臃肿，饮食以特定的三明治为主。这与其说是抽象概念，不如说更像是一则伊索寓言。

  • It’s concrete: Think of the oversized pants, the massive loss of girth, the diet composed of particular sandwiches. It’s much more like an Aesop fable than an abstraction.

  • 这很可信：它和帕姆·拉芬的反吸烟运动一样，都带有反权威的真诚。那个穿60英寸裤子的人居然在给我们提供饮食建议！

  • It’s credible: It has the same kind of antiauthority truthfulness that we saw with the Pam Laffin antismoking campaign. The guy who wore 60-inch pants is giving us diet advice!

  • 它触动人心：我们更关心的是杰瑞德这个人，而不是群体。它也符合马斯洛的需求层次理论——讲述的是一个男人在一家三明治店的帮助下实现自身潜能的故事。

  • It’s emotional: We care more about an individual, Jared, than about a mass. And it taps Maslow’s hierarchy—it’s about a guy who reached his potential with the help of a sub shop.

  • 这是一个故事：主人公克服重重困难最终取得胜利。它激励着我们其他人也这样做。

  • It’s a story: Our protagonist overcomes big odds to triumph. It inspires the rest of us to do the same.

相比之下，我们来看看清单上的“7 Under 6”。它很简单，但请注意，它的核心信息远不如贾里德的“Subway”那么吸引人。它的核心信息是“我们有各种低脂三明治”，而贾里德的则是“吃赛百味，减肥，改变人生”。前者推销的是钻头，后者教你如何挂孩子的照片。

By contrast, let’s size up “7 Under 6” on the checklist. It’s simple, but notice that it has a much less compelling core message. Its core message is “We’ve got a variety of low-fat sandwiches,” versus Jared’s “Eat Subway, lose weight, change your life.” The first message sells drill bits; the second tells you how to hang your kid’s picture.

《7 Under 6》远没有那么出人意料。贾里德的故事之所以震撼人心，是因为它打破了快餐就是高脂肪食物的根深蒂固的观念。如果《7 Under 6》也在挑战同样的观念，那它表达的方式就显得间接得多。

“7 Under 6” is far less unexpected. Jared’s story packs a wallop because it violates the powerful schema that fast food is fatty. If “7 Under 6” is attacking the same schema, it makes the point much more tangentially.

“7 Under 6”这个说法并不具体。数字本身并不具体。它之所以可信，仅仅是因为它的标准并不高——我们大多数人听到一个三明治的脂肪含量低于6克时都不会感到震惊，所以我们不需要太多说服。它缺乏感染力，也算不上一个故事。

“7 Under 6” isn’t concrete. Numbers aren’t concrete. It’s credible only because it hasn’t set the bar very high—not many of us will be floored to hear that a sandwich has less than 6 grams of fat, so we don’t need much convincing. It’s not emotional, and it’s not a story.

任何读者只要把这两份耗资数百万美元的全国性广告宣传活动对照SUCCESs清单，就能分析出正确的方案。（不过，不读这本书的人可能就没那么精明了。那位毕生致力于让创意落地生根的全国广告总监，对Jared的故事却避之不及。）

Any reader of this book could have analyzed these two multimillion-dollar national ad campaigns and chosen the right one, just by laying them side by side on the SUCCESs checklist. (Note, though, that nonreaders might not be so savvy. The national advertising director, who had a lifetime of experience in trying to make ideas stick, wanted to walk away from the Jared story.)

贾里德的故事另一个引人入胜之处在于，有多少人为此付出了艰辛的努力才使其成为现实。看看有多少看似不可能的人参与其中吧。为了让杰瑞德登上电视荧屏，一系列事件必须发生：赛百味门店经理必须足够积极主动，才能将杂志文章提交给创意总监。（你的一线员工会这么做吗？）创意总监必须足够精明，才能将资源投入到这项原本可能徒劳无功的任务中。（这项任务真的能带来丰厚的回报吗？）广告公司总裁必须免费制作广告，因为他知道自己找到了一个绝佳的机会。（免费！）赛百味全国营销团队必须放下身段，意识到他们之前没有早点启用杰瑞德是个错误。

Another compelling aspect of the Jared tale is how many people had to work hard to make it a reality. Look at how many unlikely events had to take place in order for Jared to hit TV: The Subway store manager had to be proactive enough to bring the magazine article to the creative director’s attention. (Would your frontline people do this?) The creative director had to be savvy enough to invest resources in what could have been a fruitless errand. (Was this really an errand with a good return on investment?) The president of the ad agency had to make the spot for free because he knew he was onto something big. (Free!) The national Subway marketing team had to swallow its pride and realize that it had made a mistake by not embracing Jared earlier.

这些并非无关紧要的小事。这种行为也并非惯例。有多少伟大的创意因为中间环节——创意源头与最终实现者之间的那个环节——的疏忽而夭折？在正常情况下，加盟店老板听到贾里德的故事会觉得很有趣。他会把这个故事贴在公告栏上，贴在通往洗手间的走廊墙上，给员工们带来欢乐。而这，也正是贾里德故事的高潮所在。

These are not trivial actions. This behavior is not routine. How many great ideas have been extinguished because someone in the middle—a link between the source of the idea and its eventual outlet—dropped the ball? In the normal world, a franchise owner would have been amused by the Jared tale. He would have tacked it up on the bulletin board, on the wall of the hallway leading to the bathroom, as a source of amusement for his employees. And that would have been the pinnacle of the Jared tale.

贾里德提醒我们，我们并非总是需要创造出那些“吸睛”的想法。发现它们往往更容易也更有用。试想一下，如果历史老师们勤于分享那些行之有效的教学方法，那会怎样？如果我们能够指望非营利组织的志愿者们时刻留意那些可能启发组织内其他成员的象征性事件或经历，那又会怎样？如果我们能够指望我们的老板们敢于冒险尝试一些重要的想法，那又会怎样？你不必非得喜欢赛百味三明治才能欣赏将一个伟大想法付诸实践的过程。

Jared reminds us that we don’t always have to create sticky ideas. Spotting them is often easier and more useful. What if history teachers were diligent about sharing teaching methods that worked brilliantly in reaching students? What if we could count on the volunteers of nonprofit organizations to be on the lookout for symbolic events or encounters that might inspire other people in the organization? What if we could count on our bosses to take a gamble on important ideas? You don’t have to admire Subway sandwiches to admire the process of bringing a great idea to life.

观察的艺术

The Art of Spotting

我们如何才能确保不会让一个绝妙的想法，一个潜在的“杰瑞德”（指优秀创意）从我们眼皮底下溜走？发现并不难，但也并非易事。创意不会主动吸引我们的注意。我们必须……有意识地寻找合适的人。那么，我们究竟应该寻找什么呢？

How do we make sure that we don’t let a great idea, a potential Jared, float right under our nose? Spotting isn’t hard, but it isn’t natural, either. Ideas don’t flag themselves to get our attention. We have to consciously look for the right ones. So what is it, exactly, that we should look for?

在本书的引言中，我们讨论了一项研究，该研究表明，经过培训掌握经典广告模板的普通人制作的广告质量远胜于未经培训的群体。正如一些广告模板已被证明行之有效一样，一些故事模板也已被证明行之有效。学习这些模板能极大地提升我们的识别能力。

In the introduction of the book, we discussed a study showing that laypeople who’d been trained to use classic ad templates could create ads that were vastly superior to those developed by an untrained group. Just as there are ad templates that have been proven effective, so, too, there are story templates that have been proven effective. Learning the templates gives our spotting ability a huge boost.

沃伦·巴菲特喜欢讲述罗斯·布鲁姆金的故事。布鲁姆金是他投资的一家企业的经理。她是一位俄罗斯女性，23岁时，想方设法绕过边防人员来到美国。她不会说英语，也没有接受过正规教育。

Warren Buffett likes to tell the story of Rose Blumkin, a woman who manages one of the businesses that he invested in. Blumkin is a Russian woman who, at age twenty-three, finagled her way past a border guard to come to America. She couldn’t speak English and had received no formal schooling.

布鲁姆金于1937年用她积攒的500美元创办了一家家具店。近五十年后，她的家具店年营业额达到了1亿美元。百岁高龄时，她仍然每周七天都亲自在店里忙碌。她甚至把百岁生日派对推迟到店铺关门后的晚上举行。有一次，她的竞争对手起诉她违反公平贸易协议，因为她的价格远低于其他竞争对手。他们认为她亏本销售是为了把他们挤出市场。巴菲特说：“她向法庭证明，她可以以极低的价格盈利地销售地毯，并向法官出售了价值1400美元的地毯。”

Blumkin started a furniture business in 1937 with $500 that she had saved. Almost fifty years later, her furniture store was doing $100 million in annual revenue. At age one hundred, she was still on the floor seven days a week. She actually postponed her one-hundredth birthday party until an evening when the store was closed. At one point her competitors sued her for violating the fair-trade agreement because her prices were so much lower. They thought she was selling at a loss in order to put them out of business. Buffett says, “She demonstrated to the court that she could profitably sell carpet at a huge discount and sold the judge $1,400 worth of carpet.”

罗斯·布鲁姆金的故事并非出自《心灵鸡汤》系列丛书，但它与该系列丛书颇有几分相似。《心灵鸡汤》系列丛书已成为出版界的奇迹，销量超过430万册，目前在售的《心灵鸡汤》系列丛书共有37种，包括《心灵鸡汤：父亲篇》、《心灵鸡汤：护士篇》和《心灵鸡汤：纳斯卡赛车篇》。

The story of Rose Blumkin isn’t from the book Chicken Soup for the Soul, but it could be. The Chicken Soup series has become a publishing phenomenon, with more than 4.3 million books sold and thirty-seven Chicken Soup titles in print, including Chicken Soup for the Father’s Soul, Chicken Soup for the Nurse’s Soul, and Chicken Soup for the NASCAR Soul.

《心灵鸡汤》系列丛书讲述的是鼓舞人心的故事——这些故事能够振奋人心、激励人心、赋予人力量。从这个意义上讲，这些故事与都市传说截然相反，都市传说往往会强化人们对世界的愤世嫉俗、悲观或偏执的看法。（陌生人会偷走你的肾！）Snapple支持三K党！麦当劳在汉堡里放虫子！

The Chicken Soup books traffic in inspirational stories—stories that uplift, motivate, energize. In that sense, these stories are the opposite of urban legends, which tend to reinforce a cynical, pessimistic, or paranoid view of the world. (Strangers will steal your kidneys! Snapple supports the KKK! McDonald’s puts worms in its burgers!)

这些故事最令人惊奇之处在于，作者们并非原创作者，而是发现并收集了这些故事。我们想要了解这些励志故事的魅力所在。我们仔细研读了数百个励志故事——既有来自《心灵鸡汤》系列的，也有来自其他地方的——寻找它们之间潜在的共性。

What’s amazing about these stories is that the authors didn’t write them—they merely spotted and collected them. We wanted to understand what made these inspirational stories tick. We pored over inspirational stories—hundreds of stories, both from Chicken Soup and elsewhere—looking for underlying similarities.

亚里士多德认为戏剧情节主要有四种类型：简单悲剧、简单幸运、复杂悲剧和复杂幸运。编剧大师罗伯特·麦基在他的书中列出了二十五种故事类型：现代史诗、幻灭情节等等。当我们整理完一大堆励志故事——一个范围小得多的领域——后，我们得出结论：基本情节有三种：挑战情节、联结情节和创造力情节。

Aristotle believed there were four primary dramatic plots: Simple Tragic, Simple Fortunate, Complex Tragic, and Complex Fortunate. Robert McKee, the screenwriting guru, lists twenty-five types of stories in his book: the modern epic, the disillusionment plot, and so on. When we finished sorting through a big pile of inspirational stories—a much narrower domain—we came to the conclusion that there are three basic plots: the Challenge plot, the Connection plot, and the Creativity plot.

这三种基本情节模式可以用来对原版《心灵鸡汤》系列中超过 80% 的故事进行分类。更令人惊讶的是，它们也可以用来对《人物》杂志刊登的超过 60% 的非名人故事进行分类。如果一个普通人能登上《人物》杂志，通常是因为他/她有一个鼓舞人心的故事可以激励我们其他人。如果我们想要激励和启发他人，这三种情节模式就是很好的起点。（顺便说一句，如果你比较挑剔，觉得《心灵鸡汤》系列过于煽情而非鼓舞人心，你仍然会发现这三种情节模板的价值。你可以适当降低故事的感染力。）

These three basic plots can be used to classify more than 80 percent of the stories that appear in the original Chicken Soup collection. Perhaps more surprisingly, they can also be used to classify more than 60 percent of the stories published by People magazine about people who aren’t celebrities. If an average person makes it into People, it’s usually because he or she has an inspiring story for the rest of us. If our goal is to energize and inspire others, these three plots are the right place to start. (By the way, if you’re a more jaded type of person who finds the Chicken Soup series treacly rather than inspirational, you’ll still find value in the three plot templates. You can always turn down the volume on the plots a bit.)

挑战剧情

THE CHALLENGE PLOT

大卫和歌利亚的故事是经典的挑战情节。主人公克服了巨大的挑战并最终取得了成功。大卫用自制的弹弓击倒了巨人。挑战情节有很多种变体，我们都耳熟能详：弱者逆袭的故事、白手起家的故事、凭借坚强意志战胜逆境的故事。

The story of David and Goliath is the classic Challenge plot. A protagonist overcomes a formidable challenge and succeeds. David fells a giant with his homemade slingshot. There are variations of the Challenge plot that we all recognize: the underdog story, the rags-to-riches story, the triumph of sheer willpower over adversity.

挑战型故事的关键在于，主人公面临的障碍似乎难以逾越。杰瑞德减肥到180磅是挑战型故事，而他210磅的邻居腰围减掉一英寸则不是。我们每个人脑海中都充斥着大量的挑战型故事：美国冰球队在1980年冬奥会上击败夺冠热门苏联队；阿拉莫之战；霍雷肖·阿尔杰的励志故事；美国独立战争；奔腾年代；《星球大战》系列电影；兰斯·阿姆斯特朗；罗莎·帕克斯。

The key element of a Challenge plot is that the obstacles seem daunting to the protagonist. Jared slimming down to 180 pounds is a Challenge plot. Jared’s 210-pound neighbor shaving an inch off his waistline is not. We’ve all got a huge mental inventory of Challenge plot stories. The American hockey team beating the heavily favored Russians in the 1980 Olympics. The Alamo. Horatio Alger tales. The American Revolution. Seabiscuit. The Star Wars movies. Lance Armstrong. Rosa Parks.

即使挑战性的故事远不如这些例子那样戏剧化和具有历史意义，它们依然鼓舞人心。罗斯·布鲁姆金的故事中并没有出现任何名人。挑战性的故事之所以鼓舞人心，是因为它们激发了我们的毅力和勇气。它们激励我们更加努力，迎接新的挑战，克服重重障碍。不知何故，当你听说罗斯·布鲁姆金将她的百岁生日派对推迟到商店关门后的晚上时，你就会更容易清理自己的车库。挑战性的故事激励我们采取行动。

Challenge plots are inspiring even when they’re much less dramatic and historical than these examples. The Rose Blumkin story doesn’t involve a famous character. Challenge plots are inspiring in a defined way. They inspire us by appealing to our perseverance and courage. They make us want to work harder, take on new challenges, overcome obstacles. Somehow, after you’ve heard about Rose Blumkin postponing her one-hundredth birthday party until an evening when her store was closed, it’s easier to clean out your garage. Challenge plots inspire us to act.

连接图

THE CONNECTION PLOT

如今，“好撒玛利亚人”指的是在他人危难之际自愿伸出援手的人。圣经中关于好撒玛利亚人的原著故事固然符合这一定义，但其内涵却更为深刻。

Today the phrase “good Samaritan” refers to someone who voluntarily helps others in times of distress. The original story of the Good Samaritan from the Bible is certainly consistent with this definition, but it’s even more profound.

故事的开头是一位律师来问耶稣如何才能上天堂。这位律师与其说是想向耶稣学习，不如说是想试探他。耶稣问律师他认为答案是什么，律师的回答中包含了“要爱人如己”的道理。耶稣接受了律师的回答。然后，律师（或许是想尽量减少自己需要爱的人的数量）问道：“那么，谁是我的邻舍呢？”

The story begins with a lawyer who approached Jesus with a question about how to get to heaven. The lawyer was more interested in testing Jesus than in learning from him. When Jesus asked the lawyer what he thought the answer was, the lawyer gave a reply that included the notion “You shall love your neighbor as yourself.” Jesus accepted the lawyer’s answer. Then the lawyer (perhaps wanting to limit the number of people he’s on the hook to love) says, “And who is my neighbor?”

耶稣对此讲了一个故事：

In response, Jesus told a story:

“有一个人从耶路撒冷下到耶利哥，途中落入强盗手中。他们剥去他的衣服，把他打得半死，然后扬长而去。

“A man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, when he fell into the hands of robbers. They stripped him of his clothes, beat him and went away, leaving him half dead.

“一位祭司恰巧从这条路经过，看见那人，就从路的另一边过去了。同样，一位利未人来到那里，看见他，也从路的另一边过去了。”

“A priest happened to be going down the same road, and when he saw the man, he passed by on the other side. So too, a Levite, when he came to the place and saw him, passed by on the other side.

“但有一个撒玛利亚人路过那里，看见那人，就动了慈心，上前用油和酒倒在他的伤口上，包扎好了，扶他骑上自己的驴，带他到客店里照顾他。第二天，他拿出两个银币交给店主，说：‘请你好好照顾他，等我回来的时候，我会偿还你所有的额外费用。’”

“But a Samaritan, as he traveled, came where the man was; and when he saw him, he took pity on him. He went to him and bandaged his wounds, pouring on oil and wine. Then he put the man on his own donkey, took him to an inn and took care of him. The next day he took out two silver coins and gave them to the innkeeper. ‘Look after him,’ he said, ‘and when I return, I will reimburse you for any extra expense you may have.’

“你认为这三个人中，谁是落入强盗手中的那个人的邻居？”

“Which of these three do you think was a neighbor to the man who fell into the hands of robbers?”

律师回答说：“是那个怜悯他的人。”

The lawyer replied, “The one who had mercy on him.”

耶稣对他说：“你去照样做吧。”

Jesus told him, “Go and do likewise.”

对现代读者而言，这个故事缺少一些背景信息。故事中的撒玛利亚人并非只是个好人。他跨越了巨大的社会鸿沟，帮助了受伤的人。当时，撒玛利亚人和犹太人（故事中的其他主要人物）之间存在着极大的敌意。如果用现代的例子来类比撒玛利亚人被排斥的处境，或许可以比作“无神论的摩托车帮派成员”。这个故事的寓意很明确：好邻居会展现仁慈和同情，而且不仅仅局限于自己群体的人。

What’s missing from this tale, for modern-day readers, is a bit of context. The Samaritan in the story was not simply a nice guy. He was a nice guy crossing a huge social gulf in helping the wounded man. At the time, there was tremendous hostility between Samaritans and Jews (all the other main characters in the story). A modern-day analogy to the outcast status of the Samaritan might be an “atheist biker gang member.” The lesson of the story is clear: Good neighbors show mercy and compassion, and not just to people in their own group.

这就是“联系”情节的精髓所在。它讲述的是人们建立起一种跨越鸿沟的关系——种族、阶级、民族、宗教、人口统计或其他方面的鸿沟。“联系”情节不必像“好撒玛利亚人”那样涉及生死攸关的问题。这种联系可以像一瓶可乐那样微不足道，就像著名的“恶棍乔·格林”广告中那样。一个瘦弱的年轻白人一位粉丝偶遇一位身材高大的著名黑人运动员。一瓶可乐将他们联系在一起。这并非“好撒玛利亚人”的故事，但显然是“联系”式的情节。

This is what a Connection plot is all about. It’s a story about people who develop a relationship that bridges a gap—racial, class, ethnic, religious, demographic, or otherwise. The Connection plot doesn’t have to deal with life-and-death stakes, as does the Good Samaritan. The connection can be as trivial as a bottle of a Coke, as in the famous Mean Joe Greene commercial. A scrawny young white fan encounters a towering famous black athlete. A bottle of Coke links them. It ain’t the Good Samaritan, but it’s clearly a Connection plot.

人际关系情节也非常适合爱情故事——想想《罗密欧与朱丽叶》（或者史上票房最高的电影《泰坦尼克号》） 。所有人际关系情节都能从社会层面启发我们。它们让我们想要帮助他人，更加包容他人，与他人合作，爱他人。人际关系情节是《心灵鸡汤》系列中最常见的情节类型。

Connection plots are also fabulous for romance stories—think of Romeo and Juliet (or the top-grossing movie of all time, Titanic). All Connection plots inspire us in social ways. They make us want to help others, be more tolerant of others, work with others, love others. The Connection plot is the most common kind of plot found in the Chicken Soup series.

挑战型情节讲述的是克服挑战的故事，而关系型情节则侧重于我们与他人的关系。如果你要在公司圣诞派对上讲故事，最好使用关系型情节。如果你要在新项目启动派对上讲故事，则应该选择挑战型情节。

Where Challenge plots involve overcoming challenges, Connection plots are about our relationships with other people. If you’re telling a story at the company Christmas party, it’s probably best to use the Connection plot. If you’re telling a story at the kickoff party for a new project, go with the Challenge plot.

创意情节

THE CREATIVITY PLOT

第三种主要的励志故事类型是“创意故事”。其原型可能是苹果砸在牛顿头上，启发他提出万有引力理论的故事。“创意故事”讲述的是某人取得思维突破、解决长期存在的难题，或者以创新的方式解决问题。它类似于《百战天龙》的情节。

The third major type of inspirational story is the Creativity plot. The prototype might be the story of the apple that falls on Newton’s head, inspiring his theory of gravity. The Creativity plot involves someone making a mental breakthrough, solving a long-standing puzzle, or attacking a problem in an innovative way. It’s the MacGyver plot.

英格索兰是一家生产工业研磨机等并不引人注目的产品的巨型公司，这些研磨机用于汽车修理厂打磨车身。历史上，英格索兰在新产品上市方面一直进展缓慢。一位员工对平均长达四年的产品生命周期感到沮丧，他说：“我们推出一款新产品所花费的时间比我们国家打赢二战的时间还要长。”

Ingersoll-Rand is a giant company that makes nonsexy products such as industrial grinders, used in auto shops to sand down auto bodies. Historically, Ingersoll-Rand had been slow at bringing new products to market. One employee, frustrated by the average four-year product life cycle, said, “It was taking us longer to introduce a new product than it took our nation to fight World War II.”

英格索兰公司决定采取措施，改变其缓慢的研发周期。公司成立了一个项目团队，目标是在一年内研发出一款新型研磨机——比通常所需时间缩短了四分之一。按照标准的组织文化理论，成功的可能性微乎其微。然而，研磨机团队做对了许多事情，包括运用故事来强调团队的新态度。以及文化因素。例如，其中一个故事就涉及到一个关键的决定：新研磨机的外壳是用塑料还是金属制成的。塑料材质对顾客来说会更舒适，但它的耐用性能否与金属媲美呢？

Ingersoll-Rand decided to do something about the slow development cycle. The company created a project team whose goal was to produce a new grinder in a year—one quarter the usual time. Standard theories of organizational culture would have predicted a slim chance of success. The grinder team, however, did a lot of things right, including the use of stories to emphasize the group’s new attitude and culture. One story, for instance, involved a critical decision about whether to build the new grinder’s casing out of plastic or metal. Plastic would be more comfortable for the customer, but would it hold up as well as metal?

按照英格索兰公司的传统做法，解决这个问题需要对两种材料的拉伸和压缩性能进行漫长而细致的研究。但这是研磨团队，他们必须迅速行动。团队中的几名成员想出了一个不太正式的测试方法。在一次客户现场拜访中，团队成员将两种材料的样品分别绑在租来的车的后保险杠上，然后拖着材料在停车场里转悠。他们一直这样做，直到警察过来让他们停止。最终的结论是，这种新型塑料复合材料的性能与传统的金属材料一样出色。决定就此做出。

The traditional Ingersoll-Rand method of solving this problem would have been to conduct protracted, careful studies of the tensile and compression properties of both materials. But this was the Grinder Team. They were supposed to act quickly. A few members of the team cooked up a less formal testing procedure. While on an off-site customer visit, the team members tied a sample of each material to the back bumper of their rental car, then drove around the parking lot with the materials dragging behind. They kept this up until the police came and told them to knock it off. The verdict was that the new plastic composite held up just as well as the traditional metal. Decision made.

在 Grinder 团队的历史上，这个故事被称为“拖拽测试”。“拖拽测试”是一项旨在强化团队新文化的创新举措。它暗示着：“我们仍然需要获取正确的数据来做出决策，只是需要更快地完成。”

In the history of the Grinder Team, this story has become known as the Drag Test. The Drag Test is a Creativity plot that reinforced the team’s new culture. The Drag Test implied, “We still need to get the right data to make decisions. We just need to do it a lot quicker.”

著名探险家欧内斯特·沙克尔顿在探险中面临着巨大的困难（这显然是经典的挑战情节），因此船员之间的团结至关重要。一旦发生叛变，所有人都可能丧命。沙克尔顿想出了一个巧妙的办法来应对那些爱抱怨的人。他安排他们睡在自己的帐篷里。当大家分组做杂务时，他就把这些爱抱怨的人安排在自己这组。通过他的持续陪伴，他最大限度地减少了他们的负面影响。创意情节激励我们去尝试一些不同的事情，去发挥创造力，去尝试新的方法。

The famous explorer Ernest Shackleton faced such enormous odds in his explorations (obviously a classic Challenge plot) that unity among his men was mission-critical. A mutiny could leave everyone dead. Shackleton came up with a creative solution for dealing with the whiny, complaining types. He assigned them to sleep in his own tent. When people separated into groups to work on chores, he grouped the complainers with him. Through his constant presence, he minimized their negative influence. Creativity plots make us want to do something different, to be creative, to experiment with new approaches.

T审阅这些情节的目的并非帮助我们创作故事。除非你写的是小说或广告，否则这样做意义不大。这里的目的是学习如何发现那些有潜力的故事。当贾里德的文章送到我们办公桌上时，我们希望立即找出关键要素。故事讲述的是一个男人面临巨大挑战并最终克服它们——这是一个挑战故事。挑战故事能够激励人们勇于接受挑战，更加努力。如果这种感觉与你想要达成的目标相符，那就大胆地运用这个故事；不要只是把它贴在公告板上。

The goal of reviewing these plots is not to help us invent stories. Unless you write fiction or advertisements, that won’t help much. The goal here is to learn how to spot the stories that have potential. When the Jared article hits our desk, we want to spot the crucial elements immediately. Guy faces huge obstacles and overcomes them—it’s a Challenge plot. Challenge plots inspire people to take on challenges and work harder. If that feeling is consistent with the goal you want to achieve, run with the story; don’t tack it on the bulletin board.

如果你负责运营“磨工团队”，并且试图重塑公司文化，那么你就需要密切关注那些可能引发创意迸发的苗头。当你听说你的某些员工在停车场拖着金属物件到处跑时，你就已经发现了问题所在。

If you’re running the Grinder Team, and you’re trying to reinvent the company culture, then you need to be on the lookout for Creativity plots. When you hear that some of your men dragged metal around a parking lot, you’ve found something.

要知道你在寻找什么。你不需要编造故事，不需要夸大其词，也不需要像《心灵鸡汤》里的故事那样煽情。（《变装测试》可不是煽情的。）你只需要懂得欣赏生活给予你的馈赠。

Know what you’re looking for. You don’t need to make stuff up, you don’t need to exaggerate or be as melodramatic as the Chicken Soup tales. (The Drag Test isn’t melodramatic.) You just need to recognize when life is giving you a gift.

世界银行的故事

Stories at the World Bank

1996年，斯蒂芬·丹宁在世界银行工作。世界银行是一家国际机构，向发展中国家提供贷款，用于建设学校、道路和水处理设施等基础设施项目。当时，他负责管理世界银行在非洲的业务——非洲是世界银行的第三大业务区域——而且似乎正迅速晋升到该机构的高层。

In 1996, Stephen Denning was working for the World Bank, the international institution that lends money to developing countries for infrastructure projects such as building schools, roads, and water-treatment facilities. At the time, he managed the bank’s work in Africa—the third-largest area of the bank—and seemed to be on a fast track to the top of the organization.

随后，他的两位主要导师之一退休，另一位也离开了。不久之后，他被要求卸任非洲区职务，转而“调查信息问题”。他的上级让他探索知识管理领域。丹宁说：“这家银行关心的是资金流动，而不是信息。这项新任务无异于被派往企业界的西伯利亚。”

Then one of his two main mentors retired and the second left. Shortly thereafter he was asked to step down from his Africa position and “look into the issue of information.” His superiors asked him to explore the area of knowledge management. Denning said, “Now this was a bank which cared about flows of money, not information. The new assignment was the equivalent of being sent to corporate Siberia.”

这项任务不仅在组织上缺乏吸引力，而且令人望而生畏。世界银行非常了解如何在发展中国家取得成果，但这些信息却分散在各处。世界银行在全球数十个国家开展项目——虽然设有中央官僚机构，但许多实际操作经验自然掌握在地方层面。从某种意义上说，每个项目都是一个独立的世界。赞比亚的水处理专家或许找到了一种巧妙处理当地政治谈判的方法，但他不太可能有机会与孟加拉国的公路建设专家分享。除非碰巧在同一个朋友圈或前同事圈子里，否则这两位管理者甚至都不知道对方的存在。

The task was not just organizationally unattractive, it was daunting. The World Bank knew a lot about how to achieve results in developing nations, but that information was scattered about the organization. The World Bank conducted projects in dozens of countries all over the world—and while there was a central bureaucracy, much of the operational know-how was naturally at the local level. Each project was, in a sense, its own universe. A water-treatment guru in Zambia might have figured out a great way to handle local political negotiations, but he was unlikely to have the opportunity to share it with a highway-construction guru in Bangladesh. Neither manager would know the other existed, unless they happened to be in the same circle of friends or former colleagues.

接受任务一个月后，丹宁与一位刚从赞比亚回来的同事共进午餐。这位同事正在参与一个旨在改善医疗保健的项目，尤其关注母婴健康。在赞比亚期间，他结识了卡马纳镇的一位医护人员。卡马纳镇位于赞比亚首都以西360英里处。这位医护人员正努力在当地抗击疟疾，并试图找到对抗疟疾的信息。他设法上网，并在亚特兰大疾病控制与预防中心（CDC）的网站上找到了所需的答案。（请记住，这是在1996年，当时互联网并非人们寻找信息的首选，尤其是在非洲。）

A month after accepting his assignment, Denning had lunch with a colleague who had just returned from Zambia. This colleague was working on a project to improve health care, particularly for mothers and children. While he was in Zambia he had met a health-care worker in Kamana—a small town 360 miles from Zambia’s capital—who was struggling to fight malaria in the community and was trying to find information on how to combat the disease. The worker had found a way to log on to the Internet and had discovered the answers he needed on the website of the Centers for Disease Control (CDC) in Atlanta. (Keep in mind that this was in 1996, when the Internet would not have been the obvious first stop for someone in search of information, especially in Africa.)

丹宁说，当时他并没有对这个故事想太多；这只是一个关于同事足智多谋的有趣轶事。后来，他突然意识到，赞比亚的故事完美地诠释了知识管理的力量。一位负责重要行动的人员需要信息。他四处寻找，最终找到了信息，并因此能够更有效地开展工作。这就是知识管理的愿景——只不过，这位医护人员本不应该被迫进行反复尝试，最终只能在疾控中心的网站上找到正确的信息。他本应该能够利用世界银行的知识。

Denning says that he didn’t give the story much thought at the time; it was just an interesting anecdote about the resourcefulness of a colleague. Later, it dawned on him that the Zambia story was a perfect example of the power of knowledge management. Someone in charge of a vital operation needed information. He went looking for it, found it, and, as a result, was able to act more effectively. That’s the vision of knowledge management—except that the health-care worker shouldn’t have been forced to conduct a trial-and-error search, ending at the CDC’s website, to get the right information. He should have been able to tap the knowledge of the World Bank.

丹宁开始将这个故事融入与同事的谈话中，强调世界银行为何应该传播知识。管理是头等大事。几周后，他有机会向一个高层管理委员会发言。他只有十到十二分钟的时间。在这段时间里，他必须提出一项新的组织战略，并赢得委员会的认可。这可不是件容易的事。

Denning began to incorporate the story into conversations with colleagues, stressing why the World Bank ought to make knowledge management a serious priority. Weeks later, he had an opportunity to speak to a committee of senior management. He’d have only ten to twelve minutes on the agenda. In that time he’d have to introduce a new organizational strategy and win the group’s endorsement. A tall order.

首先，丹宁提出了问题：世界银行在整合知识方面遇到的困难以及其信息系统糟糕的现状。然后，丹宁并没有像大多数人那样——老调重弹知识管理理论，引用一些权威人士关于知识管理对21世纪重要性的观点——而是另辟蹊径，讲述了赞比亚的故事。

First, Denning set up the problem: the difficulties that the World Bank had experienced in pooling its knowledge and the sorry state of its information systems. Then, rather than doing what most people would have done—i.e., rehashing the discipline of knowledge management and quoting some authorities about the importance of knowledge management for the twenty-first century—Denning did something different. He told the Zambia story.

演讲结束后，两位高管立刻冲到丹宁面前，开始滔滔不绝地向他灌输各种让项目启动起来的方案。丹宁心想：“这对话真奇怪。十分钟前，这些人还根本不愿搭理我，现在我却没尽力帮他们落实方案。太糟糕了！他们窃取了我的创意！” 随后，他又想到一个更令人欣慰的念头。“太好了！他们窃取了我的创意，现在它变成了他们的创意！”

Immediately after the presentation, two executives raced up to Denning and began to bombard him with all the things he should be doing to get the program off the ground. Denning thought, “This is a very strange conversation. Up till ten minutes ago, these people weren’t willing to give me the time of day, and now I’m not doing enough to implement their idea. This is horrible! They’ve stolen my idea!” And then he had a happier thought. “How wonderful! They’ve stolen my idea. It’s become their idea!”

一个几年后，丹宁离开世界银行，致力于传播他所学到的讲故事之道。2001年，他出版了一本极具洞见的著作《跳板》。丹宁将“跳板故事”定义为一种能够让人们看到现有问题如何改变的故事。跳板故事向人们展现各种可能性。

A few years later, after Denning had left the World Bank, he devoted himself to spreading the lessons he’d learned about storytelling. In 2001, he wrote a very insightful book called The Springboard. Denning defines a springboard story as a story that lets people see how an existing problem might change. Springboard stories tell people about possibilities.

故事的一大优势在于它们能够消除怀疑，赢得认同。丹宁说，最初讲故事的想法违背了他的直觉。他一直坚信直截了当的价值，并担心故事过于含糊、过于边缘化、过于轶事化。他想：“为什么不把事情说清楚呢？”直接传达信息？何必费尽心思、费力地间接引导听众思考，直接用抽象的指令岂不是更简单？为什么不一针见血地指出听众的真正意图呢？

One major advantage of springboard stories is that they combat skepticism and create buy-in. Denning says that the idea of telling stories initially violated his intuition. He had always believed in the value of being direct, and he worried that stories were too ambiguous, too peripheral, too anecdotal. He thought, “Why not spell out the message directly? Why go to the trouble and difficulty of trying to elicit the listener’s thinking indirectly, when it would be so much simpler if I come straight out in an abstract directive? Why not hit the listeners between the eyes?”
