问题在于，当你直击听众的要害时，他们会本能地反击。你传递信息的方式会直接影响他们的反应。如果你提出论点，你实际上是在邀请他们评估你的论点——评判它、辩论它、批评它——然后反驳它，至少在他们的潜意识里是这样。但丹宁认为，讲故事则能吸引听众——你让他们参与到你的想法中来，邀请他们与你一起思考。

The problem is that when you hit listeners between the eyes they respond by fighting back. The way you deliver a message to them is a cue to how they should react. If you make an argument, you’re implicitly asking them to evaluate your argument—judge it, debate it, criticize it—and then argue back, at least in their minds. But with a story, Denning argues, you engage the audience—you are involving people with the idea, asking them to participate with you.

丹宁谈到要调动“内心的小声音”，也就是通常会反驳说话者观点的那个声音。“传统的沟通方式是忽略内心的小声音，希望它保持安静，信息自然而然就能传达出去，”丹宁说。但他提出了不同的建议：“不要忽略内心的小声音……相反，要与它和谐共处。通过赋予它一些事情来调动它。用一种能引发内心小声音产生第二个故事的方式来讲述故事。”

Denning talks about engaging the “little voice inside the head,” the voice that would normally debate the speaker’s points. “The conventional view of communication is to ignore the little voice inside the head and hope it stays quiet and that the message will somehow get through,” Denning says. But he has a different recommendation: “Don’t ignore the little voice…. Instead, work in harmony with it. Engage it by giving it something to do. Tell a story in a way that elicits a second story from the little voice.”

除了赢得认同之外，引人入胜的故事还能激励人们采取行动。故事能让人们关注潜在的解决方案。讲述目标明确、障碍重重的故事，能引导观众进入解决问题的模式。显然，我们在不同的故事中“解决问题”的程度各不相同。我们看完《泰坦尼克号》后，不会立刻开始思考如何改进冰山探测系统。但我们确实会对主人公感同身受，并在他们面对困境时为他们加油鼓劲：“小心身后！”“现在就怼回去！”“别开那扇门！”

In addition to creating buy-in, springboard stories mobilize people to act. Stories focus people on potential solutions. Telling stories with visible goals and barriers shifts the audience into a problem-solving mode. Clearly, the amount of “problem-solving” we do varies across stories. We don’t watch Titanic and start brainstorming about improved iceberg-spotting systems. But we do empathize with the main characters and start cheering them on when they confront their problems: “Look out behind you!” “Tell him off now!” “Don’t open that door!”

但“跳板故事”的意义远不止于让我们替主角解决问题。它还能帮助我们自己解决问题。“跳板故事”是一种大规模定制的实践——每个读者都将故事作为跳板，走向略有不同的方向。

But springboard stories go beyond having us problem-solve for the main character. A springboard story helps us problem-solve for ourselves. A springboard story is an exercise in mass customization—each audience member uses the story as a springboard to slightly different destinations.

丹宁讲述完赞比亚的故事后，一位与会高管向世界银行行长提出了知识管理的理念，并强调这代表着该机构的未来。丹宁受邀向包括行长在内的世界银行高层领导汇报了他的想法。到年底，行长宣布知识管理是世界银行的首要任务之一。

After Denning told the Zambia story, one of the executives at the meeting took the idea of knowledge management to the president of the World Bank, arguing that it was the future of the organization. Denning was invited to present his ideas to the bank’s top leaders, including the president. By the end of the year, the president had announced that knowledge management was one of the bank’s top priorities.

会议故事集

The Conference Storybook

本章以护士的故事开篇，这个故事来自研究员加里·克莱因。克莱因还讲述了另一个故事，很好地概括了我们之前讨论的内容。

We started the chapter with the nurse story, which comes from the researcher Gary Klein. Klein tells another story that provides a good summary of the ground we’ve covered.

一位会议组织者曾委托克莱因的公司总结会议成果。组织者希望得到一份实用且清晰的会议总结——比会议记录更精炼，比演讲者各自不同的PPT幻灯片集合更连贯。

The organizer of a conference once asked Klein’s firm to sum up the results of a conference. The organizer wanted a useful summary of the conference—more compact than a transcript and more coherent than an idiosyncratic collection of the presenters’ PowerPoint slides.

克莱因的公司指派了一名人员分别负责监督会议的五个平行分会场。这些监督员参加了每一场小组讨论，每当有人讲述故事时，他们都会记下来。会议结束后，监督员们互相交流笔记，发现正如克莱因所说，他们收集到了一系列“既滑稽又悲惨，还很精彩”的故事。随后，他们整理并组织了这些故事，并将资料包发送给了会议组织者。

Klein’s firm assigned one person to monitor each of the conference’s five parallel tracks. The monitors attended each panel, and each time someone told a story they jotted it down. At the end of the conference, the monitors compared notes and found that, as Klein said, they had compiled a set of stories that were “funny, and tragic, and exciting.” The group structured and organized the stories and sent the packet to the conference organizer.

她欣喜若狂。她发现这份资料比一般的会议资料——一堆枯燥乏味、充斥着专业术语的摘要——生动有趣得多，也实用得多。她甚至向所在机构申请资金，将这些笔记整理成书。同时，出于礼貌，她把这些总结笔记发给了所有参会报告人。

She was ecstatic. She found the packet much more vivid and useful than the typical conference takeaway: a set of dry, jargon-filled abstracts. She even requested funds from her organization to convert the notes into a book. Meanwhile, as a courtesy, she sent the summary notes to all of the conference presenters.

他们非常愤怒。他们觉得自己的故事被从整体结构中剔除，受到了侮辱——他们不想被后人记住只是一群只会讲故事和轶事的人。他们觉得他们投入了无数时间，将自己的经验提炼成一系列建议。事实上，他们提交给会议组织者的摘要中充满了真知灼见，例如“保持沟通渠道畅通”和“问题积累时不要拖延太久”。

They were furious. They were insulted to have the stories scooped out of their overall structure—they didn’t want to be remembered as people who told a bunch of stories and anecdotes. They felt that they’d invested countless hours into distilling their experiences into a series of recommendations. Indeed, their abstracts—which had been submitted to the conference organizers—were filled with tidbits of wisdom, such as “Keep the lines of communication open” and “Don’t wait too long when problems are building up.”

克莱因说：“我们想向他们解释，与一些实际案例相比，这些口号是多么毫无意义。比如，他们曾讲述过在工厂停工的艰难事件中，他们是如何保持沟通渠道畅通的。” 但演讲者态度坚决，最终该项目被放弃。

Klein said, “We want to explain to them how meaningless these slogans are in contrast to stories, such as the one that showed how they had kept the lines of communication open during a difficult incident in which a plant was shut down.” But the presenters were adamant, and the project was abandoned.

这个故事是我们书中最喜欢的故事之一，因为它展现的动态如此清晰。我们并非想把演讲者描绘成心胸狭隘、憎恨新思想的人。请设身处地地想一想。你精心制作了这份精彩的演讲，总结了你多年的心血，你的目标是帮助人们掌握你耗费数年构建的复杂体系。你搭建了一座令人叹为观止的知识殿堂！然而，克莱因的团队却来到你的殿堂前，从墙上拔下几块砖，试图将它们冒充为你全部的劳动成果。真是厚颜无耻！

This story is one of our favorites in the book, because the dynamics are so clear. We’re not trying to portray the presenters as bad, idea-hating people. Put yourself in their shoes. You’ve created this amazing presentation, summarizing years of your work, and your goal is to help people master a complex structure that you’ve spent years constructing. You’ve erected an amazing intellectual edifice! Then Klein’s crew approaches your edifice, plucks a few bricks out of the wall, and tries to pass them off as the sum of all your labors. The nerve!

当然，问题在于，你不可能在90分钟的演讲中完整地呈现一座建筑。你最多只能传达一些基本要素。但你不可能从屋顶上摘取构件，而你提出的“保持沟通渠道畅通”这样的建议，恰恰就相当于这么做。

The problem, of course, is that it’s impossible to transfer an edifice in a ninety-minute presentation. The best you can do is convey some building blocks. But you can’t pluck building blocks from the roof, which is exactly what you’re doing with a recommendation like “Keep the lines of communication open.”

假设你是诺德斯特龙百货公司的一位经理，正在参加一个同行会议。你演示文稿的最后一页幻灯片可能写着：“来自诺德斯特龙的经验：在零售业，卓越的客户服务是竞争优势的关键来源。” 在讲解第四页幻灯片时，你可能顺便幽默地提到了一位诺德斯特龙的员工帮一位顾客包装了在梅西百货买的礼物。克莱因公司那些爱开玩笑的人想保留你这个包装礼物的故事，但又想让你把笑点藏起来。他们说得完全正确。

Suppose you’re a manager at Nordstrom, addressing a conference of your peers. The final slide in your presentation might read, “Lessons from Nordstrom: In retail, outstanding customer service is a key source of competitive advantage.” While discussing your fourth slide you might have mentioned, as a humorous aside, the Nordie who gift-wrapped a present bought at Macy’s. These jokers from Klein’s firm want to keep your gift-wrapping story but drop your punch line. And they’re absolutely right.

在“简单”和“出乎意料”两章中，我们提到好的信息必须从常识出发，逐步过渡到非常识。相比之下，诸如“保持沟通渠道畅通”和“问题积压时不要拖延”之类的建议却只有常识可言。（克莱因评论说，这些建议大概是给那些面对棘手问题时宁愿关闭沟通渠道、袖手旁观的人准备的。）

In the “Simple” and “Unexpected” chapters, we said that good messages must move from common sense to uncommon sense. In contrast, there’s nothing but common sense in recommendations such as “Keep the lines of communication open” and “Don’t wait too long when problems are building up.” (Klein comments that these lessons are presumably designed for people who would rather close lines of communication and sit around when they’re facing a daunting problem.)

知识的诅咒再次迷惑了这些演讲者。当他们分享他们的经验教训——“保持沟通渠道畅通”——时，他们的脑海中仿佛响起了一首充满激情和情感的歌。他们回忆起那些教会他们这些经验教训的往事——奋斗、政治斗争、失误和痛苦。他们情不自禁地跟着节奏摇摆。但他们忘记了，听众无法听到他们听到的同样的旋律。

Once again, the Curse of Knowledge has bewitched these presenters. When they share their lessons—“Keep the lines of communication open”—they’re hearing a song, filled with passion and emotion, inside their heads. They’re remembering the experiences that taught them those lessons—the struggles, the political battles, the missteps, the pain. They are tapping. But they forget that the audience can’t hear the same tune they hear.

故事几乎可以凭借一己之力战胜知识的诅咒。事实上，它们天然地体现了SUCCESs框架的大部分内容。故事几乎总是具体的，而且大多数都包含情感和意想不到的元素。有效运用故事最难的地方在于确保故事简洁明了——能够反映你的核心信息。仅仅讲述一个精彩的故事是不够的；故事必须反映你的目标。你肯定不希望一个将军在战前列队时，讲述一个关于“联系”的故事。

Stories can almost single-handedly defeat the Curse of Knowledge. In fact, they naturally embody most of the SUCCESs framework. Stories are almost always Concrete. Most of them have Emotional and Unexpected elements. The hardest part of using stories effectively is making sure that they’re Simple—that they reflect your core message. It’s not enough to tell a great story; the story has to reflect your agenda. You don’t want a general lining up his troops before battle to tell a Connection plot story.

故事拥有令人惊叹的双重力量，既能模拟现实，又能激发灵感。而且大多数时候，我们甚至无需运用太多创造力就能驾驭这种力量——我们只需要时刻准备着去发现生活中每天涌现的美好故事。

Stories have the amazing dual power to simulate and to inspire. And most of the time we don’t even have to use much creativity to harness these powers—we just need to be ready to spot the good ones that life generates every day.



什么能坚持下去



WHAT STICKS

S有些想法即便我们竭尽全力阻止，也难以根除。1946年，利奥·杜罗彻是道奇队的教练。他的球队当时领跑国家联盟，而他们的宿敌纽约巨人队则在积分榜垫底。

Sometimes ideas stick despite our best efforts to stop them. In 1946, Leo Durocher was the coach of the Dodgers. His club was leading the National League, while the team’s traditional archrival, the New York Giants, was languishing in the bottom of the standings.

在道奇队和巨人队的一场比赛中，杜罗彻当着一群体育记者的面嘲讽巨人队。其中一位记者打趣道：“你为什么不偶尔做个好人呢？”杜罗彻指着巨人队的休息区说：“好人！看看那边。你们认识比梅尔·奥特（巨人队经理）更和善的人吗？或者说，你们认识比巨人队其他球员更和善的人吗？他们可是世界上最和善的人！可他们现在在哪儿？第七名！”

During a game between the Dodgers and the Giants, Durocher was mocking the Giants in front of a group of sportswriters. One of the sportswriters teased Durocher, “Why don’t you be a nice guy for a change?” Durocher pointed at the Giants’ dugout and said, “Nice guys! Look over there. Do you know a nicer guy than [Giants’ manager] Mel Ott? Or any of the other Giants? Why, they’re the nicest guys in the world! And where are they? In seventh place!”

正如拉尔夫·凯斯在其关于误引的著作《好人终究排名第七》中所述，杜罗彻的这句名言的演变始于一年后。《棒球文摘》引用杜罗彻的话说：“好人在第二级别联赛中垫底。” 不久之后，随着这句俏皮话在人们口中流传，它逐渐演变，变得更加简洁、更具普适性，最终演变成一句对人生的讽刺：“好人终究垫底。” 不再提及……巨人队，别再提第七名了——事实上，别再提棒球了。好人没好报。

As recounted by Ralph Keyes in his book on misquotations, Nice Guys Finish Seventh, the metamorphosis of Durocher’s quote began a year later. The Baseball Digest quoted Durocher as saying, “Nice guys finish in last place in the second division.” Before long, as his quip was passed along from one person to another, it evolved, becoming simpler and more universal, until it emerged as a cynical comment on life: “Nice guys finish last.” No more reference to the Giants, no more reference to seventh place—in fact, no more reference to baseball at all. Nice guys finish last.

这句被思想市场润色过的名言激怒了杜罗彻。多年来，他一直否认说过这句话（当然，他是对的），但最终他还是放弃了。他的自传名为《好人没好报》。

This quote, polished by the marketplace of ideas, irked Durocher. For years, he denied saying the phrase (and, of course, he was right), but eventually he gave up. Nice Guys Finish Last was the title of his autobiography.

哦历史上最著名的误引之一，据说出自虚构侦探夏洛克·福尔摩斯之口。福尔摩斯从未说过“Elementary, my dear Watson”（这很简单，我亲爱的华生）。这听起来难以置信——这句话完全符合我们对福尔摩斯的刻板印象。事实上，如果你问别人能说出一句福尔摩斯的名言，那很可能就是这句。他最著名的名言，恰恰是他从未说过的。

One of the most famous misquotations of all time is attributed to the fictional detective Sherlock Holmes. Holmes never said, “Elementary, my dear Watson.” This seems hard to believe—the quote is perfectly suited to our schema of Holmes. In fact, if you asked someone to name one Sherlock Holmes quote, this would be it. His most famous quote is the one he never said.

这句原本不存在的名言为何流传开来？不难想象事情的缘由。福尔摩斯经常说“我亲爱的华生”，也经常说“妙哉”。对于一个喜欢引用福尔摩斯探案故事的人来说，很自然地会犯这样的错误：把这两个词组合在一起。而就像一种适应性生物突变一样，这句新组合的名言竟然如此精妙，以至于它自然而然地传播开来。毕竟，这四个字的名言包含了福尔摩斯的精髓：这位才华横溢的侦探从不吝啬对忠实的助手阿华的关怀。

Why did this nonexistent quote stick? It’s not hard to imagine what must have happened. Holmes frequently said, “My dear Watson,” and he often said, “Elementary.” A natural mistake, for someone inclined to quote from a Holmes mystery, would be to combine the two. And, like an adaptive biological mutation, the newly combined quote was such an improvement that it couldn’t help but spread. This four-word quotation, after all, contains the essence of Holmes: the brilliant detective never too busy to condescend to his faithful sidekick.

我在“简单”一章中，我们讲述了1992年克林顿竞选的故事，以及卡维尔那句著名的格言：“笨蛋，问题在于经济。”我们提到，这句格言是卡维尔写在白板上的三句话之一。这里有个小问题：另外两句话是什么？

In the “Simple” chapter, we told the story of the 1992 Clinton campaign and Carville’s famous proverb, “It’s the economy, stupid.” We mentioned that this proverb was one of three phrases that Carville wrote on a whiteboard. Here’s a trivia question: What were the other two?

另外两句口号是“变革还是墨守成规”和“别忘了医疗保健”。这些口号都没能流行起来。所以，它们也应该如此。卡维尔对“问题在于经济，笨蛋”这一理念的成功感到满意吗？一方面，他的这句话引起了强烈的共鸣，成为影响选举走向的有力工具。另一方面，他只传达了自己信息的三分之一！

The other two phrases were “Change vs. more of the same” and “Don’t forget health care.” Those phrases didn’t stick. So should Carville have been pleased with the success of “It’s the economy, stupid” as an idea? On the one hand, his phrase resonated so strongly that it became a powerful tool in framing the election. On the other hand, he got only one third of his message across!

我们之所以提出这些例子，是因为在让理念深入人心的过程中，受众拥有发言权。受众可能会改变你理念的含义，就像杜罗彻的例子一样。受众也可能改进你的想法，就像夏洛克·福尔摩斯的例子一样。或者，受众可能会保留你的一些想法，而摒弃另一些，就像卡维尔的例子一样。

We bring up these examples because, in making ideas stick, the audience gets a vote. The audience may change the meaning of your idea, as happened with Durocher. The audience may actually improve your idea, as was the case with Sherlock Holmes. Or the audience may retain some of your ideas and jettison others, as with Carville.

我们都往往对自己的想法抱有很强的“自豪感”。我们希望自己的信息能够以我们设计的形式流传下去。当听众影响杜罗彻的想法时，他的反应是先是拒绝，再拒绝，再拒绝……最终才接受。

All of us tend to have a lot of “idea pride.” We want our message to endure in the form we designed. Durocher’s response, when the audience shaped his idea, was to deny, deny, deny … then eventually accept.

在任何情况下，我们都必须扪心自问：受众对我的信息的理解是否依然是核心信息？在第一章（“简单”）中，我们讨论了聚焦核心信息的重要性——即提炼出我们需要传达的最重要的真理。如果外界接受我们的想法并加以修改——或者接受一部分而摒弃另一部分——我们只需要判断这些变异后的版本是否仍然是核心信息。如果仍然是核心信息——就像“问题在于经济，笨蛋！”——那么我们应该虚心接受受众的评判。归根结底，衡量我们作为思想创造者是否成功的标准，并非人们是否照搬我们的原话，而是我们是否实现了目标。

The question we have to ask ourselves in any situation is this: Is the audience’s version of my message still core? In Chapter 1 (“Simple”), we discussed the importance of focusing on core messages—honing in on the most important truths that we need to communicate. If the world takes our ideas and changes them—or accepts some and discards others—all we need to decide is whether the mutated versions are still core. If they are—as with “It’s the economy, stupid”—then we should humbly embrace the audience’s judgment. Ultimately, the test of our success as idea creators isn’t whether people mimic our exact words, it’s whether we achieve our goals.

发现的力量

The Power of Spotting

卡维尔、杜罗彻和柯南·道尔都是创意创造者。他们白手起家，构思创意。但我们也不要忘记，发现有潜力的创意与创造创意同样重要。

Carville, Durocher, and Arthur Conan Doyle were all creators of ideas. They produced ideas from scratch. But let’s not forget that it’s just as effective to spot sticky ideas as it is to create them.

想想诺德斯特龙百货公司。你不可能凭空捏造出一堆关于销售代表兴高采烈地包装礼物的故事。来自梅西百货。但当你遇到这样的真实故事时，你必须警惕这个想法的潜力。而这并不像听起来那么容易。

Think about Nordstrom. You can’t very well create from scratch a bunch of stories about sales reps cheerfully gift-wrapping presents from Macy’s. But when you come across a real story like that, you’ve got to be alert to the idea’s potential. And this isn’t as easy as it sounds.

发现创意的障碍在于，我们处理轶事和抽象概念的方式截然不同。如果一位诺德斯特龙百货公司的经理接到一个抽象任务，比如“本季度客户满意度提高10%”，那么这个抽象任务会立刻触发管理者的思维模式：我们该如何实现这个目标？但如果是一个关于销售代表更换防滑链、给冰冷的汽车暖车的故事，则会引发不同的思考。它很可能会被归类到其他日常琐事中——有趣但最终无关紧要，比如约翰·罗宾逊剃了光头，或者詹姆斯·施吕特连续七天迟到。从某种意义上说，我们的大脑中存在一道墙，将小事（例如故事）与大事隔开。发现创意需要我们打破这道墙。

The barrier to idea-spotting is that we tend to process anecdotes differently than abstractions. If a Nordstrom manager is hit with an abstraction, such as “Increase customer satisfaction scores by 10 percent this quarter,” that abstraction kicks in the managerial mentality: How do we get there from here? But a story about a tire-chain-exchanging, cold-car-warming sales rep provokes a different way of thinking. It will likely be filed away with other kinds of day-to-day personal news—interesting but ultimately trivial, like the fact that John Robison shaved his head or James Schlueter showed up late seven days in a row. In some sense, there’s a wall in our minds separating the little picture—stories, for instance—from the big picture. Spotting requires us to tear down that wall.

我们该如何打破这堵墙？打个比方，想想我们给亲人买礼物的方式。如果我们知道圣诞节或生日快到了，脑海中就会自动响起一个念头，提醒我们“爸爸喜欢玩电子产品，所以要留意一些酷炫的小玩意儿”。这几乎是无意识的，但如果我们在12月8日碰巧看到一款可伸缩旋转激光灯，我们很可能会立刻觉得它很适合送给爸爸。

How do we tear down the wall? As a rough analogy, think about the way we buy gifts for loved ones. If we know that Christmas or a birthday is approaching, there’s a little nagging process that opens up in our minds, reminding us that “Dad is a gadget guy, so keep an eye out for cool gadgets.” It’s barely conscious, but if we happen upon a Retractable Roto-Laser-Light on December 8, chances are we’ll immediately spot it as a possible fit for Dad.

这就像在思想世界里，我们需要始终牢记想要传达的核心信息。正如我们可以戴上“爸爸礼物眼镜”，从父亲的角度看待商品一样，我们也可以戴上“核心理念眼镜”，从这个角度筛选新的想法。如果你是 Nordstrom 的一位经理，一心想要提升客户服务，那么这种筛选机制就能帮助你把“预热汽车”事件看作是完美服务的象征，而不是一个有趣的轶事。

The analogy to the idea world is maintaining a deeply ingrained sense of the core message that we want to communicate. Just as we can put on Dad Gift Glasses, allowing us to view merchandise from his perspective, we can also put on Core Idea Glasses, allowing us to filter incoming ideas from that perspective. If you’re a Nordstrom manager, obsessed with improving customer service, this filter helps you spot the warming-cars episode as a symbol of perfection, rather than as an interesting anecdote.

在引言中，我们驳斥了那种认为你需要天生的创造力才能想出绝妙点子的常见误解。事实并非如此。但除此之外，至关重要的是要认识到，创作本身并非必要条件。

In the Introduction, we debunked the common assumption that you need natural creative genius to cook up a great idea. You don’t. But, beyond that, it’s crucial to realize that creation, period, is unnecessary.

想想这本书里那些并非创造而是发现的想法：诺迪斯（Nordies，指诺迪斯啤酒厂的创始人）、贾里德（Jared，指贾里德·哈特 ...

Think of the ideas in this book that were spotted rather than created: Nordies. Jared. The mystery of Saturn’s rings. Pam Laffin, the smoking antiauthority. The nurse who ignored the heart monitor, listened with her stethoscope, and saved the baby’s life. If you’re a great spotter, you’ll always trump a great creator. Why? Because the world will always produce more great ideas than any single individual, even the most creative one.

扬声器和贴纸

The Speakers and the Stickers

每年在斯坦福大学奇普的“让理念深入人心”课程的第二节课上，学生们都会参加一项练习，这项练习旨在检验哪些信息能够深入人心，哪些信息难以被接受。学生们会拿到一些来自政府机构的关于美国犯罪模式的数据。其中一半学生需要进行一分钟的演讲，力图说服同伴们非暴力犯罪在美国是一个严重的问题。另一半学生则被要求表明自己的观点，认为非暴力犯罪并不特别严重。

Each year in the second session of Chip’s “Making Ideas Stick” class at Stanford, the students participate in an exercise, a kind of testable credential to show what kinds of messages stick and don’t stick. The students are given some data from a government source on crime patterns in the United States. Half of them are asked to make a one-minute persuasive speech to convince their peers that nonviolent crime is a serious problem in this country. The other half are asked to take the position that it’s not particularly serious.

不出所料，斯坦福大学的学生都很聪明。他们思维敏捷，善于沟通。在场的每个人演讲都很精彩。

Stanford students, as you’d expect, are smart. They also tend to be quick thinkers and good communicators. No one in the room ever gives a poor speech.

学生们分成小组，每组进行一分钟的演讲，其他小组的同学聆听。演讲结束后，听众对演讲者进行评分：演讲效果如何？说服力如何？

The students divide into small groups and each one gives a one-minute speech while the others listen. After each speech, the listeners rate the speaker: How impressive was the delivery? How persuasive?

结果往往是，口才最好的演讲者获得最高评分。那些举止优雅、表达流畅、富有魅力的学生往往名列前茅。这并不奇怪，对吧？优秀的演讲者在演讲比赛中往往能取得好成绩。

What happens, invariably, is that the most polished speakers get the highest ratings. Students who are poised, smooth, and charismatic are rated at the top of the class. No surprise, right? Good speakers score well in speaking contests.

接下来才是出人意料的环节。练习看似结束了；事实上，奇普经常会播放一段蒙提·派森的短片来消磨时间，分散学生的注意力。然后，他突然要求学生们拿出纸笔，把他们听到的每位演讲者的所有观点都写下来。

The surprise comes next. The exercise appears to be over; in fact, Chip often plays a brief Monty Python clip to kill a few minutes and distract the students. Then, abruptly, he asks them to pull out a sheet of paper and write down, for each speaker they heard, every single idea that they remember.

学生们对自己的记忆力之差感到震惊。要知道，距离演讲结束仅仅过去了十分钟。而且，信息量本来就不大——最多也就听了八场一分钟的演讲。然而，学生们能记住每位演讲者一两个要点就已经很幸运了。很多学生对某些演讲的内容完全空白——一个概念都记不住。

The students are flabbergasted at how little they remember. Keep in mind that only ten minutes have elapsed since the speeches were given. Nor was there a huge volume of information to begin with—at most, they’ve heard eight one-minute speeches. And yet the students are lucky to recall one or two ideas from each speaker’s presentation. Many draw a complete blank on some speeches—unable to remember a single concept.

在平均一分钟的演讲中，普通学生会使用2.5个统计数据。只有十分之一的学生会讲述故事。以上是演讲统计数据。另一方面，“记忆”统计数据几乎与之相反：当要求学生回忆演讲内容时，63%的学生能记住故事，而只有5%的学生能记住任何具体的统计数据。

In the average one-minute speech, the typical student uses 2.5 statistics. Only one student in ten tells a story. Those are the speaking statistics. The “remembering” statistics, on the other hand, are almost a mirror image: When students are asked to recall the speeches, 63 percent remember the stories. Only 5 percent remember any individual statistic.

此外，“口语天赋”与能否有效传达观点之间几乎没有关联。那些口才出众的演讲者，在有效传达观点方面通常并不比其他人更胜一筹。英语水平欠佳的外国学生——他们的口语能力往往排名垫底——却突然与母语人士不相上下。真正能有效传达观点的学生，往往是通过讲述故事、激发情感或着重强调一个要点而非十个要点来阐述观点。毫无疑问，一个事先阅读过本书的“内行”学生，将会轻松碾压其他学生。一个以英语为第二语言的社区大学学生，甚至可能轻松胜过一些毫不知情的斯坦福大学研究生。

Furthermore, almost no correlation emerges between “speaking talent” and the ability to make ideas stick. The people who were captivating speakers typically do no better than others in making their ideas stick. Foreign students—whose less-polished English often leaves them at the bottom of the speaking-skills rankings—are suddenly on a par with native speakers. The stars of stickiness are the students who made their case by telling stories, or by tapping into emotion, or by stressing a single point rather than ten. There is no question that a ringer—a student who came into the exercise having read this book—would squash the other students. A community college student for whom English is a second language could easily outperform unwitting Stanford graduate students.

为什么这些聪明能干的演讲者总是无法让他们的观点深入人心？本书探讨的几个“罪魁祸首”难辞其咎。第一个罪魁祸首是人们天生倾向于把重点埋没——淹没在信息的海洋中。掌握大量知识或信息的最大弊端之一，就是我们忍不住想要把所有信息都分享出去。高中老师会告诉你，学生在撰写研究论文时，总觉得有义务把所有挖掘出来的事实都写进去，仿佛论文的价值在于数据的数量，而不是数据的意义。姿态或清晰度。为了专注于核心而剔除信息，并非出于本能。

Why can’t these smart, talented speakers make their ideas stick? A few of the villains discussed in this book are implicated. The first villain is the natural tendency to bury the lead—to get lost in a sea of information. One of the worst things about knowing a lot, or having access to a lot of information, is that we’re tempted to share it all. High school teachers will tell you that when students write research papers they feel obligated to include every unearthed fact, as though the value were in the quantity of data amassed rather than in its pur pose or clarity. Stripping out information, in order to focus on the core, is not instinctual.

第二个弊端是人们往往过于关注演讲形式而非演讲内容。演讲者自然希望自己看起来沉着冷静、魅力四射、鼓舞人心。当然，魅力确实有助于精心设计的演讲内容更好地深入人心。但是，即便拥有再多的魅力，也无法挽救一篇冗长乏味、缺乏重点的演讲，一些斯坦福大学的学生就为此付出了惨痛的代价。

The second villain is the tendency to focus on the presentation rather than on the message. Public speakers naturally want to appear composed, charismatic, and motivational. And, certainly, charisma will help a properly designed message stick better. But all the charisma in the world won’t save a dense, unfocused speech, as some Stanford students learn the hard way.

更多反派角色

More Villains

书中还有另外两个斯坦福学生不必面对的关键难题。第一个是决策瘫痪——由过多的选择或模棱两可的情况引起的焦虑和非理性。想想那些因为无法决定哪个更好而错过了一场精彩讲座和一部优秀电影的学生，或者想想Palm Pilot开发团队负责人杰夫·霍金斯为了让团队专注于少数几个问题而不是众多问题而付出的艰辛努力。

There are two other key villains in the book that the Stanford students don’t have to wrestle with. The first is decision paralysis—the anxiety and irrationality that can emerge from excessive choice or ambiguous situations. Think about the students who missed both a fantastic lecture and a great film because they couldn’t decide which one was better, or how hard it was for Jeff Hawkins, the leader of the Palm Pilot development group, to get his team to focus on a few issues rather than on many.

为了克服决策瘫痪，沟通者必须努力找到问题的核心。律师在结案陈词中必须强调一两个要点，而不是十个。教师的教案可能包含五十个要与学生分享的概念，但为了有效教学，教师必须将大部分精力集中在确保学生真正理解其中最关键的两三个概念上。管理者必须分享一些谚语——比如“人名、人名、人名”或“廉价航空公司”——帮助员工从模糊不清的情境中做出决策。

To beat decision paralysis, communicators have to do the hard work of finding the core. Lawyers must stress one or two points in their closing arguments, not ten. A teacher’s lesson plans may contain fifty concepts to share with her students, but in order to be effective that teacher must devote most of her efforts to making the most critical two or three stick. Managers must share proverbs—”Names, names, and names” or “THE low-fare airline”—that help employees wring decisions out of ambiguous situations.

正如你现在所知，阻碍人们接受某些观点的最大敌人是“知识诅咒”。斯坦福大学的学生并没有遭遇“知识诅咒”，因为他们对犯罪数据一无所知——他们更像是记者，努力避免让新闻报道的重点被埋没，而不是专家，忘记了自己曾经对某些事物一无所知是什么滋味。

The archvillain of sticky ideas, as you know by now, is the Curse of Knowledge. The Stanford students didn’t face the Curse of Knowledge because the data on crime was brand-new to them—they were more akin to reporters trying to avoid burying the lead on a news story than to experts who have forgotten what it’s like not to know something.

知识的诅咒是一个值得尊敬的对手，因为在某些情况下……感觉这是不可避免的。传递信息分为两个阶段：回答阶段和告知他人阶段。在回答阶段，你运用你的专业知识得出你想分享的观点。医生需要学习十年才能给出答案。企业管理者可能需要数月深思熟虑才能得出答案。

The Curse of Knowledge is a worthy adversary, because in some sense it’s inevitable. Getting a message across has two stages: the Answer stage and the Telling Others stage. In the Answer stage, you use your expertise to arrive at the idea that you want to share. Doctors study for a decade to be capable of giving the Answer. Business managers may deliberate for months to arrive at the Answer.

问题就在这里：在“寻找答案”阶段对你有利的因素，在“分享答案”阶段反而会对你产生负面影响。要找到答案，你需要专业知识，但你无法将专业知识与“知识诅咒”割裂开来。你知道别人不知道的事情，却记不起以前不知道这些事情是什么感觉。所以，当你最终决定分享答案时，你往往会以自己的视角去表达。

Here’s the rub: The same factors that worked to your advantage in the Answer stage will backfire on you during the Telling Others stage. To get the Answer, you need expertise, but you can’t dissociate expertise from the Curse of Knowledge. You know things that others don’t know, and you can’t remember what it was like not to know those things. So when you get around to sharing the Answer, you’ll tend to communicate as if your audience were you.

你会强调那些对得出答案至关重要的海量统计数据——但就像斯坦福大学的学生一样，你会发现事后根本没人记得这些数据。你会分享最终的结论——经过数月研究和分析得出的至理名言——但就像那些向一线员工强调“股东价值最大化”的CEO一样，没人会明白你的结论和日常工作有什么关系。

You’ll stress the scads of statistics that were pivotal in arriving at the Answer—and, like the Stanford students, you’ll find that no one remembers them afterward. You’ll share the punch line—the overarching truth that emerged from months of study and analysis—and, like the CEO who stresses “maximizing shareholder value” to his frontline employees, no one will have a clue how your punch line relates to the day-to-day work.

我们投入大量时间培训人们如何找到答案，却很少花时间培训他们如何向他人传达答案，这之间存在着一种奇怪的脱节。很多人从医学院或MBA毕业时，可能从未修过任何沟通技巧课程。大学教授们在其专业领域修读数十门课程，却从未学习过如何教学。许多工程师甚至会对“如何向他人传达答案”的培训项目嗤之以鼻。

There is a curious disconnect between the amount of time we invest in training people how to arrive at the Answer and the amount of time we invest in training them how to Tell Others. It’s easy to graduate from medical school or an MBA program without ever taking a class in communication. College professors take dozens of courses in their areas of expertise but none on how to teach. A lot of engineers would scoff at a training program about Telling Others.

企业管理者似乎认为，只要他们点击浏览完一份展示结论的PPT，就等于成功地传达了自己的想法。他们所做的，其实只是分享数据。如果他们口才好，或许还能在员工和同事心中树立起一种“果断”、“管理有方”或“激励人心”的印象。但是，就像斯坦福大学的学生一样，当他们意识到自己所做的一切，其实都毫无意义时，他们才会感到惊讶。他们说产生了影响。他们分享了数据，但没有提出任何有用且持久的想法。一切都毫无成效。

Business managers seem to believe that, once they’ve clicked through a PowerPoint presentation showcasing their conclusions, they’ve successfully communicated their ideas. What they’ve done is share data. If they’re good speakers, they may even have created an enhanced sense, among their employees and peers, that they are “decisive” or “managerial” or “motivational.” But, like the Stanford students, the surprise will come when they realize that nothing they’ve said had impact. They’ve shared data, but they haven’t created ideas that are useful and lasting. Nothing stuck.

让想法深入人心：

沟通框架

Making an Idea Stick:

The Communication Framework

一个想法要想深入人心，要想有用且持久，就必须让受众：

For an idea to stick, for it to be useful and lasting, it’s got to make the audience:

  1. 注意

  2. Pay attention

  3. 理解并记住它

  4. Understand and remember it

  5. 同意/相信

  6. Agree/Believe

  7. 关心

  8. Care

  9. 能够采取行动

  10. Be able to act on it

这本书原本可以围绕这五个步骤展开，但它们被放在结论部分是有原因的。“知识诅咒”很容易使这个框架失效。当一位专家问“人们会理解我的想法吗？”时，她的答案会是“会”，因为她自己理解。（“当然，我的团队会理解‘股东价值最大化’！”）当一位专家问“人们会关心这件事吗？”时，她的答案也会是“会”，因为她自己关心。想想默里·德拉诺夫双钢琴乐团，他们曾说：“我们存在的意义在于保护、传承和推广双钢琴音乐。”当他们发现这句话并没有像他们自己那样激起其他人的热情时，他们感到非常震惊。

This book could have been organized around these five steps, but there’s a reason they were reserved for the conclusion. The Curse of Knowledge can easily render this framework useless. When an expert asks, “Will people understand my idea?,” her answer will be Yes, because she herself understands. (“Of course, my people will understand ‘maximizing shareholder value!’ “) When an expert asks, “Will people care about this?,” her answer will be Yes, because she herself cares. Think of the Murray Dranoff Duo Piano people, who said, “We exist to protect, preserve, and promote the music of the duo piano.” They were shocked when that statement didn’t arouse the same passion in others that it did in them.

SUCCESs清单可以替代上述框架，它的优势在于更具体，也更不容易受到“知识诅咒”的影响。事实上，如果你回顾一下已经读过的章节，你会发现这个框架与它们非常契合：

The SUCCESs checklist is a substitute for the framework above, and its advantage is that it’s more tangible and less subject to the Curse of Knowledge. In fact, if you think back across the chapters you’ve read, you’ll notice that the framework matches up nicely:

1. 注意： 意外
2. 理解并记住它： 具体的
3. 同意/相信： 可信的
4. 护理： 情绪化的
5. 能够付诸行动： 故事

所以，与其猜测人们是否能理解我们的想法，不如问问自己：“它是否具体？”与其臆测人们是否会关心，不如问问自己：“它是否触动人心？它是否超越了马斯洛的需求层次理论？它是否能促使人们进行分析思考，或者让他们产生共鸣？”（顺便一提，“简洁”并未列入上述清单，因为它主要关乎“回答”阶段——提炼信息的核心并使其尽可能精炼。但简洁的信息在整个过程中都至关重要，尤其是在帮助人们理解和行动方面。）

So, rather than guess about whether people will understand our ideas, we should ask, “Is it concrete?” Rather than speculate about whether people will care, we should ask, “Is it emotional? Does it get out of Maslow’s basement? Does it force people to put on an Analytical Hat or allow them to feel empathy?” (By the way, “Simple” is not on the list above because it’s mainly about the Answer stage—honing in on the core of your message and making it as compact as possible. But Simple messages help throughout the process, especially in helping people to understand and act.)

因此，SUCCESs清单是处理沟通问题的理想工具。让我们来看看沟通问题的一些常见症状以及我们应该如何应对。

The SUCCESs checklist, then, is an ideal tool for dealing with communication problems. Let’s look at some common symptoms of communication problems and how we can respond to them.

症状及解决方法

SYMPTOMS AND SOLUTIONS

如何让人们

关注某个信息

Problems getting people to

pay attention to a message

症状： “没人听我说话”或“他们好像很无聊——他们总是听到这些话。”

SYMPTOM: “No one is listening to me” or “They seem bored—they hear this stuff all the time.”

解决方案：打破他们的猜测机制，给他们一个惊喜——告诉他们一些不为人知的常识。（比如，下周四不用上学！诺迪斯百货会用梅西百货的包装纸包装礼物！）

SOLUTION: Surprise them by breaking their guessing machines—tell them something that is uncommon sense. (The lead is, There will be no school next Thursday! Nordies gift-wrap packages from Macy’s!)

症状： “讲到一半我就跟丢了他们”或“讲到最后他们的注意力开始分散”。

SYMPTOM: “I lost them halfway through” or “Their attention was wavering toward the end.”

解决方案：制造好奇心空白——只透露足够的信息，让人们意识到自己知识体系中缺失的那一部分。（想想鲁恩·阿利奇在大学橄榄球赛开场时是如何介绍比赛背景的。）或者，制造一些谜题或谜语，在交流过程中逐步解开。（就像那位每节课都以一个谜题开场的教授，比如关于土星环的谜题。）

SOLUTION: Create curiosity gaps—tell people just enough for them to realize the piece that’s missing from their knowledge. (Remember Roone Arledge’s introductions to college football games, setting the context for the rivalry.) Or create mysteries or puzzles that are slowly solved over the course of the communication. (Like the professor who started each class with a mystery, such as the one about Saturn’s rings.)

如何让人们

理解和记住

Problems getting people to

understand and remember

症状： “当我向他们解释时，他们总是点头，但似乎从来没有转化为实际行动。”

SYMPTOM: “They always nod their heads when I explain it to them, but it never seems to translate into action.”

解决方案：简化信息，使用具体语言。利用人们已有的知识来阐明你的意图，例如运用启发性的类比（就像迪士尼的“演员”比喻）。或者使用具体的、现实生活中的例子。不要谈论“知识管理”，而是讲述一个赞比亚的卫生工作者如何从互联网上获取疟疾信息的故事。

SOLUTION: Make the message simpler and use concrete language. Use what people already know as a way to make your intentions clearer, as with a generative analogy (like Disney’s “cast member” metaphor). Or use concrete, real-world examples. Don’t talk about “knowledge management;” tell a story about a health worker in Zambia getting information on malaria from the Internet.

症状： “我们开会时，感觉每个人都在各说各话”或“每个人的知识水平都参差不齐，很难教他们”。

SYMPTOM: “We have these meetings where it seems like everyone is talking past each other” or “Everyone has such different levels of knowledge that it’s hard to teach them.”

解决方案：创建一个具体明确的环境，让人们能够运用所学知识。（想想看，在推销便携式电脑的风险投资路演中，创业者把活页夹扔到桌子上，引发了头脑风暴。）让人们着重分析具体的例子或案例，而不是空泛的概念。

SOLUTION: Create a highly concrete turf where people can apply their knowledge. (Think of the venture-capital pitch for a portable computer where the entrepreneur tossed his binder onto the table, sparking brainstorming.) Have people grapple with specific examples or cases rather than concepts.

难以让人们

相信你或同意你的观点

Problems getting people to

believe you or agree

症状： “他们不买账。”

SYMPTOM: “They’re not buying it.”

解决方案：找到能传达你信息的关键细节——就像那个跳舞的七十三岁老人，或者那家纺织厂一样。它环保，因为它确实能净化流经的水。减少官方权力的使用，增加反官方权力的使用。

SOLUTION: Find the telling details for your message—the equivalent of the dancing seventy-three-year-old man, or the textile factory so environmentally friendly that it actually cleans the water pouring through it. Use fewer authorities and more antiauthorities.

症状： “他们对我说的每句话都吹毛求疵”或“我把所有时间都花在和他们争论这件事上”。

SYMPTOM: “They quibble with everything I say” or “I spend all my time arguing with them about this.”

解决方案：用一个引人入胜的故事打消听众的疑虑，引导他们进入创作模式。摒弃统计数据和事实，转而举出有意义的例子。使用一个经得起“弗兰克·辛纳屈测试”的轶事。

SOLUTION: Quiet the audience’s mental skeptics by using a springboard story, switching them into creative mode. Move away from statistics and facts toward meaningful examples. Use an anecdote that passes the Sinatra Test.

如何让人们关心

Problems getting people to care

症状： “他们非常冷漠”或“似乎没有人对此感到热情”。

SYMPTOM: “They are so apathetic” or “No one seems fired up about this.”

解决方案：记住特蕾莎修女效应——人们更关心个体而非抽象概念。给他们讲一个鼓舞人心的挑战故事或创意故事。激发他们的自我认同感，就像“别惹德州”的广告一样，暗示不乱扔垃圾是德州人的风范。

SOLUTION: Remember the Mother Teresa effect—people care more about individuals than they do about abstractions. Tell them an inspiring Challenge plot or Creativity plot story. Tap into their sense of their own identities, like the “Don’t Mess with Texas” ads, which suggested that not littering was the Texan thing to do.

症状： “过去能让人兴奋的事物现在都无法让人兴奋了。”

SYMPTOM: “The things that used to get people excited just aren’t doing it anymore.”

解决方案：跳出马斯洛的需求层次理论，尝试诉诸更深层次的自身利益。

SOLUTION: Get out of Maslow’s basement and try appealing to more profound types of self-interest.

如何促使人们采取行动

Problems getting people to act

症状： “大家都点头，然后什么也没发生。”

SYMPTOM: “Everyone nods their heads and then nothing happens.”

解决方案：用挑战故事（例如贾里德、大卫和歌利亚的故事）激励他们，或者用引子故事（例如世界银行的故事）吸引他们。确保你的信息足够简单具体，以便于理解和应用——可以把它变成一句谚语（“人名，人名，还是人名”）。

SOLUTION: Inspire them with a Challenge plot story (Jared, David and Goliath) or engage them by using a springboard story (the World Bank). Make sure your message is simple and concrete enough to be useful—turn it into a proverb (“Names, names, and names”).

约翰·F·肯尼迪对阵弗洛伊德·李

John F. Kennedy versus Floyd Lee

“我相信，在本十年结束之前，这个国家应该致力于实现将人类送上月球并安全返回地球的目标。”这是约翰·F·肯尼迪在1961年5月发表的讲话。这是一项鼓舞人心的使命，也是一段振奋人心的讲话。正是这一个简单的想法，激励着整个国家为之奋斗了十年，最终取得了历史性的、令人难忘的成功。但关键在于：你不是肯尼迪。

“I believe that this nation should commit itself to achieving the goal, before this decade is out, of landing a man on the moon and returning him safely to the earth.” Those were John F. Kennedy’s words in May 1961. An inspiring message for an inspiring mission. It was a single idea that motivated a nation to a decade of work—and an eventual, historic, unforgettable success. But here’s the thing: You’re not JFK.

我们也不是。我们一点儿也没有他那样的魅力和权力。我们更关心的是，比如早上出门时记得带钱包，而不是登月。所以，如果只有成为肯尼迪才能让一个想法深入人心，那这本书读起来确实会令人沮丧。

And neither are we. We don’t have an ounce of his charisma or power. We are less concerned with traveling to the moon than with, say, remembering our wallets when we leave home in the morning. So, if being JFK was what it took to make an idea stick, this would be a depressing book indeed.

肯尼迪并非标准。事实上，他是个例外。请记住，在我们首次提到“登月演讲”的同一章节中，也提到了肯德基炸鼠。我们并非异想天开。

JFK isn’t the standard. In fact, he’s the aberration. Keep in mind that the same chapter where we first mentioned the “Man on the Moon” speech also contains a reference to the Kentucky Fried Rat. Our heads are not entirely in the clouds.

那些令人着迷的想法都有一些共同点，而本书正是对它们进行了逆向工程。我们研究过一些荒谬的想法：比如偷肾贼和他们装满冰块的浴缸。我们也研究过一些绝妙的想法：比如溃疡是由细菌引起的。我们研究过一些原本枯燥乏味的想法变得有趣：比如飞行安全须知。我们还研究过一些原本有趣的想法变得枯燥乏味：比如可以拯救成千上万儿童生命的口服补液盐。我们还探讨过与报纸、会计、核战争、福音传播、安全带、灰尘、跳舞、垃圾、足球、艾滋病、航运和汉堡包等相关的各种想法。

Sticky ideas have things in common, and in this book we’ve reverse-engineered them. We’ve studied preposterous ideas: the kidney thieves and their ice-filled bathtub. We’ve studied brilliant ideas: Ulcers are caused by bacteria. We’ve studied boring ideas made interesting: the flight-safety announcement. We’ve studied interesting ideas made boring: Oral rehydration salts that could save the lives of thousands of kids. We’ve seen ideas related to newspapers, accounting, nuclear war, evangelism, seat belts, dust, dancing, litter, football, AIDS, shipping, and hamburgers.

我们发现，所有这些想法——无论深刻还是平凡，严肃还是荒诞——都具有共同的特征。我们希望，既然您已经理解了这些特征，就能将它们运用到您自己的想法中。当您分享一个故事而不是统计数据时，他们会笑。但当这个想法深入人心时……

And what we’ve seen is that all these ideas—profound and mundane, serious and silly—share common traits. Our hope is that, now that you understand these traits, you’ll be able to apply them to your own ideas. They laughed when you shared a story instead of a statistic. But when the idea stuck …

SUCCESs清单旨在成为一款极具实用性的工具。它之所以是清单而非公式，绝非偶然。它并不难，也并非高深莫测。但它也并非自然而然或凭直觉就能做到。它需要勤勉和觉察。

The SUCCESs checklist is intended to be a deeply practical tool. It’s no accident that it’s a checklist and not an equation. It’s not hard, and it’s not rocket science. But neither is it natural or instinctive. It requires diligence and it requires awareness.

这本书讲述的是一群普通人面对着普通问题，却仅仅通过运用这些原则（即便他们自己可能并未意识到这一点）就取得了惊人的成就。这些人如此平凡，以至于你见到他们时可能都认不出他们的名字。他们的名字并不出名，但他们的故事却令人难忘。

This book is filled with normal people facing normal problems who did amazing things simply by applying these principles (even if they weren’t aware that they were doing it). These people are so normal that you probably won’t even recognize their names when you see them. Their names aren’t sticky, but their stories are.

有位名叫阿特·西尔弗曼的人。他让全国人民不再吃那些极其不健康的电影院爆米花。他把一整天的油腻食物摆在一桶爆米花旁边，然后说：“这就是这种零食里饱和脂肪的含量。”一个有着普通工作、却改变了世界的人。

There was Art Silverman. He was the guy who stopped a nation from eating obscenely unhealthy movie popcorn. He laid out a full day’s worth of fatty foods next to a tub of popcorn and said, “This is how much saturated fat is in this snack.” A normal person with a normal job who made a difference.

诺拉·艾芙隆的新闻学老师。可怜的家伙，我们甚至都没提他的名字。他跟学生们说：“头条新闻是‘下周四停课’。”就这么一句话，他彻底改变了学生们对新闻的认知。他激励了艾芙隆——无疑也激励了许多其他人——走上新闻之路。一个普通人，一份普通的工作，却成就了一番事业。

There was Nora Ephron’s journalism teacher. Poor guy, we didn’t even mention his name. He told his class, “The lead is ‘There will be no school next Thursday.’” And in that one sentence he rewrote his students’ image of journalism. He inspired Ephron—and doubtless many others—to become journalists. A normal person with a normal job who made a difference.

鲍勃·奥克维亚呢？你肯定不记得他的名字。他是一家赛百味加盟店的老板，每天都给一个胖子做三明治，从中发现了一个绝佳的故事。正是因为奥克维亚，才有了后来大获成功的“杰瑞德运动”。一个普通人，一份普通的工作，却改变了世界。

What about Bob Ocwieja? No chance you remember his name. He’s the Subway franchise owner who served sandwiches to a fat guy every day and spotted a great story in the making. Because of Ocwieja the hugely successful Jared campaign was discovered and launched. A normal person with a normal job who made a difference.

还有弗洛伊德·李，他是驻伊拉克“飞马”食堂的负责人。他将自己的职责定义为鼓舞士气，而非提供餐饮服务。他和其他士兵一样，领取同样的物资，但士兵们却蜂拥至他的帐篷，他的糕点师甚至开始用“感性”来形容她做的甜点。一个普通人，一份普通的工作，却成就了非凡。

Then there was Floyd Lee, the leader of the Pegasus mess hall in Iraq. He defined his role as being about morale, not food service. He got the same supplies as everyone else, but soldiers flocked to his tent and his pastry chef started describing her desserts as “sensual.” A normal person with a normal job who made a difference.

还有简·艾略特。她在课堂上模拟种族偏见的教学活动，至今仍深深地印在学生们的脑海中，其影响之深远，远超其他任何人。二十年后。毫不夸张地说，她提出了一个像疫苗一样消除偏见的理念。一个从事普通工作的普通人，却做出了改变。

And there was Jane Elliott. Her classroom simulation of racial prejudice is still etched in the minds of her students more than twenty years later. It’s not a stretch to say that she came up with an idea that prevented prejudice, like a vaccine. A normal person with a normal job who made a difference.

这些人之所以脱颖而出，是因为他们提出了能够产生影响的理念。他们没有权力，没有名望，没有公关公司，没有广告预算，也没有舆论操纵者。他们拥有的只有理念。

All these people distinguished themselves by crafting ideas that made a difference. They didn’t have power or celebrity or PR firms or advertising dollars or spinmeisters. All they had were ideas.

这就是思想世界的伟大之处——我们每个人，只要拥有正确的洞察力和正确的信息，就能让一个想法深入人心。

And that’s the great thing about the world of ideas—any of us, with the right insight and the right message, can make an idea stick.

置顶建议

STICKY ADVICE

一、谈论策略

I. TALKING STRATEGY

S自《让创意更有粘性》（Made to Stick）出版以来，我们有机会与众多机构合作，并惊讶地发现，他们的对外沟通通常比内部沟通要复杂得多。不妨比较一下典型的客户和典型的员工。企业花费数百万美元试图了解典型的客户。他们研究、分析客户，记录客户的需求和喜好，并精心定制信息，以迎合客户的关注点，并通过便捷的渠道传递给客户。

Since the release of Made to Stick, we’ve had the chance to work with a lot of organizations, and we’ve been surprised to find that their external communications are usually far more sophisticated than their internal communications. Compare a typical customer with a typical employee. Companies spend millions trying to understand the Typical Customer. He is studied and analyzed. His whims are plotted and charted. Messages are laboriously tailored to his concerns and delivered to him via convenient media.

与此同时，普通员工每月都会收到一份平淡无奇（但令人愉悦）的电子邮件简报，这份简报是由一位倒霉的人力资源员工在 90 分钟内拼凑而成的。

Meanwhile, the Typical Employee receives a bland (but cheerful) monthly e-mail newsletter, which an unlucky HR employee hacked together in ninety minutes.

我们当然是在开玩笑，但趋势显而易见：客户沟通备受重视，而员工沟通却被忽视。这对组织领导者来说是一个绝佳的机会。员工需要了解组织的价值观、发展方向以及成功之道。换句话说，他们需要能够“谈论战略”。如果他们能够就战略问题与你进行交流，你就能获得原本无法挖掘和察觉的宝贵洞察。

We are being facetious, of course, but the trend is unmistakable: Customer communication is taken very seriously, and employee communication isn’t. And that’s a tremendous opportunity for organizational leaders. Employees need to understand what your organization stands for, where it’s headed, and what will make it successful. In other words, they need to be able to “talk strategy.” And if they can talk strategy back to you, you’ll benefit from insights that would otherwise be untapped and invisible.

要了解为什么战略沟通能力如此有效，不妨看看Cranium公司。这家公司生产热门桌游Cranium以及其他众多产品。Cranium的联合创始人惠特·亚历山大回忆起有一次，他打电话给一家中国制造合作伙伴，描述一种新型塑料游戏配件的概念。这个配件是紫色的，由多个需要粘合的部件组成。他的合作伙伴却犹豫了。“这不是CHIFF，”他说。亚历山大感到非常惊讶。远在地球另一端的供应商竟然用Cranium公司自己的战略语言纠正了他。而且，供应商的说法完全正确。

To see why the ability to talk strategy can be so effective, consider Cranium, the company that manufactures the hit board game Cranium and many other products. Whit Alexander, the co-founder of Cranium, recalls a time when he called a Chinese manufacturing partner to describe a concept for a new plastic game piece. The piece would be purple and made of multiple parts that would need to be glued together. His partner balked. “It’s not CHIFF,” he said. Alexander was astonished. His supplier, halfway across the globe, had just corrected him using Cranium’s own strategic language. And the supplier was absolutely right.

CHIFF 是“巧妙、高质量、创新、友好、有趣”的缩写。CHIFF 理念定义了 Cranium 在竞争异常激烈的桌游市场中的战略差异化。CHIFF 指导着公司各个层面的决策——从品牌塑造到包装设计，再到每个问题的具体内容。（例如：游戏中曾有一个问题问最高法院有多少位大法官。这个问题因为不够巧妙有趣而被否决，不符合 CHIFF 的理念。于是，问题被修改为：“在下列哪项运动中，美国最高法院的成员可以组成一支合格的队伍，而‘法官席’上却空无一人？”）

CHIFF is an acronym that stands for “Clever, High-quality, Innovative, Friendly, Fun.” The CHIFF concept defines Cranium’s strategic differentiation in the extremely competitive board-game market. CHIFF informs decisions across the organization—from branding to package design and the content of individual questions. (Example: A suggested question for the game asked how many justices were on the Supreme Court. It was rejected for being insufficiently clever and fun to be CHIFF. So it was rewritten: “In which of these sports could the members of the U.S. Supreme Court field a regulation team, with no justices left on the ‘bench’?”)

那位中国制造商曾批评亚历山大的游戏棋子设计过于粗糙。用胶水粘起来？这既不创新也不高品质；棋子的触感肯定也不好。然而，制造商最终设计出的棋子却光滑而新颖，以至于玩家在游戏过程中会把备用棋子拿在手里反复把玩，享受触觉上的愉悦。制造商不仅提高了棋子的质量，还赋予了它真正的乐趣。亚历山大对此印象深刻。

The Chinese manufacturer had chastised Alexander for his kludgy idea for a game piece. Glued together? That’s not particularly innovative or high-quality; the feel of the piece would be all wrong The manufacturer came back with design so smooth and novel that during a game players would hold spare pieces in their hand, turning them over and over just for tactile pleasure. Not only had the manufacturer improved the quality, he had also made a game piece fun. Alexander was impressed.

这是一个游戏棋盘制造的成功案例。但更重要的是，这是一个战略成功的案例。Cranium 的高管们开发了一种方法，能够以实用且易于理解的方式传达公司战略的关键要素——使其优于竞争对手的竞争优势。“CHIFF”就是一个清晰、可操作的战略差异化声明。Cranium 的员工、供应商和渠道合作伙伴都利用 CHIFF 来做出数百项实际决策，从而捍卫 Cranium 的竞争优势。

This is a game-board manufacturing success story. More important, though, it is a strategy success story. The executives of Cranium developed a way to communicate a crucial element of the company’s strategy—the competitive advantage that makes it better than its competitors—in a useful, comprehensible way. “CHIFF” is simply a clear, actionable statement of strategic differentiation. Cranium employees, suppliers, and channel partners all use CHIFF to make hundreds of on-the-ground decisions that defend Cranium’s competitive differentiation.

说实话，如果一个身处异国他乡、母语不同的制造供应商用该战略来纠正公司创始人的错误，这无疑是对一项战略已正确传达的最清晰的证明。

Let’s face it, there is no clearer proof that a strategy has been communicated properly than when a manufacturing supplier, in another country, with a different native language, uses it to correct the founder of the company.

CHIFF之所以有效，是因为它尊重那些使理念“深入人心”的原则——易于理解、令人难忘且能有效改变现状。思想或行为。这些创造深入人心理念的原则可以用来改变公司内部战略沟通的方式。

CHIFF works because it respects the principles that make ideas “sticky”—understandable, memorable, and effective in changing thought or behavior. And these principles for creating sticky ideas can be used to transform the way strategy is communicated within a firm.

策略对话

Talking Strategy

战略的核心在于指导行为。它通过影响组织内员工做出的成千上万个大大小小的决策而得以发挥作用。好的战略能够驱动企业采取差异化行动，从而实现财务成功。而糟糕的战略则会导致企业竞争力下降，差异化程度降低。然而，许多战略却形同虚设。它们的好坏难以判断，因为它们无法驱动实际行动。它们或许以完美的形式存在于PPT文档、“战略规划”文件夹或高管演讲稿中。但如果它们无法转化为实际行动，它们就毫无意义，只是纸上谈兵。

A strategy is, at its core, a guide to behavior. It comes to life through its ability to influence thousands of decisions, both big and small, made by employees throughout an organization. A good strategy drives actions that differentiate the company and produce financial success. A bad strategy drives actions that lead to a less competitive, less differentiated position. A lot of strategies, though, are simply inert. Whether they are good or bad is impossible to determine, because they do not drive action. They may exist in pristine form in a PowerPoint document, or in a “strategic planning” binder, or in speeches made by top executives. But if they don’t manifest themselves in action they are inert, irrelevant. They’re academic.

战略失效并非源于缺乏努力或良好意愿。每位高管都希望团队能够理解战略。然而，有三个棘手的障碍会使战略沟通更加困难。我们将探讨这些障碍，并提出克服它们的建议。

It’s not a lack of effort or good intentions that renders a strategy inert. Every executive wants his team to understand. But there are three nasty barriers that make strategic communication more difficult. We’ll discuss them and offer suggestions for overcoming them.

障碍一：知识的诅咒

Barrier 1: The Curse of Knowledge

如果说《让创意更有粘性》这本书中有一个概念我们希望能够更着重强调，那就是“知识诅咒”。我们处处都能看到它的影响。正如我们在书中讨论的所有领域一样，“知识诅咒”会在领导者试图向组织其他成员传达战略时困扰他们。它导致高管们在谈论战略时，仿佛自己就是听众。它诱使他们使用笼统、高屋建瓴且抽象的语言：最高效的半导体制造商！成本最低的音响设备供应商！世界一流的客户服务！

If there’s one concept we wish we had emphasized more in Made to Stick, it’s the Curse of Knowledge. We see its effects everywhere. And, as in all the domains we discussed in the book, the Curse of Knowledge afflicts leaders when they try to communicate a strategy to the rest of an organization. It leads executives to talk about strategy as though they themselves were the audience. It tempts them to use language that is sweeping, high-level, and abstract: The most efficient manufacturer of semiconductors! The lowest-cost provider of stereo equipment! World-class customer service!

很多时候，领导者甚至没有意识到自己是在进行抽象的表达。当一位CEO敦促她的团队“释放股东价值”时，这个挑战对她来说意义非凡。就像“敲击者与聆听者”游戏一样，她的脑海中响起一首员工听不到的歌。“释放股东价值”对我如何对待这位特定客户意味着什么？成为“最高质量的生产者”对我与这位难缠的供应商谈判又意味着什么？

Often, leaders aren’t even aware that they’re speaking abstractly. When a CEO urges her team to “unlock shareholder value,” that challenge means something vivid to her. As in the Tappers and Listeners game, there’s a song playing in her head that the employees can’t hear. What does “unlocking shareholder value” mean for how I treat this particular customer? What does being the “highest-quality producer” mean for my negotiation with this difficult vendor?

领导者无法忘记他们已知的知识，但他们可以通过将战略“转化”成具体的语言来克服知识诅咒。例如，Trader Joe's 是一家专营特色食品的超市，出售价格低廉但风味独特的食品。在 Trader Joe's，您可以花 2.53 美元买到摩洛哥炖菜酱，或者花 1.99 美元买到一夸脱红椒汤。Trader Joe's 将其目标客户描述为“一位开着破旧沃尔沃的失业大学教授”。显然，这种描述过于简化——在任何特定时刻，Trader Joe's 的“目标客户”可能一个也没有。“失业大学教授”的形象对 Trader Joe's 的作用在于：它确保了组织中的每个人都对客户有共同的认知。

Now, leaders can’t unlearn what they know. But they can thwart the Curse of Knowledge by “translating” their strategies into concrete language. For instance, Trader Joe’s is a specialty food market that carries inexpensive but exotic food. At Trader Joe’s, you might purchase some Moroccan simmer sauce for $2.53 or a quart of red-pepper soup for $1.99. Trader Joe’s describes its target customer as an “unemployed college professor who drives a very, very used Volvo.” The image is a simplification, obviously—at any given moment, there are probably zero of these “target customers” in Trader Joe’s. What the “unemployed college professor” image does for Trader Joe’s is this: It ensures that everyone in the organization has a common picture of the customer.

每项战略的关键要素之一，就是决定公司将服务于哪些市场和客户。“失业大学教授”的形象就直接触及了这一点。Trader Joe's 本可以把顾客描述为“社会经济地位较高、既注重品质又注重预算、重视多样性和新鲜体验的人群”。但这种堆砌形容词的表述，远不如那位失业大学教授的形象那样清晰生动。这位教授会喜欢红椒汤吗？当然会。知识的诅咒已被打破。

A crucial element of every strategy is deciding which markets and customers a company will serve. The “unemployed college professor” speaks directly to this issue. Trader Joe’s could have referred to its customers as “people who are of high socioeconomic status and are quality-conscious but also budget-conscious, and who value variety and new experiences.” But this adjective-filled statement doesn’t provide as clear an image as the unemployed college professor. Would the professor like the red-pepper soup? Yep. The Curse of Knowledge has been thwarted.

讲故事尤其能有效地规避“知识诅咒”，因为它们迫使我们使用具体的语言。例如，联邦快递设立了“紫色承诺”奖，旨在表彰那些信守联邦快递“绝对保证”包裹隔夜送达承诺的员工。“紫色承诺”奖表彰的故事包括：在圣文森特岛，一起拖车事故堵塞了主干道。通往机场的道路。一名司机和一名地勤人员尝试了所有可能的替代路线前往机场，但都被交通堵塞所阻挡。最终，走投无路之下，他们只好步行，将所有包裹扛着走完最后一英里到达机场，确保了航班准时起飞。在纽约，一辆送货卡车抛锚，而替代的货车又迟迟未到，联邦快递的司机最初步行送了一些包裹，但后来，由于担心无法按时完成配送路线，她设法说服了竞争对手的一名司机帮她完成最后几单。

Stories work particularly well in dodging the Curse of Knowledge, because they force us to use concrete language. For instance, FedEx has an award called the Purple Promise, which honors employees who keep FedEx’s delivery promise that packages will “absolutely, positively” arrive overnight. The Purple Promise award honors stories like these: In St. Vincent, a tractor-trailer accident blocked the main road going into the airport. Together, a driver and a ramp agent tried every possible alternate route to the airport, but they were stymied by traffic jams. Eventually, having run out of options, they struck out on foot, carrying every package the last mile to the airport, which ensured an on-time departure. In New York, after a delivery truck broke down and the replacement van was running late, the FedEx driver initially delivered a few packages on foot, but then, despairing of finishing her route on time, she managed to persuade a driver from a competitor to take her on her last few deliveries.

这些故事不仅仅是引人入胜的趣闻轶事，它们更是公司竞争优势的切实体现——以生动、具体、贴近实际的方式，展现了公司成为全球最可靠的物流公司这一核心竞争力。与CHIFF一样，这些故事能够为整个组织的决策提供依据。一位资深销售主管可以借用纽约的故事来传达：“这就是我们对可靠性的重视程度。”一位新入职的送货司机可以以此为行为准则：“我的工作不是跑完一条路线，下午5点就回家；我的工作是尽我所能把包裹送到目的地。”一位运营人员可以借鉴这个故事，更好地制定维护合同——例如，争取为送货卡车争取最快的维护周期是值得的。

These are not just interesting stories. They are tangible demonstrations—in vivid, concrete, on-the-ground terms—of the company’s competitive advantage, which is to be the most reliable shipping company in the world. Like CHIFF, these stories can work to inform decisions across the organization. A top sales executive can use the New York story to convey, “This is how seriously we take reliability.” A new delivery driver can use the story as a guide to behavior: “My job is not to drive a route and go home at 5 P.M.; my job is to get packages delivered any way I can.” An operations person can use the story to make better decisions about maintenance contracts—for example, it’s worth negotiating for the fastest possible maintenance cycles on delivery trucks.

好的战略应该指导行为，而讲故事比千篇一律的宣传口号更能起到这样的作用。正如伊芙琳·克拉克在《企业篝火旁》（Around the Corporate Campfire ）一书中描述的那样，在好市多（Costco） ，人们会讲述“三文鱼的故事”。好市多联合创始人吉姆·西内加尔（Jim Sinegal）说道：“1996年，我们公司每周以每磅5.99美元的价格售出价值15万至20万美元的三文鱼片。后来，我们的采购员以更优惠的价格获得了一种改良产品，这种产品去除了腹部脂肪、背鳍和锁骨。因此，我们将零售价降至每磅5.29美元。所以，他们既改进了产品，又降低了价格。”

A good strategy should guide behavior, and a story can work better in this role than the standard boilerplate missionspeak. At Costco, as described in the book Around the Corporate Campfire, by Evelyn Clark, people talk about “salmon stories.” Jim Sinegal, the co-founder of Costco, said, “In 1996 we were selling between $150,000 and $200,000 of salmon fillets company-wide every week at $5.99 a pound. Then our buyers were able to get an improved product with belly fat, back fins, and collarbones removed at a better price. As a result, we reduced our retail price to $5.29. So they improved the product and lowered the price.”

但买家们并未就此罢休。随后，他们又以更优惠的价格采购了去刺去皮的三文鱼，并将这种高品质三文鱼的价格降至每磅4.99美元。之后，由于低价带来了巨大的销量，Costco开始直接向加拿大和智利的三文鱼养殖场大量订购，最终将零售价降至每磅4.79美元。这说明了什么？Costco代表着对品质不断提升、价格不断下降的不懈追求。“三文鱼故事”，就像CHIFF的元素一样，为传达公司的竞争优势提供了一种绝妙的方式。

But the buyers weren’t finished. They subsequently negotiated for salmon at an even better price that had the pin bones and skin removed. They lowered the price on this higher-quality salmon to $4.99 a pound. Later, because the lower prices were driving large volumes of sales, Costco began to place big orders directly with Canadian and Chilean salmon farms, which drove the retail price down to $4.79. The point? Costco stands for the relentless pursuit of ever-increasing quality at ever-decreasing prices. “Salmon stories,” like the elements of CHIFF, provide a brilliant way to communicate the company’s competitive advantage.

Sinegal 说：“我们经常把这个故事当作教学工具，以至于公司里的其他买家，比如加拿大的一位服装买家，都走过来对我说：‘我也有一个关于鲑鱼的故事要跟你讲。’”

Sinegal says, “We’ve used that story so much as a teaching tool that I’ve had other buyers in the company, such as a clothing buyer in Canada, come up to me and say, ‘I’ve got a salmon story to tell you.’”

前两段，你读到了“Costco 致力于以不断降低的价格提供不断提升的品质”这句话。请注意，这句话是对鲑鱼故事的概括——句末的标点符号起到了关键作用。但这里有个反直觉的地方：脱离了故事，这句话本身就很难成立。像这样脱离相关故事的抽象句子，就好比在“敲击者与聆听者”游戏中扮演敲击者的角色。“以不断降低的价格提供不断提升的品质”对于一位多年来耳濡目染鲑鱼故事的高管来说，或许意义深远，但对于没有类似经历的人来说，这句话就显得枯燥乏味、语焉不详。（我们该如何降低价格？如果既不能降低价格又要维持同样的盈利水平怎么办？）

Two paragraphs back, you came across the sentence “Costco stands for the relentless pursuit of ever-increasing quality at ever-decreasing prices.” Note that the sentence works as a summary of the salmon story—it’s punctuation on the end of the sentence. But here’s the counterintuitive part: It doesn’t stand alone very well without the story. Saying an abstract sentence like that one, without the related story, is the same as being the Tapper in the Tapper and Listener game. “Ever-increasing quality at ever-decreasing prices” is something that is powerful and profound to an executive who has internalized years of salmon stories, but it’s sort of dry and vague to someone who doesn’t have access to those same experiences. (How do we decrease prices? What if you can’t decrease the prices and maintain the same level of profitability?)

能够体现组织战略的故事包含两部分：故事本身和故事蕴含的寓意。两者兼备固然好。但如果必须二选一，那就选择故事本身。因为寓意蕴含在故事之中，而故事本身却并非寓意所蕴含。而且，故事——凭借其具体的语言、鲜明的人物和真实的背景——更有可能引导人们的行为。

Stories that speak to an organization’s strategy have two parts. There’s the story itself, and there’s the moral of the story. It’s nice to have both. If you have to choose between the two, though, choose the story. Because the moral is implicit in the story, but the story is not implicit in the moral. And the story—with its concrete language, specific protagonists, and real-world setting—is more likely to guide behavior.

故事和具体语言都能帮助领导者避免知识诅咒，组织中的每个人都能从对战略的共同理解中受益。

Both stories and concrete language help leaders dodge the Curse of Knowledge, and everyone in the organization benefits from a shared understanding of the strategy.

障碍二：决策瘫痪

Barrier 2: Decision paralysis

组织中的大多数人并不负责制定战略；他们只需要理解战略并以此为基础做出决策。但许多战略不够具体，无法解决一种根深蒂固的心理偏差，即决策瘫痪。

Most people in an organization aren’t in charge of formulating strategy; they just have to understand the strategy and use it to make decisions. But many strategies aren’t concrete enough to resolve a well-established psychological bias called decision paralysis.

心理学家发现，即使只是在几个不错的选项中做出选择，也会让我们陷入决策瘫痪。（我们在“简单”一章中讨论过一个例子。）在《选择的悖论》一书中，巴里·施瓦茨探讨了许多其他决策瘫痪的例子。想象一下，在一家杂货店里有两张桌子，上面摆放着各种果酱供顾客品尝。一张桌子上有24种果酱，另一张桌子上只有6种。两张品尝桌都很受顾客欢迎。但是，当统计果酱销量时，结果令人震惊：只有6罐果酱的那张桌子的销量竟然是另一张桌子的十倍！（人们根本无法决定在24罐果酱中买哪一罐。）拥有401(k)退休计划的公司员工也会经历决策瘫痪。计划中每增加10种共同基金作为投资选择，员工的退休储蓄就会减少1%。决策瘫痪甚至会影响到爱情领域。单身人士参加快速约会活动，与六个人见面后，建立的恋爱关系比与十二个人见面时更多。

Psychologists have uncovered situations where the mere existence of choice, even choice among several good options, seems to paralyze us in making decisions. (We discuss one example in the “Simple” chapter). In The Paradox of Choice, Barry Schwartz discusses many other examples of decision paralysis. Imagine two tables in a grocery store where you can taste different kinds of jam. One table has twenty-four kinds of jam, and the other has six. Both tasting tables were popular with customers. But when the sales of jam were tallied, there was a shock: The table with only six jars generated ten times as many sales as the other table! (People simply couldn’t decide which jar, among twenty-four, to buy.) Employees of companies with 401(k) plans also experience decision paralysis. For every additional ten mutual funds offered as investment choices by the plan, the employees reduce their retirement saving by 1 percent. Decision paralysis even affects the domain of love. When singles attended a speed-dating session where they met six other people, they formed more relationships than they did when they met twelve.

如果决策瘫痪影响零售、金融和约会行业，那么你完全可以肯定，它也影响着你的员工。想想公司里决策瘫痪的根源是什么。每个组织都必须在各种诱人的选项中做出选择：客户服务与成本最小化；收入增长与利润最大化；质量与上市速度；人才发展与季度需求。把这些矛盾交织在一起——营造出一种充满潜在机遇、风险、不确定性和信息不完整的氛围——就很容易导致决策瘫痪。

If decision paralysis affects retailing and finance and dating, you can feel pretty confident that it’s affecting your employees, too. Think about the sources of decision paralysis in your company. Every organization must make choices among attractive options: Customer service versus cost minimization. Revenue growth versus maximizing profitability. Quality versus speed to market. People development versus the needs of the quarter. Mix together lots of these tensions—an atmosphere full of potential opportunities and risks and uncertainties and incomplete information—and you’ve got a recipe for paralysis.

此外，许多经典战略陈述，例如探索，都与此相关。为了成为“低成本供应商”，企业往往忽略了许多权衡取舍——例如质量与上市速度之间的权衡。当然，领导者可以通过将所有因素都写入规则来解决决策瘫痪：尝试所有可用的药物后再进行手术！事实上，许多公司确实采用了这种方法——看看那些发给新员工的厚达三英寸的文件夹，上面详细解释了“公司政策”就知道了。但是，你永远无法制定出足够多的规则来涵盖员工必须做出的所有决策。世界是复杂的，而且在不断变化。然而，规则却阻止了任何人去适应这个世界，除了制定规则的领导者。

Furthermore, many classic strategy statements, such as the quest to be the “low-cost provider,” simply don’t speak to many of these trade-offs—for instance the trade-off between quality and speed to market. Now, leaders could solve decision paralysis by encoding everything into a rule: Try all available medications before proceeding to surgery! Many companies do, in fact, adopt this approach—witness the three-inch-thick binders given to new employees to explain “company policy.” But you can never generate enough rules to encompass all the decisions that must be made by your employees. The world is complex, and it evolves. Yet rules forbid anyone to adapt to the world except the leaders who write the rules.

如何通过战略帮助员工摆脱决策瘫痪？当人们能够讨论战略时，他们更有可能做出正确的决策，而不是仅仅将战略视为一套规则。一线员工都想做正确的事。他们中的大多数人都能轻松地分辨正确与错误。真正的难题在于如何在两个“正确”的选项之间做出选择。

How can strategy liberate employees from decision paralysis? When people are able to talk about strategy, they’re more likely to make good decisions than when strategy exists only as a set of rules. Frontline employees want to do the right thing. Most of them find it quite easy to decide between the right thing and the wrong thing. The problem is deciding between the right thing and the right thing.

毕竟，最难的决定往往是在两个不错的选择之间做出抉择。以Costco的鲑鱼为例。假设你以每磅5.99美元的价格大量销售鲑鱼，随后你又以更低的价格获得了更高品质的鲑鱼货源，你会怎么做？你知道，即使以5.99美元的价格出售，鲑鱼的需求量也足以售罄。那么，你是维持价格（甚至提高价格）以提高股东利润，还是降低价格以专注于为顾客提供价值？这是一个在两个不错的选择之间做出抉择的问题。要做出这样的选择，你需要一个优先级指标，而鲑鱼的故事就提供了这样一个指标。鲑鱼的故事展现了Costco的竞争优势，它有力地传达了Costco的首要任务是顾客至上，而非股东利益。（或者更准确地说，是顾客价值高于短期股东利润。）

The hardest decisions, after all, are the ones where we must decide between two good options. Consider the Costco salmon story. If you’re selling scads of salmon at $5.99 per pound, and subsequently you secure a supply of higher-quality salmon at a lower price, what do you do? You know that there’s enough demand for the salmon to exhaust your supply at the $5.99 price point. So do you maintain the price (or even raise it) to deliver a better bottom line for shareholders? Or do you cut the price to maintain your focus on value for customers? This is a choice between two good options. To make such a choice, you need an index of priorities, and the salmon story provides it. The salmon story is a statement of competitive advantage that drives home the message that Costco’s priority is the customer over the shareholder. (Or, to be more precise, customer value over short-term shareholder profits.)

组织在制定战略时，不仅要考虑竞争优势，还要考虑自身的内部能力。我们需要哪些能力才能发展壮大？我们需要哪些技能？员工需要成功地取悦客户，而我们又该如何随着时间的推移不断提升客户服务水平呢？托马斯·阿尔瓦·爱迪生（留声机和电灯泡的发明者）就是一个很好的例子，他用战略性的语言阐述了内部能力。爱迪生并非孤军奋战；他在新泽西州门洛帕克创建了第一个工业研发实验室。他实验室里的研究人员被称为“muckers”（意为“混日子的人”）。这个词源于当时的两个俚语——“to muck in”意为伙伴合作，“to muck around”意为胡闹。为什么爱迪生的研究人员会用这种方式来讨论战略呢？

Organizations, in formulating their strategies, must grapple not just with competitive advantage but with their internal capabilities. What capabilities do we need in order to grow? What skills will our employees need to successfully please customers, and how will we get better at serving our customers over time? An example of strategic language that speaks to internal capabilities comes from Thomas Alva Edison, the inventor of the phonograph and the lightbulb. Edison was not a lone inventor; he created the first industrial R&D lab in Menlo Park, New Jersey. The researchers in his labs were called “muckers.” The term comes from two slang phrases of the time—“to muck in” was to work together as mates, and “to muck around” was to fool around. Why was this a good way for Edison’s researchers to talk strategy?

在任何创业型组织中，效率与实验之间都存在着天然的张力。创新需要实验和自由，而这必然会伴随着死胡同、时间浪费和错误——所有这些反过来都会降低效率。因此，爱迪生所处的环境极易导致决策瘫痪：我们该如何在效率和实验之间做出选择？效率意味着降低成本、提高利润率和获得更多订单。实验则意味着开发新产品和其他机遇。在日常生活中，当这种冲突出现时，我们该如何抉择？（例如，“我可以花一个小时在实验室里瞎捣鼓吗？”）

In any entrepreneurial organization, there’s a natural tension between efficiency and experimentation. Innovation requires experimentation and freedom, and it necessarily involves dead ends and wasted time and errors—all of which, in turn, will reduce efficiency. Edison’s environment, then, is ripe for decision paralysis: How do we decide between efficiency and experimentation? Efficiency promises reduced costs, better margins, and more orders. Experimentation promises new products and other opportunities. How do you choose in the myriad daily situations where the conflict will arise? (E.g., “Is it okay to spend the next hour of my time fooling around in the lab?”)

“实干家”这个词其实是一种伪装成昵称的策略表述。它明确地表明，在效率和实验之间做出艰难抉择时，你会选择实验。为什么？因为你是个实干家。实干家不会执着于甘特图。实干家就是实干。而实干家之所以实干，正是因为这种组织能力将使门洛帕克取得成功。深思熟虑地讨论策略可以减轻决策瘫痪的负担。

The term “muckers” is a strategy statement masquerading as a nickname. It makes it clear that, given the tough choice between efficiency and experimentation, you choose experimentation. Why? Because you’re a mucker. Muckers don’t obsess over Gantt charts. Muckers muck. And muckers muck because that is precisely the organizational capability that will make Menlo Park successful. Talking strategy in a thoughtful way can relieve the burden of decision paralysis.

障碍三：缺乏共同语言

Barrier 3: Lack of a common language

在20世纪50年代经典的传播模型中，“发送者”与“接收者”进行沟通。这种比喻表明，信息是通过发送者传递的。传递信息就像一个包裹——一面包裹严实，另一面则完全打开。很多沟通方式确实如此——教授给学生讲课，牧师给信众布道等等。战略沟通也应该如此吗？

In the classic 1950s models of communication, a “sender” communicates with a “receiver.” The metaphor suggests that the message passed is a kind of package—wrapped up on one side and unwrapped on the other. There is certainly a lot of communication that operates in this way—professors lecturing to their students, ministers preaching to their congregations, etc. Should strategic communication work this way?

绝对不是。良好的战略沟通就像世界语。它能促进母语不同的人们之间的沟通，并开辟出一片可以共享的领域。员工依靠领导者制定组织的行动计划。领导者依靠员工反馈行动进展情况。要使这种对话有效，双方都必须能够互相理解。说起来容易做起来难。

Absolutely not. Good strategic communication is like Esperanto. It facilitates communication among people who have different native languages and carves out turf that people can share. Employees rely on leaders to define the organization’s game plan. Leaders rely on employees to tell them how the game is going. For this dialogue to work, both sides must be able to understand each other. This is easier said than done.

战略的表述方式往往让员工难以反驳领导。例如，假设 Cranium 公司宣称的战略是“成为领先的桌面娱乐产品供应商”。现在，假设你是这家中国制造商，你对新游戏组件的设计不满意。你会基于什么理由提出异议呢？战略如此高屋建瓴、如此抽象，以至于你会觉得自己的反驳毫无意义。你会说什么呢？“使用这种粘合在一起的组件会威胁到我们领先的供应商地位”？恐怕不会。

Strategy is often articulated in a way that makes it hard for employees to talk back to leaders. For instance, suppose Cranium’s stated strategy had been “To be the No. 1 provider of engaging table-top entertainment.” Now, imagine that you are the Chinese manufacturer, and that you are displeased with the design of the new game piece. On what grounds do you state your objection? The strategy is so high-level, so abstract, that it would make you feel foolish to talk back. What are you going to say? “Using this glued-together piece will threaten our No. 1 provider position”? Doubtful.

这家位于澳大利亚阿德莱德的储蓄贷款信用合作社（Savings & Loans Credit Union）是一家不拘一格的公司，它发展出一套通用的战略语言。公司内部是这样定义其战略的：“我们不想当第一，但我们绝对不想当第三。” 这意味着：他们希望公司成为快速追随者。他们会退居幕后，让先行者承担风险，抢占创新的荣光，然后紧随其后进行复制，并且复制的内容要比原版更加精炼。例如，一家竞争对手推出了一款信用卡，将部分佣金捐赠给一个环保组织。这张卡最终失败了，但与此同时，储蓄贷款信用合作社推出了自己的信用卡，与当地儿童医院合作，这张卡一炮而红——该卡的收益资助了急诊科250万美元的翻新工程。

The scrappy Savings & Loans Credit Union, based in Adelaide, Australia, has developed a common strategic language. Internally, the company defines its strategy this way: “We don’t want to be first, but we sure as hell don’t want to be third.” The meaning: They want the company to be a fast-follower. They’ll stand back and let the first mover take the risk and grab the glory of innovation, then they’ll come in right behind and copy it, while making the copy crisper than the original. For instance, a competitor offered a credit card that paid part of its commission to an environmental group. The card was a flop, but meanwhile Savings & Loans had ginned up its own card affiliated with the local children’s hospital, which was an instant hit—proceeds from the card funded a $2.5 million renovation of the Emergency Department.

战略清晰明了，很容易看出它如何影响整个公司的行为。营销人员应该不断扫描环境，寻找好的创意。人力资源部门需要寻找执行力强、行动迅速的新员工，而不是创意先锋。高管需要通过激励机制奖励那些改进者，而不是发明家。战略格言简洁明了，为充满选择的复杂环境带来了清晰的思路。

The strategy is clear, and it’s easy to see how it informs behavior across the firm. Marketers should be constantly scanning the environment for good ideas. HR needs to find new employees who are good, quick executors, not creative pioneers. The executives need to use incentives to reward people who are improvers, not inventors. The strategy motto is a one-liner that brings clarity to an environment muddied with choices.

同样重要的是，它为一线员工提供了一个与高管争论的渠道。假设总裁正在力推一项新的移动银行计划；他坚信这是市场的发展方向，而储蓄贷款机构需要保持竞争力。但是，一位柜员却说：等等，我们不想做第一个吃螃蟹的人。为什么不等等看一些竞争对手是否愿意尝试移动银行，然后我们密切关注哪些有效，哪些无效呢？

Just as important, it provides a way for front-liners to quibble with executives. Let’s say the president is pushing for a new initiative in mobile banking; he’s convinced that’s where the market is heading, and Savings & Loans needs to stay competitive. But wait a second, says a teller: We don’t want to be first. Why not wait for some of our competitors to experiment with mobile banking, and we can monitor closely what’s working and what’s not.

如果组织中的每个人都对战略有相同的理解，那么大家就可以进行建设性的异议讨论。举个例子，如果你在玩飞镖，你的朋友总是射得太高，你可以给出有用的反馈。但正是因为靶心的位置显而易见，你的评论才有可能成立。如果你和你的朋友对靶心的位置看法不一致呢？在这种情况下，你们的沟通将变得毫无成效，而且会让双方都感到恼火——如果你们讨论的是“商业”而不是飞镖，那么权力更大的人就会赢得讨论。共同的战略语言能够让每个人都参与进来。

If everyone in your organization has the same understanding of its strategy, people can disagree constructively. As an analogy, if you’re playing darts and your friend consistently aims too high, you can give useful feedback. But it’s the obvious location of the bull’s-eye that makes your comment possible. What if you and your friend don’t agree on where it is? In that case, your communication will be unproductive and irritating for both of you—and if you were playing “business” rather than darts, the person with more power would win the discussion. A common strategic language allows everyone to contribute.

让策略奏效：三个原则

Making strategies stick: Three principles

阻碍战略讨论的三大障碍——知识诅咒、决策瘫痪和缺乏共同的战略词汇——出现的原因各不相同，但它们可以用类似的方法克服。Cranium 的 CHIFF 模型克服了所有这三点。它以一种能够克服“知识诅咒”的方式，传达了高层管理者对品牌的看法。它引导人们在相互竞争的选择中做出决定，从而克服了决策瘫痪。它还建立了一套词汇体系，使组织中的每个人都能在同一语境下进行沟通；它甚至帮助一家中国供应商与公司创始人进行了有说服力的辩论。

The three barriers to talking strategy—the Curse of Knowledge, decision paralysis, and the lack of a common strategic vocabulary—emerge for different reasons, but they can be overcome in similar ways. Cranium’s CHIFF overcomes all three. It communicates how top managers see the brand in a way that overcomes the Curse of Knowledge. It guides people in selecting among competing choices, which overcomes decision paralysis. And it establishes vocabulary that allows everyone in the organization to communicate on the same turf; it even helps a Chinese supplier to argue credibly with the company’s founder.

谈论战略的关键在于让战略理念深入人心。以下是一些让你的战略理念深入人心的技巧：

The trick to talking strategy is making strategic ideas sticky. Here are a few tips for making your strategy stick with people:

  1. 要具体。具体语言——即具体且富有感官体验的语言——的妙处在于，每个人都能以类似​​的方式理解你的信息。比如，Trader Joe's 的“失业大学教授”就容易引起共鸣；而“注重品质但预算有限的顾客”则不然。

  2. Be concrete. The beauty of concrete language—language that is specific and sensory—is that everyone understands your message in a similar way. Trader Joe’s “unemployed college professor” provides a common understanding; “upscale but budget-conscious customer” does not.

  3. 说些出人意料的话。如果一项策略是常识性的，那就别浪费时间去宣传了。（如果它是常识性的，何必呢？）然而，对于领导者来说，识别策略中的非同寻常之处至关重要。这项策略的新颖之处在哪里？有何不同？爱迪生的“闲散人员”概念就是非同寻常的——在那个年代，辛勤的劳动意味着从早到晚的农活，而爱迪生却鼓励人们在工作中放松一下。

  4. Say something unexpected. If a strategy is common sense, don’t waste your time communicating it. (If it’s common sense, why bother?) It’s critical, though, for leaders to identify the uncommon sense in their strategies. What’s new about the strategy? What’s different? Edison’s muckers concept was uncommon sense—in an era when hard work involved farming from dawn until dusk, Edison was telling people to goof around at work.

  5. 讲故事。一个好故事胜过抽象的战略宣言。记住，你可以从故事中提炼出寓意，但你无法从寓意中重构故事。想想联邦快递“紫色承诺”奖背后的故事，或是好市多关于三文鱼的故事。如果你的公司没有能够传达战略的故事，这应该引起你的警惕——你的战略可能不够清晰，无法影响人们的行为。（否则，你就有很多故事可以讲了。）

  6. Tell stories. A good story is better than an abstract strategy statement. Remember, you can reconstruct the moral from the story, but you can’t reconstruct the story from the moral. Think of the power packed into the FedEx Purple Promise award stories, or Costco’s salmon stories. If your company doesn’t have stories that convey your strategy, that should be a warning flag about your strategy—it may not be sufficiently clear to influence how people act. (Otherwise, you’d have some stories to tell.)

避免惰性策略

Avoiding inert strategies

传统观点认为，领导者应该花大量时间阐述和讨论战略。战略沟通中最常见的说法就是重复、重复、再重复。一遍又一遍地重复战略，直到最终深入人心。问题在于：重复并不能避免“知识诅咒”，也不能促进双向沟通。事实上，一些生动形象的战略表达方式，比如用鲑鱼的故事来讲解，并不需要反复强调；无数心理学研究表明，具体的语言和故事更容易被记住。

The conventional wisdom is that leaders should spend a lot of their time presenting and discussing strategy. The most common refrain in strategic communication is repetition, repetition, repetition. Keep repeating the strategy, again and again, until it finally sinks in. Here’s the problem: Repetition doesn’t prevent the Curse of Knowledge or encourage two-way communication. Indeed, sticky ways of talking strategy, such as salmon stories, don’t need much repetition; innumerable psychology studies tell us that it’s much easier to remember concrete language and stories.

关于如何制定好的战略，已有一套成熟的理论体系，本文并未对此做出任何贡献。相反，我们建议领导者将战略视为一个两步流程：第一步是确定正确的战略；第二步是以一种能够让战略融入组织语言的方式进行沟通。这两步都必不可少。

There’s a well-established canon of knowledge about what makes a good strategy, and in this article we haven’t contributed anything to it. Rather, we are proposing that leaders treat strategy as a two-step process: Step 1 is determining the right strategy. Step 2 is communicating it in a way that allows it to become part of the organizational vocabulary. Both are necessary.

遗憾的是，许多组织止步于第一步。或者，他们执行了第一步，随后发表了150场高管演讲，宣扬一种员工根本无法记住和运用的愿景。如果战略要真正鲜活起来——如果要体现在员工和外部合作伙伴的行动中——就必须融入到日常对话和决策中。

Unfortunately, many organizations stop at Step 1. Or they implement Step 1 and follow up with 150 executive speeches broadcasting a vision that is impossible for employees to remember and use. If strategies are to be living and active—if they are to become embodied in the actions of employees and outside partners—they must be woven into day-to-day conversations and decisions.

融入组织沟通方式的战略不可能是僵化的。如果你的一线员工能够谈论你的战略，能够讲述与战略相关的案例，能够与他们的经理进行有效沟通并感到自己的观点可信，那么该战略就真正发挥了其预期的作用：引导员工行为。

A strategy that is built into the way an organization talks cannot be inert. If your frontline employees can talk about your strategy, can tell stories about it, can talk back to their managers and feel credible doing so, then the strategy is doing precisely what it was intended to do: guide behavior.

二、有效教学

II. TEACHING THAT STICKS

作为一名教师，你站在学生注意力难以集中的第一线。每天，你都努力让学生记住你讲授的内容。说实话，你的任务并不轻松。很少有学生会兴高采烈地冲进教室，满怀期待地学习标点符号、多项式或清教徒的故事。你该如何吸引他们呢？

As a teacher, you’re on the front lines of stickiness. Every single day, you go to work and try to make ideas stick. Let’s face it, your mission is not easy. Few students burst into the classroom, giddy with anticipation for the latest lesson on punctuation, polynomials, or Pilgrims. How can you reach them?

在本节中，我们将详细介绍如何将“粘性”的六个特征应用到您的教学中。（温馨提示：本文假设您已经阅读或至少浏览过本书的其余部分。我们不会重新介绍基本概念。顺便一提，如果您想将免费的粘性教学资源转发给尚未阅读本书的同事，只需访问www.madetostick.com/teachers即可。）

In this section, we’ll give you more detail about how to apply the six traits of stickiness to your teaching. (A quick note: This article assumes that you’ve read, or at least skimmed, the rest of the book. We won’t reintroduce the basic concepts. By the way, if you want to forward free sticky resources to a colleague who hasn’t read the book, simply visit www.madetostick.com/teachers.)

有很多非常实用的方法可以让你的教学更引人入胜。例如，每节地球科学课都会讲到地球磁场。但一位老师决定增添一些神秘感。她问学生们：“你们知道吗？如果25000年前你们拿着指南针，并且按照指南针的方向向北走，那么你们最终会径直走向南极。” 这就是利用知识鸿沟让概念更出人意料的例子。（更多关于知识鸿沟的内容，请参阅《好奇心的“鸿沟理论》》。）

There are very practical ways of making your teaching stickier. For instance, every Earth Science class talks about the earth’s magnetic field. But one teacher decided to add a bit of mystery. She asked the students, “Did you know that if you’d been holding a compass 25,000 years ago, and you were walking north according to the compass, you’d be headed straight for the South Pole?” That’s an example of making an idea more unexpected using a knowledge gap. (See The “Gap Theory” of Curiosity for more on knowledge gaps.)

我们将逐一探讨“粘性”的六个特征，并展示如何通过一些专注的努力，让几乎任何想法都更具粘性。而一个具有粘性的想法更有可能改变学生的思维和行为方式。

We’ll take the six traits of stickiness, one by one, and show how a little focused effort can make almost any idea stickier. And a sticky idea is one that’s more likely to change how your students think and act.

简单的

Simple

安德鲁·卡尔·辛格在伊利诺伊大学厄巴纳-香槟分校教授数字信号处理课程。这是一门复杂的学科，很容易迷失在数学的海洋中。因此，他努力寻找课程的核心。他首先问自己一个简单的问题：

Andrew Carl Singer taught a class on digital signal processing at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign. It’s a complex subject, and it’s easy to get lost in the mathematics. So he worked hard to find the core of his class. He began by asking himself a simple question:

当一名伊利诺伊大学的学生去公司面试时说：“我曾跟随辛格教授学习数字信号处理”，为了获得这份工作，并让伊利诺伊大学为有一位在该领域工作的毕业生而感到自豪，他需要知道哪三件事？

When a student from the University of Illinois interviews at a company and says, “I took digital signal processing from Professor Singer,” what are the three things he needs to know in order to get the job and make the University of Illinois proud to have its graduate working in this field?

这是教师最艰巨的职责之一。你要教给学生的知识远超他们所能记住的。那么，哪些概念最重要？你又该如何利用课堂时间确保学生牢记这些要点？辛格教授说，通过聚焦课程的核心概念，“我剔除了无关紧要的细节——那些能区分A +++学生和A ++学生的细节，对其他学生来说却无关紧要。”他确定了几个他希望每个学生在学期中都能掌握的核心概念。然后，他画了一幅图来提醒学生这些核心信息。图中描绘了声音（例如音乐录音）被采样并变成数字文件的过程。然后，这个数字文件经过处理，并通过数模转换器播放出来。他说：“在学期初向全班展示这张图片，并经常提及，我发现这样可以帮助学生牢记我想让他们学习的核心信息。我自己也运用了这个核心信息——来决定课程中保留哪些内容，删除哪些内容。”

This is one of the hardest responsibilities of being a teacher. You’ve got more things to teach your students than they could ever remember. So which concepts are most important? And how can you use your class time to make sure those points stick?
