Professor Singer said that by focusing on the core ideas of the course, “I whitted away the extraneous details—ones that served to separate the A+++ students from the A++ students but weren’t so relevant for the rest of the class.” He identified several core concepts that he wanted every student to learn during the semester. Then he drew a picture that served as a reminder of these core messages. It depicted a process where sounds, such as musical recordings, are sampled and become a digital file. Then the digital file is manipulated and played out through a digital-to-analog converter. He said, “By showing this picture to the class at the beginning of the term and referring to it often, I found that I could keep the class on track with the core messages I wanted everyone to learn. And I also used the core message myself—in deciding which material to keep in the course and which to leave out.”

一旦你找到了课程的核心内容，就需要尽可能用最简单的方式进行解释。为了使解释更易于理解，你应该将其与学生已有的概念联系起来。通过这种联系，你可以利用他们已有的知识作为学习新内容的基础。例如，在19世纪末汽车问世时，它常被称为“无马马车”。汽车制造商正是利用了“马车”这一当时已是常识的概念。再举一个例子，在“简单”这一章中，我们讨论了经典的玻尔原子模型。教师通常会这样解释它：

Once you’ve found the core of your lesson, you’ll need to explain it as simply as you can. To make explanations simpler, you should anchor them in concepts that students already know. By anchoring, you use the knowledge they already have as a platform for new learning. In the late nineteenth century, when the automobile was introduced, it was often called a “horseless carriage.” Automobile makers were anchoring in the concept of a “carriage,” which was common knowledge. As another example, in the “Simple” chapter, we discussed the classic Bohr model of an atom. Teachers often explain it like this:

“电子绕原子核运行的方式就像行星绕太阳运行一样。”他们将玻尔模型与学生已有的知识联系起来。使用类比是巩固新概念的一种简单方法。南非一位教授Java编程课的高中教师比约恩·霍尔特（Bjorn Holdt）在解释“变量”的概念时遇到了困难。于是他想出了一个类比：“变量就像杯子。它们是用来盛放信息的容器。”每个学生都拿到了不同类型的杯子。玻璃杯只能存放数字。啤酒杯只能存放文本。咖啡杯只能存放“真”和“假”。内容物绝对不能混放——例如，你不能把数字放进咖啡杯里。（这种限制说明了一种叫做“类型安全编程”的程序。）霍尔特表示，这个类比帮助学生更快地理解了变量的概念，并且记忆更牢固。他说，他经常能通过用咖啡杯或玻璃杯来解释事情，从而消除误会。

“Electrons orbit the nucleus the way that planets orbit the sun.” They’re anchoring the Bohr model in knowledge the students already have. Using an analogy is an easy way to anchor a new concept. Bjorn Holdt, a high school teacher in South Africa who teaches a Java programming class, was having a hard time communicating the concept of “variables.” So he came up with an analogy: “Variables are just like cups. They are containers that hold some information.” Each student was given a different type of cup. Glass mugs were able to store only numbers. Beer mugs were allowed to store only text. Coffee mugs could store only “true” and “false.” Contents were never allowed to be mixed—for instance, you couldn’t put a number in a coffee mug. (This limitation illustrated a procedure called “type-safe programming.”) Holdt reported that this analogy helped students understand the concept of a variable more quickly and retain it longer. He said that he was frequently able to untangle misunderstandings by explaining things in terms of the coffee cup or the glass mug.

因此，要把一个想法简单化，首先要找到课程的核心，然后将其与学生已有的知识联系起来。

To make an idea simple, then, first find the core of your lesson, then anchor it in knowledge that your students already have.

温馨提示： 书中一些对教师有用的“简单”概念：生成性类比、通过图式理解复杂性、柚子图式、倒金字塔、埋没关键信息。

REMINDERS: “Simple” concepts from the book that are useful for teachers: Generative analogies. Complexity through schemas The Pomelo Schema. The inverted pyramid Burying the Lead.

意外

Unexpected

威廉·B·叶芝曾说过：“教育不是灌满一桶水，而是点燃一把火。” 这番话很有道理，但如何才能点燃学生学习的热情，让他们对哺乳动物生理学之类的知识感兴趣呢？你或许可以从我们最近在书店看到的一本书中得到一些启发，这本书的书名是：《男人为什么有乳头？》。我们猜想，十秒钟前你肯定没在思考哺乳动物的生理学。但当你看到这个问题，并且意识到自己没有现成的答案时，它就激发了你的好奇心。好奇心点燃了你的求知欲，而这正是求知之火的开端。

William B. Yeats once said, “Education is not filling a bucket, but lighting a fire.” That’s a great sentiment, but how do you light the fire of your students to learn about, say, mammalian physiology? Well, you might take a hint from a book we recently spotted in the bookstore that had this title: Why Do Men Have Nipples? We suspect that ten seconds ago you weren’t pondering mammalian physiology. But when you see this question and realize that you don’t have a ready explanation, it makes you wonder. It sparks curiosity, and that’s the beginning of a fire.

在“意想不到”这一章中，我们讨论了乔治·洛温斯坦的好奇心差距理论。该理论认为，好奇心源于我们已知和未知之间的差距。教师可以有效地运用这一技巧。例如，科罗拉多州的一位物理老师问他的学生：“你们有没有注意到，冬天汽车轮胎看起来有点瘪？空气都去哪儿了？” 《魔鬼经济学》这本书也巧妙地运用了好奇心差距理论，例如：“为什么这么多毒贩都和妈妈住在一起？”

In the “Unexpected” chapter, we discuss George Loewenstein’s gap theory of curiosity, which says that curiosity comes from a gap between what we know and what we want to know. Teachers can make powerful use of this technique. For instance, a physics teacher in Colorado asked his students, “Have you ever noticed that in the winter your car tires look a little flat? So where did the air go?” The book Freakonomics also makes great use of curiosity gaps: “Why do so many drug dealers live with their moms?”

好奇心可以激发一系列学习。圣地亚哥动物园开设了一个暑期项目，让初中生学习DNA分析。该项目的设计者玛吉·雷恩博尔德（Maggie Reinbold）用一个堪比《犯罪现场调查》（CSI）的谜团引入了这门课程：一只动物偷偷溜进宠物动物园的食物箱，吃掉了其他动物的食物。山羊们因为缺粮而体重下降。（你肯定不希望你的山羊患上厌食症。）学生们必须展开调查，找出究竟是哪只动物在偷东西。

Curiosity can provide the fuel for a series of lessons. The San Diego Zoo teaches a summer program in which junior high school students learn to do DNA analysis. Maggie Reinbold, the designer of the program, introduced the topic with a mystery worthy of a CSI episode: An animal has been sneaking into the food bin at the petting zoo and eating the animals’ food stores. The goats, deprived of their vittles, are losing weight. (And you do not want your goats getting anorexic.) The students must investigate and figure out which animal is doing the thieving.

两天前，偷吃食物的罪魁祸首在喂食器上留下了几根黑色​​的毛发。不幸的是，这只能稍微缩小嫌疑人的范围。疑似沾有黑色毛发的动物包括一只山羊、一头猪、一只绵羊和一匹马。只有DNA分析才能揭开窃贼的真面目。在接下来的一周里，莱因博尔德利用这个谜团，为她的学生们上了一堂完整的分子生物学迷你课程。学生们使用解剖显微镜提取了一些细胞进行DNA分析。他们学习了诺贝尔奖获奖技术——聚合酶链式反应（PCR），这项技术可以将少量DNA扩增至数十亿份，然后他们进行了……他们穿上白大褂，咨询动物园的研究人员，了解如何在动物园的仪器上进行PCR分析。他们利用凝胶电泳技术，将窃贼的DNA图谱与猪、山羊、绵羊和马的DNA图谱进行比对。经过一番调查，他们发现罪魁祸首竟然是……小马艾德。（不过别指望它会痛哭流涕地认罪。）

Two nights earlier, the food-thieving culprit left a few threads of black hair on the feeding station. Unfortunately, this narrows down the suspects only a little. The lineup of black-haired animals includes a goat, a pig, a sheep, and a horse. Only DNA analysis can reveal the truth about the thief. Over the course of the week, Reinbold used this mystery to teach her students a whole mini-course in molecular biology. Students used dissecting microscopes to extract some cells for DNA analysis. They learned about the Nobel Prize—winning Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) procedure that can be used to turn a few copies of DNA into billions of copies, and then they put on their white lab coats and consulted with zoo researchers about how to conduct a PCR analysis on the zoo’s machine. They used gel electrophoresis to compare the DNA pattern of the thief with DNA patterns of pigs, goats, sheep, and horses. After enough legwork, they discover that the villain was…Ed the Pony. (But don’t expect a tearful confession.)

这就是好奇心的价值所在：它能让孩子们在一周的时间里全神贯注地探索严肃的科学问题。要想在课堂上发挥好奇心的作用，就要利用知识空白和神秘感。

That’s the value of curiosity in a nutshell: It can hold kids’ attention for a week as they tackle serious science. To make it work in your lessons, use knowledge gaps and the power of mystery.

温馨提示： 书中一些对教师有用的“意想不到”的概念：好奇心差距；好奇心的“差距理论”；西奥迪尼教授的土星环之谜；诺拉·艾芙隆的新闻老师；新闻学入门。

REMINDERS: “Unexpected” concepts from the book that are useful for teachers: Curiosity gaps The “Gap Theory” of Curiosity. Professor Cialdini’s mystery of Saturn’s rings The Mystery of the Rings. Nora Ephron’s journalism teacher Journalism 101.

具体的

Concrete

在数学中，学生们常常难以理解“函数”的概念。函数究竟是什么？它那奇怪的“f(x)”符号又是什么意思？这种符号看起来和学生们以前见过的任何东西都不一样。

In math, students often struggle with the notion of a “function.” What exactly is a function, and what is meant by its strange “f(x)” notation, which looks like nothing else that students have seen before?

它看起来如此抽象，如此神秘。因此，弗吉尼亚州劳登科学学院的数学老师戴安娜·维戈让学生们体验函数在现实世界中的作用。她把一群鸣叫的蟋蟀带进教室，提出一个问题：随着温度变化，蟋蟀的鸣叫声会发生什么变化？会变快还是变慢？蟋蟀的反应是否具有可预测性，以至于我们可以绘制一个函数图像来预测它们的鸣叫速度？我们的函数就像一台小型机器：你输入一个温度（比如 85 度），然后就会显示鸣叫的频率（比如每分钟 60 次鸣叫）。

It seems so abstract, so mysterious. So Diana Virgo, a math teacher at the Loudoun Academy of Science in Virginia, gives students a more real-world experience with functions. She brings a bunch of chirping crickets into the classroom and poses a question: What will happen to the crickets’ chirping as the temperature changes? Will it get faster or slower? And might the crickets’ reaction be so predictable that we can actually graph a function that predicts how fast they’ll chirp? Our function would be like a little machine: You feed in a temperature (say, 85 degrees) and out pops the rate of chirping (say, sixty chirps per minute).

于是，全班同学开始做实验：蟋蟀鸣叫。学生们数蟋蟀的鸣叫声。处女座老师改变了温度。蟋蟀们显然对这种变化感到困惑，鸣叫声也随之改变。学生们再次计数。很快，全班同学就收集到了一大堆数据，这些数据可以输入到一个软件中，生成预测函数。结果发现，温度越高，蟋蟀鸣叫得越快——而且这是可以预测的！突然间，函数的概念变得清晰易懂——它与现实紧密相连。学生们亲身经历了整个过程——函数的由来、构建方式以及应用方法。（顺便提一下，处女座老师也提醒学生们，人的判断始终不可或缺。例如，如果你在函数中输入温度“1000度”，它会预测蟋蟀的鸣叫频率会非常非常快！但遗憾的是，在1000度时，蟋蟀根本不会鸣叫。）

So the class runs the experiment: The crickets chirp. The students count the chirps. Virgo changes the temperature. The crickets, undoubtedly puzzled by the weather, chirp differently. The students count again. And soon the class has gathered a bunch of data that can be plugged into a software package, which generates the predictive function. It turns out that the hotter it is, the faster the crickets chirp—and it’s predictable! Suddenly, the idea of a function makes sense—it’s been grounded in reality. Students have personally experienced the entire context—where functions come from, how they’re constructed, and how they can be used. (As a side note, Virgo also warns her students that human judgment is always indispensable. For instance, if you plug into the function the temperature “1,000 degrees,” it’ll predict a really, really fast rate of chirping! Sadly, though, at 1,000 degrees crickets don’t chirp at all.)

蟋蟀的形象生动地展现了如何将概念具象化——避免抽象的概念性语言，而是将想法建立在感官现实之上。这就像阅读葡萄酒的描述（“口感浓郁而平衡”）和品尝葡萄酒之间的区别。我们赋予一个想法的感官“钩子”越多，它就越容易被记住。

The cricket function is an example of making a concept concrete—avoiding abstraction and conceptual language and grounding an idea in sensory reality. It’s the difference between reading about a wine (“bold but balanced”) and tasting it. The more sensory “hooks” we can put into an idea, the better it will stick.

一位名叫萨布丽娜·理查森的八年级老师用通心粉帮助学生“看懂”标点符号。理查森描述了她的练习：

An eighth-grade teacher named Sabrina Richardson helped students “see” punctuation by using macaroni. Richardson described her exercise:

学生们拿到一些卡片，卡片上写着缺少标点符号（例如引号、句号、感叹号、逗号和撇号）的句子。学生们被分成两人一组或三人一组，每组都拿到装有弯管通心粉、小贝壳通心粉和斜管通心粉的小袋子。学生们需要将这些通心粉放到句子中的正确位置。例如，他们拿到的句子是：

The students were given cards with sentences that were missing punctuation like quotation marks, periods, exclamation points, commas, apostrophes. The students were divided into groups of two and three and were given baggies that contained elbow macaroni, small macaroni shells, and ritoni. The students were asked to place the pieces of macaroni in the correct place in the sentence. For example, they were given the sentence:

杰基喊道：“格温，回来！”

Jackie shouted Gwen come back here

学生们需要用弯管通心粉代替逗号和引号，用小号通心粉壳代替句号。他们还可以将弯管通心粉和小号通心粉壳组合起来表示感叹号。我知道很多学生都搞不清楚逗号应该放在引号里面还是外面，所以这个练习让他们有机会“看到”正确的引号标点方法。完成后，他们就知道句子应该是这样的：杰基喊道：“格温，回来！”

The students had to use the elbow macaroni as commas and quotation marks and a small macaroni shell as a period. They could combine the ritoni and the small macaroni shell for an exclamation point. I knew that a lot of my students were confused about whether the comma went inside or outside the quotation marks, so this gave all of them a chance to “see” the correct way to punctuate quotations. Once they were finished, they knew the sentence would read: Jackie shouted, “Gwen, come back here!”

具体、感官体验能将想法铭刻在我们的大脑中——想想看，记住一首歌比记住一张信用卡号码容易得多，尽管一首歌包含的数据要多得多！

Concrete, sensory experiences etch ideas into our brain—think of how much easier it is to remember a song than a credit card number, even though a song contains much more data!

温馨提示：书中对教师有用的“具体”概念：亚洲的数学教学（理解减法）。记忆的魔术贴理论。简·艾略特的小学偏见模拟（棕色眼睛，蓝色眼睛）。

REMINDERS: “Concrete” concepts from the book that are useful for teachers: Math instruction in Asia (Understanding Subtraction). The Velcro theory of memory. Jane Elliott’s elementary-school simulation of prejudice (Brown Eyes, Blue Eyes).

可信的

Credible

艾米·海耶特是波士顿布鲁克莱恩高中的一位美国文学老师，她教授一门关于超验主义的课程。她说，当学生们读梭罗的作品，了解到他独自在荒野中度过那么多时间时，他们通常会有这样的反应：呃，他为什么要这样做？所以，为了培养学生的同理心，她给他们布置了一项非同寻常的作业：独自在大自然中待上三十分钟。不带手机。不带iPod。不带宠物。不带游戏机。只有你和大自然。

Amy Hyett, an American literature teacher at Brookline High School in Boston, teaches a unit on transcendentalism. She says that when students read Thoreau, and learn how much time he spent alone in the wilderness, they have a common reaction: Er, why would he do that? So, in the spirit of building empathy, she gives them an unorthodox assignment: Spend thirty minutes alone in nature. No cell phone. No iPod. No pet companions. No Game Boy. Just you and the great outdoors.

海耶特说：“这非常神奇，因为几乎每个学生都有了启发性的体验。他们惊讶于这种体验对他们的影响之深。即使是最怀疑的学生，最终也会对超验主义和自然有更深刻的理解。”

Hyett says, “It’s quite amazing, because almost every student has an illuminating experience. They are surprised by how much the experience moves them. Even the most skeptical students come away with a deeper understanding of transcendentalism and nature.”

一个想法要想深入人心，首先必须具有可信度。YouTube时代的学生们很难相信独自在户外闲逛能激发伟大的思考。那么，如何消除他们的怀疑呢？让他们亲眼所见。有时候，你必须亲眼所见或亲身经历才能相信。例如，你可能不相信往两升装的汽水瓶里加曼妥思糖会引发火山爆发，把汽水喷溅到十到十五英尺远的地方。但如果你亲眼所见，就会相信了。（在此之前，不妨上网搜一下，图个乐子。）许多科学实验室实验都遵循这个原则：眼见为实。（还要注意的是，实验室在教学上还有其他用途：它们常常出人意料——“看，这些化学物质混合后会变成亮蓝色！”而且它们总是具体的——你不是在谈论某种现象，而是在观察或制造它。）

For an idea to stick, it needs to be credible. YouTube-era students don’t find it credible that hanging out outside, alone, could be conducive to great thinking. So how do you combat their skepticism? You let them see for themselves. Sometimes you have to see something, or experience it, to believe it. For instance, you might not believe that adding Mentos candy to a two-liter bottle of soda would cause a volcanic eruption that sends soda spewing ten to fifteen feet from the bottle. But you’d believe it if you saw it. (In the meantime, just Google it for a laugh.) Lots of science-lab experiments operate on this principle: See for yourself. (Notice, too, that labs are pedagogically useful for other reasons: They are often unexpected—“Look, the chemicals turn bright blue when mixed!” And they are always concrete—instead of talking about a phenomenon, you’re seeing it or producing it.)

另一种使观点更具说服力的技巧是运用统计数据——但或许并非你想象的那样。统计数据很难让人记住。数字往往很容易被左耳进右耳出。但统计数据所揭示的关系却能让人印象深刻。托尼·普拉特是新奥尔良复兴学区的一名四年级教师，他正在教学生们概率论的基础知识。他举例说，他们中彩票的概率非常非常小，只有百万分之一。但这个统计数据如此极端，以至于我们的大脑难以理解。我们的大脑很难区分“百万分之一”和“万分之一”，尽管两者之间存在着巨大的差距！于是，普拉特将概率与一种关系联系起来。他说：“你被闪电击中的概率都比中彩票的概率高。”这让学生们惊叹不已——它让他们直观地感受到中彩票的概率有多么低。事实上，有好几个孩子都迫不及待地跑回家告诉家人。

Another technique for making ideas credible is to use statistics—but perhaps not in the way you’d expect. It’s difficult to make a statistic stick. Numbers tend to slide easily in one ear and out the other. But the relationships illustrated by statistics can be quite sticky. Tony Pratt, a fourth-grade teacher in the New Orleans Recovery School District, was teaching his students the basics of probability, and as an example he told them that they had a really, really small probability of winning the lottery. The odds are one in millions. But this statistic is so extreme that it fuzzes our brains. Our brains can’t easily distinguish between “one in millions” and “one in tens of thousands,” even though there’s an enormous gap there! So Pratt grounded the probability in a relationship. He said, “You’re more likely to be struck by lightning than to win the lottery.” That amazed the students—it gave them an intuition for just how rare it is to win. In fact, several of them rushed home to tell their families.

一名学生贾里德讲述了他的故事：“我昨晚看到我叔叔在买彩票。我告诉他，他被闪电击中的概率都比中彩票的概率高，而且从概率上讲，买彩票是个坏主意。”

One student, Jarred, relayed his story: “I saw my uncle buying lottery tickets last night. I told him that he was more likely to be struck by lightning than he was to win the lottery and that buying lottery tickets was a bad idea because of probability.”

他说了什么？

“What did he say?”

“他叫我滚出他的视线。”

“He told me to get the f____out of his face.”

有些人比其他人更难接受根深蒂固的想法。

Some people are more resistant to sticky ideas than others.

温馨提示：书中一些对教师有用的“可信”概念：NBA与艾滋病教育（新秀入职培训）。吞噬细菌的科学家。运用以人为本的原则。

REMINDERS: “Credible” concepts from the book that are useful for teachers: The NBA and AIDS education (Rookie Orientation). The bacteria-chugging scientist. Using the human-scale principle.

情感

Emotion

巴特·米勒是俄勒冈州波特兰市林肯高中的一位美国历史老师，他很难让学生们对南北战争产生兴趣。“我们讨论了武器、战术、战略等等。学生们很尊重这些内容，但仅此而已。”他说。

Bart Millar, an American history teacher at Lincoln High School in Portland, Oregon, was having a hard time getting his students to care about the Civil War. “We talked about the weaponry, the tactics, the strategy, and so on. The students were respectful, but not much beyond that,” he said.

为了做得更好，他访问了国家档案馆网站，下载了战地外科医生及其手术帐篷的照片。他把这些照片展示给学生们，让他们想象战争的声音：爆炸声、制服的窸窣声、偶尔出现的诡异寂静。还有战争的气味：尘土、火药、鲜血、粪便。这项活动将感官信息融入到“枯燥”的课程中，生动形象，令人叹为观止。但米勒还为学生们准备了另一个惊喜。

Determined to do better, he went to the National Archives website and downloaded photos of battlefield surgeons and their surgical tents. He presented these to his students and asked them to imagine the sounds of war: the explosions, the rustle of uniforms, the occasional eerie quiet. And the smells of war: dust, gunpowder, blood, excrement. This activity, which brought sensory information into a “dry” subject, was beautifully concrete. But Millar had one more surprise in store for the students.

房间角落里有一张桌子，上面盖着防水布。米勒他们迅速掀开遮布，露出两块秒表、两根粗壮的骨头和两把手锯。这些骨头是从当地屠夫那里弄来的牛腿，重量和粗细都与人类股骨相仿。两名学生志愿者被要求扮演战地外科医生，被迫为一名士兵截肢，以期挽救他的生命。他们的任务是：用力快速地锯断骨头——毕竟，当时麻醉剂非常匮乏。

In a corner of the room was a table covered with a tarp. Millar whisked away the tarp to reveal two stopwatches, two thick-looking bones, and two handsaws. The bones were cow legs procured from a local butcher that approximated the weight and thickness of a human femur. Two student volunteers were asked to play the role of a battlefield surgeon, forced to amputate a soldier’s leg in the hope of saving his life. Their mission: Saw through bone forcefully and quickly—after all, at the time there was very little anesthesia.

米勒说：“整堂课只用了大约十五分钟，但十年后，偶尔过来打招呼的学生还会谈起那堂课。” 原因不难理解：他找到了一种方法，让学生们关心战争，让他们得以窥见战争的残酷现实。

Millar says, “The entire lesson only took about fifteen minutes, but ten years later students who stop in to say hi still talk about that lesson.” And it’s not hard to see why: He found a way to make his students care, to give them a peek into the brutal realities of war.

这就是情感对一个想法的作用——它能让人们关心，让人产生某种感觉。在一些科学系，教授“实验室安全”课程时，老师会做一件令人震惊的事：他们会用学生们即将接触的酸溶解一颗牛眼球。很多学生看到这一幕都会不寒而栗，他们会有所感触。（值得注意的是，有些学生，尤其是男生，觉得这“很酷”。）实验室安全的“注意事项”或许不会让你感到不适，但一颗溶解的眼球却能让你触动心弦。

That’s what emotion does for an idea—it makes people care. It makes people feel something. In some science departments, during the lesson on “lab safety,” instructors will do something shocking: They’ll take some of the acid that the students will be handling and use it to dissolve a cow eyeball. A lot of students shudder when they see the demonstration. They feel something. (It should also be noted that some students, mostly male, think it’s “cool.”) Lab safety “dos and don’ts” don’t grab you in the gut, but a dissolving eyeball sure does.

这就是情感在使想法深入人心方面的作用：将想法从分析性的、抽象的或理论性的事物，转变为触动学生内心（或心灵）的事物。

And that’s the role of emotion in making ideas sticky: to transform the idea from something that’s analytical or abstract or theoretical and make it hit the students in the gut (or the heart).

温馨提示： 书中一些对教师有用的“情感”概念：体育精神的淡化（《体育精神案例》）；为什么要学习代数？（ 《代数的必要性与马斯洛的需求层次理论》）；违背自身利益投票的选民（《爆米花机与政治学》）。

REMINDERS: “Emotional” concepts from the book that are useful for teachers: The dilution of “sportsmanship” (The Case of “Sportsmanship”). Why study algebra? (The Need for Algebra and Maslow’s Basement). Voters who vote against their self-interest (The Popcorn Popper and Political Science).

故事

Story

你有没有注意到，在教学过程中，只要你开始和学生分享个人故事，他们就会立刻集中注意力？你明白故事的价值。但有些老师很少在课堂上讲故事，因为他们担心自己没有引人入胜的故事可讲，或者觉得自己讲故事的能力不够强。那么，或许值得探究一下哪些类型的故事能有效地帮助学生记住知识点。答案是：几乎任何类型的故事都有效。

Have you ever noticed, when you teach, that the moment you start sharing a personal story with your students, they instantly snap to attention? You understand the value of stories. But some teachers don’t insert many stories into their lessons, because they’re worried that they don’t have gripping stories to tell, or that they aren’t good storytellers. So maybe it’s worth identifying which kinds of stories are effective in making ideas stick. The answer is this: virtually any kind.

故事不必跌宕起伏，不必引人入胜，也不必妙趣横生。故事本身就足以发挥其作用——即使是枯燥乏味的故事也比罗列事实更能深入人心。本书中，我们多次见证了故事的力量，它能牢牢抓住学生的注意力——还记得用来教授会计的“安全外出”创业故事吗？它如此有效，以至于学生们更倾向于选择会计专业。或者回想一下西奥迪尼讲述的关于破解土星环之谜的竞赛故事。就在几页前，我们还讨论了动物园偷吃宠物食物的故事。这些故事都算不上奥斯卡级别的佳作，但它们对学生来说却有着无法抗拒的吸引力。

The stories don’t have to be dramatic, they don’t have to be captivating, and they don’t have to be very entertaining. The story form itself does most of the heavy lifting—even a boring story will be stickier than a set of facts. Several times in the book, we’ve seen the power of a story to keep students engaged—remember the “Safe Night Out” entrepreneurial story, used to teach accounting? It was so effective that it made students more likely to major in accounting. Or recall Cialdini’s story of the race to solve the mystery of Saturn’s rings. Just a few pages back, we discussed the tale of the petting-zoo food thief. None of these stories were Oscar material, but they were irresistible to students.

故事不仅对学习有益，对纪律管理也很有帮助。鹰岩高中的九年级英语老师格雷格·金就用一个故事来管教一个不守规矩的学生，我们姑且称他为约翰。约翰心地善良，但就是控制不住自己在课堂上和同学们闲聊或嬉闹。金老师多次和他谈话，试图解释他的行为扰乱了课堂秩序，也影响了他的成绩。约翰通常都会把这些谈话记在心里，改变自己的行为——但也就维持几天而已。更糟糕的是，在那些难得表现好的日子里，约翰还会说：“你不为我感到骄傲吗？我今天表现得很好。”

Stories can be useful for discipline as well as academics. Greg Kim, a ninth-grade English teacher at Eagle Rock High School, used a story to reach an unruly student, whom we’ll call John. John was a well-meaning student who just couldn’t seem to stop socializing or horsing around in class. Kim talked to him several times and tried to explain that his behavior was disrupting the class and endangering his grade. Often, John would take these talks to heart and change his behavior—for a few days. Making matters worse, on the rare days that he did behave well, John would say, “Aren’t you proud of me? I was good today.”

金说：“我试着跟他谈保持专注的重要性，以及他每天都需要集中精力。但约翰一脸困惑地看着我……”

Kim said, “I tried to talk to him about consistency, and how he needed to be focused every day. But John looked at me, confused….

在他看来，偶尔做好人就是一直做好人。金他苦恼于如​​何让约翰理解行为一致性的重要性。他尝试用“前进一步，后退两步”之类的比喻，但约翰只是茫然地看着他。

In his mind, being good sometimes was being good always.” Kim struggled with the problem of how to get John to understand the need for consistent behavior. He tried analogies like “one step forward two steps back.” But all he got from John was a blank look.

后来，金和另一位老师谈起了这件事，这位老师去年教过约翰英语。这位老师也遇到过类似的问题，事实上，约翰唯一一次表现出色，就是写了一篇关于自己如何减掉三十磅的个人随笔。突然，金意识到自己应该怎么做：

Later, Kim was discussing the situation with another teacher who had taught John in English class the previous year. The teacher had similar problems with John and, indeed, the only time John had shone was when he wrote a personal narrative about how he’d lost thirty pounds. Suddenly, Kim realized what he should do:

第二天，我和约翰谈起他行为上的反复无常，并把他比作我一个减肥失败的朋友。我朋友决定节食和运动来减肥。第一天他表现得很好，吃得健康，也坚持运动。但第二天他就破戒了，既没节食也没运动。第三天他又表现得很好，但接下来的两天他又破戒了。就这样反反复复，没完没了。我告诉约翰，我朋友会在表现好的时候缠着我，让我知道他做得很好，但几周后，他的体重却不知怎么地增加了。我把这个故事告诉约翰，问他知不知道问题出在哪里。他笑着说答案显而易见。约翰咧嘴一笑，说道：“他没有每天都坚持节食。”我盯着他，看着他恍然大悟，笑容也变得意味深长起来。

The next day I spoke to John about his behavioral inconsistency and compared it to a friend of mine who had struggled with weight loss. The friend had decided to go on a diet and exercise regimen to lose weight. The first day, he was good. He ate right and exercised, but the second day he broke his diet and didn’t exercise. The next day he was good, but the following two days he was bad again. And so it went, on and on. I told John that my friend would beg for my approval by letting me know he was good on the days that he was, but weeks later he had somehow gained weight. I told John this story and asked if he knew what the problem was. He laughed and said the answer was obvious. With a big smile, John said, “He didn’t stick to his diet every day.” I stared at him and watched the realization engulf him, and his smile became thoughtful.

这次谈话大约发生在三周前，虽然约翰并非每天都完美无缺，但情况已经逆转，他现在几乎每天都能保持专注。

This conversation was about three weeks ago, and while John isn’t perfect every day, the ratio has reversed and he is consistently focused most every day.

约翰一直“看不到”自己的行为，也不明白为什么需要改变，直到他听到一个故事，才让他以全新的视角看待事物。持续不断的唠叨并没有改变他，一个故事却做到了。故事有一种独特的力量，能够吸引和激励人心。你如何才能利用这种力量，让你的教诲深入人心呢？

John couldn’t “see” his behavior, couldn’t understand why it needed to change, until he was confronted with a story that made him see things in a different way. Continual nagging didn’t change him—a story did. Stories have a unique power to engage and inspire. How can you harness that power to make your lessons stick?

温馨提示： 书中对教师有用的“故事”概念：参见上文本节第二段。此外，还有故事作为飞行模拟器（《非被动的听众》）。三种鼓舞人心的故事（《发现的艺术》）。

REMINDERS: “Story” concepts from the book that are useful for teachers: See the second paragraph of this section above. Also, stories as flight simulators (The Un-passive audience). The three kinds of inspiring stories (The Art of Spotting).

知识的诅咒

The Curse of Knowledge

别忘了书中最大的敌人——知识的诅咒。它指出，一旦你掌握了某项知识，就很难想象自己不知道它是什么感觉。反过来，这又会让你难以清晰地向初学者传授知识。这是一个难以避免的问题——每年，你走进教室时，都带着一年积累的知识积累。你每年都在教授同样的概念，而你的理解也每年都在加深。如果你是一名生物老师，你根本无法想象第一次听到“有丝分裂”这个词时的感受，也无法想象自己不知道人体是由细胞构成的。你无法忘记你已经知道的东西。

Let’s not forget the villain of the book, the Curse of Knowledge, which says that once you know something, it’s hard to imagine not knowing it. And that, in turn, makes it harder for you to communicate clearly to a novice. It’s a tough problem to avoid—every year, you walk into class with another year’s worth of mental refinement under your belt. You’ve taught the same concepts every year, and every year your understanding gets sharper. If you’re a biology teacher, you simply can’t imagine anymore what it’s like to hear the word “mitosis” for the first time, or to lack the knowledge that the body is composed of cells. You can’t unlearn what you already know.

这就是“粘性”工具发挥作用的地方。“粘性”某种程度上就像第二语言。当你开口交流时，无需思考自己说了什么，你就是在说你的母语：专家术语。但学生们不会说专家术语。他们却会说“粘性”。每个人都会说“粘性”。从某种意义上说，它是通用语言。“粘性”的语法——简洁、讲故事、通过感官学习——使任何人都能理解所传达的思想。

That’s where these tools of stickiness can help. Stickiness is a second language of sorts. When you open your mouth to communicate, without thinking about what’s coming out of your mouth you’re speaking your native language: Expertese. But students don’t speak Expertese. They do speak Sticky, though. Everyone speaks Sticky. In a sense, it’s the universal language. The grammar of stickiness—simplicity, storytelling, learning through the senses—enables anyone to understand the ideas being communicated.

什么能坚持下去

What Sticks

让想法更深入人心并不难，只需要花点时间和精力。我们讨论过的六个让想法更深入人心的原则可以作为一份清单——想象一下把清单写在便签纸上，贴在……一边在桌子旁构思课程，一边说：“好的，明天的课我要比较沉积岩和火成岩。怎样才能把这部分讲得简单易懂呢？学生们有没有什么相关的知识可以让我打底？怎样才能让讲解更具体呢？我能不能弄些岩石样本给他们看看？怎样才能讲个故事呢？我能不能找到一个考古学家利用岩层知识解决有趣问题的例子？” 你明白我的意思了吧。

Making ideas stickier isn’t hard. It just takes a bit of time and focus. The six principles of stickiness that we’ve discussed can be used as a checklist—imagine the checklist written on a Post-it note, stuck to the side of your desk as you outline a lesson. “Okay, for tomorrow’s lesson I’ve got to compare sedimentary and igneous rock. How can I make this Simple? Do students have some knowledge I can anchor in? How can I make it Concrete? Can I get a sample of the kinds of rock to show them? How can I tell a Story? Can I find a story of an archaeologist who used knowledge of the rock layers to solve an interesting problem?” You get the idea.

劳登科学学院的三位教师——琳达·古尔登、詹妮弗·林恩和丹·克罗——在修订海洋学单元时正是这样做的。他们对过去的教学方式并不满意，因此投入了大量精力进行改进。以下是新的教案：

A group of teachers at the Loudon Academy of Science—Linda Gulden, Jennifer Lynn, and Dan Crowe—did exactly this in revising their oceanography unit. They weren’t happy with the way things had gone in the past, so they put a lot of energy into revamping it. Here’s the new lesson plan:

第一节课，他们以一个谜题开场：“假设你把一封信装进漂流瓶，开车到海边，然后尽可能远地把它扔进海里。瓶子最终会漂到哪里？”他们让学生们猜测。（“海浪会把它带回岸边。”“它会漂到南极洲。”“它会沉入海底。”）但他们不给出答案（因为这个问题没有标准答案）。

In the first class, they start with a mystery: “Let’s say you put a message in a bottle, drive out to the coast, and throw it as far as you can into the ocean. Where will the bottle end up?” They let students make their guesses. (“The waves will bring it right back to shore.” “It’ll end up in Antarctica.” “It’ll sink.”) But they don’t provide an answer (since there isn’t a clear answer).

然后，他们开始以一种更引人入胜的方式探索这个谜团。学生们读到一篇精彩的文章，讲述了一艘货轮在1992年1月遭遇强风暴，一个集装箱落入太平洋某处。集装箱破裂，里面的28800只橡皮鸭、乌龟、海狸和青蛙等漂浮物散落一地。多年后，我们知道了其中许多动物漂浮物的最终去向。数百只橡皮鸭搁浅在阿拉斯加锡特卡附近，距离事发地点约2200英里——有些在六个月内到达，有些则用了十二年。通过追踪这些橡皮鸭的游动路线，我们对洋流的运作方式有了更深入的了解。

Then they begin to explore this same mystery in a more dramatic form. Students read a fascinating article about a cargo ship that hit a severe storm in January 1992 and lost a container overboard, somewhere in the Pacific Ocean. The container broke apart and released its contents: 28,800 floating duckies, turtles, beavers, and frogs. Years later, we know where many of these animal floaties ended up. Many hundreds of them beached near Sitka, Alaska, about 2,200 miles away—some made it there within six months, some twelve years later. By tracing the paths that these rubber duckies swam, we learn a lot about the way ocean currents work.

在其他课程中，老师会帮助孩子们进行一些动手实验。他们设置不同盐度和温度的水箱，让孩子们观察这些变量如何影响水流。实际上，学生们能够模拟出自己的洋流。

In other classes, the teachers help the kids do some hands-on experimentation. They set up tanks of water with different levels of salinity and different temperatures, and let them see how those variables change the water current. In essence, the students are able to create their own ocean currents.

最后，他们转向海洋在全球气候中扮演的关键角色。他们首先提出一个问题：是什么决定了像纽约这样的城市的天气？学生们不出所料地回答说，这取决于纬度——城市越靠近赤道，气候就越温暖；越靠近两极，气候就越寒冷。这固然有一定道理，但也存在巨大的差异：例如，纽约和马德里的纬度大致相同，但纽约每年冬天都会下雪，而马德里却不会。这其中的区别是什么？这就引出了洋流如何影响气候的话题。

Finally, they pivot to the critical role that oceans play in global climate. They start with a question: What determines the weather of a city, like New York City? Inevitably, students say it depends on latitude—the closer to the equator the city is, the warmer it is, and the closer to the poles it is, the colder it is. There is much truth in that, but there are huge discrepancies: For instance, New York City and Madrid are at roughly the same latitude, but it snows every winter in New York City and it doesn’t snow in Madrid. What’s the difference? That opens the topic of how ocean currents influence climate.

最后，请注意，这些教师制定的教学计划运用了所有吸引人的理念要素。

In closing, notice that these teachers have developed a teaching plan that uses all the elements of sticky ideas.

  • 简单来说：巩固学生对天气的认知（纽约 vs. 马德里）。

  • Simple: Anchoring the students’ knowledge of weather (New York vs. Madrid).

  • 意想不到的问题：瓶子最终会去哪里？小鸭子们最终去了哪里？为什么马德里的天气和纽约的天气不同？

  • Unexpected: Where will the bottle end up? Where did the duckies end up? Why is Madrid’s weather different than New York’s?

  • 具体例子：漂流瓶中的信息、橡皮鸭、可触摸的水箱、提及特定城市。

  • Concrete: The message in a bottle, the rubber duckies, the hands-on tanks of water, the mention of specific cities.

  • 可信：亲自使用这个水箱，看看温度如何影响水流。

  • Credible: See for yourself, using this tank, how temperature affects water current.

  • 情感方面：想想将一条重要的信息抛入大海，然后想知道它会漂向何方，其中蕴含着希望、神秘和焦虑。

  • Emotional: Think of the hope, mystery, and anxiety involved in tossing an important message into the sea and wondering where it will go.

  • 故事：成千上万只落水的橡皮鸭环游世界的故事。

  • Story: The tale of thousands of rubber duckies that fell overboard—and the journey they took around the world.

我们向这些老师们致以崇高的敬意。我们希望我们能够巩固你们已经行之有效的方法，并启发你们尝试一些新的方法。愿你们的想法能够持续下去！

Our hats are off to these teachers. We hope we’ve reinforced what you’re doing that is already sticky, and that we’ve inspired you to try something new. May your ideas stick!

三、打破思维定势

III. UNSTICKING AN IDEA

自从《让创意粘住》出版以来，许多焦虑的人问我们：“如何消除一个粘性的想法？”他们想消除关于公司的谣言，或是消除对特定产品的错误认知。他们想消除关于政治候选人的窃窃私语和不实言论。我们甚至还被问过：“你们会如何消除帕丽斯·希尔顿的影响力？”

Since Made to Stick came out, many anxious people have asked us, “How do I unstick a sticky idea?” They want to unstick a rumor about their company or a false perception of a particular product. They want to unstick whispered mistruths about political candidates. Once, we were even asked, “How would you unstick Paris Hilton?”

对于最后一个问题，我们的回答来得有点慢。最终我们承认：“你做不到。” 想法没有“除垢剂”，一旦形成，就很难消除。数以百万计的人，无论心甘情愿还是被迫，都开始关注一位派对女王的种种行为。没有什么神奇的咒语能让我们把注意力转移到替代能源或其他更有价值的话题上。关于帕丽斯·希尔顿这件事，我们最好的建议是：耐心等待。随着年龄增长，记忆会逐渐消退，或许那些相关的神经元也会彻底消失。（但愿它们能比那些“自己穿衣服”的神经元先消失。）

Our answer on that last one was a bit slow in coming. We finally admitted, “You can’t.” There’s no Goo Gone for ideas. Sticky ideas stick. There are millions of people who’ve come to follow, willingly or unwillingly, the antics of a party-girl heiress. There’s no magic sticky incantation that will make us divert our attention to alternative energy, or some other worthy topic. Our best advice, on the Paris Hilton matter, was: Just wait it out. As we age, the memories will fade, and perhaps those neurons will die off entirely. (With any luck, they’ll go before the “dress ourselves” neurons.)

但“如何才能让一个想法摆脱困境？”这个问题一直困扰着我们。于是，我们深入研究了相关的学术文献。这是一项漫长而令人沮丧的探索，因为针对这个主题的研究并不多。但我们最终找到了一条很有希望的线索，这条线索已经有大约六十五年的历史了。

But the question—How do I unstick an idea?—nagged at us. So we dug into the relevant academic research. It was a long and frustrating search, because there’s not much research tackling this topic. But we did find one promising lead that was about sixty-five years old.

二战期间，社会科学家们对谣言控制抱有浓厚的爱国热情。当时大约三分之二的谣言都是“煽动性谣言”，即煽动民众对特定社会群体（黑人、犹太人、英国人）的愤怒。这些谣言不实且具有社会破坏性，因此政府希望采取强硬措施予以打击。一种似乎对付煽动性谣言有效的策略是转移民众的愤怒，让他们对散布谣言者感到愤怒。例如，谣言控制人员会张贴纳粹间谍向容易受骗的人散布谣言的海报。这促使听众在听到谣言时做出愤怒的反应：你散布纳粹的谎言，是在破坏美国的战争努力！

During World War II, social scientists had a keen patriotic interest in rumor control. About two-thirds of the rumors during were “wedge-drivers,” accusations that provoked anger at various social groups (blacks, Jews, the Brits). These rumors were false and socially destructive, so the government wanted to fight back aggressively. One tactic that seemed to work against wedge-drivers was to redirect the anger and make people mad at the rumormongers. For instance, the rumor-control people would put up posters of Nazi spies spreading rumors to gullible dupes. This primed listeners to react angrily when someone spread a rumor: You’re undermining the American war effort by spreading Nazi untruths!

起初，这种战时宣传工作似乎与我们读者的关注点相当脱节，读者们希望他们的理念能够深入人心。无论是在商界还是在学校，我们都曾遇到过这种情况。但后来我们突然意识到：试图消除某种观念并非明智之举。二战时期的谣言控制人员并非试图消除某种观念，而是转移阵地，传播另一种与之竞争的观点。他们没有辩称谣言本身毫无根据，而是反驳道：纳粹分子试图欺骗你们，你们会上当吗？

At first, this work in wartime propaganda seemed pretty removed from the concerns of our readers, who want their ideas to stick in business or in school. But then it dawned on us: Trying to unstick an idea is a bad strategy. The World War II rumor-control people weren’t trying to unstick an idea. They were shifting the turf and propagating a different, competing idea. Instead of arguing that the rumors themselves were baseless, they argued: The Nazis are trying to trick you. Are you going to fall for that?

这说明我们不应该试图消除那些看似牢固的想法。我们应该用更牢固的方法来对抗牢固的想法，用强力胶带对抗透明胶带。

This suggests that we shouldn’t try to unstick ideas. We should fight sticky with stickier, meet Scotch tape with duct tape.

几十年来，麦当劳一直与关于其汉堡里添加蚯蚓的谣言作斗争。起初，该公司试图澄清事实。1978年，麦当劳的官员曾公开谴责这些谣言“完全没有根据，也未经证实”。（引自《新闻周刊》 ，转自Snopes.com——都市传说辟谣圣地。）你猜猜哪个说法更让人难以忘怀：“肉饼里有蚯蚓”还是“完全没有根据，也未经证实”？

For decades, McDonald’s fought rumors that it used earthworms as filler in its burgers. At first, the company tried to unstick the idea. In 1978, McDonald’s officials had denounced the rumors as “completely unfounded and unsubstantiated.” (Quotes taken from Newsweek via Snopes.com, the mecca of urban-legend debunking.) Guess which idea was stickier: “earthworms in your meat patties” or “unfounded and unsubstantiated”?

到了1992年，麦当劳最著名的CEO雷·克罗克想出了一个更好的办法。他说：“我们可负担不起把蚯蚓磨成肉。汉堡肉饼一磅才一块五美元，而蚯蚓要六美元！” 这招真妙；克罗克用“粘”来对抗“粘”。注意其中的可信度（每磅的价格）和出人意料的元素（“我们可负担不起用蚯蚓做肉”）。他甚至可能更进一步，拿这件事开了个玩笑：“如果有人想卖给你‘蚯蚓汉堡’，你得担心他们会偷偷往里面塞牛肉。”

By 1992, Ray Kroc, McDonald’s most famous CEO, had come up with a better approach. He said, “We couldn’t afford to grind worms into meat. Hamburger costs a dollar and a half a pound, and night crawlers cost six dollars!” That’s nice; Kroc is fighting sticky with sticky. Notice the elements of credibility (dollars per pound) and unexpectedness (We can’t afford to serve you earthworms). He might even have gone a step further and made a joke about it: “If someone ever tries to sell you a WormBurger, you should worry about them secretly filling it with beef.”

另一个以恶制恶的例子发生在 20 世纪 90 年代末，当时关于恶意电脑病毒的电子邮件谣言层出不穷。根据这些谣言，如果你点击了错误的链接或打开了错误的邮件，你的电脑就会彻底崩溃。有一天，一位年轻的系统管理员厌倦了每天收到的几十条虚假警告，于是写了一篇讽刺这些谣言的文章：

Another case of fighting sticky with sticky came during the late 1990s, when e-mailed rumors about nasty computer viruses circulated constantly. According to these rumors, if you clicked the wrong link, or opened the wrong e-mail, you’d destroy your computer. One day, a young systems operator, fed up with the dozens of bogus warnings he received every day, wrote a parody of the rumors:

警告：如果您收到主题包含“Goodtimes”的电子邮件，请勿打开！！！Goodtimes 会重写您的信息。它会损坏你的硬盘。它还会扰乱任何靠近你电脑的磁盘。它会重新校准你冰箱的制冷设置，让你的冰淇淋全部融化。它会使你信用卡上的磁条消磁，并利用亚空间场谐波刮伤你的CD。它会把你的新电话号码给你的前任。它会在你的鱼缸里混入酷爱饮料。它会在停车场里随机移动你的车，让你找不到它。

Warning: if you receive an e-mail with “Goodtimes” in the subject line, DO NOT OPEN IT!!!!! Goodtimes will rewrite your hard drive. It will also scramble any disks that are even close to your computer. It will recalibrate your refrigerator’s coolness setting so all your ice cream goes melty. It will demagnetize the strips on your credit cards and use subspace field harmonics to scratch your CDs. It will give your ex your new phone number. It will mix Kool-Aid into your fish tank. It moves your car randomly around parking lots so you can’t find it.

这则恶搞视频迅速走红，其受欢迎程度堪比它所嘲讽的那些谣言。宾夕法尼亚州立大学黑泽尔顿分校的民俗学家比尔·埃利斯记录道，随着这则恶搞视频的传播，那些末日病毒警告逐渐销声匿迹。这则恶搞视频巧妙地为人们提供了一种过度渲染的警告模式。之后，如果你收到更多符合这种模式的电子邮件——充斥着夸张的言辞和危言耸听的警告——你就知道应该一笑置之，而不是忧心忡忡。这位年轻的系统操作员用一个更“粘稠”的想法对抗了一个“粘稠”的想法。

The parody became a viral hit, as popular as the rumors it mocked. Bill Ellis, a folklorist at Penn State Hazleton, has documented that, as this parody spread, the apocalyptic virus warnings faded away. The parody cleverly provided people with a schema of an overhyped warning. Afterward, if you received more e-mails that fit the schema—full of overheated language and dire warnings—you knew to laugh rather than get worried. The young systems operator fought a sticky idea with a stickier idea.

但有时候，对抗顽固观念的最佳方法并非传递信息，即便信息本身再顽固也不行。有时候，你需要的是一种强硬的行动。想想汽车时代的曙光。正如海亚格里瓦·拉奥在《市场反叛者：行动主义者如何成就或摧毁激进创新》一书中描述的那样，汽油动力汽车最初遭遇了怀疑和恐惧。人们称它为“魔鬼的玩意儿”。它引发了激烈的反对。例如，宾夕法尼亚州农民反汽车协会要求，夜间在乡村道路上行驶的汽车“每行驶一英里就必须发射一枚火箭，然后等待十分钟，直到道路畅通。如果司机看到一群马，他必须靠边停车，用毯子或防尘罩盖住汽车，防尘罩的颜色要与周围环境融为一体。”当时一位技术专家对汽油发动机会被广泛采用的想法嗤之以鼻：“你不可能让人们坐在一个爆炸物上。”

But sometimes the best way to fight a sticky idea is not with a message at all, even a stickier one. Sometimes what you need is a sticky action. Consider the dawn of the automobile era. As described in Hayagreeva Rao’s book Market Rebels: How Activists Make or Break Radical Innovations, the gasoline-powered car was greeted, at first, with skepticism and outright fear. People called it a “devilish contraption.” It spawned rabid opposition. The Farmer’s Anti-Automobile Society of Pennsylvania, for example, demanded that cars traveling at night on country roads “must send up a rocket every mile, then wait ten minutes for the road to clear. If a driver sees a team of horses, he is to pull to one side of the road and cover his machine with a blanket or dust cover that has been painted to blend into the scenery.” One technologist of the time scoffed at the idea that gasoline engines would ever be widely adopted: “You can’t get people to sit on an explosion.”

这是一个棘手的想法：简单、具体、感性。如果你是一位富有创业精神的汽车制造商，你会如何应对？嗯，愚蠢的做法莫过于试图用某种信息来“化解危机”：不妨试试告诉潜在客户，“别担心，你其实坐在一个可控的爆炸装置上。”哦，对了，所有顶尖的汽车权威人士都说你的担忧“毫无根据，缺乏证据”。

That’s a sticky idea: simple, concrete, emotional. If you were an entrepreneurial automaker, how would you combat it? Well, the dumb thing to do would be to try to “unstick it” with a message: Go ahead, try telling potential customers, “Don’t worry, you’re actually sitting on a contained explosion.” Oh, and all the top automotive authorities say your fears are “unfounded and unsubstantiated.”

汽车爱好者们决定采取行动。他们举办了一系列“可靠性竞赛”，汽车发明家们将自己的汽车聚集在一起，在耐久性、燃油经济性和爬坡能力等方面展开角逐。可靠性竞赛既是产品测试，也是一场盛会。首届竞赛于1895年举行，到1912年便停止了，因为汽车已成为人们普遍接受的社会现实。在此期间，汽车制造商让成千上万的人有机会亲眼见证汽车的潜力——让他们明白汽车并无可怕之处。（事实上，亨利·福特在可靠性竞赛中取得的成就使他得以在1903年创立福特汽车公司。）

Auto enthusiasts chose to act. They created a series of “reliability races” in which automobile inventors would bring their autos together and have them compete on endurance, fuel economy, and hill-climbing ability. Reliability contests were one part product testing and one part festival. The first contest took place in 1895, and by 1912 they had been discontinued, because cars were an accepted social reality. What happened in between was that the automakers gave thousands of people the chance to see firsthand the promise of automobiles—to see that there was nothing to fear. (In fact, the acclaim Henry Ford received from his performance in the reliability contests enabled him to launch the Ford Motor Company in 1903.)

值得注意的是，汽车爱好者们并没有试图用争论来消除恐惧；他们用行动来表达。他们选择的演示方式出乎意料（直到今天，我一直认为汽车既危险又不可靠）；确凿无疑（你看到它爬坡了吗？）；令人动容（我仿佛看到了自己成为那些自由驾驶者中的一员）；而且可信（这一切都是我亲眼所见的！）。

Note that the auto enthusiasts didn’t try to argue their way out of the fears; they acted their way out. They chose a demonstration that was Unexpected (Until today I thought cars were dangerous and unreliable); Concrete (Did you see it take that hill?); Emotional (I can see myself becoming one of those liberated drivers); and Credible (I saw it all with my own eyes!).

那么，如何才能打破一个根深蒂固的观念呢？首先，要现实一点。汽车可靠性竞赛花了十七年才建立起公众对汽车的信任。尽管雷·克罗克做出了精彩的回应，但关于麦当劳汉堡里有蚯蚓的谣言至今仍在某些地方流传。根深蒂固的观念会持续存在，正如我们在书中看到的，这有时是一件好事。但如果你面对的是一个错误的根深蒂固的观念，那它也会变成一件非常麻烦的事情。

So how do you unstick an idea? First of all, be realistic. It took seventeen years for reliability races to establish public trust in the automobile. The rumor about earthworms in McDonald’s hamburgers still circulates in some places, despite Ray Kroc’s brilliant response. Sticky ideas endure, and, as we’ve seen in the book, that can be a great thing. It can also be a real nuisance if you’re working against a sticky idea that’s false.

我们的建议很简单：用更吸引人的想法对抗那些难以摆脱的想法。我们希望我们已经为你提供了一些让你的想法更具吸引力的实用工具。如果你想让帕丽斯·希尔顿摆脱困境，或许你应该寻找另一位渴望成名的女继承人来取代她？（我们不确定女继承人选美比赛是否能奏效。）

Our advice is simple: Fight sticky ideas with stickier ideas. We hope we’ve given you some useful tools for making your ideas sticky. And if you want to unstick Paris Hilton, maybe you should be looking for another fame-hungry heiress to take her place? (We’re not sure heiress races will do the trick.)

让创意深入人心：简易参考指南

MAKING IDEAS STICK: THE EASY REFERENCE GUIDE

什么能粘住？

What Sticks?

肾脏盗窃案。万圣节糖果。电影院爆米花。

Kidney heist. Halloween candy. Movie popcorn.

粘性 = 易于理解、令人难忘，并且能够有效改变思想或行为。

Sticky = understandable, memorable, and effective in changing thought or behavior.

六项原则：成功。

SIX PRINCIPLES: SUCCESS.

实现意想不到的具体可信的情感故事。​​​​

SIMPLE UNEXPECTED CONCRETE CREDIBLE EMOTIONAL STORIES.

反派：知识的诅咒。成为 一名敲击者并不容易。创造力始于模板：用成功清单战胜诅咒。

THE VILLAIN: CURSE OF KNOWLEDGE. It’s hard to be a tapper. Creativity starts with templates: Beat the Curse with the SUCCESs checklist.

1. 简单

1. Simple

找到核心。

FIND THE CORE.

指挥官意图。确定最重要的事：“低价航空公司”。倒金字塔结构：不要把重点放在次要位置。决策瘫痪的痛苦。通过不懈的优先级排序来克服决策瘫痪：“关键在于经济形势，笨蛋。”诊所：阳光照射。名字，名字，名字。

Commander’s Intent. Determine the single most important thing: “THE low-fare airline.” Inverted pyramid: Don’t bury the lead. The pain of decision paralysis. Beat decision paralysis through relentless prioritization: “It’s the economy, stupid.” Clinic: Sun exposure. Names, names, names.

分享核心。

SHARE THE CORE.

简洁 = 核心 + 精炼。谚语：精辟的格言。视觉谚语：Palm Pilot 掌上电脑的木块。如何用简洁的信息传递强有力的信息：（1）利用现有资源：挖掘已有的认知图式。例如柚子。（2）创造一个高概念的推介：“公交车上的《虎胆龙威》”。（3）运用启发性的类比：例如迪士尼的“演员阵容”。

Simple = core + compact. Proverbs: sound bites that are profound. Visual proverbs: The Palm Pilot wood block. How to pack a lot of punch into a compact communication: (1) Using what’s there: Tap into existing schemas. The pomelo. (2) Create a high concept pitch: “Die Hard on a bus.” (3) Use a generative analogy: Disney’s “cast members.”

2. 出乎意料

2. Unexpected

请注意：惊喜。

GET ATTENTION: SURPRISE.

成功的飞行安全广播。打破常规！打破人们对核心问题的猜测机制。惊讶的表情：停顿片刻以收集信息。避免花哨的惊喜——让它“可预测”。“那个北欧人……”“下周四停课。”诊所：对外援助是否过多？

The successful flight safety announcement. Break a pattern! Break people’s guessing machines (on a core issue). The surprise brow: a pause to collect information. Avoid gimmicky surprise—make it “postdictable.” “The Nordie who …” “There will be no school next Thursday.” Clinic: Too much on foreign aid?

保持关注：兴趣。

HOLD ATTENTION: INTEREST.

制造悬念：土星环是由什么构成的？剧本如何激发好奇心？好奇心差距理论：突出知识差距。采用新闻预告的方式：“哪家本地餐馆的制冰机里有黏液？”案例：筹款。填补知识空白：鲁恩·阿利奇如何让非球迷也对NCAA橄榄球感兴趣。保持长期兴趣：“口袋收音机”和“登月”。

Create a mystery: What are Saturn’s rings made of? Screenplays as models of generating curiosity. The Gap Theory of Curiosity: Highlight a knowledge gap. Use the news-teaser approach: “Which local restaurant has slime in the ice machine?” Clinic: Fund-raising. Priming the gap: How Roone Arledge made NCAA football interesting to nonfans. Hold long-term interest: the “pocketable radio” and the “man on the moon.”

3. 混凝土

3. Concrete

帮助人们理解和记住。

HELP PEOPLE UNDERSTAND AND REMEMBER.

用寓言故事的具体方式写作。（酸葡萄心理。）将抽象概念具体化：把大自然保护协会的景观比作生态明星。提供具体的语境：亚洲教师的数学教学方法。让人物融入故事：用肥皂剧的形式教授会计课。运用记忆的“魔术贴”理论：你的想法中“钩子”越多越好。棕色眼睛，蓝色眼睛：一个“治愈”种族偏见的模拟实验。

Write with the concreteness of a fable. (Sour grapes.) Make abstraction concrete: The Nature Conservancy’s landscapes as eco-celebrities. Provide a concrete context: Asian teachers’ approach to teaching math. Put people into the story: accounting class taught with a soap opera. Use the Velcro theory of memory: The more hooks in your idea, the better. Brown eyes, blue eyes: a simulation that “cured” racial prejudice.

帮助人们协调。

HELP PEOPLE COORDINATE.

工程师与制造商：在共同的理解层面上找到共同点。设定切实可行的共同目标：我们的飞机将降落在 4-22 号跑道上。让目标落地：法拉利去迪士尼乐园。具体化的重要性：白色物品与冰箱里的白色食物。创建一个让人们能够充分发挥知识的领域：风险投资。 推介和栗色投资组合。诊所：口服补液疗法。谈论人，而不是数据：汉堡助手上门服务和“鞍背山姆”。

Engineers vs. manufacturers: Find common ground at a shared level of understanding. Set common goals in tangible terms: Our plane will land on Runway 4-22. Make it real: The Ferraris go to Disney World. Why concreteness helps: white things versus white things in your refrigerator. Create a turf where people can bring their knowledge to bear: The VC pitch and the maroon portfolio. Clinic: Oral Rehydration Therapy. Talk about people, not data: Hamburger Helper’s in-home visits and “Saddleback Sam.”

4. 可信的

4. Credible

帮助人们相信。

HELP PEOPLE BELIEVE.

诺贝尔奖得主提出的溃疡病见解，却无人相信。食肉香蕉。

The Nobel-winning ulcer insight no one believed. Flesh-eating bananas.

外部信誉。权威与反权威。帕姆·拉芬，吸烟者。

EXTERNAL CREDIBILITY. Authority and antiauthority. Pam Laffin, smoker.

内部信誉。

INTERNAL CREDIBILITY.

运用令人信服的细节。陪审员和达斯·维达牙刷。跳舞的七十三岁老人。

Use convincing details. Jurors and the Darth Vader Toothbrush. The dancing seventy-three year old.

让统计数据更易于理解。将核弹头比作BB弹。“人性化尺度”原则。史蒂芬·柯维将工作场所比作足球队的比喻。诊所：鲨鱼袭击恐慌。

Make statistics accessible. Nuclear warheads as BBs. The Human Scale principle. Stephen Covey’s analogy of a workplace to a soccer team. Clinic: Shark attack hysteria.

找一个符合“辛纳屈测试”的例子。“如果你能在那里成功，你就能在任何地方成功。” 运输宝莱坞电影：“我们承运过《哈利·波特》和你弟弟的会考。” 一位亲商的环保主义者和一家真正净化了水源并生产出可食用布料的纺织厂。

Find an example that passes the Sinatra Test. “If you can make it there, you can make it anywhere.” Transporting Bollywood movies: “We handled Harry Potter and your brother’s board exams.” A business-friendly environmentalist and the textile factory that actually purified the water that fed it—and yielded fabric that was edible.

使用可验证的资质。“先试后买。” 牛肉在哪儿？Snapple支持三K党？！教练：拆散比建设容易：补充情感能量。NBA新秀入职培训：“这些女人都得了艾滋病。”

Use testable credentials. “Try before you buy.” Where’s the beef? Snapple supports the KKK?! Coaches: It’s easier to tear down than to build up: Filling the Emotional Tank. NBA rookie orientation: “These women all have AIDS.”

5. 情感因素使人们关心。

5. Emotional MAKE PEOPLE CARE.

特蕾莎修女原则：如果我关注一个人，我就会采取行动。人们捐给罗基亚的钱比捐给非洲大片地区的钱还要多。“真相”反吸烟运动：让孩子们关心吸烟的不是健康问题，而是反抗企业的行动。

The Mother Teresa principle: If I look at the one, I will act. People donate more to Rokia than to a huge swath of Africa. The Truth anti-smoking campaign: What made kids care was not health concerns but anticorporate rebellion.

利用联想的力量。

USE THE POWER OF ASSOCIATION.

需要抵制语义延伸：“相对性”含义的稀释以及“独特”为何不再独特。将“体育精神”转变为“尊重比赛”。

The need to fight semantic stretch: the diluted meaning of “relativity” and why “unique” isn’t unique anymore. Transforming “sportsmanship” into “honoring the game.”

诉诸自身利益（而且不仅仅是基本的自身利益）。

APPEAL TO SELF-INTEREST (AND NOT JUST BASE SELF-INTEREST).

邮购广告——“当我坐在钢琴前时，他们都笑了……” WIIFY。坦佩的有线电视：想象一下它能为你带来什么。避免陷入马斯洛的“地下室”：我们错误地认为其他人比我们低劣。弗洛伊德·李和他的伊拉克军营帐篷：“我负责士气。”

Mail-order ads—“They laughed when I sat down at the piano….” WIIFY. Cable television in Tempe: Visualizing what it could do for you. Avoid Maslow’s basement: our false assumption that other people are baser than we are. Floyd Lee and his Iraq mess tent: “I’m in charge of morale.”

诉诸身份认同。

APPEAL TO IDENTITY.

拒绝爆米花机的消防员们。理解人们如何基于身份认同做出决定。（我是谁？这是什么样的情况？像我这样的人在这种情况下会怎么做？）诊所：为什么要学习代数？别惹德州：德州人从不乱扔垃圾。别忘了知识的诅咒——别像那些双钢琴的拥护者一样，想当然地认为别人和你一样在意。

The firemen who rejected the popcorn popper. Understand how people make decisions based on identity. (Who am I? What kind of situation is this? And what do people like me do in this kind of situation?) Clinic: Why study algebra? Don’t mess with Texas: Texans don’t litter. Don’t forget the Curse of Knowledge—don’t assume, like the defenders of the duo piano, that others care at the same level that you do.

6. 故事

6. Stories

让人们采取行动。

GET PEOPLE TO ACT.

故事作为模拟（告诉人们如何行动）。

STORIES AS SIMULATION (TELL PEOPLE HOW TO ACT).

心电监护仪出错的那天：护士的反应。施乐公司内部的闲聊：维修工的反应。可视化“我是如何走到这一步的”：模拟问题并解决问题。将故事用作飞行模拟器。临床：如何处理问题学生。

The day the heart monitor lied: how the nurse acted. Shop talk at Xerox: how the repairman acted. Visualizing “how I got here”: simulating problems to solve them. Use stories as flight simulators. Clinic: Dealing with problem students.

故事激励人心（给予人们行动的能量）。

STORIES AS INSPIRATION (GIVE PEOPLE ENERGY TO ACT).

体重425磅的快餐减肥者贾里德。如何发现鼓舞人心的故事？寻找三个关键情节：挑战（克服障碍）、联系（建立或重建联系）、创造力（激发新的思维方式）。讲述一个引人深思的故事：一个能帮助人们看到现有问题如何改变的故事。世界银行的斯蒂芬·丹宁：一位在赞比亚工作的卫生工作者。你可以从故事中提炼出寓意，但你无法从寓意中提炼出故事。为什么演讲者会因为人们把他们的演讲内容简化成故事而生气？

Jared, the 425-pound fast-food dieter. How to spot inspiring stories. Look for three key plots: Challenge (to overcome obstacles), Connection (to get along or reconnect), Creativity (to inspire a new way of thinking). Tell a springboard story: a story that helps people see how an existing problem might change. Stephen Denning at the World Bank: a health worker in Zambia. You can extract a moral from a story, but you can’t extract a story from a moral. Why speakers got mad when people boiled down their presentations to stories.

哪些内容能引起共鸣。

What Sticks.

用那些能粘住的东西。

USE WHAT STICKS.

好人没好报。这道理很简单，我的华生。问题出在经济上，笨蛋。洞察力的力量。为什么好的口语能力未必是好的口才：斯坦福学生的演讲练习。关于知识诅咒的最后警告。

Nice guys finish last. Elementary, my dear Watson. It’s the economy, stupid. The power of spotting. Why good speaking skills aren’t necessarily good sticking skills: Stanford students and the speech exercise. A final warning about the Curse of Knowledge.

记住SUCCESS是如何帮助人们做到以下几点的：

Remember how SUCCESs helps people to:

注意 意外
理解并记住 具体的
相信并同意 可信的
关心 情绪
行为 故事

简单易懂的语言在很多方面都很有帮助。最重要的是，它能告诉你该说什么。

Simple helps at many stages. Most important, it tells you what to say.

症状和解决方法：有关实用指导，请参阅“症状和解决方法”。

Symptoms and solutions: For practical guidance, see Symptoms and Solutions.

约翰·F·肯尼迪与弗洛伊德·李：普通人在普通情况下，如何凭借他们富有创意的想法产生深远的影响。

John F. Kennedy versus Floyd Lee: How normal people, in normal situations, can make a profound difference with their sticky ideas.

引言：什么能坚持下去？

Introduction: What Sticks?

“全面社区建设”：托尼·普罗西奥，《换句话说：呼吁基金会直言不讳》，埃德娜·麦康奈尔·克拉克基金会，2000 年。

“Comprehensive community building”: Tony Proscio, “In Other Words: A Plea for Plain Speaking in Foundations,” Edna McConnell Clark Foundation, 2000.

关于电影院爆米花的真相：霍华德·库尔茨 (Howard Kurtz) 的文章“爆米花爆炸大揭秘”（ 《华盛顿邮报》，1994 年 5 月 12 日，C1 版）对爆米花的故事进行了很好的描述。

The Truth About Movie Popcorn: A good account of the popcorn story is in Howard Kurtz, “The Great Exploding Popcorn Exposé,” Washington Post, May 12, 1994, C1.

谁毁了万圣节？：乔尔·贝斯特和杰拉尔德·T·堀内合著的《剃刀与苹果：都市传说的社会建构》（载于《社会力量》第32卷，1985年，第488-499页）讲述了万圣节糖果被污染的传说。乔尔·贝斯特是研究社会问题“建构”的社会学家之一。人们对诸如酒驾、吸毒或万圣节糖果中毒等各种问题的社会关注，并不总是与这些问题的实际发生率相符，社会学家一直试图理解社会问题是如何被定义为“问题”的。关于这个主题，还有一篇值得一读的文章，即乔尔·贝斯特的《随机暴力：我们如何谈论新的犯罪和新的受害者》（伯克利：加州大学出版社，1999年）。

Who Spoiled Halloween?: The story of the contaminated Halloween candy legend is told in Joel Best and Gerald T. Horiuchi, “The Razor Blade and the Apple: The Social Construction of Urban Legends,” Social Forces 32 (1985): 488–99. Joel Best is one of a group of sociologists who study the “construction” of social problems. Social concerns about various problems such as drunk driving, drug abuse, or poisoned Halloween candy do not always match the underlying incidence of problems, and sociologists have tried to understand how social problems become defined as “problems.” For another interesting read on this topic, see Joel Best, Random Violence: How We Talk About New Crimes and New Victims (Berkeley: University of California Press, 1999).

知识诅咒：知识诅咒的概念源于CF Camerer、G. Loewenstein和M. Weber的论文“经济环境中的知识诅咒：一项实验分析”，发表于《政治经济学杂志》 97 (1989): 1232–54。知识诅咒会随着人们专业知识的积累而加剧。帕梅拉·欣兹曾询问专家（一家移动通信公司的销售人员）……预测新手手机用户学习执行各种任务（例如，在语音信箱中存储问候语或保存一些信息并删除其他信息）所需的时间。专家们严重低估了新手用户完成这些任务所需的时间（例如，他们估计执行实际需要33分钟的功能只需13分钟），而且即使要求他们回忆自己最初学习时遇到的问题，他们的估计也没有改善。参见Pamela J. Hinds的《专家的诅咒：专家经验和去偏方法对预测新手表现的影响》，载于《实验心理学杂志：应用》5 (1999): 205–221。另见Boas Keysar、Linda E. Ginzel和Max H. Bazerman的心理学杰作《事态与心境：知识对信念的影响》，载于《组织行为与人类决策过程》 64 (1995): 283–293。具有讽刺意味的是，“知识诅咒”在经济和市场环境中已被充分证实，而人们在这些环境中本应最有动力去克服它（参见http://curse-of-knowledge.behaviouralfinance.net/）。如果你在“知识诅咒”给你造成巨大损失时都无法克服它，那么在日常生活中就更难发现并克服它了。<sup>19</sup>伊丽莎白·牛顿 (Elizabeth Newton) 于 1990 年发表了题为《沟通意图中的过度自信：已听见和未听见的旋律》的博士论文，斯坦福大学。

the Curse of Knowledge: The Curse of Knowledge concept comes from C. F. Camerer, G. Loewenstein, and M. Weber, “The Curse of Knowledge in Economic Settings: An Experimental Analysis,” Journal of Political Economy 97 (1989): 1232–54. The Curse of Knowledge increases as people gain more expertise. Pamela Hinds asked experts (salespeople at a cellular company) to predict how long novice cell-phone users would take to learn to perform various tasks (e.g., storing a greeting on voice mail or saving some messages and deleting others). Experts dramatically underestimated the amount of time it would take novice users to accomplish the tasks (i.e., they estimated that it would take thirteen minutes to perform functions that actually took thirty-three minutes), and their estimates did not improve when they were specifically asked to think about the problems they encountered while they were originally learning. See Pamela J. Hinds, “The Curse of Expertise: The Effects of Expertise and Debiasing Methods on Predicting Novice Performance,” Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied 5 (1999): 205–21. See also brilliant work in psychology by Boas Keysar, Linda E. Ginzel, and Max H. Bazerman, “States of Affairs and States of Mind: The Effect of Knowledge on Beliefs,” Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes 64 (1995): 283–93. Ironically, the Curse of Knowledge has been well documented in economic and market settings, where people should have the greatest incentives to try to overcome it (see http://curse-of-knowledge.behaviouralfinance.net/). If you can’t overcome the Curse of Knowledge when it’s costing you lots of money, it’s going to be even harder to detect and overcome it in day-to-day situations. 19 In 1990, Elizabeth Newton: L. Newton, “Overconfidence in the Communication of Intent: Heard and Unheard Melodies,” Ph.D. diss., Stanford University, 1990.

1999年，以色列研究团队Jacob Goldenberg、David Mazursky和Sorin Solomon在《营销科学》 18（1999）：333-51页发表了题为“优质广告的基本模板”的文章。其中，“图像类比”模板以视觉化的方式呈现极端的类比。例如，耐克的一则广告以一个人从高楼跳下的视角拍摄。一群消防员在楼下的街道上，准备用一只超大的耐克运动鞋来缓冲跳楼者的下落。广告语是：“在你和路面之间，有柔软的东西相伴。” 大多数获奖广告都采用了“图像类比”和“极端后果”两种手法。其他模板包括：极端情况（展示产品在不寻常情况下的性能，或将产品的属性夸大到极致）、竞争（展示产品在与另一产品的竞争中获胜，通常是在不寻常的使用情况下）、互动实验（让听众直接与产品互动——参见第 4 章中的“可测试的凭证”）和维度改变（例如，时间跳跃，显示决策的长期影响）。

In 1999, an Israeli research team: Jacob Goldenberg, David Mazursky, and Sorin Solomon, “The Fundamental Templates of Quality Ads,” Marketing Science 18 (1999): 333–51. The Pictorial Analogy template features extreme analogies rendered visually. For instance, a Nike ad is shot from the perspective of someone jumping from a tall building. A group of firemen are on the street below, preparing to cushion the jumper’s fall with an oversized Nike sneaker. The tagline reads, “Something soft between you and the pavement.” The majority of the winners are composed of Pictorial Analogy and Extreme Consequences. The other templates were Extreme Situations (in which a product is shown performing under unusual circumstances or in which a product’s attribute is exaggerated to the extreme), Competition (in which a product is shown winning in competition with another product, often in an unusual usage situation), Interactive Experiments (where listeners interact with the product directly—see “Testable Credentials” in Chapter 4), and Dimensionality Alteration (e.g., a time leap that shows the long-run implications of a decision).

1. 简单

1. Simple

赫伯·凯莱赫……曾说过：詹姆斯·卡维尔和保罗·贝加拉，《振作起来，忍气吞声，犯错后再回来》（纽约：西蒙与舒斯特出版社，2002 年），第 88 页。这是我们发现的关于政治竞选动态的最有趣的书之一，其中有一章是关于如何在政治竞选中进行沟通的，这与我们在本书中涵盖的几个原则相呼应：讲故事（“事实能说明问题，但故事能打动人心”），要感性，要独特（他们版本的“出乎意料”）。

Herb Kelleher … once told: James Carville and Paul Begala, Buck Up, Suck Up, and Come Back When You Foul Up (New York: Simon & Schuster, 2002), 88. This is one of the most interesting books we’ve found about the dynamics of political campaigns, and there’s a chapter on how to communicate in a political campaign that echoes several of the principles we cover in this book: tell stories (“facts tell but stories sell”), be emotional, and be unique (their version of “Unexpected”).

一颗健康的 17 岁心脏：乔纳森·博尔，《它跳动起来，成为了布鲁斯·默里的心脏》，《锡拉丘兹邮报》 ，1984 年 5 月 12 日。

A healthy 17-year-old heart: Jonathan Bor, “It Fluttered and Became Bruce Murray’s Heart,” Syracuse Post-Standard, May 12, 1984.

耶路撒冷，11 月 4 日：巴顿·盖尔曼，《以色列总理伊扎克·拉宾遇刺身亡》，《华盛顿邮报》，1995 年 11 月 5 日。奇普·斯坎伦收集了大量描述新闻从业技巧的在线专栏文章，其中一篇包含了此处的两个标题：www.poynter.org/column.asp?id= 52&aid=35609。

JERUSALEM, Nov. 4: Barton Gellman, “Israeli Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin Is Killed,” Washington Post, November 5, 1995. Chip Scanlon has a great collection of online columns describing the tradecraft of journalism, including one that contains the two headlines here: www.poynter.org/column.asp?id= 52&aid=35609.

倒金字塔结构由此诞生： Rich Cameron，“理解导语和倒金字塔结构是新闻学入门课程的要点”，《倒金字塔》，2003 年。参见www.cerritosjournalism.com，标题为“101—新闻写作”的部分。

the inverted pyramid arose: Rich Cameron, “Understanding the Lead and the Inverted Pyramid Structure Are Staples of Journalism 101 Classes,” The Inverted Pyramid, 2003. See www.cerritosjournalism.com, in the section entitled “101—Newswriting.”

“这很简单”：卡维尔关于记住基本原则的建议出自玛丽·马塔林和詹姆斯·卡维尔的《一切公平：爱情、战争与竞选总统》（纽约：兰登书屋，1994 年），第 244 页。克林顿关于“如果你说三件事，你就什么也没说”的引言之前的对话也是如此。

“It was simple”: Carville’s advice to remember the basics is from Mary Matalin and James Carville, All’s Fair: Love, War, and Running for President (New York: Random House, 1994), 244. The Clinton interchange leading up to the “If you say three things, you don’t say anything” quote is.

1954 年，经济学家 LJ Savage 出版了Leonard J. Savage 的《统计学基础》（纽约：Wiley，1954 年）。

In 1954, the economist L. J. Savage: Leonard J. Savage, The Foundations of Statistics (New York: Wiley, 1954).

阿莫斯·特沃斯基和埃尔达·沙菲尔：关于夏威夷圣诞假期的研究见于阿莫斯·特沃斯基和埃尔达·沙菲尔的论文《不确定性下的选择中的分离效应》，发表于《心理科学》 3 (1992): 305–9。关于讲座/外国电影/图书馆的研究见于唐纳德·A·雷德尔迈尔和埃尔达·沙菲尔的论文《在提供多种选择的情况下进行医疗决策》，发表于《美国医学会杂志》 273 (1995): 302–6。决策瘫痪现象即使对于专业人士来说也十分显著。雷德尔迈尔和沙菲尔的研究表明，当医生被迫在多种有效的治疗方案中做出选择时，他们会延迟开具处方。

Amos Tversky and Eldar Shafir: The study about the Christmas vacation in Hawaii study is in Amos Tversky and Eldar Shafir, “The Disjunction Effect in Choice Under Uncertainty,” Psychological Science 3 (1992): 305–9. The lecture/foreign film/library study is found in Donald A. Redelmeier and Eldar Shafir, “Medical Decision Making in Situations That Offer Multiple Alternatives,” Journal of the American Medical Association 273 (1995): 302–6. The phenomenon of decision paralysis is pronounced even for professionals. Redelmeier and Shafir show that doctors will delay in prescribing any treatment when they are forced to choose among multiple good treatments.

日光照射：预防措施和防护：日光照射信息 1

Sun Exposure: Precautions and Protection: Message 1 of the Sun Exposure

“理念诊所”来自http://ohioline.osu.edu/hyg-fact/5000/5550.html。47塞万提斯对谚语的定义：关于谚语的讨论基于保罗的观点。Hernandi 和 Francis Steen，“Proverbial 的热带景观：跨学科游记”，《风格》 33 (1999)：1-20。

Idea Clinic is from http://ohioline.osu.edu/hyg-fact/5000/5550.html. 47 Cervantes defined proverbs: The discussion of proverbs is based on Paul Hernandi and Francis Steen, “The Tropical Landscapes of Proverbial: A Crossdisciplinary Travelogue,” Style 33 (1999): 1–20.

“最初的 PDA 的真正障碍”：汤姆·凯利，《创新的艺术：来自美国领先设计公司 IDEO 的创造力课程》（纽约：Doubleday Currency，2001 年）。

“The real barrier to the initial PDAs”: Tom Kelley, The Art of Innovation: Lessons in Creativity from IDEO, America’s Leading Design Firm (New York: Doubleday Currency, 2001).

J FKFB INAT OUP：字母/首字母缩写词练习展示了认知心理学中的经典“组块”原理。工作记忆仅能容纳大约七个独立的信息片段。（参见乔治·米勒在《心理学评论》63 ( 1956): 81–97 中的经典描述《神奇的数字七，加减二》）。在第一个练习中，组块是字母，大多数人可以记住大约七个字母。在第二个练习中，组块是预先存储的首字母缩写词；人们可以记住大约七个首字母缩写词，即使每个缩写词包含多个字母。通过利用预先存在的信息组块，我们可以将更多信息塞进有限的注意力空间。

J FKFB INAT OUP: The letter/acronym exercise demonstrates the classic principle of “chunking” from cognitive psychology. Working memory is sufficient to hold only about seven independent pieces of information. (See George Miller’s classic description in “The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two,” Psychological Review 63 (1956): 81–97. In the first exercise, the chunks are letters, and most people can hold about seven of them. In the second, the chunks are pre-stored acronyms; people can remember around seven acronyms even though they each contain multiple letters. By taking advantage of preexisting chunks of information, we can cram more information into a limited attentional space.

心理学家对图式进行了定义：图式是认知心理学和社会心理学的标准工具之一。关于社会知觉中的图式，可参阅Susan T. Fiske和Shelley E. Taylor合著的《社会认知》（第二版，纽约：麦格劳-希尔出版社，1991年）第4章和第5章。关于认知心理学中图式研究的精彩总结，可参阅David C. Rubin的《口头传统中的记忆：史诗、民谣和数数童谣的认知心理学》（牛津：牛津大学出版社，1995年）第2章。

Psychologists define schema: Schemas are part of the standard tool kit of cognitive and social psychology. For an interesting discussion of schemas in social perception, see Chapters 4 and 5 of Susan T. Fiske and Shelley E. Taylor, Social Cognition, 2nd ed. (New York: McGraw-Hill, 1991). For an interesting summary of cognitive psychology research on schemas, see Chapter 2 of David C. Rubin, Memory in Oral Traditions: The Cognitive Psychology of Epic, Ballads, and Counting-out Rhymes (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1995).

类比是帮助人们理解图式知识的有效方法。教育心理学家发表了大量关于类比在学习新知识中价值的论文，其中理查德·梅耶（Richard Mayer）的研究尤为突出。在1980年的一篇论文中，他帮助学生学习数据库编程语言。他给一组学生提供了计算机结构的类比：“计算机的长期存储功能被描述为文件柜；排序功能被描述为办公桌上的收件箱、保存箱和丢弃箱。” 当学生遇到简单问题时，类比的作用并不明显；但当问题变得更加复杂时，接受过类比指导的学生表现比其他学生好大约两倍。参见R. Mayer，“Elaborate Techniques That Increase the Meaningfulness of Technical Text: An Experimental Test of the Learning Strategy Hypothesis”，《教育心理学杂志》 72 (1980): 770–84。

An analogy is a good way of helping people access the knowledge in a schema. Educational psychologists have published a number of papers on the value of analogies in learning new material, particularly Richard Mayer. In a 1980 paper, he helped students learn to program the language of a database. One group of students was given analogies for the structure of the computer: “The long-term storage function of the computer was described as a file cabinet; the sorting function was described as an in-basket, save basket, and discard basket on an office desk.” When students were confronting easy problems, the analogies didn’t matter much, but when the problems became more complex, students who had been given analogies were about twice as good as the others. See R. Mayer, “Elaborate Techniques That Increase the Meaningfulness of Technical Text: An Experimental Test of the Learning Strategy Hypothesis,” Journal of Educational Psychology 72 (1980): 770–84.

隐喻是另一种让人们获取知识的方式。图式。乔治·莱考夫在多部著作中阐述了深刻的隐喻如何构建我们理解和谈论世界的方式（例如，乔治·莱考夫和马克·约翰逊合著的《我们赖以生存的隐喻》[芝加哥：芝加哥大学出版社，1980]）。例如，我们谈论和思考爱情时，会将其比作一段旅程（“看看我们走了多远。”“我们正站在十字路口。”“我们偏离了轨道。”）。莱考夫比任何人都更深刻地认识到这类隐喻对我们沟通的普遍影响，但隐喻并不需要深刻或普遍才能有效地传递信息；它们只需要被相关的受众所理解，就像好莱坞讨论“高概念”时那样。

Metaphor is another way of allowing people to access the knowledge in a schema. George Lakoff has, in a number of books, shown how deep metaphors structure the way that we understand and talk about the world (e.g., George Lakoff and Mark Johnson, Metaphors We Live By [Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1980]). For example, we talk about and think of love as a journey (Look at how far we’ve come. We’re at a crossroads. We’re off track.) Lakoff has been better than anyone else at recognizing the pervasive ways in which such metaphors affect our communication, but metaphors need not be deep or pervasive to be useful in conveying a message; they just need to be shared by the relevant audience, as in the discussion of “high concept” in Hollywood.

好的隐喻是“生成性的”： DA Schon，“生成性隐喻：社会政策问题解决的视角”，载于《隐喻与思想》，第 2 版，A. Ortony 编辑，（剑桥：剑桥大学出版社，1993 年）。

Good metaphors are “generative”: D. A. Schon, “Generative Metaphor: A Perspective on Problem-Solving in Social Policy,” in Metaphor and Thought, 2nd ed., edited by A. Ortony, (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1993).

迪士尼称其员工为“演职人员”：迪士尼的例子来自：迪士尼学院，《做我们的客人：完善客户服务的艺术》（纽约：迪士尼出版社，2001 年）。

Disney calls its employees “cast members”: The examples from Disney are from: Disney Institute, Be Our Guest: Perfecting the Art of Customer Service (New York: Disney Editions, 2001).

2. 出乎意料

2. Unexpected

一位名叫凯伦·伍德的空乘人员：凯伦·伍德曾是西南航空公司的空乘人员，这绝非偶然。参见凯文·弗雷伯格和杰基·弗雷伯格合著的《疯狂！西南航空公司疯狂的商业和个人成功秘诀》（德克萨斯州奥斯汀：巴德出版社，1996 年），第 209-210 页。

A flight attendant named Karen Wood: It’s no accident that Karen Wood was a flight attendant on Southwest Airlines. See Kevin Freiberg and Jackie Freiberg, Nuts! Southwest Airlines’ Crazy Recipe for Business and Personal Success (Austin, Tex.: Bard Press, 1996), 209–10.

“惊讶的眉毛”：保罗·埃克曼和华莱士·V·弗里森，《揭开面孔的面具：从面部线索识别情绪指南》（新泽西州恩格尔伍德崖：普伦蒂斯-霍尔出版社，1975年）。惊讶在心理学中是一个研究不足的课题，因为它处于心理学各学科之间的空白地带。研究注意力和学习的认知研究者认为它过于情绪化；研究愤怒、恐惧和厌恶等情绪的社会心理学家则认为它过于认知化。然而，你也可以认为惊讶是最重要的情绪，因为它在控制注意力和学习方面发挥着重要作用。

“the surprise brow”: Paul Ekman and Wallace V. Friesen, Unmasking the Face: A Guide to Recognizing Emotions from Facial Clues (Englewood Cliffs, N.J.: Prentice-Hall, 1975). The role of surprise is an understudied topic in psychology, because it falls in the cracks between psychological disciplines. Cognitive researchers who study attention and learning find it too emotional; social psychologists who study emotions such as anger, fear, and disgust find it too cognitive. Yet you could make the case that surprise is the most important emotion because of its role in controlling attention and learning.

“PHRAUG 和 TAYBL”： Bruce WA Whittlesea 和 Lisa D. Williams，“差异归因假设 II：期望、不确定性、惊讶和熟悉感”，《实验心理学杂志：学习、记忆和认知》 27 (2001)：14-33。

“PHRAUG and TAYBL”: Bruce W. A. Whittlesea and Lisa D. Williams, “The Discrepancy-Attribution Hypothesis II: Expectation, Uncertainty, Surprise, and Feelings of Familiarity,” Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition 27 (2001): 14–33.

熨烫衣服的诺迪：吉姆·柯林斯和杰里·I·波拉斯，《基业长青：富有远见的公司的成功习惯》（纽约：哈珀商业出版社，1994 年），第 118 页。

The Nordie who ironed: Jim Collins and Jerry I. Porras, Built to Last: Successful Habits of Visionary Companies (New York: HarperBusiness, 1994), 118.

75新闻学入门：埃夫隆的记述见洛林·格伦农和玛丽·莫勒，《那些能够……去教书的人！庆祝那些做出贡献的教师》（加州伯克利：野猫峡谷出版社，1999 年），第 95-96 页。

75 Journalism 101: Ephron’s account is in Lorraine Glennon and Mary Mohler, Those Who Can … Teach! Celebrating Teachers Who Make a Difference (Berkeley, Calif.: Wildcat Canyon Press, 1999), 95–96.

美国人坚持认为：来自对外援助诊所的信息 1 来自www.ipjc.org/journal/fall02/nick_mele.htm。

Americans persist in thinking: Message 1 from the foreign aid Clinic is from www.ipjc.org/journal/fall02/nick_mele.htm.

民意调查显示，大多数美国人：请参阅马里兰大学国际政策态度项目 (PIPA) 进行的调查。

Polls suggest that most Americans: See surveys conducted by the Program for International Policy Attitudes (PIPA) at the University of Maryland.

撒哈拉以南非洲地区：截至2001年，撒哈拉以南非洲地区和整个亚洲地区每年分别获得略高于10亿美元的援助。假设美国有2.8亿人口，12瓶软饮料以每瓶0.33美元的优惠价格出售，总价值为11亿美元。每年一部电影，每张电影票8美元，总价值为22.4亿美元。

All of sub-Saharan Africa: As of 2001, sub-Saharan Africa and the entire region of Asia each received a little more than $1 billion per year. Assuming that there are 280 million Americans, twelve soft drinks at a bargain price of $.33 equals $1.1 billion. One movie per year at $8 per movie ticket equals $2.24 billion.

如何保持 读者 的注意力？如何激发并保持读者的兴趣是教育心理学家们经常讨论的话题，他们致力于让孩子们对教科书保持兴趣。他们的许多研究结果与本书探讨的主题不谋而合：当文本能够唤起行动和图像（具体性）或情感（情感性）时，或者当内容新颖（出乎意料，尽管我们认为新颖性更容易吸引兴趣而非持续兴趣）时，孩子们会更投入。其他一些话题则超出了本书的讨论范围。例如，当教材内容个性化时（例如，他们会更关注包含自己或朋友名字的数学题），孩子们会更投入，尽管这种个性化策略难以普遍应用。然而，教育心理学领域的大多数研究都存在局限性，它们只关注文本的句子或段落层面的特征，而忽略了更广泛的问题，例如如何让读者阅读整节、整章甚至整本书。西奥迪尼关于神秘感的观察和洛温斯坦的好奇心差距理论将极大地丰富这一重要的研究领域。有关教育研究的综述，请参阅 Suzanne Hidi 的文章“兴趣及其作为学习心理资源的贡献”，载于《教育研究评论》 60 (1990): 549–71。

How do we keep people’s attention?: Arousing people’s interest and keeping it are topics that have been discussed frequently among educational psychologists who want to keep kids engaged in textbooks. Many of their findings are consistent with the topics we consider in this book: Kids are more engaged when texts evoke action and images (Concrete) or emotions (Emotional) or when something is novel (Unexpected, though in our view novelty is more likely to attract interest than to sustain it). Other topics go beyond what we discuss here. For example, kids are more engaged when material is personalized (e.g., they pay more attention to math problems that feature their names or the names of their friends), though this customization strategy is hard to apply in general. But most of the research in educational psychology has been limited in that it focuses only on sentence- or paragraph-level characteristics of texts rather than on broader questions, such as how do you get readers to read whole sections, chapters, or books. Cialdini’s observations on mystery and Loewenstein’s gap theory of curiosity would add a great deal to this important area of research. For a review of educational research, see Suzanne Hidi, “Interest and Its Contribution as a Mental Resource for Learning,” Review of Educational Research 60 (1990): 549–71.

几年前，罗伯特·西奥迪尼（Robert Cialdini）写了一篇文章：西奥迪尼的文章是为心理学教授写的，但对所有教师来说都是极好的建议。参见罗伯特·B·西奥迪尼，《激发学生兴趣的最佳秘诀是什么？答案就在标题里》，《社会与临床心理学杂志》 24（2005）：22-29。

A few years ago, Robert Cialdini: Cialdini wrote his article for psychology professors, but it’s excellent advice for all teachers. See Robert B. Cialdini, “What’s the Best Secret Device for Engaging Student Interest? The Answer Is in the Title,” Journal of Social and Clinical Psychology 24 (2005): 22–29.

麦基说：“好奇心”：罗伯特·麦基，《故事：实质、结构、风格和剧本创作原则》（纽约：ReganBooks出版社，1997年）。麦基对电影中噱头式的惊喜和能带来结局的惊喜之间的区别也有着深刻的见解：“我们总是可以通过切换到观众意想不到的画面或偏离他们预期的画面来震撼他们。”继续下去。”但他表示，“真正的惊喜”发生在预期与实际发生之间突然出现合理差距之时。这种合理性源于顿悟，揭示了先前隐藏的真相。

McKee says, “Curiosity”: Robert McKee, Story: Substance, Structure, Style and the Principles of Screenwriting (New York: ReganBooks, 1997). McKee also has good insight into the difference between gimmicky surprise and surprise that leads to resolution in movies: “We can always shock filmgoers by cutting to something it doesn’t expect to see or away from something it expects to continue.” But he says that “true surprise” happens when a legitimate gap is suddenly revealed between what we expect and what actually happens. The legitimacy comes from a rush of insight, revealing some truth that was previously hidden.

1994 年，乔治·洛温斯坦 (George Loewenstein) 发表了题为《好奇心的心理学：回顾与重新诠释》的文章，刊登于《心理学公报》 116 (1994): 75–98。这是一篇精彩的文章，回顾了数十年的心理学研究。

In 1994, George Loewenstein: George Loewenstein, “The Psychology of Curiosity: A Review and Reinterpretation,” Psychological Bulletin 116 (1994): 75–98. This is a brilliant article that reviews decades of psychological research.

严重的停车问题：停车研究由 Charles F. Gettys、Rebecca M. Pliske、Carol Manning 和 Jeff T. Casey 完成，“人类行为生成绩效评估”，《组织行为与人类决策过程》 39 (1987): 23–51。

serious parking problem: The parking study is by Charles F. Gettys, Rebecca M. Pliske, Carol Manning, and Jeff T. Casey, “An Evaluation of Human Act Generation Performance,” Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes 39 (1987): 23–51.

此前，电视节目曾报道过：鲁恩·阿利奇的故事出自他的自传《鲁恩：回忆录》（纽约：哈珀柯林斯出版社，2003年）。备忘录中的引文是：

Heretofore, television has done: The Roone Arledge story is from his autobiography, Roone: A Memoir (New York: HarperCollins, 2003). The quote from the memo is.

在东京的废墟中：索尼的历史来自约翰·内森的《索尼：私生活》（波士顿：霍顿·米夫林出版社，1999 年）。

In the rubble of Tokyo: The Sony history is from John Nathan, Sony: The Private Life (Boston: Houghton Mifflin, 1999).

“如果人们 喜欢 好奇心”：洛温斯坦，《好奇心心理学》，86。

“If people like curiosity”: Loewenstein, “Psychology of Curiosity,” 86.

3. 混凝土

3. Concrete

“商业流行语生成器”：该流行语生成器由南卡罗来纳大学艾肯分校的 W. Davis Folsom 发明。参见http://highered.mcgraw-hill.com/sites/0072537892/student_view0/business_jargon_exercise.html。

“Business Buzzword Generator”: The buzzword generator was invented by W. Davis Folsom at the University of South Carolina-Aiken. See http://highered.mcgraw-hill.com/sites/0072537892/student_view0/business_jargon_exercise.html.

具体性有助于我们避免这些问题：具体概念的优势在心理学领域随处可见。具体概念更容易记忆。或许对这一证据最精彩的总结来自杜克大学认知心理学家大卫·鲁宾的著作。鲁宾多年来致力于研究文化元素——史诗、民谣和童谣——如何在人与人之间、代代相传。他的著作《口头传统中的记忆》精辟地总结了人文学科和心理学领域的研究成果。具体元素最容易在人与人之间传承，因为它们最容易理解和记忆。参见大卫·C·鲁宾，《口头传统中的记忆：史诗、民谣和数数童谣的认知心理学》（牛津：牛津大学出版社，1995）。

Concreteness helps us avoid these problems: The advantages of concrete ideas show up across psychology. Concrete ideas are more memorable. Perhaps the most interesting summary of this evidence is from a book by David Rubin, a cognitive psychologist at Duke University who has spent years trying to understand how aspects of culture—epic sagas, ballads, and children’s rhymes—propagate from person to person and generation to generation. His book Memory in Oral Traditions is a masterful summary of work across the humanities and psychology. Concrete elements are the most likely to survive transmission from one person to another because they are the easiest to understand and remember. See David C. Rubin, Memory in Oral Traditions: The Cognitive Psychology of Epic, Ballads, and Counting-Out Rhymes (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1995).

具体的概念也更容易理解。在教育研究领域，马克·萨多斯基、欧内斯特·戈茨及其同事发表了许多相关研究成果。一些有趣的论文表明，具体的概念更容易理解、更容易记忆，而且也更有趣。参见 Mark Sadoski、Ernest T. Goetz 和 Maximo Rodriguez 合著的《引人入胜的文本：具体性对四种文本类型中可理解性、兴趣和回忆的影响》，载于《教育心理学杂志》 92 (2000): 85–95。

Concrete ideas are also more understandable. In education research, Mark Sadoski, Ernest Goetz, and colleagues have published a number of interesting papers illustrating that concrete ideas are more understandable, memorable, and, as a side benefit, more interesting. See Mark Sadoski, Ernest T. Goetz, and Maximo Rodriguez, “Engaging Texts: Effects of Concreteness on Comprehensibility, Interest, and Recall in Four Text Types,” Journal of Educational Psychology 92 (2000): 85–95.

耶鲁大学研究员埃里克·哈夫洛克： EA Havelock，《柏拉图序言》（马萨诸塞州剑桥：哈佛大学出版社，1963 年）。

Yale researcher Eric Havelock: E. A. Havelock, Preface to Plato (Cambridge, Mass.: Harvard University Press, 1963).

佐治亚州立大学的两位教授： Carol W. Springer 和 A. Faye Borthick，“商业模拟在入门会计中培养批判性思维：原理、设计和实施”，会计教育问题19 (2004)：277–303。

Two professors from Georgia State University: Carol W. Springer and A. Faye Borthick, “Business Simulation to Stage Critical Thinking in Introductory Accounting: Rationale, Design, and Implementation,” Issues in Accounting Education 19 (2004): 277–303.

棕色眼睛，蓝色眼睛：简·艾略特的反偏见模拟描述摘自PBS Frontline纪录片《分裂的阶级》。该片是PBS历史上点播率最高的节目之一，并于1985年荣获艾美奖杰出信息、文化或历史节目奖。您可以在www.pbs.org/wgbh/pages/frontline/shows/divided/etc/view.html上观看。

Brown Eyes, Blue Eyes: The description of Jane Elliott’s antiprejudice simulation is taken from a PBS Frontline documentary, “A Class Divided.” It’s one of the most frequently requested programs in the station’s history, winning an Emmy for Outstanding Informational, Cultural, or Historical Programming in 1985. It can be seen on the Web at www.pbs.org/wgbh/pages/frontline/ shows/divided/etc/view.html.

十年和二十年后进行的研究：菲尔·津巴多，《心理学与生活》，第 12 版（伊利诺伊州格伦维尤：斯科特·福雷斯曼，1985 年），第 634 页。

Studies conducted ten and twenty years later: Phil Zimbardo, Psychology and Life, 12th ed. (Glenview, Ill.: Scott, Foresman, 1985), 634.

一位名叫贝丝·贝奇基（Beth Bechky）的研究员： BA Bechky，“跨越职业”

A researcher named Beth Bechky: B. A. Bechky, “Crossing Occupational

《边界：生产车间的沟通与学习》，斯坦福大学博士论文，1999 年。

Boundaries: Communication and Learning on a Production Floor,” Ph.D. diss., Stanford University, 1999.

727 必须能容纳 131 名乘客： Jim Collins 和 Jerry I. Porras，《基业长青 ：富有远见的公司的成功习惯》（纽约：HarperBusiness，1994 年），第 93 页。

The 727 must seat 131 passengers: Jim Collins and Jerry I. Porras, Built to Last: Successful Habits of Visionary Companies (New York: HarperBusiness, 1994), 93.

《法拉利去迪士尼乐园》： Stone-Yamashita 与 HP 的合作基于 Victoria Chang 和 Chip Heath 的《Stone-Yamashita 和 PBS：商学院案例研究》，斯坦福大学商学院案例研究 SM119 (2004)。

The Ferraris Go to Disney World: The Stone-Yamashita work with HP is based on Victoria Chang and Chip Heath, “Stone-Yamashita and PBS: A Case at the Graduate School of Business,” Stanford University Graduate School of Business case study SM119 (2004).

卡普兰和Go Computers：这个例子出自杰瑞·卡普兰的佳作《创业：硅谷历险记》（波士顿：霍顿·米夫林出版社，1995年）。这本书生动地描绘了创业者在创建公司过程中所面临的日常不确定性和种种挑战，堪称我们读过的最佳案例之一。而且，它还非常幽默风趣。

Kaplan and Go Computers: This example is from a great book by Jerry Kaplan: Start-Up: A Silicon Valley Adventure (Boston: Houghton Mifflin, 1995). It’s one of the best accounts we’ve read about the day-to-day uncertainty and struggle of being an entrepreneur and building a company. And it’s very funny.

我的听众似乎很紧张：同上，25-26。

My audience seemed tense: Ibid., 25–26.

他们的投资价值：卡普兰的公司，后来更名为Go Computers，最终失败了，因为当时的技术不足以支持基于笔的计算机。尽管如此，基于笔的计算理念却如此深入人心——堪称当时的“口袋收音机”——以至于其他几家公司也纷纷效仿。除了 Kaplan 的公司之外，其他公司也纷纷涌现，吸引了风险投资（以及熟练的工程人才）来追求这项技术。

Their investment valued: Kaplan’s company, later named Go Computers, ultimately failed because the technology of its time wasn’t sufficient to support pen-based computers. Nonetheless, the idea of pen-based computing was so sticky—the “pocketable radio” of its generation—that several other firms in addition to Kaplan’s arose and attracted venture capital dollars (and skilled engineering talent) to pursue the technology.

腹泻是主要致死原因之一：口服补液疗法诊所的第一条信息来自PSI，这是一家位于华盛顿特区的非营利组织，致力于为低收入人群提供创新性的健康服务。请访问www.psi.org/our_programs/products/ors.html。

Diarrhea is one of the leading killers: Message 1 of the Oral Rehydration Therapy Clinic is from PSI, a Washington, D.C., nonprofit organization that is doing innovative work on health for low-income populations. See www.psi.org/our_programs/products/ors.html.

“你知道吗？”：口服补液疗法诊所的第二条信息出自詹姆斯·格兰特之口。格兰特曾在联合国儿童基金会任职，他所做的改革被认为拯救了超过2500万儿童的生命。例如，疫苗接种率从20%提高到了80%。戴维·伯恩斯坦在其著作《如何改变世界：社会企业家与新思想的力量》（牛津：牛津大学出版社，2004年）中讲述了这个非凡的故事。请参阅该书以获取引文。
